topics.title: Application-Layer Paradigms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAMT632 - BASICS OF COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Application-Layer Paradigms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Application-Layer Paradigm (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> An *application-layer paradigm* is the fundamental architectural model that defines **how two or more communicating end-system processes interact, request, and deliver network services** at the application layer (Layer 7) of the TCP/IP reference model. The two canonical paradigms mandated by the KTU 2024 Scheme are the **Client–Server Paradigm** and the **Peer-to-Peer (P2P) Paradigm**, with the **Hybrid Paradigm** as their commercial evolution.

In any networked application (Web, Email, File Transfer, Video Streaming), the paradigm dictates:
- **Who initiates** communication (the *active opener* / *client*).
- **Who waits** for incoming requests (the *passive opener* / *server*).
- **How data, processing, and bandwidth** are distributed among participating hosts.

### 1.2 The Three Paradigms at a Glance

| Paradigm | Initiator | Resource Owner | Symmetry |
|---|---|---|---|
| Client–Server | Client | Centralized Server | Asymmetric |
| Pure Peer-to-Peer | Any Peer | Every Peer | Symmetric |
| Hybrid (Client–Server + P2P) | Any Peer | Server indexes, Peers transfer | Mixed |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 1 – Client–Server = Restaurant 🍽️**
> You (the *client*) walk in, sit down, and a single kitchen (the *server*) prepares **every** dish. If 500 customers arrive simultaneously, the kitchen becomes the bottleneck. The chef never eats their own food; there is strict role separation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 2 – Peer-to-Peer = Potluck Dinner 🥘**
> Every guest (peer) **brings a dish AND eats dishes**. The more guests arrive, the *more food* is available for everyone. The system scales with its users — this is why BitTorrent downloads **speed up** as more peers join (a phenomenon called *swarming*).

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 3 – Hybrid = Co-Operative Supermarket 🛒**
> A central directory (the *server*) tells you **who has what**, but the actual groceries are handed to you **peer-to-peer** at the checkout. Modern applications like **Skype, WhatsApp, and Spotify** use this hybrid model.

### 1.4 KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchors

The KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly lists the following under *Module 1 – Application Layer*:
- **Application architectures**: Client–Server, Peer-to-Peer, Hybrid.
- **Process communication**: Sockets, addressing (IP + Port), Transport service requirements (Reliable Data Transfer, Throughput, Timing, Security).
- **Standard service metrics**: Bandwidth in **bits/second (bps)**, Latency in **milliseconds (ms)**, Packet loss as a **percentage (%)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Port Anchors to Memorize for KTU:**
> - HTTP → **Port 80**, HTTPS → **Port 443**, FTP Control → **21**, FTP Data → **20**
> - SMTP → **25**, POP3 → **110**, IMAP → **143**, DNS → **53**, SSH → **22**, Telnet → **23**
> These are governed by **IANA (Internet Assigned Numbers Authority)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Client–Server Paradigm — Step-by-Step Logic

- **Step 1 — Role Definition:** The *server* is an always-on host with a well-known, **static IP address** (e.g., 142.250.193.206 for Google). Clients are intermittently connected and may have *dynamic* IP addresses assigned by DHCP.
- **Step 2 — Request Initiation:** The client process actively opens a socket (typically a 5-tuple: *Protocol, Source IP, Source Port, Dest IP, Dest Port*) and sends a request.
- **Step 3 — Concurrency Handling:** Modern servers use multi-threading, multi-process forking, or epoll/event loops to handle **thousands of simultaneous client requests**.
- **Step 4 — Data Center Scalability:** Google, Meta, and Amazon deploy massive **Data Centers** housing **hundreds of thousands of servers** to absorb peak load.
- **Step 5 — Why this paradigm?** Centralized control → easy security, easy billing, easy auditing, and a *single source of truth*.

**Disadvantages:**
- **Single Point of Failure (SPOF)** — if the server crashes, everyone is disconnected.
- **Scalability bottleneck** — the server's uplink (access link bandwidth) caps aggregate throughput.
- **High capital expenditure (CAPEX)** — racks, cooling, power, and redundant links are expensive.

### 2.2 The Peer-to-Peer (P2P) Paradigm — Step-by-Step Logic

- **Step 1 — Symmetric Role:** Each *peer* acts as **both client and server** (called a *servent* — a portmanteau of **serv**er + cli**ent**).
- **Step 2 — Self-Scaling:** Peers contribute *upload bandwidth* to the swarm, so aggregate capacity **grows linearly with the number of peers**.
- **Step 3 — Minimal Infrastructure:** No dedicated server farm is needed; a lightweight *tracker* (in BitTorrent) or *Distributed Hash Table (DHT)* (in Kademlia) coordinates peer discovery.
- **Step 4 — Why this paradigm?** Excellent for **file distribution** (BitTorrent), **voice/video calls** (early Skype), and **content delivery** where demand is bursty and distributed.

**Disadvantages:**
- **Security and trust** — peers can be malicious (poisoning, sybil attacks).
- **ISP friction** — many ISPs throttle P2P traffic.
- **Dependence on churn** — if peers leave, download speed collapses.

### 2.3 The Hybrid Paradigm — The Commercial Compromise

Modern apps blend both worlds. Examples:
- **Skype / WhatsApp voice calls:** Signaling (login, contact lookup) uses **central servers**; voice/video traffic flows **P2P** (or via a relay if NAT blocks P2P).
- **Napster (historical):** Central server held the *index*; actual MP3s transferred **P2P**.
- **Spotify:** Music catalog lives on **servers**, but discovery and social features are P2P-flavoured.

### 2.4 Process Communication: Sockets, Addressing & Transport Needs

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Socket = the Application-Layer Door between Process and Transport Layer.**
> A socket is the **API endpoint** through which an application process injects data into (or extracts data from) the TCP/UDP stack.

For two processes on different hosts to communicate, **four addressing ingredients** are mandatory:
1. **IP address of source host**
2. **Port number of source process** (ephemeral, typically 1024–65535)
3. **IP address of destination host**
4. **Port number of destination process** (well-known, e.g., 80 for HTTP)

**Transport Service Requirements Matrix (KTU Favourite):**

| Application | Data Loss Tolerance | Throughput Need | Time-Sensitive? |
|---|---|---|---|
| File Transfer (FTP) | **No loss — Lossless** | Elastic | No |
| E-mail (SMTP) | **No loss — Lossless** | Elastic | No |
| Web (HTTP) | **No loss — Lossless** | Elastic | No (mostly) |
| Audio/Video Streaming | **Tolerant** (small glitches OK) | Audio ≥ 5 kbps, Video ≥ 100 kbps | **Yes — Low latency critical** |
| Interactive Games | **Tolerant** | ≥ 50 kbps | **Yes — Ultra-low latency** |
| Financial Trading | **No loss** | Elastic | **Yes — Microseconds matter** |

- **TCP (Transmission Control Protocol)** → reliable, in-order, congestion-controlled → used for lossless apps.
- **UDP (User Datagram Protocol)** → unreliable, low-overhead, no congestion control → used for loss-tolerant, time-sensitive apps.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Equation / Rule | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | File Distribution Time (Client–Server) | $D_{CS} \;=\; \max\!\left\{\dfrac{N \cdot F}{u_s},\; \dfrac{F}{d_{\min}}\right\}$ | seconds | $N$ = clients, $F$ = file size, $u_s$ = server upload, $d_{\min}$ = min client download |
| 2 | File Distribution Time (P2P) | $D_{P2P} \;=\; \max\!\left\{\dfrac{F}{u_s},\; \dfrac{F}{d_{\min}},\; \dfrac{N \cdot F}{u_s \;+\; \textstyle\sum_{i=1}^{N} u_i}\right\}$ | seconds | Self-scaling due to $\sum u_i$ |
| 3 | Minimum Client–Server Server Upload Needed | $u_s \;\geq\; N \cdot F / t_{req}$ | bits/sec | For SLA: meet deadline $t_{req}$ |
| 4 | Minimum P2P Aggregate Upload Needed | $u_s \;+\; \sum u_i \;\geq\; N \cdot F / t_{req}$ | bits/sec | Distributes across swarm |
| 5 | Round-Trip Time (RTT) | $\text{RTT} \;=\; t_{\text{trans}} \;+\; t_{\text{prop}} \;+\; t_{\text{queue}}$ | ms | $t_{\text{trans}} = L/R$, $t_{\text{prop}} = d/s$ |
| 6 | Transmission Delay | $t_{\text{trans}} = L / R$ | s | $L$ = packet length (bits), $R$ = link rate (bps) |
| 7 | Propagation Delay | $t_{\text{prop}} = d / s$ | s | $d$ = link length (m), $s$ = propagation speed (~2×10⁸ m/s in fibre) |
| 8 | Throughput Bottleneck | $R_{\text{eff}} = \min\!\left\{R_{\text{link}_1}, R_{\text{link}_2}, \dots\right\}$ | bps | Min-link rule of end-to-end path |
| 9 | Number of Simultaneously Open Sockets (TCP 4-tuple uniqueness) | $2^{32} \times 2^{16} \times 2^{16}$ | – | (Src-IP, Dst-IP, Src-Port, Dst-Port) tuples |
| 10 | Bandwidth-Delay Product | $\text{BDP} = R \times \text{RTT}$ | bits | Determines TCP window size needed |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Always use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of `\vert` for absolute-value bars in KTU formula sheets** to prevent Markdown table breakage in our rendered notes.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **CDN Engineering (Akamai, Cloudflare):** Optimises the *server* leg of Client–Server distribution by placing caches geographically close to clients.
- **Blockchain (Bitcoin, Ethereum):** Pure P2P ledger with no central server; uses **Gossip Protocol** for state propagation.
- **5G MEC (Multi-access Edge Computing):** Hybrid paradigm — central orchestrator + P2P device-to-device (D2D) offload.
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT):** SCADA systems use Client–Server; modern mesh sensor networks use P2P (e.g., Zigbee, Thread).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Client–Server File Distribution Time

> **Problem Setup (KTU Classic):**
> Distribute a file of size $F$ bits to $N$ peers. The server has upload capacity $u_s$, each client (peer $i$) has download $d_i$ and upload $u_i$. The minimum client download is $d_{\min} = \min\{d_i\}$.

**Step 1 — Server's transmission ceiling.**
The server can push at most $u_s$ bits/second. To ship $N$ copies of an $F$-bit file, the total bits the server must push are $N \cdot F$. Therefore the *server-bound* time is:

$$\frac{N \cdot F}{u_s} \quad \text{seconds}$$

**Step 2 — Each peer's download ceiling.**
Each individual client cannot receive faster than its own access link download rate. The *slowest* client (with rate $d_{\min}$) gates the entire distribution:

$$\frac{F}{d_{\min}} \quad \text{seconds}$$

**Step 3 — Combine via the *max* operator.**
The distribution completes when **both** constraints are satisfied, i.e., the slowest of the two determines the wall-clock time:

$$\boxed{\,D_{CS} \;=\; \max\!\left\{\dfrac{N \cdot F}{u_s},\; \dfrac{F}{d_{\min}}\right\}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** As $N \to \infty$, the first term dominates → $D_{CS} \to \infty$ linearly. Hence **Client–Server scales poorly** for viral file distribution.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Peer-to-Peer File Distribution Time

**Step 1 — Server (seed) ceiling.**
The original server (called the *seed* in BitTorrent jargon) can push $u_s$ bits/sec. The slowest single client still cannot receive faster than $d_{\min}$, so the same two terms from §3.1 still apply:

$$\frac{F}{u_s}, \quad \frac{F}{d_{\min}}$$

**Step 2 — Aggregate swarm upload (the new P2P term).**
Once clients have received **at least one bit** of the file, they can *re-upload* it to other peers. The total bits that the *entire swarm* must push is again $N \cdot F$. But the aggregate upload capacity of the swarm is $u_s + \sum_{i=1}^{N} u_i$. Hence the *aggregate-upload* time is:

$$\frac{N \cdot F}{u_s + \sum_{i=1}^{N} u_i}$$

**Step 3 — Combine via *max* over all three constraints.**

$$\boxed{\,D_{P2P} \;=\; \max\!\left\{\dfrac{F}{u_s},\; \dfrac{F}{d_{\min}},\; \dfrac{N \cdot F}{u_s + \textstyle\sum_{i=1}^{N} u_i}\right\}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** With $N$ large and peer upload rates comparable to $u_s$, the third term shrinks roughly as $1$ (because $\sum u_i \approx N \cdot u_s$ if all peers are symmetric), giving $D_{P2P} \approx F/u_s$ — a **constant** independent of $N$. This is the *self-scaling* miracle of P2P.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Minimum Server Upload to Meet a Deadline

Suppose the KTU paper states: *"Distribute a 2 Gbit file to 1000 clients in at most 2000 seconds. Each client has $d_{\min} = 10$ Mbps. Find the minimum server upload $u_s$."*

**Step 1 — Identify the dominant term.**
For large $N$, the first term of $D_{CS}$ dominates. Check:

$$\frac{F}{d_{\min}} = \frac{2 \times 10^9}{10 \times 10^6} = 200 \text{ seconds} \quad \text{(client side is fast)}$$

So the deadline is gated by the *server*:

$$\frac{N \cdot F}{u_s} \;\leq\; t_{req}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $u_s$.**

$$u_s \;\geq\; \frac{N \cdot F}{t_{req}} = \frac{1000 \times 2 \times 10^9}{2000} = 1 \times 10^{9} \text{ bps}$$

**Step 3 — Convert units.**

$$u_s \;\geq\; 1 \text{ Gbps}$$

$$\boxed{\,u_s^{\min} = 1 \; \text{Gbps}\,}$$

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU Exam Favourite)

| Scenario | $N$ (peers) | Symmetric $u = d$ | $D_{CS}$ | $D_{P2P}$ | Speed-up Factor |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Small swarm | 10 | 1 Mbps | $\max\{10F/u_s, F/u\} \approx 10F/u_s$ | $\max\{F/u_s, F/u, 10F/(u_s+10u)\} \approx F/u$ | **10×** |
| Medium swarm | 100 | 1 Mbps | $\approx 100F/u_s$ | $\approx F/u_s$ (if $u_s \approx u$) | **~100×** |
| Viral swarm | 10⁶ | 5 Mbps | $\approx 10^{6} F / u_s$ | $\approx F / u_s$ (asymptotic) | **~10⁶×** |

### 3.5 Worked Numerical — KTU 2024 Typical Question

> **Q (adapted):** Distribute a $F = 1$ Gbit file from one server to $N = 100$ peers. Each peer has $d_i = 5$ Mbps download and $u_i = 1$ Mbps upload. Server has $u_s = 100$ Mbps. Compute $D_{CS}$ and $D_{P2P}$.

**Client–Server:**

$$D_{CS} = \max\!\left\{\frac{100 \times 1 \times 10^9}{100 \times 10^6},\; \frac{1 \times 10^9}{5 \times 10^6}\right\}$$

$$= \max\!\left\{1000 \text{ s},\; 200 \text{ s}\right\} = \boxed{1000 \text{ seconds} \approx 16.67 \text{ min}}$$

**Peer-to-Peer:**

$$D_{P2P} = \max\!\left\{\frac{F}{u_s},\; \frac{F}{d_{\min}},\; \frac{N \cdot F}{u_s + \sum u_i}\right\}$$

$$= \max\!\left\{\frac{10^9}{10^8},\; \frac{10^9}{5 \times 10^6},\; \frac{100 \times 10^9}{10^8 + 100 \times 10^6}\right\}$$

$$= \max\!\left\{10 \text{ s},\; 200 \text{ s},\; \frac{10^{11}}{2 \times 10^8}\right\} = \max\!\left\{10,\; 200,\; 500\right\} = \boxed{500 \text{ seconds} \approx 8.33 \text{ min}}$$

**Interpretation:** P2P is **2× faster** than Client–Server for this scenario. As $N$ grows, the gap widens dramatically.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Client–Server Architecture (Block-Level Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    c1[Client A Browser] -- HTTP GET request over TCP port 80 --> lb[Load Balancer Layer 4]
    c2[Client B Mobile App] -- HTTPS POST over TCP port 443 --> lb
    c3[Client C Desktop Client] -- HTTPS GET over TCP port 443 --> lb
    lb -- round robin or least connections --> s1[Web Server Node 1]
    lb -- sticky session --> s2[Web Server Node 2]
    lb -- weighted routing --> s3[Web Server Node 3]
    s1 -- SQL query --> db1[(Primary Database)]
    s2 -- read replica --> db2[(Read Replica DB)]
    s3 -- cache lookup --> cache[(Redis Cache Cluster)]
    db1 -- async replication --> db2
    cache -- miss fallback --> db1
```

**Reading the diagram:** Three clients connect to a single **Load Balancer**, which fans out to three redundant **Web Servers** that share a **Database Cluster** and a **Cache Cluster**. This is the canonical **scalable Client–Server** topology used by every major web service.

### 4.2 Pure Peer-to-Peer Architecture (BitTorrent-style Swarm)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    tr[Tracker Server] -. peer list lookup .-> p1
    tr -. peer list lookup .-> p2
    tr -. peer list lookup .-> p3
    tr -. peer list lookup .-> p4
    p1[Peer A has chunk 1 2 3] -- chunk 4 download --> p2
    p2[Peer B has chunk 2 3 4] -- chunk 3 upload --> p1
    p3[Peer C has chunk 1 4] -- chunk 2 download --> p1
    p4[Peer D has chunk 3] -- chunk 1 upload --> p3
    p2 -- chunk 1 download --> p4
    p3 -- chunk 4 upload --> p4
```

**Reading the diagram:** The **Tracker** is a *lightweight coordinator* (not a content server). It tells each peer *who else has what*. The actual chunks flow **directly between peers** as bidirectional solid arrows. Note that Peer A *downloads from* p2 and p3 *and uploads to* p2 — true symmetric roles.

### 4.3 Hybrid Paradigm (Skype/WhatsApp-like)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    u1[User Alice] -- 1 login and contact lookup --> sigs[Signaling Server TLS 443]
    u2[User Bob] -- 1 login and contact lookup --> sigs
    u3[User Carol] -- 1 login and contact lookup --> sigs
    sigs -- 2 returns Bobs IP and port to Alice --> u1
    sigs -- 2 returns Alices IP and port to Bob --> u2
    u1 -- 3 direct RTP audio video UDP --> u2
    u2 -- 3 direct RTP audio video UDP --> u1
    u1 -- 3 direct RTP audio video UDP --> u3
    sigs -- presence heartbeat --> u1
    sigs -- presence heartbeat --> u2
```

**Reading the diagram:** Steps 1 and 2 use a **centralized signaling server** (Client–Server leg). Step 3 shows **direct peer-to-peer media** using RTP/UDP. Step 4 shows the **persistent presence/heartbeat** back to the signaling server. The dotted line from Carol to Alice illustrates a *fallback relay* path through the signaling server when NAT traversal fails.

### 4.4 Decision Flowchart: Choosing the Right Paradigm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([New Networked Application]) --> q1{Is content owner a single entity with strict control needs?}
    q1 -- Yes --> cs[Use Client Server Paradigm]
    q1 -- No --> q2{Is the workload file or content distribution heavy?}
    q2 -- Yes --> q3{Does your business model need a central authority for billing, auth, or analytics?}
    q3 -- Yes --> hyb[Use Hybrid Paradigm: server for control plane and P2P for data plane]
    q3 -- No --> p2p[Use Pure P2P Paradigm: BitTorrent or blockchain style]
    q2 -- No --> cs2[Use Client Server for low latency interactive apps]
    cs --> done([Deploy behind load balancer and CDN])
    hyb --> done
    p2p --> done
    cs2 --> done
```

**Reading the diagram:** This is the **KTU-recommended decision tree** for selecting a paradigm based on workload characteristics and business needs.

### 4.5 Process Communication Socket Anatomy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    app[Application Process PID 4521] -- send and recv system calls --> sock[Socket API Endpoint]
    sock -- pushes TCP segment or UDP datagram --> tcp[TCP or UDP Transport Layer]
    tcp -- IP packet --> ip[IP Network Layer]
    ip -- frames --> nic[NIC Driver]
    nic -- electrical or optical signals --> phys[Physical Medium]
    sock -. bound to .-> port1[Local Port 49152 ephemeral]
    sock -. connects to .-> port2[Remote Port 443 HTTPS]
```

**Reading the diagram:** A *socket* is the **software door** between an application and the OS networking stack. It is identified locally by an **ephemeral port** (≥ 1024) and connects to a **well-known port** on the remote host. The `send()` and `recv()` system calls are the API surface KTU students must know.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Q1, CO1, Bloom Level: Remember/Understand, 3 Marks]**

**Q1. Compare the Client–Server and Peer-to-Peer application architectures. List two advantages and two disadvantages of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Client–Server | Peer-to-Peer |
|---|---|---|
| **Architecture** | Centralized server, distributed clients | Symmetric, no dedicated server |
| **Resource Owner** | Server holds all data | Each peer contributes data |
| **Advantages** | (i) Centralized security and control, (ii) Easy backup and auditing | (i) Excellent scalability — self-scaling with $N$, (ii) No single point of failure, no expensive server farm |
| **Disadvantages** | (i) Single point of failure, (ii) Server bandwidth becomes bottleneck as $N$ grows | (i) Security and trust issues, (ii) Performance depends on peer *churn* |

> **Examiner's Tip:** Always draw a **comparison table** in the answer book — KTU examiners reward structured answers with full 3 marks.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Q2, CO1, Bloom Level: Understand, 3 Marks]**

**Q2. Explain the role of *sockets* in process-to-process communication over TCP/IP. Why are both IP address and port number required to uniquely identify a process?**

**Model Answer:**

A **socket** is the application-layer endpoint of a two-way communication link between two processes running on different hosts. It is the API through which the application process hands data to (or receives data from) the TCP or UDP layer of the operating system.

A unique conversation between two processes requires **four identifiers** (the *socket 4-tuple*):
- **Source IP address** of the sending host,
- **Source port number** of the sending process (assigned *ephemeral*, typically ≥ 1024),
- **Destination IP address** of the receiving host,
- **Destination port number** of the receiving process (a *well-known* port, e.g., 80 for HTTP, 443 for HTTPS).

**Why both are needed:** The IP address alone identifies only the *host machine*, but a single host runs hundreds of processes (browser, mail client, Skype). The port number disambiguates the specific *process* inside that host. Together they identify the precise end of the conversation.

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> **Instructions to students:** KTU 2024 ESE for Module 1 typically offers an internal choice between two 14-mark questions. Answer **either** the OR option in full.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Set 1**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, CO1 & CO2, Bloom Level: Apply/Analyze, 14 Marks]**

**Q (a)** *(7 Marks)* — Derive the expression for the **minimum file distribution time** in a *Client–Server* architecture, clearly defining all variables. *(Bloom: Apply)*

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** A server with upload capacity $u_s$ bits/second, $N$ client peers, file size $F$ bits, minimum client download rate $d_{\min}$ bits/second.

**Derivation (each step carries 1 mark under KTU valuation):**

**Step 1:** The server must transmit $N$ copies, one to each client, so it must push $N \cdot F$ bits in total.

**Step 2:** The minimum time the server needs to do this, bounded by its upload rate, is:

$$\frac{N \cdot F}{u_s} \quad \text{seconds}$$

**Step 3:** Each individual client cannot receive faster than its access link download speed. The slowest client (with $d_{\min}$) governs this.

**Step 4:** The minimum time any single client needs to download the file is:

$$\frac{F}{d_{\min}} \quad \text{seconds}$$

**Step 5:** The overall distribution completes only when **both** constraints are satisfied. The bottleneck (slowest) determines wall-clock time:

$$\boxed{\,D_{CS} = \max\!\left\{\dfrac{N \cdot F}{u_s},\; \dfrac{F}{d_{\min}}\right\}\,}$$

**Conclusion:** As $N$ increases, the first term grows linearly while the second remains constant, so the server's upload link is the bottleneck for large $N$.

---

**Q (b)** *(7 Marks)* — A server with upload rate $u_s = 30$ Mbps must distribute a $F = 15$ Gbit file to $N = 1500$ peers. Each peer has download rate $d_i = 2$ Mbps. Calculate (i) the **Client–Server distribution time**, and (ii) the **minimum server upload rate** required to finish the distribution in **2000 seconds** (deadline-based engineering design). *(Bloom: Apply/Analyze)*

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Client–Server distribution time:**

Substitute values into the derived formula:

$$D_{CS} = \max\!\left\{\dfrac{N \cdot F}{u_s},\; \dfrac{F}{d_{\min}}\right\}$$

$$= \max\!\left\{\dfrac{1500 \times 15 \times 10^9}{30 \times 10^6},\; \dfrac{15 \times 10^9}{2 \times 10^6}\right\}$$

$$= \max\!\left\{750{,}000 \text{ s},\; 7500 \text{ s}\right\}$$

$$= \boxed{750{,}000 \text{ seconds} \approx 8.68 \text{ days}}$$

The first term dominates because the server's uplink is severely oversubscribed (1500 clients × 2 Mbps demand = 3 Gbps demand, but only 30 Mbps supply).

**(ii) Minimum server upload for 2000 s deadline:**

Set $D_{CS} \leq 2000$ s and isolate $u_s$:

$$\frac{N \cdot F}{u_s} \leq 2000 \;\Rightarrow\; u_s \geq \frac{N \cdot F}{2000}$$

$$u_s \geq \frac{1500 \times 15 \times 10^9}{2000} = 1.125 \times 10^{10} \text{ bps}$$

$$= \boxed{11.25 \text{ Gbps}}$$

This illustrates why a single server *cannot* viral-distribute — a **Data Center** or **CDN** with 11.25 Gbps uplink is needed, or — as in (b)-next — a P2P scheme.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Set 2** *(Internal Choice Alternative)*

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, CO1 & CO2, Bloom Level: Apply/Analyze, 14 Marks]**

**Q (a)** *(7 Marks)* — Derive the expression for the **minimum file distribution time in a Pure P2P architecture**, explaining how peer upload capacity contributes to the formula. *(Bloom: Apply)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Server (seed) capacity term:** The original server (seed) can upload at $u_s$, so any single peer must wait at least $F / u_s$ seconds to get the *first* chunk:

$$\frac{F}{u_s}$$

**Step 2 — Slowest client download term:** As before, the slowest client cannot receive faster than $d_{\min}$:

$$\frac{F}{d_{\min}}$$

**Step 3 — Aggregate swarm upload term (the P2P advantage):** Once a peer has even one chunk, it can *re-upload* it. The total bits the *entire swarm* must push to deliver $N$ complete copies is $N \cdot F$. The aggregate upload bandwidth of the swarm is:

$$u_s + \sum_{i=1}^{N} u_i$$

So the aggregate time is:

$$\frac{N \cdot F}{u_s + \sum_{i=1}^{N} u_i}$$

**Step 4 — Combine via max()** because the slowest of the three constraints wins:

$$\boxed{\,D_{P2P} = \max\!\left\{\dfrac{F}{u_s},\; \dfrac{F}{d_{\min}},\; \dfrac{N \cdot F}{u_s + \sum_{i=1}^{N} u_i}\right\}\,}$$

**Key engineering insight:** If $N$ is large and peer upload rates are non-negligible, the third term shrinks (because the denominator grows), giving $D_{P2P}$ that **scales sublinearly** with $N$, unlike Client–Server.

---

**Q (b)** *(7 Marks)* — For the same scenario as Question A(b) but using a **P2P** scheme where each peer also has $u_i = 1$ Mbps upload, compute the **P2P distribution time** and comment on the scalability improvement. *(Bloom: Analyze)*

**Model Solution:**

Substitute into the P2P formula with $u_s = 30$ Mbps, $d_{\min} = 2$ Mbps, $u_i = 1$ Mbps for each of $N = 1500$ peers, $F = 15$ Gbit.

**Compute each term:**

$$\frac{F}{u_s} = \frac{15 \times 10^9}{30 \times 10^6} = 500 \text{ s}$$

$$\frac{F}{d_{\min}} = \frac{15 \times 10^9}{2 \times 10^6} = 7500 \text{ s}$$

$$\frac{N \cdot F}{u_s + \sum u_i} = \frac{1500 \times 15 \times 10^9}{30 \times 10^6 + 1500 \times 1 \times 10^6} = \frac{2.25 \times 10^{13}}{1.53 \times 10^9}$$

$$= 14{,}705.88 \text{ s}$$

**Take the maximum:**

$$D_{P2P} = \max\!\left\{500,\; 7500,\; 14{,}705.88\right\} = \boxed{14{,}705.88 \text{ seconds} \approx 4.08 \text{ hours}}$$

**Comment on scalability (valuing 1 mark of the 7):**

Although 14,705 s *sounds* worse than 7500 s, this is because the aggregate term is still the bottleneck due to the asymmetric access links ($u_i = 1$ Mbps ≪ $d_i = 2$ Mbps). In real deployments (fibre broadband, 5G FWA), $u_i$ and $d_i$ are comparable (e.g., 100/100 Mbps fibre), in which case $\sum u_i \gg u_s$ and the third term collapses to:

$$\frac{N \cdot F}{\sum u_i} \approx \frac{N \cdot F}{N \cdot u} = \frac{F}{u}$$

giving $D_{P2P} \approx F / d_{\min}$, **independent of $N$**. This is the *self-scaling* property that makes P2P ideal for content delivery networks.

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the `max{}` operator** — writing only the *sum* or *first* term. KTU awards 0 marks if the bottleneck analysis is missing.
> 2. **Unit conversion errors** — KTU papers often mix Mbps, MB, and GB. Always convert to **bits per second** *before* substituting. 1 Byte = 8 bits, 1 GB = 10⁹ bytes (SI) or 2³⁰ bytes (IEC). **Read the question carefully.**
> 3. **Ignoring the third P2P term** — many students write only the first two terms they memorised from the Client–Server case, costing **3 marks** out of 7.
> 4. **Not defining variables** — KTU mandates a "Given" or "Notation" section before substitution. Skipping it costs **1 mark**.
> 5. **Confusing upload ($u_i$) and download ($d_i$)** — the formula's $u$ always refers to **upload** bandwidth, not download.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — Module 1 / Application-Layer Paradigms — Rapid-Revision Bullets**

- ✅ The two **canonical paradigms** are **Client–Server** and **Peer-to-Peer (P2P)**. The **Hybrid** paradigm combines their strengths.
- ✅ **Client–Server** = centralized server with **static IP**, **well-known port**, and **always-on** availability.
- ✅ **P2P** = **symmetric roles**; each peer is a *servent* (server + client).
- ✅ **Hybrid** = centralized *control plane* (signaling, auth, discovery) + distributed *data plane* (media, file chunks).
- ✅ **Sockets** are the **API endpoints** of process communication. The *4-tuple* is (Src-IP, Dst-IP, Src-Port, Dst-Port).
- ✅ **Well-known ports** are 0–1023; **Registered** are 1024–49151; **Ephemeral** are 49152–65535.
- ✅ **TCP** is used for **lossless** apps (HTTP, FTP, SMTP); **UDP** is used for **loss-tolerant, time-sensitive** apps (VoIP, video streaming, DNS, gaming).
- ✅ The **Client–Server file distribution time** is $D_{CS} = \max\{N F / u_s, F / d_{\min}\}$.
- ✅ The **P2P file distribution time** is $D_{P2P} = \max\{F / u_s, F / d_{\min}, N F / (u_s + \sum u_i)\}$.
- ✅ **Self-scaling**: As $N$ grows, $D_{P2P}$ approaches a **constant** $F / d_{\min}$, while $D_{CS}$ grows **linearly** — a critical KTU comparison point.
- ✅ **Transport service requirements** are: **Reliable Data Transfer, Throughput, Timing, Security**.
- ✅ **Examples** to memorise: HTTP/HTTPS → Web, SMTP/POP3/IMAP → Email, DNS → Name resolution, FTP → File transfer, BitTorrent → P2P file sharing, Skype/WhatsApp → Hybrid voice/video.
- ✅ **BitTorrent terminology**: *Seed* = peer with full file, *Leech* = peer downloading, *Tracker* = peer discovery server, *Swarm* = all peers sharing a torrent, *Chunk* = piece of file.
- ✅ **Architectural trend (2024–2026)**: Almost all real-world large-scale apps use **Hybrid** (CDN + P2P + Edge Compute) — the *pure* Client–Server and *pure* P2P models are pedagogical limits.
- ✅ **KTU 2024 Examiner Focus**: Weightage of this topic in ESE is typically **14 marks** (1 full question) out of 60, plus 3–6 marks in Part A. Mastery of the $D_{CS}$ and $D_{P2P}$ derivations is **non-negotiable**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Client-Server Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAMT632 - BASICS OF COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Client-Server Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Client-Server Model (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> The **Client-Server Model** is a distributed application architecture that partitions tasks or workloads between the providers of a resource or service, called **Servers**, and the service requesters, called **Clients**. It is a foundational network computing paradigm where a centralized host (server) responds to service requests initiated by multiple distributed end-user hosts (clients) over a network infrastructure governed by standard protocols such as **TCP/IP**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus context, **PEAMT632 – Basics of Computer Networks** introduces Client-Server Applications as the primary mode of *distributed computing* that enables resource sharing, centralized management, and scalable service delivery across the Internet and enterprise intranets.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Restaurant Kitchen"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Box — Think of a Restaurant**
>
> Imagine a busy restaurant:
> - **The Kitchen (Server)** has all the resources — chefs, raw ingredients, ovens, recipes. The kitchen is **always open**, **waits for orders**, and **processes many tables simultaneously**.
> - **The Customer (Client)** walks in, reads the menu, places an order, and waits. The customer **initiates the request** and **consumes the result** (the served food).
> - **The Waiter (Protocol / Network)** carries the order from the customer to the kitchen and brings the food back.
> - **The Menu (Service Interface / API)** is a defined contract: the customer only sees what's offered, not how the food is cooked.
>
> Just as a single kitchen serves hundreds of customers, **one server** can serve **thousands of clients**. The customer never enters the kitchen — analogous to how a client never directly accesses server internals.

## 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU High-Yield Glossary)

> [!NOTE]
> **Mandatory Definitions to Memorize for KTU ESE**

| Term | Definition | KTU Significance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Client** | A program/process that initiates communication by sending a request to a server. | Most exam questions test the *role* of the client. |
| **Server** | A program/process that listens for requests on a well-known port, processes them, and returns a response. | Always **passive** at start; **waits** for inbound calls. |
| **Service Port** | A **16-bit logical identifier** (range **0 to 65535**) used by transport layer (TCP/UDP) to multiplex services. | HTTP=**80**, HTTPS=**443**, FTP=**21**, SSH=**22**. |
| **Socket** | The combination of **IP Address + Port Number** that uniquely identifies a network endpoint. | Core concept for socket programming questions. |
| **Request-Response Pattern** | Synchronous communication where each client request is matched by exactly one server response. | Basis of REST, HTTP, RPC. |
| **Middleware** | Software layer that sits between the OS and the application, providing services like RPC, queuing, and authentication. | Frequently asked in 7-mark sub-questions. |
| **Daemon** | A background server process (e.g., `httpd`, `sshd`, `mysqld`) that runs continuously listening for requests. | Linux server terminology. |
| **Well-Known Port** | Ports numbered **0 – 1023** reserved for system/root-level services as defined by IANA. | Critical for viva and short-answer questions. |
| **Registered Port** | Ports numbered **1024 – 49151** assigned to user applications by IANA. | |
| **Dynamic / Ephemeral Port** | Ports numbered **49152 – 65535** assigned temporarily by the OS to clients. | |

## 1.4 Why Client-Server? (The Engineering Motivation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Engineering Reasons for Adopting Client-Server Architecture:**
> 1. **Centralized Data Management** — Single source of truth; no data duplication.
> 2. **Resource Sharing** — Expensive hardware (printers, databases, supercomputers) shared by many.
> 3. **Scalability** — Server can be upgraded independently of clients.
> 4. **Security & Access Control** — Authentication happens at the server.
> 5. **Maintainability** — Application upgrades deployed once on the server reach all clients.
> 6. **Backup & Reliability** — Backups taken at one central location.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Characteristics — Server vs. Client

> [!NOTE]
> **The Server Side**
> - Always running, **passive listener** (waits for connection).
> - Bound to a **well-known port** (e.g., HTTP server on TCP 80).
> - Uses the `listen()` and `accept()` system calls.
> - Has a **privileged IP** or domain name.
> - High-performance hardware: multi-core CPU, ECC RAM, RAID storage.
> - Capable of handling **multiple clients simultaneously** (concurrency).
> - Examples: Web server (Apache, Nginx), Database server (MySQL, PostgreSQL), Mail server (Postfix).

> [!NOTE]
> **The Client Side**
> - Active initiator, **transient** (connects only when needed).
> - Uses a **dynamic / ephemeral port** assigned by the OS.
> - Calls `connect()` to the server's socket.
> - Limited hardware resources (laptop, mobile, IoT).
> - Single user interface per session.
> - Examples: Web browser, Email client (Outlook, Thunderbird), Mobile banking app.

## 2.2 Communication Pattern — The Request-Response Cycle

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Step-by-Step Logical Flow of a Client-Server Transaction:**
>
> 1. **Client Bootstraps** — The client application starts and prepares to request a service.
> 2. **Service Discovery** — The client resolves the server's hostname via **DNS lookup**, obtaining the server's IP address.
> 3. **Socket Creation** — The client OS allocates an **ephemeral port** and creates a socket bound to `Client_IP : Ephemeral_Port`.
> 4. **Connection Establishment** — Using TCP, the client performs the **three-way handshake** (`SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK`) to the server's well-known port.
> 5. **Request Transmission** — The client sends a service request over the established connection (e.g., `GET /index.html HTTP/1.1`).
> 6. **Server Processing** — The server daemon receives the request, forks/threads a worker, and processes it.
> 7. **Response Transmission** — The server sends back the result (HTML page, JSON data, error code).
> 8. **Connection Termination** — TCP **four-way handshake** (`FIN → ACK → FIN → ACK`) gracefully closes the connection (or kept alive for HTTP/1.1).
> 9. **Client Rendering** — The client application interprets the response and displays it to the user.

## 2.3 Categories of Servers (KTU 2024 Classification)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Server Types — Must-Know for KTU**

| Server Type | Primary Function | Standard Protocol | Port |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Web Server** | Serves static/dynamic web pages. | HTTP / HTTPS | 80 / 443 |
| **File Server** | Stores and shares files across LAN. | SMB, NFS, FTP | 445, 2049, 21 |
| **Database Server** | Manages structured data, query processing. | SQL (proprietary) | 3306 (MySQL), 5432 (PostgreSQL) |
| **Mail Server** | Sends, receives, stores email. | SMTP, POP3, IMAP | 25, 110, 143 |
| **Print Server** | Manages shared printers on a network. | IPP, LPD | 631, 515 |
| **Application Server** | Hosts business logic (ERP, CRM). | RMI, RPC, gRPC | varies |
| **Proxy Server** | Intermediary for client requests (caching, filtering). | HTTP | 8080 |
| **DNS Server** | Translates domain names to IP addresses. | DNS | 53 |
| **DHCP Server** | Dynamically assigns IP addresses. | DHCP | 67/68 |
| **Authentication Server** | Verifies user credentials (RADIUS, LDAP). | RADIUS, LDAP | 1812, 389 |

## 2.4 Concurrency Models (KTU 14-Mark Favourite)

> [!NOTE]
> **Iterative Server** — Handles **one client at a time**. New clients wait in a queue.
> - **Pros:** Simple, no race conditions.
> - **Cons:** Poor scalability; a slow client blocks everyone.
> - **Analogy:** A single-counter bank with one teller.

> [!NOTE]
> **Concurrent Server** — Handles **multiple clients in parallel**.
> - **Three Sub-models:**
>   1. **Process-based** (e.g., `fork()` in Linux) — Each client gets a new OS process. Heavy but isolated.
>   2. **Thread-based** (e.g., `pthread_create()`) — Lighter than processes; shares memory space.
>   3. **Event-driven / Asynchronous** (e.g., `epoll`, `select`, Node.js) — Single thread, non-blocking I/O; used in **Nginx**, **Redis**, modern web servers.
> - **Analogy:** A supermarket with many cashiers; or a single highly-efficient cashier with a queuing system.

## 2.5 Stateful vs. Stateless Servers

> [!NOTE]
> **Stateful Server** — Maintains session information (e.g., logged-in user, shopping cart). Examples: FTP, Telnet, PHP sessions.
>
> **Stateless Server** — Treats each request as independent; no session memory. Examples: **HTTP** (original), **DNS**, REST APIs.
>
> **Trade-off:** Stateless = horizontally scalable (load balancing easy); Stateful = better user experience but harder to scale.

## 2.6 KTU Formula / Metric Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quantitative Formulas Used in Client-Server Problems**

$$
\text{Socket Address} = (\text{IP Address},\ \text{Port Number})
$$

$$
\text{Number of Theoretical Sockets per Host} = 2^{16} = 65{,}536
$$

$$
\text{Throughput (Little's Law applied to a server)} = \frac{\text{Concurrent Users} \times \text{Avg. Service Rate}}{\text{Avg. Think Time} + \text{Avg. Service Time}}
$$

$$
\text{Response Time} = T_{\text{transmit}} + T_{\text{propagate}} + T_{\text{queue}} + T_{\text{process}}
$$

$$
T_{\text{transmit}} = \frac{\text{File Size (bits)}}{\text{Bandwidth (bps)}}
$$

$$
\text{Memory Used by Concurrent Server} = N_{\text{clients}} \times S_{\text{per-connection}}
$$

where $N_{\text{clients}}$ is the number of simultaneous connections and $S_{\text{per-connection}}$ is the stack + buffer size (typically **8 KB – 64 KB per thread**).

| Parameter | Unit | Typical Value |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Well-Known Port Range** | dimensionless | **0 – 1023** |
| **Registered Port Range** | dimensionless | **1024 – 49151** |
| **Ephemeral Port Range** | dimensionless | **49152 – 65535** |
| **TCP Three-Way Handshake Latency** | ms | **1.5 × RTT** |
| **Apache/Nginx default threads** | count | **150 – 500** |
| **HTTP keep-alive timeout** | seconds | **15 – 75 s** |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Client-Server model is the **backbone of modern computing**:
- **Cloud Computing (AWS, Azure, GCP)** — Users run thin clients; massive data centers act as servers.
- **Banking Systems** — ATM/phone = client; core banking engine = server.
- **E-Commerce (Amazon, Flipkart)** — Browser/mobile app = client; product catalog service = server.
- **IoT** — Smart sensor = client; analytics dashboard = server.
- **Gaming** — Console/PC = client; multiplayer match server = server.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation: Connection Setup Cost for a Web Server

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (KTU-Style Numerical):**
> A client wants to download a web page consisting of **3 embedded objects** (1 HTML + 2 images) from a server located at a network distance with **Round Trip Time (RTT) = 100 ms**. The base HTML file size is negligible (transmission time ≈ 0), and each image is small enough to fit in a single TCP segment. Calculate the total response time under:
> 1. **Non-persistent HTTP** (one TCP connection per object).
> 2. **Persistent HTTP without pipelining**.
> 3. **Persistent HTTP with pipelining**.

### 3.1.1 Case A — Non-Persistent HTTP

> Each object requires its own TCP connection, hence its own **3-way handshake** costing $1.5 \times \text{RTT}$ for setup, plus $0.5 \times \text{RTT}$ for the request-response transaction.

$$
T_{\text{non-persistent}} = N \times (1.5 \cdot \text{RTT} + 0.5 \cdot \text{RTT})
$$

Substituting $N = 3$ objects and $\text{RTT} = 100\ \text{ms}$:

$$
T_{\text{non-persistent}} = 3 \times (1.5 \times 100 + 0.5 \times 100)\ \text{ms}
$$

$$
= 3 \times (150 + 50)\ \text{ms}
$$

$$
= 3 \times 200\ \text{ms}
$$

$$
= 600\ \text{ms}
$$

**Interpretation:** 6 RTTs total — extremely inefficient for pages with many small objects.

### 3.1.2 Case B — Persistent HTTP without Pipelining

> One TCP connection is established and reused. The handshake is paid **once**, but each subsequent request must wait for the previous response.

$$
T_{\text{persistent, no-pipeline}} = \underbrace{1.5 \cdot \text{RTT}}_{\text{initial handshake}} + N \times (0.5 \cdot \text{RTT})
$$

$$
= 1.5 \times 100 + 3 \times (0.5 \times 100)
$$

$$
= 150 + 150
$$

$$
= 300\ \text{ms}
$$

**Interpretation:** Halved the time; 4.5 RTTs total. This is the **default for HTTP/1.1**.

### 3.1.3 Case C — Persistent HTTP with Pipelining

> The client sends all 3 requests back-to-back, then waits once for all 3 responses.

$$
T_{\text{persistent, pipelined}} = 1.5 \cdot \text{RTT} + 0.5 \cdot \text{RTT}
$$

$$
= 1.5 \times 100 + 0.5 \times 100
$$

$$
= 150 + 50
$$

$$
= 200\ \text{ms}
$$

**Interpretation:** Only 2 RTTs total — the most efficient. The **head-of-line blocking** is the trade-off.

### 3.1.4 Summary Table

| HTTP Mode | RTTs | Time (ms) | Efficiency |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| Non-Persistent | 6.0 | 600 | Lowest |
| Persistent (no pipeline) | 4.5 | 300 | Medium |
| Persistent (pipelined) | 2.0 | 200 | Highest |

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Tiers of Client-Server Architecture

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Multi-Tier Architecture — KTU Favourite 7-Mark Topic**

$$
\text{Total Tiers} = \text{Presentation Tier} + \text{Logic Tier} + \text{Data Tier} + \cdots
$$

### 3.2.1 1-Tier (Standalone)

$$
\text{Application} = \text{UI} + \text{Logic} + \text{Data} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{All on one machine.}
$$

- Example: MS Excel on a PC.
- No network involved.

### 3.2.2 2-Tier (Client-Server Proper)

$$
\text{Client} \xrightarrow{\text{Direct Query}} \text{Server (DB)}
$$

- **Thick client**: Client holds the business logic; server only holds data.
- Example: A desktop banking app directly querying a remote Oracle database via **SQL*Net**.
- *Drawback:* Heavy client install; logic duplication; hard to update.

### 3.2.3 3-Tier (Web-Based Standard)

$$
\text{Clients} \rightarrow \text{Application Server} \rightarrow \text{Database Server}
$$

- **Thin client**: Browser is just a renderer.
- Example: Amazon.com — Browser → Tomcat → MySQL.
- *Advantage:* Centralized business logic; clients are platform-independent.

### 3.2.4 N-Tier (Distributed Enterprise)

$$
\text{Clients} \rightarrow \text{Web Server} \rightarrow \text{App Server}_1 \rightarrow \text{App Server}_2 \rightarrow \cdots \rightarrow \text{DB}
$$

- Adds **load balancers**, **caching layers (Redis)**, **message queues (Kafka)**, **microservices**.
- Example: Netflix microservices architecture.
- Maximum scalability and fault isolation.

### 3.2.5 Comparison Matrix

| Architecture | Logic Location | Client Type | Example | Scalability |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **1-Tier** | Local | None | MS Excel | None |
| **2-Tier** | Client | Thick | Desktop DB app | Poor |
| **3-Tier** | App Server | Thin | Web app | High |
| **N-Tier** | Distributed | Ultra-thin | Netflix, Google | Very High |

## 3.3 Analytical Derivation: Server Throughput via Little's Law

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A web server observes an average of **$L = 200$** active requests in the system at any given time. The average lifetime of a request in the system is **$W = 2\ \text{seconds}$**. Calculate the server's throughput (requests per second) using **Little's Law**.

$$
L = \lambda \cdot W
$$

Solving for the throughput $\lambda$:

$$
\lambda = \frac{L}{W}
$$

$$
\lambda = \frac{200\ \text{requests}}{2\ \text{seconds}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\lambda = 100\ \text{requests/second}}
$$

**Engineering Insight:** If the marketing team claims the site gets **100,000 hits/sec**, the server must be scaled up by **1000×** — usually achieved via load balancing across **$N$** identical servers, where $N$ is chosen such that the per-server $\lambda$ falls within the sustainable range.

$$
N_{\text{servers}} = \left\lceil \frac{\lambda_{\text{total}}}{\lambda_{\text{per-server}}} \right\rceil
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Derivation: Concurrent Server Memory Footprint

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A thread-based concurrent server allocates **$S = 64\ \text{KB}$** of stack + buffer per connection. It must support **$C = 10{,}000$** concurrent users. Calculate the total memory consumed by the connection pool.

$$
M_{\text{total}} = C \times S
$$

$$
M_{\text{total}} = 10{,}000 \times 64\ \text{KB}
$$

$$
= 640{,}000\ \text{KB}
$$

$$
= 625\ \text{MB}
$$

**Insight:** This is why **asynchronous event-driven servers (Nginx, Node.js)** are preferred for C10K problems — they handle 10,000+ connections in a single thread, drastically reducing memory.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Generic Client-Server System

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram 1 — High-Level Functional Architecture of a Client-Server Application**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ClientSide["CLIENT SIDE - USER DEVICE"]
        UI["Presentation Layer - UI - GUI Web or Mobile App"]
        CL["Client Logic - Business Validation and Caching"]
        CS["Client Socket - IP Address plus Ephemeral Port - eg 49152"]
    end

    subgraph Net["NETWORK - TCP IP INFRASTRUCTURE"]
        NW1["Router and Switches - Packet Forwarding"]
        DNS["DNS Resolver - Name to IP Translation"]
        FW["Firewall and Load Balancer - Security and Distribution"]
    end

    subgraph ServerSide["SERVER SIDE - DATA CENTER"]
        LS["Listener Socket - Bound to Well Known Port - eg 80 or 443"]
        AD["Acceptor - Accepts Incoming Connections"]
        WK["Worker Pool - Threads or Processes - Concurrent Handling"]
        APP["Application Logic - Business Rules"]
        DB["Database Tier - Persistent Storage"]
    end

    UI --> CL
    CL --> CS
    CS -->|HTTP Request| NW1
    CS -.->|Step 0 - DNS Query| DNS
    DNS -.->|IP Returned| CS
    NW1 --> FW
    FW --> LS
    LS --> AD
    AD --> WK
    WK --> APP
    APP <-->|SQL Queries| DB
    DB --> APP
    APP --> WK
    WK --> AD
    AD --> LS
    LS -->|HTTP Response| FW
    FW --> NW1
    NW1 -->|Response Packets| CS
    CS --> CL
    CL --> UI
```

## 4.2 Sequence Diagram — End-to-End HTTP Transaction

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram 2 — Time-Sequenced Interaction Between Browser Client and Web Server (DNS + TCP + HTTP)**

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User
    participant BR as Browser Client
    participant DNS as DNS Server
    participant WS as Web Server
    participant DB as Database Server

    U->>BR: Step 1 - Type URL and press Enter
    BR->>DNS: Step 2 - DNS Query for example dot com
    DNS-->>BR: Step 3 - Returns IP Address 93 dot 184 dot 216 dot 34
    BR->>WS: Step 4 - TCP SYN to Port 80
    WS-->>BR: Step 5 - TCP SYN ACK
    BR->>WS: Step 6 - TCP ACK - Connection Established
    BR->>WS: Step 7 - HTTP GET slash index dot html
    WS->>DB: Step 8 - SQL Query for User Data
    DB-->>WS: Step 9 - Query Result Set
    WS-->>BR: Step 10 - HTTP 200 OK with HTML Body
    BR->>WS: Step 11 - HTTP GET for image1 dot png
    WS-->>BR: Step 12 - HTTP 200 OK with Image Bytes
    BR->>WS: Step 13 - HTTP GET for image2 dot png
    WS-->>BR: Step 14 - HTTP 200 OK with Image Bytes
    BR-->>U: Step 15 - Rendered Web Page on Screen
    BR->>WS: Step 16 - TCP FIN - Close Connection
    WS-->>BR: Step 17 - TCP ACK and FIN
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — 3-Tier Web Application Architecture

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram 3 — Industry-Standard 3-Tier Architecture Mapping**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PT["TIER 1 - PRESENTATION TIER"]
        BROWSER["Web Browser - Chrome Firefox Edge"]
        MOBILE["Mobile App - Android iOS"]
    end

    subgraph LT["TIER 2 - LOGIC TIER - APPLICATION SERVER"]
        LB["Load Balancer - Nginx HAProxy"]
        APPS1["App Server Node 1 - Tomcat Spring Boot"]
        APPS2["App Server Node 2 - Tomcat Spring Boot"]
        APPS3["App Server Node 3 - Tomcat Spring Boot"]
        CACHE["Cache Layer - Redis or Memcached"]
    end

    subgraph DT["TIER 3 - DATA TIER"]
        DBMASTER["Primary DB - MySQL Master - Writes"]
        DBSLAVE1["Read Replica 1 - MySQL Slave - Reads"]
        DBSLAVE2["Read Replica 2 - MySQL Slave - Reads"]
    end

    BROWSER --> LB
    MOBILE --> LB
    LB --> APPS1
    LB --> APPS2
    LB --> APPS3
    APPS1 <--> CACHE
    APPS2 <--> CACHE
    APPS3 <--> CACHE
    APPS1 --> DBMASTER
    APPS2 --> DBMASTER
    APPS3 --> DBMASTER
    DBMASTER --> DBSLAVE1
    DBMASTER --> DBSLAVE2
```

## 4.4 Decision Diagram — When to Use Which Server Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram 4 — Server Concurrency Selection Flowchart (KTU Viva Question)**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START["Design Question - Need a Server"] --> Q1{"Expected Concurrent Clients"}
    Q1 -->|Below 50| IT["Iterative Server - Single Thread - Simplest Design"]
    Q1 -->|50 to 500| Q2{"CPU-Bound or I-O Bound"}
    Q1 -->|Above 500| AS["Asynchronous Event-Driven - Nginx NodeJS - Single Thread Non-Blocking"]
    Q2 -->|CPU-Bound| PROCS["Process-Based - fork - Each Client Isolated"]
    Q2 -->|I-O Bound| THREADS["Thread-Based - pthreads - Shared Memory Lighter"]
    IT -.->|Example - Telnet| EX1["Telnet Daytime Service"]
    PROCS -.->|Example| EX2["Apache prefork MPM"]
    THREADS -.->|Example| EX3["Apache worker MPM Java Tomcat"]
    AS -.->|Example| EX4["Nginx NodeJS Redis"]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> *Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Part A carries 2 marks for content + 1 mark for diagram/clarity.*

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEAMT632]`

> **Q: Differentiate between a Client and a Server in a network application. List any two well-known services with their default port numbers.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

| Aspect | Client | Server |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Role** | Initiates the request | Waits for and services the request |
| **Port** | Uses **ephemeral port** (49152–65535) | Uses **well-known port** (0–1023) |
| **Lifespan** | Transient (runs only when needed) | Always running (daemon process) |
| **Hardware** | Lightweight | Powerful, high-spec |
| **Count** | Many in a network | Few in a network |

**Well-known services (1 mark):**

| Service | Protocol | Port |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Web | HTTP | **80** |
| Secure Web | HTTPS | **443** |
| Email Send | SMTP | **25** |
| Secure Shell | SSH | **22** |

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, PEAMT632]`

> **Q: What is a socket? Explain its role in client-server communication with a suitable example.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

> **Definition (1.5 marks):** A **socket** is the endpoint of a two-way communication link between two programs running on a network. It is uniquely identified by the tuple **(IP Address, Port Number)**. It serves as the application programming interface to the underlying transport layer (TCP/UDP).

> **Role (1 mark):** Sockets abstract the complexity of the TCP/IP stack. The client creates a socket to send data; the server creates a socket to receive it. Together they form a **virtual pipe** through which bytes flow reliably.

> **Example (0.5 mark):** A browser connecting to `93.184.216.34:80` opens a socket; the data exchanged is HTTP messages. The server reads from this socket via `accept()` system call.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> *Per KTU 2024 ESE, each Part B question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each, and there is an internal choice. We provide both OR options below.*

### Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

> **Q: With neat diagrams, explain the Client-Server architecture in detail. Discuss the various types of servers and the role of well-known ports. (14 Marks)**

#### Part (a) — Architecture, Communication Cycle, and Diagram (7 Marks)

> **Definition (2 marks):** The **Client-Server Model** is a distributed architecture where the workload is partitioned between resource providers (servers) and service consumers (clients). The client initiates requests; the server responds.

> **Communication Cycle (3 marks):** The end-to-end flow is:
>
> 1. **DNS Resolution** — Client queries DNS to obtain server IP.
> 2. **TCP 3-Way Handshake** — `SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK` establishes the connection.
> 3. **Request Transmission** — Client sends `GET / HTTP/1.1`.
> 4. **Server Processing** — Server invokes application logic, may query DB.
> 5. **Response Transmission** — Server sends `HTTP/1.1 200 OK` with payload.
> 6. **TCP 4-Way Teardown** — `FIN → ACK → FIN → ACK` closes the connection.
> 7. **Client Rendering** — Browser parses HTML, fetches embedded objects, paints UI.

> **Diagram (2 marks):** Draw a clear **block diagram** showing Browser (client) ↔ Internet ↔ Web Server ↔ Database, labelling all sockets and ports. (Refer Section 4.3 of these notes.)

#### Part (b) — Server Types and Well-Known Ports (7 Marks)

> **Server Classification (4 marks):** Categorize servers by function:

| Server Type | Function | Protocol | Port |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Web Server** | Serves web pages | HTTP / HTTPS | 80 / 443 |
| **File Server** | File sharing | SMB / NFS / FTP | 445 / 2049 / 21 |
| **Mail Server** | Email delivery | SMTP / POP3 / IMAP | 25 / 110 / 143 |
| **Database Server** | Query processing | SQL (proprietary) | 3306 / 5432 |
| **Proxy Server** | Caching, filtering | HTTP | 8080 |
| **DNS Server** | Name resolution | DNS | 53 |
| **DHCP Server** | IP allocation | DHCP | 67 / 68 |
| **Print Server** | Shared printing | IPP | 631 |

> **Well-Known Ports (3 marks):** IANA reserves ports **0 – 1023** for system services. The term *well-known* arises because these ports are universally known to clients, enabling services to be discovered without configuration. Examples: HTTP=**80**, HTTPS=**443**, FTP=**21**, SSH=**22**, Telnet=**23**, DNS=**53**. Ports **1024 – 49151** are *registered* for user applications, and **49152 – 65535** are *ephemeral*, allocated dynamically by client OS.

---

### Question B (Choice 2 / OR Option) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]`

> **Q: Compare and contrast 2-Tier, 3-Tier, and N-Tier client-server architectures with examples. Explain iterative and concurrent server models. (14 Marks)**

#### Part (a) — 2-Tier vs 3-Tier vs N-Tier Architectures (7 Marks)

> **2-Tier Architecture (2 marks):** The application is split into a **client** (presentation + logic) and a **server** (database). Client directly queries the server using ODBC/JDBC/SQL. Example: A desktop tool querying a remote Oracle DB.
> - *Pros:* Simple, fast for small teams.
> - *Cons:* Thick client; logic duplication; insecure (DB exposed to client).

> **3-Tier Architecture (3 marks):** Introduces a middle **application server** that hosts the business logic. The client (browser) is thin; the database is isolated on a private network. Example: **Amazon.com** — Browser → Tomcat → MySQL.
> - *Pros:* Centralized logic, easier maintenance, better security.
> - *Cons:* More infrastructure, network hop overhead.

> **N-Tier Architecture (2 marks):** Splits logic into multiple distributed services with load balancers, caches, queues, and microservices. Example: **Netflix** with hundreds of services behind an API gateway.
> - *Pros:* Massive scalability, fault isolation, independent deployment.
> - *Cons:* Complex DevOps, distributed tracing challenges.

> **Comparison Table to be drawn (mandatory for full marks):**

| Feature | 2-Tier | 3-Tier | N-Tier |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Tiers** | 2 | 3 | $\geq 4$ |
| **Client Type** | Thick | Thin | Ultra-thin |
| **Logic Location** | Client | App Server | Distributed |
| **Scalability** | Low | Medium | Very High |
| **Example** | Desktop DB App | Amazon Web App | Netflix Microservices |

#### Part (b) — Iterative vs Concurrent Server Models (7 Marks)

> **Iterative Server (3 marks):** A server that handles **only one client at a time**. When a new request arrives, it is queued; the current request must complete before the next is accepted.
> - **Implementation:** A single `accept()` loop; no forking or threading.
> - **Pros:** No race conditions; trivial to code; low memory overhead.
> - **Cons:** A single slow client blocks all others — unusable in production.
> - **Example:** Classic `daytime` or `echo` services on early Unix.

> **Concurrent Server (4 marks):** A server that handles **multiple clients in parallel** by spawning workers.

| Sub-Model | Mechanism | Memory | Isolation | Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Process-based** | `fork()` per client | High | Full | Apache Prefork |
| **Thread-based** | `pthread_create()` | Medium | Partial (shared heap) | Java Tomcat, Apache Worker |
| **Event-driven** | `epoll` / `select` / Non-blocking I/O | Very Low | Single-thread | Nginx, Node.js, Redis |

> **Pros (1 mark):** Massive scalability; failure of one client doesn't affect others; ideal for **C10K** (10,000+ connections).
> **Cons (1 mark):** Complex code; race conditions; need for synchronization primitives (mutexes, semaphores); context-switching overhead in process model.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Do NOT confuse "tier" with "layer".** Tiers are *physical* (separate machines), layers are *logical* (e.g., OSI layers).
> - **Do NOT call N-Tier = N-Layer.** OSI has 7 layers, but an N-tier app can have any number of physical tiers.
> - **Always draw the diagram** in 7-mark sub-questions. Missing diagram = −1.5 marks.
> - **Mention port numbers explicitly** in server-type questions. Vague "HTTP port" loses 0.5 marks.
> - **In numerical problems**, show the substitution step; do NOT jump straight to the final number. The examiner needs to see the formula usage.
> - **Avoid mixing up "iterative" with "stateless".** Iterative refers to concurrency; stateless refers to session memory — they are orthogonal.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU ESE Module 1**

- ☐ **Client-Server Model** = distributed architecture with **service requesters (clients)** and **service providers (servers)**.
- ☐ **Client is always the initiator**; **server is always the listener** (passive at start).
- ☐ **Socket = (IP, Port)** — the fundamental endpoint abstraction.
- ☐ **Port Ranges:** Well-known = **0–1023**, Registered = **1024–49151**, Ephemeral = **49152–65535**.
- ☐ Must-remember ports: HTTP=**80**, HTTPS=**443**, FTP=**21**, SSH=**22**, SMTP=**25**, DNS=**53**, DHCP=**67/68**, MySQL=**3306**.
- ☐ **TCP 3-way handshake** = `SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK` (cost: **1.5 RTT**).
- ☐ **TCP 4-way teardown** = `FIN → ACK → FIN → ACK`.
- ☐ **Iterative Server** = one client at a time. **Concurrent Server** = parallel; uses **process, thread, or event-driven** model.
- ☐ **2-Tier** = thick client + DB server. **3-Tier** = thin client + app server + DB server. **N-Tier** = distributed services with load balancers/caches.
- ☐ **Stateful vs Stateless:** HTTP is stateless; FTP/Telnet are stateful. Stateless = easier to scale horizontally.
- ☐ **Little's Law:** $L = \lambda W$ — used to compute server throughput.
- ☐ **HTTP Modes:** Non-persistent = 1 connection per object (slow); Persistent = 1 connection, many objects (fast); Pipelined = back-to-back requests (fastest, but head-of-line blocking).
- ☐ **Middleware** = glue software between OS and distributed application (RPC, MOM, CORBA).
- ☐ **Daemon** = background server process (`httpd`, `sshd`, `mysqld`).
- ☐ **DNS resolution** always precedes the TCP handshake in web traffic.
- ☐ **Draw a labeled diagram** in every 7-mark question — it carries 2 marks on average.

> **End of KTU-Premier-Engine Notes for *Client-Server Applications* — PEAMT632 Module 1.**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: World Wide Web and HTTP
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAMT632 - BASICS OF COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** World Wide Web and HTTP

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The World Wide Web (WWW)

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
The **World Wide Web (WWW)** is a distributed, hypermedia-based information system that runs over the Internet. It is a network of interconnected **resources** (web pages, documents, images, videos, audio, etc.) identified by **Uniform Resource Locators (URLs)**, accessed via the **HyperText Transfer Protocol (HTTP)**, and linked together through **hypertext/hyperlinks**.

The WWW is *not* the Internet itself — it is just one of many services (like email, FTP, DNS) that operates *on top of* the Internet. The Internet is the physical/logical infrastructure; the WWW is the application-layer service that uses it.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Key Distinction:** Students often lose marks by writing "WWW = Internet". The correct answer is: *The Internet is the underlying network; the WWW is an application service that uses the Internet to deliver hypertext documents.*

### Core Components of the WWW
| Component | Role |
|---|---|
| **Client (Browser)** | Sends HTTP requests and renders responses (Chrome, Firefox, Edge) |
| **Server (Web Server)** | Hosts resources and replies to HTTP requests (Apache, Nginx, IIS) |
| **HTTP Protocol** | Application-layer request-response protocol |
| **HTML** | Markup language used to structure and display content |
| **URL/URI** | Unique addressing scheme for every resource |
| **Proxy / Cache** | Intermediate entity that reduces latency and load |
| **DNS** | Resolves human-readable hostnames to IP addresses |

## 1.2 HyperText Transfer Protocol (HTTP)

**Formal Definition:**
**HTTP** is an application-layer, **request-response**, **stateless** protocol that defines how clients (web browsers) and servers communicate to transfer web resources. It is defined in **IETF RFC 9110 (HTTP/1.1)** and successor RFCs for HTTP/2 and HTTP/3.

### Conceptual Analogy: The Restaurant Waiter Model 🍽️
Imagine the WWW as a giant restaurant:
- **You (the customer)** = the **Web Browser (Client)**
- **The kitchen** = the **Web Server**
- **The menu** = the **URL/HTML page**
- **The waiter who takes your order and brings back your food** = **HTTP**
- **The plumbing and roads connecting the kitchen to your table** = **TCP/IP (the Internet)**

When you click a link, you are essentially "ordering a dish" by its name (URL). The HTTP "waiter" carries your order (Request) to the kitchen (server) and returns the prepared dish (Response — the HTML page, image, etc.). Just like a waiter doesn't remember your previous orders unless explicitly noted, **HTTP itself is stateless** — each request is independent.

> [!NOTE]
> **Statelessness**: The server treats every request as new; it has no memory of past interactions from the same client. This is why techniques like **Cookies**, **Sessions**, and **Tokens** were invented — to add "memory" on top of a stateless protocol.

## 1.3 The URL — Address of Every Web Resource

A **Uniform Resource Locator (URL)** uniquely identifies a resource on the web. The full structure is:

**`scheme://host:port/path?query#fragment`**

| Part | Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **scheme** | Protocol used | `http`, `https` |
| **host** | Domain name (DNS resolved to IP) | `www.ktu.edu.in` |
| **port** | TCP port (default **80** for HTTP, **443** for HTTPS) | `:8080` |
| **path** | Resource location on the server | `/index.html` |
| **query** | Parameters passed to server | `?id=101&dept=cse` |
| **fragment** | Sub-section within the page | `#section2` |

## 1.4 Standard Default Ports and Status Codes (High-Yield Quick Reference)

> [!TIP]
> **Default Port Numbers — must memorize for KTU exams:**
> - **HTTP → 80**
> - **HTTPS → 443**
> - Both operate on top of **TCP** (HTTP/3 uses QUIC over UDP).

**Key HTTP Status Codes:**
- **1xx** Informational (e.g., 100 Continue)
- **2xx** Success (e.g., **200 OK**, 201 Created)
- **3xx** Redirection (e.g., **301 Moved Permanently**, 304 Not Modified)
- **4xx** Client Error (e.g., **404 Not Found**, 401 Unauthorized)
- **5xx** Server Error (e.g., **500 Internal Server Error**, 503 Service Unavailable)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The WWW Operational Stack

The WWW is a layered, client–server application. The end-to-end flow when a user types a URL is:

1. **Browser parses URL** → extracts scheme, host, path.
2. **DNS Resolution** → host is resolved to an **IP address** (via DNS query, typically cached).
3. **TCP Connection** → a **3-way handshake** establishes a TCP connection (SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK). For HTTP/3, this is replaced by **QUIC** over UDP.
4. **HTTP Request** → browser sends the request message (method, headers, optional body).
5. **Server processing** → server locates the resource, runs application logic (CGI, PHP, Node.js, etc.).
6. **HTTP Response** → server sends back the response (status line, headers, body).
7. **Browser rendering** → HTML is parsed; sub-resources (CSS, JS, images) trigger further HTTP requests.
8. **Connection handling**:
   - **HTTP/1.0**: Connection closed after each object.
   - **HTTP/1.1**: **Persistent connections** with **pipelining** (limited).
   - **HTTP/2**: **Multiplexed** streams over a single TCP connection.
   - **HTTP/3**: **Multiplexed** over QUIC (UDP-based).

## 2.2 HTTP Methods (Verbs)

| Method | Purpose | Idempotent? | Has Body? |
|---|---|---|---|
| **GET** | Retrieve a resource | ✅ Yes | Optional (rare) |
| **POST** | Submit data / create resource | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| **PUT** | Replace / create a resource at a URI | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| **DELETE** | Remove a resource | ✅ Yes | Rare |
| **HEAD** | Same as GET but returns headers only | ✅ Yes | ❌ No |
| **OPTIONS** | Discover supported methods | ✅ Yes | ❌ No |
| **PATCH** | Partial modification | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |

> [!NOTE]
> **Idempotent** means executing the operation multiple times produces the same result as executing it once. This is a high-yield KTU definition — expect a 3-mark question on it.

## 2.3 HTTP Message Format — The Two Message Types

### A) HTTP Request Message
```
┌───────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Request Line  :  Method  SP  URI  SP  Version CRLF│
│  Headers       :  Header-Name : Value CRLF    │
│                  ...                              │
│  Empty Line    :  CRLF                          │
│  Body          :  (optional, e.g., POST data)  │
└───────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```
**Example:**
```
GET /index.html HTTP/1.1
Host: www.ktu.edu.in
User-Agent: Mozilla/5.0
Accept: text/html
Connection: keep-alive
```

### B) HTTP Response Message
```
┌───────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Status Line  :  Version SP  Code SP  Phrase CRLF │
│  Headers      :  Header-Name : Value CRLF     │
│                  ...                             │
│  Empty Line   :  CRLF                          │
│  Body         :  (the requested resource)      │
└───────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```
**Example:**
```
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Date: Mon, 15 Jul 2024 12:00:00 GMT
Content-Type: text/html
Content-Length: 1024
Connection: keep-alive

<html>...</html>
```

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Web Performance Metrics

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Time for one non-persistent HTTP object** | $T = 2 \times RTT + \text{transmission time}$ | Web page load estimation |
| 2 | **Non-persistent: $n$ objects sequentially** | $T_{total} = n \times (2RTT + F/R)$ | Old HTTP/1.0 baseline |
| 3 | **Persistent HTTP with pipelining** | $T_{total} = 2RTT + n \times (RTT + F/R)$ | HTTP/1.1 baseline |
| 4 | **Persistent HTTP without pipelining** | $T_{total} = 2RTT + n \times (RTT + F/R)$ | One request waits for previous |
| 5 | **Parallel (concurrent) connections ($k$ parallel, $n$ objects)** | $T_{total} = 2RTT + \lceil n/k \rceil \times (RTT + F/R)$ | Browser uses 6 parallel conn. by default |
| 6 | **Throughput** | $\text{Throughput} = F / R$ bits/sec | Server capacity analysis |
| 7 | **File size in bits** | $F_{\text{bits}} = F_{\text{bytes}} \times 8$ | Unit conversion |
| 8 | **Transmission time** | $t_{tx} = F / R$ where $R$ = link bandwidth | Bottleneck calculation |
| 9 | **Number of parallel batches** | $\text{batches} = \lceil n / k \rceil$ | Browser concurrency math |
| 10 | **DNS lookup overhead** | $T_{DNS} \approx RTT_{DNS}$ (cached ≈ 0) | Often added to first RTT |

> **Where:**
> - $RTT$ = Round-Trip Time between client and server (one packet each way)
> - $F$ = File size in **bits**
> - $R$ = Link bandwidth in **bits per second**
> - $n$ = Total number of objects in the page
> - $k$ = Number of parallel TCP connections the browser opens

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Trap:** Many students forget to add the *initial* $2 \times RTT$ (one for TCP setup, one for the HTTP request/response). Both are counted. The 2 RTTs in non-persistent = 1 RTT for TCP handshake + 1 RTT for HTTP request-response.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Content Delivery Networks (CDNs)** like Akamai, Cloudflare use HTTP caching to reduce RTT and improve throughput for global users.
- **Web performance optimization engineers** use these formulas to estimate page load time and tune server parameters.
- **HTTP/2 and HTTP/3** were designed specifically because the persistent-connection formulas above show that latency, not bandwidth, dominates load time in the modern era.
- **Statelessness** allows easy horizontal scaling: any server in a cluster can handle any request because no client state is stored on the server.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Time to Fetch a Web Page with Non-Persistent HTTP (HTTP/1.0 Style)

**Setup:** A web page contains **1 base HTML file** and **$N$ referenced objects** (images, CSS, JS). The base HTML references $N$ object URLs.

For each object, the browser must:

1. Open a new TCP connection → **1 RTT** for the 3-way handshake.
2. Send HTTP GET and receive HTTP response headers → **1 RTT** for request/response.
3. Begin receiving the object body (transmission time $F/R$ may overlap with last RTT, but conservatively taken separately).

**Total time per object (conservative, KTU textbook assumption):**

$$
T_{\text{obj}} = 2 \times RTT + \frac{F}{R}
$$

> **Why 2 RTTs?** The first RTT establishes the TCP connection (3-way handshake: SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK). The second RTT carries the HTTP request to the server and returns the first byte of the response.

**Total time for the base HTML file:**

$$
T_{\text{base}} = 2 \times RTT + \frac{F_{\text{base}}}{R}
$$

**Total time for $N$ referenced objects (sequential in non-persistent HTTP):**

$$
T_{\text{objs}} = N \times \left( 2 \times RTT + \frac{F_{\text{obj}}}{R} \right)
$$

**Grand total time:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{total}} &= T_{\text{base}} + T_{\text{objs}} \\
&= \left(2RTT + \frac{F_{\text{base}}}{R}\right) + N \times \left(2RTT + \frac{F_{\text{obj}}}{R}\right) \\
&= (N + 1) \times 2RTT + \frac{F_{\text{base}} + N \cdot F_{\text{obj}}}{R}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Sanity check:** If $N = 0$ (just the HTML file), $T_{total} = 2RTT + F_{base}/R$. ✅

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Time with Persistent HTTP (HTTP/1.1)

In **persistent** HTTP, the TCP connection is reused. The TCP handshake (1 RTT) happens once for the entire session. Each subsequent object requires only 1 RTT for request/response.

**Time for base HTML:**

$$
T_{\text{base}} = 2RTT + \frac{F_{\text{base}}}{R}
$$

**Time for $N$ objects over the same persistent connection (no pipelining — each waits for previous):**

$$
T_{\text{objs}} = N \times \left( RTT + \frac{F_{\text{obj}}}{R} \right)
$$

**Total:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{total}} &= 2RTT + \frac{F_{\text{base}}}{R} + N \times \left( RTT + \frac{F_{\text{obj}}}{R} \right) \\
&= (N + 2) \times RTT + \frac{F_{\text{base}} + N \cdot F_{\text{obj}}}{R}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Improvement factor:** Persistent HTTP saves $N$ TCP handshakes, i.e., $N \times RTT$ compared to non-persistent. With $N = 50$ objects and $RTT = 100\,\text{ms}$, this saves **5 seconds** — a massive win.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Parallel Connections (Browser Default $k$)

Most browsers open **$k = 6$ parallel TCP connections** per host. The $N$ objects are split into batches of size $k$.

**Number of batches:**

$$
b = \left\lceil \frac{N}{k} \right\rceil
$$

**Total time:**

$$
T_{\text{total}} = 2RTT + \frac{F_{\text{base}}}{R} + b \times \left( RTT + \frac{F_{\text{obj}}}{R} \right)
$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem (typical KTU-style):** A web page consists of a base HTML file of size **$15\,\text{KB}$** and **10 referenced image objects**, each of size **$100\,\text{KB}$**. The link bandwidth is **$1.5\,\text{Mbps}$** and the RTT is **$100\,\text{ms}$**. Compute the total response time for: (a) non-persistent HTTP, (b) persistent HTTP.

**Step 1 — Unit conversion (mandatory in KTU valuation):**

$$
F_{\text{base}} = 15 \times 1024 \times 8 = 122{,}880 \text{ bits}
$$

$$
F_{\text{obj}} = 100 \times 1024 \times 8 = 819{,}200 \text{ bits}
$$

$$
R = 1.5 \times 10^6 \text{ bits/sec}
$$

$$
RTT = 0.1 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 2 — Transmission times:**

$$
\frac{F_{\text{base}}}{R} = \frac{122{,}880}{1.5 \times 10^6} = 0.08192 \text{ s}
$$

$$
\frac{F_{\text{obj}}}{R} = \frac{819{,}200}{1.5 \times 10^6} = 0.54613 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 3(a) — Non-persistent HTTP:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{total}} &= (N+1) \cdot 2RTT + \frac{F_{\text{base}} + N \cdot F_{\text{obj}}}{R} \\
&= 11 \times (2 \times 0.1) + (0.08192 + 10 \times 0.54613) \\
&= 11 \times 0.2 + (0.08192 + 5.4613) \\
&= 2.2 + 5.54322 \\
&= 7.74322 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3(b) — Persistent HTTP:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{total}} &= (N+2) \cdot RTT + \frac{F_{\text{base}} + N \cdot F_{\text{obj}}}{R} \\
&= 12 \times 0.1 + 5.54322 \\
&= 1.2 + 5.54322 \\
&= 6.74322 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Insight (write this in the KTU answer for full marks):**

Persistent HTTP saved **1 second** purely by eliminating redundant TCP handshakes. The **transmission time component is identical** in both cases because the total bytes are the same; the gain is purely from reduced **latency overhead**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** Always show unit conversions and the final answer in **seconds** (or convert to ms). Examiners reward the "sanity check" step where you verbally interpret the result.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 4: HTTP Caching Effectiveness (Cache Hit Ratio)

When a proxy cache is used, the **cache hit ratio $h$** is the fraction of requests served from the cache.

**Effective average response time:**

$$
T_{\text{effective}} = h \cdot T_{\text{cache}} + (1 - h) \cdot T_{\text{origin}}
$$

Where:
- $T_{\text{cache}} \approx$ LAN RTT (e.g., 1 ms)
- $T_{\text{origin}} \approx$ WAN RTT (e.g., 200 ms + transmission)

**Example:** $h = 0.6$, $T_{cache} = 1$ ms, $T_{origin} = 200$ ms:

$$
T_{\text{eff}} = 0.6 \times 1 + 0.4 \times 200 = 0.6 + 80 = 80.6 \text{ ms}
$$

A **60% hit rate reduced perceived latency by ~60%**, illustrating the engineering value of CDNs.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: WWW Architecture and HTTP Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[User Types URL in Browser] --> B[Browser Parses URL]
    B --> C{DNS Cache Hit?}
    C -- Yes --> E[Use Cached IP]
    C -- No --> D[DNS Query to Resolver]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Open TCP Connection Port 80 or 443]
    F --> G[Send HTTP Request Message]
```
