```mermaid
    G --> H[Web Server Receives Request]
    H --> I[Server Locates Resource]
    I --> J[Server Sends HTTP Response]
    J --> K[Browser Renders HTML]
    K --> L{Embedded Objects?}
    L -- Yes --> M[Spawn HTTP GETs for CSS JS IMG]
    L -- No --> N[Page Fully Rendered]
    M --> F
```

## 4.2 HTTP Request-Response Sequence Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant CL as Client Browser
    participant SV as Web Server
    CL->>SV: 1 TCP SYN
    SV-->>CL: 2 TCP SYN ACK
    CL->>SV: 3 TCP ACK
    Note over CL,SV: TCP Connection Established
    CL->>SV: 4 HTTP GET /index.html HTTP 1.1
    SV-->>CL: 5 HTTP 1.1 200 OK + HTML body
    CL->>SV: 6 HTTP GET /style.css HTTP 1.1
    SV-->>CL: 7 HTTP 1.1 200 OK + CSS body
    CL->>SV: 8 HTTP GET /logo.png HTTP 1.1
    SV-->>CL: 9 HTTP 1.1 200 OK + Image bytes
    Note over CL,SV: Persistent Connection Reused
    CL->>SV: 10 TCP FIN
    SV-->>CL: 11 TCP ACK
    Note over CL,SV: Connection Closed
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: HTTP Message Structure (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph REQ[HTTP REQUEST MESSAGE]
        A1[Request Line: Method URI Version] --> A2[Header Section: Host User Agent Accept Content Type]
        A2 --> A3[Blank Line CRLF]
        A3 --> A4[Optional Body: Form Data JSON Upload]
    end
    subgraph RES[HTTP RESPONSE MESSAGE]
        B1[Status Line: Version Code Phrase] --> B2[Header Section: Date Content Type Content Length]
        B2 --> B3[Blank Line CRLF]
        B3 --> B4[Body: HTML JSON Image Binary Data]
    end
    REQ -.transmitted over TCP.-> RES
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram: WWW vs Internet Conceptual Layers

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L5[Application Layer: HTTP HTTPS FTP SMTP DNS] --> L4[Transport Layer: TCP UDP]
    L4 --> L3[Network Layer: IP]
    L3 --> L2[Data Link Layer: Ethernet WiFi]
    L2 --> L1[Physical Layer: Fiber Copper Radio]
    L1 -.signals.-> L2
    Note1[WWW is built on top of HTTP which sits at the Application Layer. The Internet is the entire stack L1 to L5]
    L5 -.WWW lives here.-> Note1
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Explain the World Wide Web (WWW). List its main components.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **World Wide Web (WWW)** is a distributed hypermedia information system that operates over the Internet. It allows users to access linked resources (documents, images, videos) using a browser. The WWW is *not* the Internet itself — it is an **application service** running on top of the Internet.

**Main Components of WWW (3 components for full marks):**
1. **Web Client (Browser)** – the user interface that issues HTTP requests and renders responses (e.g., Chrome, Firefox).
2. **Web Server** – the host machine that stores web resources and replies to HTTP requests (e.g., Apache, Nginx).
3. **HTTP Protocol** – the request-response application-layer protocol that governs client-server communication.
4. **Resources / HTML pages** – the actual hypertext documents identified by URLs.
5. **URLs** – the unique addressing scheme for resources.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Why is HTTP called a stateless protocol? How is state maintained in real web applications?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

HTTP is called **statateful** ... correction, **stateless** because the server does not retain any information about previous client requests. Each HTTP request is processed independently, without any memory of past interactions from the same client.

**State is maintained in practice using:**
1. **Cookies** – small key-value pieces of data sent by the server and stored on the client; resent with every request to the same server.
2. **Sessions** – server-side storage indexed by a session ID (often stored in a cookie).
3. **URL Rewriting** – appending a session token to every URL.
4. **Hidden Form Fields** – embedding state in HTML forms.
5. **Tokens (e.g., JWT)** – used in modern RESTful APIs for stateless authentication.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Spelling "stateless" as "stateful" or "stateles" loses 0.5 marks immediately. Also, students forget to mention *how* state is added — the question explicitly asks about the mechanism.

---

## Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **client-server architecture of the World Wide Web** and the role of **HTTP** in it. List **six HTTP request methods** with one-line descriptions. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A web page contains a base HTML file of size **20 KB** and references **15 image objects**, each of size **120 KB**. The RTT between client and server is **80 ms**, and the bottleneck link bandwidth is **2 Mbps**. Compute the **total response time** for:
   (i) Non-persistent HTTP
   (ii) Persistent HTTP

Also state, in one sentence, which is faster and why. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-A(a) — 7 marks**

**Architecture Diagram (4 marks):**

```
[Browser] ---HTTP Request---> [Web Server]
[Client ] <--HTTP Response--- [Origin/Host]
   |                              |
   v                              v
[Renders HTML]              [Accesses File System]
   |                              |
   v                              v
[Sub-requests for CSS,JS,IMG]   [Returns resources]
```

**Description (2 marks):** The WWW follows a **client–server model**. The **web client (browser)** initiates communication by sending an **HTTP request message** to the **web server**. The server processes the request, locates the resource, and sends back an **HTTP response message** containing the resource. The browser parses HTML and issues further requests for embedded objects.

**Role of HTTP (0.5 mark):** HTTP is the application-layer protocol that defines the format and semantics of the request/response messages exchanged.

**Six HTTP methods (0.5 mark):**
1. **GET** – retrieve a resource.
2. **POST** – submit data to the server.
3. **PUT** – create or replace a resource at a given URI.
4. **DELETE** – remove a resource.
5. **HEAD** – retrieve headers only.
6. **OPTIONS** – list the methods supported by the server.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-A(b) — 7 marks**

**Given:**
- $F_{\text{base}} = 20 \text{ KB} = 20 \times 1024 \times 8 = 163{,}840$ bits
- $N = 15$, $F_{\text{obj}} = 120 \text{ KB} = 120 \times 1024 \times 8 = 983{,}040$ bits
- $RTT = 0.08$ s
- $R = 2 \times 10^6$ bps

**Step 1 — Transmission times:**

$$
t_{\text{base}} = \frac{163{,}840}{2 \times 10^6} = 0.08192 \text{ s}
$$

$$
t_{\text{obj}} = \frac{983{,}040}{2 \times 10^6} = 0.49152 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 2(i) — Non-persistent HTTP:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{np} &= (N+1) \cdot 2RTT + t_{\text{base}} + N \cdot t_{\text{obj}} \\
&= 16 \times (2 \times 0.08) + 0.08192 + 15 \times 0.49152 \\
&= 16 \times 0.16 + 0.08192 + 7.3728 \\
&= 2.56 + 0.08192 + 7.3728 \\
&= 10.01472 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2(ii) — Persistent HTTP:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{p} &= (N+2) \cdot RTT + t_{\text{base}} + N \cdot t_{\text{obj}} \\
&= 17 \times 0.08 + 0.08192 + 7.3728 \\
&= 1.36 + 0.08192 + 7.3728 \\
&= 8.81472 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Persistent HTTP is **faster by 1.2 seconds** because it eliminates $N$ redundant TCP 3-way handshakes, saving $N \times RTT = 15 \times 0.08 = 1.2$ s.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Explain the structure of an **HTTP request message** and an **HTTP response message** with examples. Differentiate between **persistent** and **non-persistent** HTTP connections. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** What is a **URL**? Explain each component of a URL with an example. A user requests `https://www.ktu.edu.in:8443/results/index.html?sem=6&dept=cse#timetable`. Identify the **scheme, host, port, path, query, and fragment**. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-B(a) — 7 marks**

**HTTP Request Message (3 marks):**

Structure: `Request Line | Headers | Blank Line | Body`

```
GET /index.html HTTP/1.1           ← Request Line (method, URI, version)
Host: www.ktu.edu.in                ← Header
User-Agent: Mozilla/5.0
Accept: text/html
Connection: keep-alive
                                    ← Blank line (CRLF) ends headers
(optional body for POST/PUT)
```

The **request line** contains the method, the URI, and the HTTP version. **Header lines** carry metadata in `Name: Value` format. The body is optional (used in POST, PUT).

**HTTP Response Message (2 marks):**

```
HTTP/1.1 200 OK                     ← Status Line (version, code, phrase)
Date: Mon, 15 Jul 2024 12:00:00 GMT
Content-Type: text/html
Content-Length: 1024
                                    ← Blank line
<html>...</html>                    ← Body
```

The **status line** carries the protocol version, status code, and reason phrase. Status codes follow the `1xx/2xx/3xx/4xx/5xx` pattern.

**Persistent vs Non-Persistent (2 marks):**

| Feature | Non-Persistent (HTTP/1.0) | Persistent (HTTP/1.1) |
|---|---|---|
| TCP connection per object | New connection | Reused |
| TCP handshakes | One per object | One for whole session |
| Server overhead | High | Low |
| Latency | High | Reduced by $N \times RTT$ |
| Pipelining | Not supported | Supported |

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-B(b) — 7 marks**

**URL Definition (1 mark):** A **Uniform Resource Locator (URL)** is a reference (address) to a resource on the web. It specifies the protocol, location, and retrieval method.

**URL Components (3 marks):**

| Component | Purpose | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Scheme** | Protocol | `https` |
| **Host** | Domain name of server | `www.ktu.edu.in` |
| **Port** | TCP port number | `8443` |
| **Path** | Resource location | `/results/index.html` |
| **Query** | Parameters to server | `?sem=6&dept=cse` |
| **Fragment** | Sub-section anchor | `#timetable` |

**URL Breakdown (3 marks):** Given `https://www.ktu.edu.in:8443/results/index.html?sem=6&dept=cse#timetable`:

- **Scheme:** `https` (HTTP over TLS/SSL)
- **Host:** `www.ktu.edu.in`
- **Port:** `8443` (custom HTTPS port; default HTTPS is 443)
- **Path:** `/results/index.html`
- **Query:** `sem=6&dept=cse` (two key-value parameters)
- **Fragment:** `timetable` (anchor inside the page)

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (B-Part-b):** Students frequently confuse the **port** with the **path** when both contain slashes. The port always comes **before the first single slash** (`/`) after the host. Marks are deducted for not identifying the fragment as client-side only (it is not sent to the server).

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **WWW ≠ Internet.** Internet is the network; WWW is the application service.
- ✅ HTTP is **application layer**, **stateless**, **request-response**.
- ✅ **Default Ports:** HTTP → **80**, HTTPS → **443** (memorize!).
- ✅ HTTP runs on **TCP** (HTTP/1.1, HTTP/2). **HTTP/3 runs on QUIC (UDP)**.
- ✅ **Idempotent methods:** GET, PUT, DELETE, HEAD, OPTIONS. **Non-idempotent:** POST, PATCH.
- ✅ **Status Code Ranges:** 1xx Info | 2xx Success | 3xx Redirection | 4xx Client Error | 5xx Server Error.
- ✅ **URL format:** `scheme://host:port/path?query#fragment`.
- ✅ **Non-persistent HTTP** opens a new TCP connection for every object → high latency.
- ✅ **Persistent HTTP (1.1)** reuses a single TCP connection → saves $N \times RTT$.
- ✅ **Parallel connections** (browser default = 6) reduce wall-clock time further.
- ✅ **Key formula (non-persistent):** $T = (N+1) \cdot 2RTT + (F_{\text{base}} + N F_{\text{obj}})/R$.
- ✅ **Key formula (persistent):** $T = (N+2) \cdot RTT + (F_{\text{base}} + N F_{\text{obj}})/R$.
- ✅ **Statelessness** is overcome using **Cookies, Sessions, JWT Tokens**.
- ✅ **Always convert file sizes to bits** ($F_{bytes} \times 8$) and bandwidth to **bits/sec** when computing transmission time.
- ✅ **HTTP/2 → Multiplexing** (no head-of-line blocking at HTTP layer); **HTTP/3 → QUIC over UDP** (no head-of-line blocking at transport layer).
- ✅ **Proxy/Cache hit ratio formula:** $T_{\text{eff}} = h \cdot T_{\text{cache}} + (1-h) \cdot T_{\text{origin}}$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: FTP
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAMT632 - BASICS OF COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** FTP

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **File Transfer Protocol (FTP)** is a standard **Application Layer** protocol (defined in **RFC 959**) that operates over **TCP/IP** to provide reliable, bidirectional file transfer between a client and a server on a computer network. It is built on a **Client–Server architecture** and uses **two parallel TCP channels** — a *Control Connection* on **Port 21** and a *Data Connection* on **Port 20** (in active mode).

FTP is one of the oldest and most enduring protocols of the Internet, originally defined in **1971** (pre-TCP) and formalized in its modern TCP form in **RFC 959 (1985)**. It belongs to the same family of Internet text-based command protocols as **SMTP** and **HTTP**.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The Post Office Analogy:** Imagine you want to send/receive physical parcels between two offices.
> - You first walk to the **counter (Port 21 — Control Channel)** and tell the clerk: *"I want to send 3 boxes to Office B; please prepare a receiving dock."* The clerk fills out forms and confirms — this is the **command/response dialogue**.
> - Then, the actual parcels travel through a **separate loading bay (Port 20 — Data Channel)** that was opened specifically for this transfer.
> - When the transfer is done, the loading bay closes, but you may keep talking to the clerk to send more parcels.
> 
> This is exactly how FTP works: a **persistent control conversation** governs a **temporary, dedicated data pipe** for the actual file bytes.

### 1.3 Key Technical Highlights

- **Default Control Port:** **TCP Port 21** (persistent, stays open for entire session)
- **Default Data Port:** **TCP Port 20** (active mode only; passive mode uses an ephemeral port negotiated on the fly)
- **Transport Protocol:** **TCP** (guarantees reliability, ordered delivery, and flow control)
- **Connection Type:** **Stateful**, **Connection-Oriented**
- **Data Type:** **Out-of-band signaling** — control and data use **separate TCP connections** (a rare property among TCP applications)
- **Command Language:** **Text-based ASCII commands** (e.g., `USER`, `PASS`, `GET`, `PUT`, `LIST`, `QUIT`)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 FTP Architecture — The Two-Channel Model

FTP is unique among popular Internet protocols because it **simultaneously maintains two distinct TCP connections** between the same client and server.

| Channel | Port | Purpose | Lifetime |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Control Connection (PI — Protocol Interpreter)** | **21** | Sends FTP **commands** from client and receives **status replies** (response codes) from server | **Persistent** — open from login to `QUIT` |
| **Data Connection (DTP — Data Transfer Process)** | **20** (active) / **Ephemeral** (passive) | Carries the actual **file contents**, **directory listings**, and **directory change results** | **Transient** — opened per transfer, closed after each file |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why two channels?** It allows the user to keep issuing commands (browse, delete, rename) while a large file is being uploaded/downloaded in the background — a form of **out-of-band control** over the data stream.

### 2.2 Active Mode vs. Passive Mode

This is one of the most frequently tested KTU concepts. The distinction lies in **who initiates the data connection**.

#### 2.2.1 Active Mode (PORT Command)
1. Client opens control connection **from an ephemeral port → server port 21**.
2. Client sends `PORT <clientIP>,<clientPort>` to tell the server *where* to connect back.
3. Server initiates data connection **from port 20 → client's specified port**.
4. Server must accept **incoming** connections — broken by client-side **firewalls / NAT**.

#### 2.2.2 Passive Mode (PASV Command)
1. Client opens control connection **from ephemeral port → server port 21**.
2. Client sends `PASV`.
3. Server replies `227 Entering Passive Mode (h1,h2,h3,h4,p1,p2)` — its IP and a new ephemeral listening port.
4. Client initiates data connection **from its own ephemeral port → server's announced port**.
5. Works through **client firewalls and NAT** — preferred mode today.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Trick:** *"Active = server ACTIVELY dials the client; Passive = client dials the server."*

### 2.3 FTP Command Set (High-Yield)

| Command | Function |
|---|---|
| `USER <username>` | Send user ID for authentication |
| `PASS <password>` | Send password |
| `LIST` | List files in current directory (sent over data connection) |
| `RETR <filename>` | **Retrieve (download)** a file |
| `STOR <filename>` | **Store (upload)** a file |
| `DELE <filename>` | Delete a file |
| `CWD <dir>` | Change working directory |
| `PWD` | Print working directory |
| `QUIT` | Logout and close control connection |
| `PORT a,b,c,d,p1,p2` | Active mode address/port specification |
| `PASV` | Request passive mode |
| `TYPE A` / `TYPE I` | Set transfer mode (ASCII / Binary) |
| `QUIT` | Disconnect |

### 2.4 FTP Response Codes (Reply Format)

Every server reply is a **3-digit code** followed by a human-readable message. The first digit defines the category.

| Code Range | Category | Meaning | Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1xx** | **Positive Preliminary** | Action started, expect another reply before sending next command | `125 Data connection already open` |
| **2xx** | **Positive Completion** | Command successfully completed | `200 Command okay`, `226 Transfer complete`, `230 User logged in` |
| **3xx** | **Positive Intermediate** | Command accepted, need more info | `331 User name okay, need password`, `350 Requested file action pending` |
| **4xx** | **Transient Negative Completion** | Temporary failure, retry possible | `425 Can't open data connection`, `450 File unavailable` |
| **5xx** | **Permanent Negative Completion** | Permanent failure, do not retry | `500 Syntax error`, `530 Not logged in`, `550 Permission denied` |

### 2.5 Data Representation Types

- **ASCII (TYPE A)** — 7-bit NVT-ASCII; used for text files. FTP automatically converts line endings between systems (CR-LF ↔ LF).
- **Binary / Image (TYPE I)** — Raw 8-bit byte stream; **no conversion**; used for images, executables, compressed files.
- **EBCDIC (TYPE E)** — Used between IBM mainframes.

> [!WARNING]
> Uploading a binary file in ASCII mode is one of the most common FTP pitfalls — it silently corrupts the file.

### 2.6 FTP vs. TFTP (Frequently Compared in KTU)

| Feature | **FTP** | **TFTP** |
|---|---|---|
| **Transport** | **TCP** (reliable) | **UDP** (unreliable) |
| **Port** | **21** (control), 20 (data) | **69** |
| **Authentication** | Yes (USER/PASS) | **No** |
| **Commands** | Many (rich set) | Only 5: `RRQ`, `WRQ`, `DATA`, `ACK`, `ERROR` |
| **Data Representation** | ASCII, Binary, EBCDIC | Only 8-bit binary |
| **Use Case** | Internet file transfers, web publishing | Booting, config backup, network devices (PXE, routers) |
| **Direction** | Bidirectional | Read or Write only |

### 2.7 Anonymous FTP

- Login as username **`anonymous`** and password = **email address** (by convention).
- Used to distribute public files (open-source software, docs) without per-user accounts.
- Server restricts anonymous users to a **chrooted** directory tree.

### 2.8 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Value / Rule** |
|---|---|
| OSI Layer | **Application Layer (Layer 7)** |
| Transport Protocol | **TCP** |
| Control Port | **21** |
| Data Port (Active) | **20** |
| Data Port (Passive) | **Ephemeral** (negotiated via PASV reply) |
| RFC | **RFC 959** (1985) |
| Architecture | **Client–Server** |
| Channels | **Two** (Control + Data) |
| State | **Stateful, Connection-Oriented** |
| Active Mode Initiation | **Server** initiates data connection |
| Passive Mode Initiation | **Client** initiates data connection |
| Response Code Digit 1 | 1=preliminary, 2=success, 3=intermediate, 4=transient fail, 5=permanent fail |
| Transfer Modes | ASCII (A), Binary/Image (I), EBCDIC (E) |
| Anonymous Login User | `anonymous` |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Step-by-Step Working of an FTP Session (Active Mode)

> [!NOTE]
> Below is the **complete chronological dialogue** of a typical FTP session. We will trace **every command, every response code, and every TCP-level connection event** that occurs when a user `alice` downloads `report.pdf` from server `S` to client `C`.

**Step 1 — Establish the Control Connection**
The FTP client `C` opens a TCP connection to server `S` on **port 21**. This is the **control channel** (PI channel) and stays open for the entire session.

$$\text{C}: \text{ephemeral port } p_C \;\longrightarrow\; \text{S}:\text{port } 21 \quad [\text{TCP SYN / SYN-ACK / ACK 3-way handshake}]$$

**Step 2 — Server Greets the Client**
Upon accepting the connection, server sends a **220** (Service ready) banner.

$$\text{S} \rightarrow \text{C}: \quad \texttt{220 \text{ ftp.example.com FTP server ready}}$$

> This is a **2xx Positive Completion** code — the server announces it is ready to serve.

**Step 3 — Client Sends Credentials (Two-Stage Login)**
For a non-anonymous user, the client first sends the username. The server responds with a **331** (intermediate — need more info), prompting the password.

$$\text{C} \rightarrow \text{S}: \quad \texttt{USER alice}$$

$$\text{S} \rightarrow \text{C}: \quad \texttt{331 \text{ Password required for alice}}$$

$$\text{C} \rightarrow \text{S}: \quad \texttt{PASS secret123}$$

$$\text{S} \rightarrow \text{C}: \quad \texttt{230 \text{ User alice logged in, proceed}}$$

> The **230** is a **2xx** code — login is complete. Authentication is now established on the control channel.

**Step 4 — Set Transfer Type**
Before any file is sent, the client typically sets the data representation. For `.pdf`, this must be **binary**.

$$\text{C} \rightarrow \text{S}: \quad \texttt{TYPE I}$$

$$\text{S} \rightarrow \text{C}: \quad \texttt{200 \text{ Type set to I (Image/Binary)}}$$

> ASCII type (`TYPE A`) would corrupt the binary PDF — this is why KTU questions always emphasize setting `TYPE I` for non-text files.

**Step 5 — Tell the Server to Retrieve the File (Active Mode)**
The client issues the `RETR` command. But first, in **active mode**, the client must announce where the server can connect for data.

$$\text{C} \rightarrow \text{S}: \quad \texttt{PORT 192,168,1,5,4,210}$$

Here the address and port are encoded as 6 comma-separated numbers. The data port is decoded as:

$$\text{Data Port} = (p_1 \times 256) + p_2 = (4 \times 256) + 210 = 1234$$

So the client says: *"Server, open a TCP connection to me at IP 192.168.1.5, port 1234, for the data channel."*

$$\text{S} \rightarrow \text{C}: \quad \texttt{200 \text{ PORT command successful}}$$

**Step 6 — Request the File**
Now the actual retrieval command is issued.

$$\text{C} \rightarrow \text{S}: \quad \texttt{RETR report.pdf}$$

The server first replies **150** (preliminary 1xx — "data connection accepted, transfer starting"), then opens a **new TCP connection from port 20 → client port 1234**, and starts streaming the file.

$$\text{S} \rightarrow \text{C}: \quad \texttt{125 \text{ Data connection already open; starting transfer}}$$

The byte stream of `report.pdf` flows over the data channel:

$$\text{S}:\text{port 20} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{C}:\text{port 1234} \quad [\text{TCP segments carrying file bytes}]$$

**Step 7 — Transfer Complete**
When the last byte is delivered, the server closes the data connection and sends **226** over the **control channel** (note: control is still open).

$$\text{S} \rightarrow \text{C}: \quad \texttt{226 \text{ Transfer complete}}$$

> This **226** code confirms the data channel can now be torn down — the file transfer portion is finished.

**Step 8 — Session Termination**
The user issues `QUIT` and the server replies **221** before closing the control connection.

$$\text{C} \rightarrow \text{S}: \quad \texttt{QUIT}$$

$$\text{S} \rightarrow \text{C}: \quad \texttt{221 \text{ Service closing control connection}}$$

$$\text{TCP FIN exchange} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Control channel closed}$$

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Active vs Passive Mode Trace

| **Step** | **Active Mode (`PORT`)** | **Passive Mode (`PASV`)** |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Client → Server:21 (control) | Client → Server:21 (control) |
| 2 | Client → Server: `PORT a,b,c,d,p1,p2` | Client → Server: `PASV` |
| 3 | Server replies `200 PORT ok` | Server replies `227 Entering Passive Mode (h1,h2,h3,h4,p1,p2)` |
| 4 | **Server:20 → Client:port** (server dials client) | **Client:port → Server:port** (client dials server) |
| 5 | File data flows on this new connection | File data flows on this new connection |
| 6 | Data channel closed after each file | Data channel closed after each file |

### 3.3 Worked Example — Decoding PASV Reply

A passive-mode server replies:

$$\texttt{227 Entering Passive Mode (192,168,10,5,17,84)}$$

The actual server IP and port for the data connection are:

$$\text{IP} = 192.168.10.5$$

$$\text{Data Port} = (17 \times 256) + 84 = 4352 + 84 = 4436$$

So the client opens a TCP connection to **192.168.10.5:4436** to begin the file transfer.

### 3.4 Time Diagram of an FTP Session (ASCII Timeline)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Time} \; t_0 &: \;\; \text{TCP handshake on port 21} \\
t_1 &: \;\; \text{220 banner} \\
t_2 &: \;\; \text{USER} \rightarrow \text{331} \\
t_3 &: \;\; \text{PASS} \rightarrow \text{230} \\
t_4 &: \;\; \text{TYPE I} \rightarrow \text{200} \\
t_5 &: \;\; \text{PORT} \;/\; \text{PASV} \rightarrow \text{200 / 227} \\
t_6 &: \;\; \text{RETR file} \rightarrow \text{150} \\
t_7 &: \;\; \text{Data channel open} \\
t_8 &: \;\; \text{Bytes flow S} \rightarrow \text{C} \\
t_9 &: \;\; \text{Data channel close} \\
t_{10} &: \;\; \text{226 Transfer complete} \\
t_{11} &: \;\; \text{QUIT} \rightarrow \text{221} \\
t_{12} &: \;\; \text{Control channel FIN}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.5 Failure-Handling Matrix (Negative Responses)

| Error Scenario | Response Code | Category | Action by Client |
|---|---|---|---|
| Wrong password | `530 Login incorrect` | 5xx Permanent | Re-prompt or abort |
| File does not exist | `550 File not found` | 5xx Permanent | Notify user |
| Data connection blocked by firewall | `425 Can't open data connection` | 4xx Transient | Switch to passive mode, retry |
| Syntax error in command | `500 Syntax error` | 5xx Permanent | Re-send corrected command |
| Server busy | `421 Service not available` | 4xx Transient | Retry after delay |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **4xx = retry possible; 5xx = don't retry, change command or abort.** This single line is worth 2–3 marks in KTU exams.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 FTP Two-Channel Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["FTP CLIENT HOST"] -->|Control Channel\nPort 21\nCommands USER PASS RETR QUIT| B["FTP SERVER HOST"]
    B -->|Control Channel\nPort 21\nResponse codes 220 230 226| A
    A -->|Data Channel\nPort 20 active or ephemeral passive\nFile bytes directory listing| B
    B -->|Data Channel\nFile bytes or directory listing| A
```

### 4.2 Active vs Passive Mode Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0["Client connects to Server Port 21"] --> S1["Client decides transfer mode"]
    S1 -->|"Sends PORT command"| A1["ACTIVE MODE\nServer dials client from Port 20"]
    S1 -->|"Sends PASV command"| A2["PASSIVE MODE\nServer replies 227 with IP and port"]
    A1 --> A3["Server opens data channel to client"]
    A2 --> A4["Client opens data channel to server"]
    A3 --> A5["File data transferred"]
    A4 --> A5
    A5 --> A6["Data channel closed"]
    A6 --> A7["Control channel remains open for more commands"]
```

### 4.3 FTP Session State Machine (High-Level)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Disconnected
    Disconnected --> ControlOpen: TCP connect to port 21
    ControlOpen --> AwaitingAuth: Receive 220 banner
    AwaitingAuth --> Authenticated: USER plus PASS success 230
    Authenticated --> FileTransfer: RETR STOR LIST
    FileTransfer --> Authenticated: 226 Transfer complete
    Authenticated --> Disconnected: QUIT plus 221
    AwaitingAuth --> Disconnected: 530 Login failed
    FileTransfer --> FileTransfer: PORT or PASV to transfer next file
```

### 4.4 Functional Architecture — Client & Server Components

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CLIENT
        CP["User Interface\nCommand Parser"]
        PIc["Protocol Interpreter PI\nSends USER PASS RETR"]
        DTPc["Data Transfer Process DTP\nSends and receives file bytes"]
    end
    subgraph SERVER
        PIs["Protocol Interpreter PI\nParses commands sends response codes"]
        DTPs["Data Transfer Process DTP\nReads or writes file system"]
        FS["File System\nStores files and directories"]
    end
    CP --> PIc
    PIc <-->|Control Channel Port 21| PIs
    DTPc <-->|Data Channel Port 20 or ephemeral| DTPs
    PIs --> DTPs
    DTPs --> FS
```

### 4.5 Encapsulation of FTP in the TCP/IP Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L7["Layer 7 Application\nFTP Commands and Responses"]
    L4["Layer 4 Transport\nTCP for both channels"]
    L3["Layer 3 Network\nIP addressing and routing"]
    L2["Layer 2 Data Link\nEthernet or WiFi frames"]
    L1["Layer 1 Physical\nElectrical or optical signals"]
    L7 --> L4 --> L3 --> L2 --> L1
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
> **List any three FTP response codes and state their meaning.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **`226 Transfer complete`** — Code 2xx (Positive Completion). The file/data transfer over the data connection has finished successfully and the data connection is being closed.
2. **`331 User name okay, need password`** — Code 3xx (Positive Intermediate). The `USER` command was accepted; the server now expects the `PASS` command to complete authentication.
3. **`550 Permission denied`** — Code 5xx (Permanent Negative Completion). The requested file action (e.g., `RETR`, `STOR`, `DELE`) cannot be performed, usually due to insufficient privileges or non-existent file. Client should **not retry**.

*(Any three valid codes with correct categories and meaning — full 3 marks.)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]**
> **Differentiate between FTP and TFTP in any three aspects.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — table form recommended):**

| Aspect | FTP | TFTP |
|---|---|---|
| **Transport Protocol** | Uses **TCP** (reliable, connection-oriented) | Uses **UDP** (unreliable, connectionless) |
| **Authentication** | Supports **username/password** login | **No authentication**; open access |
| **Port Number** | **Port 21** (control) and **Port 20** (data, active mode) | **Port 69** |
| **Complexity** | Many commands (USER, RETR, STOR, LIST…) | Only 5 message types (RRQ, WRQ, DATA, ACK, ERROR) |
| **Use Case** | Internet file transfer, web publishing | Network booting (PXE), router config transfer |

*(Any three points with contrast — full 3 marks.)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **Q3 (A). [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Model Paper]**
> **(a)** Explain the **FTP architecture** in detail. With a neat diagram, describe the **control connection** and **data connection**, including their port numbers and lifetimes. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Explain **Active Mode** and **Passive Mode** of FTP data connection establishment. Clearly state **who initiates** the data connection in each mode and the practical reason passive mode is preferred today. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Q3(A)(a) [7 marks]

**FTP Architecture (Client–Server, Two-Channel Model):**

FTP is a **TCP-based application layer protocol** standardized in **RFC 959**. It uses a **client–server model** where the client initiates a session, and the server hosts the file system being accessed.

FTP is architecturally unique because it uses **two parallel TCP connections** between the same endpoints:

1. **Control Connection (PI channel):**
   - Opened by the **client** to **server port 21**.
   - Carries **FTP commands** (e.g., `USER`, `PASS`, `RETR`, `STOR`, `LIST`, `QUIT`) from client to server.
   - Carries **response codes** (e.g., `220`, `230`, `226`, `550`) from server to client.
   - **Persistent** — remains open for the entire FTP session, from login to `QUIT`.
   - Character-oriented (uses **NVT-ASCII**).

2. **Data Connection (DTP channel):**
   - Carries the **actual file bytes** or **directory listings**.
   - In **Active mode**, server opens it from **port 20** to a client-specified port.
   - In **Passive mode**, client opens it to a server-announced ephemeral port.
   - **Transient** — opened per file, closed after each transfer (signalled by `226`).

**Diagram (refer to Section 4.1 block diagram):**

The client sends `RETR report.pdf` over the control channel; the server replies `150 File status okay`, opens a data connection, streams the file, then replies `226 Transfer complete`. The data connection closes; the control connection remains alive for further commands.

**Conclusion:** The separation of control and data channels allows the user to issue new commands (browse, rename) while a file transfer is in progress. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Q3(A)(b) [7 marks]

**Active vs Passive Mode:**

| Parameter | Active Mode (PORT) | Passive Mode (PASV) |
|---|---|---|
| Command used | `PORT a,b,c,d,p1,p2` | `PASV` |
| Who initiates data conn. | **Server** (from port 20 → client port) | **Client** (client port → server port) |
| Data port | Server **port 20** | Server **ephemeral port** (announced in `227` reply) |
| Direction of opening | Server → Client (inbound to client) | Client → Server (outbound from client) |
| Works through client firewall? | **No** (client must accept incoming) | **Yes** |
| Works with NAT? | Often breaks | Preferred |

**Why Passive Mode is Preferred Today:**

Most modern clients sit behind **NAT routers** and **stateful firewalls** that block **incoming** connections by default. In active mode, the server's *incoming* data connection is blocked because the client's firewall never permitted inbound traffic on the announced port. Passive mode reverses the direction: the **client** dials the server, which is an **outbound** connection from the client's perspective and is therefore allowed by virtually all firewalls. FTP clients in browsers, FileZilla, and OS shells default to PASV for this reason.

**Conclusion:** Passive mode is the de-facto standard in modern deployments because of NAT and firewall compatibility. **(7 marks)**

---

> **Q3 (B). [Alternative 14-Mark Choice]**
> **(a)** Write the **complete FTP session** in chronological order for a user downloading a file using **active mode**, including the **response codes** returned by the server. State the meaning of each response code. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** What is **Anonymous FTP**? Explain the **three data representation types** supported by FTP and state which one is mandatory for binary files. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Q3(B)(a) [7 marks]

**Complete FTP Session (Active Mode Download):**

| # | Direction | Command / Reply | Code | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | C → S :21 | TCP SYN (3-way handshake) | — | Control channel opened |
| 2 | S → C | `220 ftp.example.com FTP server ready` | **220** | Service ready for new user |
| 3 | C → S | `USER alice` | — | Sending username |
| 4 | S → C | `331 Password required for alice` | **331** | User name okay, need password |
| 5 | C → S | `PASS secret` | — | Sending password |
| 6 | S → C | `230 User logged in, proceed` | **230** | Login successful |
| 7 | C → S | `TYPE I` | — | Request binary mode |
| 8 | S → C | `200 Type set to I` | **200** | Command okay |
| 9 | C → S | `PORT 192,168,1,5,4,210` | — | Data port = (4×256)+210 = 1234 |
| 10 | S → C | `200 PORT command successful` | **200** | Address accepted |
| 11 | C → S | `RETR report.pdf` | — | Request the file |
| 12 | S → C | `150 Opening data connection` | **150** | File status okay, about to open data conn |
| 13 | S:20 → C:1234 | File bytes stream | — | Data channel in use |
| 14 | S → C | `226 Transfer complete` | **226** | Data connection closing; transfer OK |
| 15 | C → S | `QUIT` | — | Logout |
| 16 | S → C | `221 Service closing control connection` | **221** | Goodbye |

**(7 marks — full sequence with correct codes and meanings)**

#### Model Solution — Q3(B)(b) [7 marks]

**Anonymous FTP (3 marks):**
Anonymous FTP is a variant of FTP that allows **any user** to log in without a pre-issued account. The user logs in with the username **`anonymous`** (or `ftp`) and provides **their email address as the password** by convention. The FTP server restricts anonymous users to a **chrooted** subdirectory containing only public files (e.g., open-source software, documentation). It is widely used by universities, Linux distributions (e.g., `ftp.gnu.org`), and open-source projects to distribute files to the public.

**Three Data Representation Types (3 marks):**

1. **ASCII Mode (`TYPE A`)** — 7-bit NVT-ASCII. Used for plain text. FTP automatically converts line endings (CR-LF ↔ LF) between heterogeneous systems (Windows ↔ Unix).
2. **Binary / Image Mode (`TYPE I`)** — 8-bit raw byte stream. **No conversion** whatsoever. Used for images (`.jpg`, `.png`), PDFs, `.zip`, executables.
3. **EBCDIC Mode (`TYPE E`)** — IBM's 8-bit character encoding; used when both endpoints are IBM mainframes.

**Mandatory mode for binary files:** **`TYPE I` (Binary/Image mode)**. **(1 mark — conclusion)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks on FTP Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the second port:** Many students write only Port 21 and miss the *separate* data connection. Always mention **two channels**.
> 2. **Active vs Passive confusion:** Saying "client opens data channel in active mode" is **wrong** — the **server** opens it in active mode. Marks deducted immediately.
> 3. **Wrong category for response codes:** `226` is **2xx** (completion), `331` is **3xx** (intermediate), not all codes are "2xx success". Examiners check the first digit.
> 4. **Forgetting `TYPE I` for binary files:** A download scenario without setting the transfer type is considered incomplete in 14-mark answers.
> 5. **Mixing FTP with TFTP facts:** Writing that TFTP uses Port 21 or supports authentication will cost marks.
> 6. **Missing passive-mode NAT rationale:** Examiners specifically look for the keyword **"firewall"** or **"NAT"** in passive-mode answers.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **FTP = File Transfer Protocol**, application layer, **TCP-based**, defined in **RFC 959**.
- Uses a **Client–Server** model with **two TCP channels**: **Control (Port 21, persistent)** and **Data (Port 20 active / ephemeral passive, transient)**.
- **Active Mode (`PORT`)** → **Server** dials the **client's** data port. Breaks with firewalls/NAT.
- **Passive Mode (`PASV`)** → **Client** dials the **server's** announced data port. Preferred in modern use.
- Commands are **NVT-ASCII** text; responses are **3-digit numeric codes** categorized as 1xx/2xx/3xx/4xx/5xx.
- Transfer types: **ASCII (A)**, **Binary/Image (I)**, **EBCDIC (E)**. Use `TYPE I` for non-text files.
- **Anonymous FTP** uses username `anonymous` and an email as password.
- **TFTP** differs from FTP: uses **UDP**, **Port 69**, **no auth**, only 5 message types, used for booting and network-device config.
- Always remember: **Control = 21, Data = 20 (active only), State = Stateful, Transport = TCP, Layer = Application (7).**
- For KTU 14-mark answers, always draw a **diagram** of the two-channel architecture and provide a **chronological command-response table** for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electronic Mail
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAMT632 - BASICS OF COMPUTER NETWORKS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Electronic Mail # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Electronic Mail (E-mail)** is an asynchronous, store-and-forward method of exchanging digital messages between two or more networked computer users through the Internet, governed by a layered set of application-layer protocols standardized by the **Internet Engineering Task Force (IETF)**. > [!IMPORTANT] KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition > Electronic Mail is the most heavily used application on the Internet. It is an asynchronous communication medium — meaning the sender and receiver do not need to be online at the same time. E-mail supports three sub-protocols of its own: **SMTP** (for sending/relaying), **POP3 / IMAP** (for retrieving), and **MIME** (for multimedia extension). The three principal protocols that constitute the e-mail ecosystem in the KTU curriculum are: | Protocol | Full Form | Primary Role | |----------|-----------|--------------| | **SMTP** | Simple Mail Transfer Protocol | Pushing mail from client to server, and between servers (RFC 5321) | | **POP3** | Post Office Protocol v3 | Pulling mail from server to local client (RFC 1939) | | **IMAP** | Internet Message Access Protocol | Managing mail while it remains on the remote server (RFC 3501) | | **MIME** | Multipurpose Internet Mail Extensions | Encoding non-ASCII / multimedia content (RFC 2045–2049) | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition Think of e-mail exactly like the **traditional postal system**, but compressed into milliseconds: - **You (Sender)** → The **post office counter clerk** who accepts your letter. In e-mail terms, this is the **Mail User Agent (MUA)** — e.g., Gmail web, Outlook, Thunderbird. - **Sorting Office (Local Post Office)** → Accepts the letter, stamps it, and routes it to the destination city. In e-mail, this is the **Mail Transfer Agent (MTA)** — e.g., Postfix, Sendmail, Microsoft Exchange. - **Final Delivery Office** → The destination post office that holds your letter in the recipient's mailbox. In e-mail, this is the **Mail Delivery Agent (MDA)** combined with the **Mailbox Store**. - **Recipient's Home Mailbox** → The place where letters physically sit until the owner checks. In e-mail, this is the **IMAP/POP3 server** that holds the inbox. - **Recipient (Walking to mailbox)** → The **Mail Retrieval Agent (MRA)** or the receiving MUA that connects via IMAP/POP3. > [!NOTE] Key Distinction > **SMTP = PUSH protocol** (sender pushes mail toward destination). > **POP3 / IMAP = PULL protocols** (recipient pulls mail from the server). ## 1.3 Why E-Mail is Asynchronous E-mail is **asynchronous** because: - The sender composes a message and submits it to the local MTA. - The local MTA may queue the message for **minutes, hours, or even days** if the destination server is temporarily unreachable. - Internet mail uses a **store-and-forward** architecture: every intermediate MTA stores the message before forwarding it. This is fundamentally different from synchronous media like a **telephone call** or a **video conference**, where both parties must be present simultaneously. ## 1.4 Physical / Standard Metrics in E-Mail - **Standard SMTP default port:** **25** (plain), **587** (submission), **465** (legacy SMTPS). - **Standard POP3 default port:** **110** (plain), **995** (POP3S). - **Standard IMAP default port:** **143** (plain), **993** (IMAPS). - **Maximum SMTP line length:** **998 octets** + CRLF (RFC 5321 §4.5.3.1). - **Minimum SMTP line length:** **512 octets** (server must support at least 512). > [!IMPORTANT] > A bare SMTP envelope can transmit only **7-bit ASCII** text. Binary data (images, audio, PDFs) **must** be encoded using **MIME** (Base64, Quoted-Printable). --- # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Five-Stage Lifecycle of an E-Mail Every e-mail travels through a deterministic, well-defined sequence: **Stage 1 — Composition (MUA → MSA)** The user types the message in a MUA (Mail User Agent). When "Send" is pressed, the MUA connects to a **Mail Submission Agent (MSA)** — typically on port **587** — and hands the message over using SMTP. **Stage 2 — Submission (MSA → MTA)** The MSA authenticates the user (using SASL, e.g., LOGIN / PLAIN / CRAM-MD5). On success, the MSA acts as a client and transfers the message to the local **MTA** using SMTP. **Stage 3 — Routing (MTA → MTA across the Internet)** The originating MTA performs a **DNS MX (Mail Exchange) lookup** for the recipient's domain. $$ \text{MX query} \rightarrow \text{resolver returns MX records (with priority)} $$ > [!NOTE] DNS MX Record > A domain like `ktu.edu.in` may have MX records: `mail1.ktu.edu.in` (priority 10) and `mail2.ktu.edu.in` (priority 20). The **lowest priority number is preferred**. The MTA opens an SMTP connection (port 25) to the chosen MX host and forwards the message. The message may traverse **multiple hops (relay MTAs)** before reaching the destination. **Stage 4 — Delivery (MTA → MDA → Mailbox Store)** The destination MTA hands the message to the local **MDA (Mail Delivery Agent)**, which writes the message into the recipient's **mailbox store** (typically `mbox` or `Maildir` format on disk). **Stage 5 — Retrieval (MUA ↔ MRA via POP3 / IMAP)** The recipient's MUA connects to a **Mail Retrieval Agent (MRA)** running on the destination server. The MRA speaks either POP3 (port 110) or IMAP (port 143) and synchronizes the mailbox with the local client. ## 2.2 SMTP Command–Response Dialogue (RFC 5321) SMTP is a **human-readable, line-oriented, text-based protocol** using commands (4-letter codes) and three-digit numeric response codes. ### Core SMTP Commands | Command | Purpose | |---------|---------| | `HELO` / `EHLO` | Identify sender to server (`EHLO` = Extended SMTP, mandatory in modern usage) | | `MAIL FROM` | Start a new mail transaction; declare the envelope sender | | `RCPT TO` | Declare one recipient (can be repeated for multiple recipients) | | `DATA` | Begin message body; terminated by `.` | | `RSET` | Abort current transaction | | `NOOP` | No-op; used to keep connection alive | | `QUIT` | Close connection | | `VRFY` | Verify a user address | | `AUTH` | Authenticate the client (ESMTP extension) | | `STARTTLS` | Upgrade plaintext connection to TLS (ESMTP extension) | ### Three-Digit SMTP Response Codes (First-Digit Categories) | Code Range | Category | Meaning | |------------|----------|---------| | **2yz** | Positive Completion | Command accepted | | **3yz** | Positive Intermediate | More input needed (e.g., `334` waiting for AUTH data) | | **4yz** | Transient Negative | Temporary failure (try again later) | | **5yz** | Permanent Negative | Fatal error; message will not be retried | ### Commonly Seen Replies - `220 Service ready` - `250 OK` - `354 Start mail input; end with .` - `421 Service not available, closing transmission channel` - `550 Mailbox unavailable / User not local` ## 2.3 MIME — Multipurpose Internet Mail Extensions Plain RFC 5322 messages are restricted to **7-bit US-ASCII text**. MIME solves this by adding structured headers. ### The Five MIME Header Fields 1. `MIME-Version: 1.0` 2. `Content-Type: ` — describes the data 3. `Content-Transfer-Encoding: ` — how to decode the bytes 4. `Content-ID: ` — optional unique identifier 5. `Content-Description: ` — optional human description ### Major Content Types | Top-Level | Examples | |-----------|----------| | `text` | `text/plain`, `text/html`, `text/enriched` | | `image` | `image/jpeg`, `image/png`, `image/gif` | | `audio` | `audio/basic`, `audio/mpeg` | | `video` | `video/mpeg`, `video/mp4` | | `application` | `application/pdf`, `application/octet-stream`, `application/zip` | | `multipart` | `multipart/mixed`, `multipart/alternative`, `multipart/related`, `multipart/form-data` | | `message` | `message/rfc822`, `message/partial` | ### Multipart Structure (Visualization) > A `multipart/mixed` message containing both plain text and an attached PDF has a body like: ``` Content-Type: multipart/mixed; boundary="BOUNDARY1" --BOUNDARY1 Content-Type: text/plain; charset="utf-8" Hello, please find the report attached. --BOUNDARY1 Content-Type: application/pdf Content-Transfer-Encoding: base64 Content-Disposition: attachment; filename="report.pdf" JVBERi0xLjQKJeLjz9MKMyAwIG9iago8PC9MZW5ndGggMTQ0Pj5zdHJlYW0K... --BOUNDARY1-- ``` ### Content-Transfer-Encoding Values | Encoding | Use Case | |----------|----------| | `7bit` | Already pure ASCII (default) | | `8bit` | Allows non-ASCII bytes (requires SMTP 8BITMIME extension) | | `binary` | Raw 8-bit (rare, non-standard) | | `quoted-printable` | Mostly ASCII with occasional 8-bit chars | | `base64` | Arbitrary binary data (encodes 3 bytes → 4 printable chars) | ## 2.4 POP3 vs IMAP — Comparison Matrix | Feature | POP3 (Post Office Protocol v3) | IMAP (Internet Message Access Protocol) | |---------|-------------------------------|------------------------------------------| | Default port | 110 (995 for POP3S) | 143 (993 for IMAPS) | | Storage model | Download-and-delete (default) | Server-side; messages stay on server | | Folder support | Single `INBOX` | Hierarchical folders, flags, search | | Bandwidth | Low (one-time download) | Higher (sync operations) | | Multiple-device access | Poor (mail exists on one device) | Excellent (any device sees same state) | | Connection model | Online + Offline + Update | Online + Offline + Disconnected | | UID tracking | Optional | Mandatory `UID` per message | | Idle push (IDLE) | Not defined | `IDLE` command for server-initiated push | | RFC | 1939 | 3501 (current) | ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat Sheet | Symbol / Item | Definition | Standard Value / Formula | |---------------|------------|--------------------------| | $L_{\text{line}}$ | Maximum line length (SMTP) | $L_{\text{line}} \le 998$ octets + CRLF | | $L_{\min}$ | Minimum acceptable line length | $L_{\min} \ge 512$ octets | | $E_{\text{base64}}$ | Base64 encoding expansion | $E_{\text{base64}} = \lceil \frac{n}{3} \rvert \rvert \cdot 4$ chars for $n$ input bytes | | $E_{\text{QP}}$ | Quoted-Printable expansion (worst case) | $E_{\text{QP}} \le 3n$ (each byte → `=XX`) | | $C_{\text{port}}$ | SMTP submission port | **587** (MSA) / **25** (MTA relay) | | $C_{\text{POP3}}$ | POP3 default port | **110** (995 encrypted) | | $C_{\text{IMAP}}$ | IMAP default port | **143** (993 encrypted) | | $R_{\text{MX}}$ | MX priority rule | Lowest numerical priority wins; tie broken by random | | $C_{\text{code}}$ | SMTP reply code | $C_{\text{code}} \in \{2yz, 3yz, 4yz, 5yz\}$ | > [!NOTE] Why Base64 Inflates Size > Base64 encodes **3 input bytes → 4 output characters**, so the output is always $\tfrac{4}{3} \approx 1.33\times$ the input. A **10 MB** attachment therefore becomes approximately **13.3 MB** in transit. ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility E-mail infrastructure powers the modern enterprise: - **Transactional systems** — OTPs, password resets, order confirmations. - **Notification systems** — alerts from IoT, monitoring dashboards (Prometheus, Grafana). - **Marketing automation** — drip campaigns, newsletters (Mailchimp, SendGrid). - **Compliance archiving** — regulated industries (banking, healthcare) legally archive all e-mail. - **Forensic evidence** — RFC 5322 headers (`Received:` chain) reconstruct message path for legal investigation. --- # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Base64 Encoding Size > [!NOTE] Why this matters > KTU frequently asks students to compute the encoded size of attachments transmitted via SMTP. The derivation is fundamental. **Problem Setup:** Suppose an arbitrary binary file of size $n$ bytes is attached to an e-mail. It is encoded using **Base64** for transport through a 7-bit-ASCII SMTP channel. Derive the encoded length $E$ in characters. **Step 1 — Group input bytes into 3-byte blocks.** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Number of input blocks} &= \frac{n}{3} \\ \text{Output per block} &= 4 \text{ characters} \\ \text{If } n \bmod 3 \neq 0, &\text{ pad with zero bytes to complete the last block} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Account for padding.** $$ \begin{aligned} r &= n \bmod 3 \\ \text{If } r = 0 &: \text{ output} = \frac{n}{3} \times 4 = \frac{4n}{3} \\ \text{If } r = 1 &: \text{ output} = \frac{n}{3} \times 4 \;\text{(integer division)} + 4 \;\text{with two }`=' \text{ paddings} \\ \text{If } r = 2 &: \text{ output} = \frac{n}{3} \times 4 \;\text{(integer division)} + 4 \;\text{with one }`=' \text{ padding} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Closed-form expression using the ceiling function.** $$ E_{\text{base64}}(n) = 4 \cdot \left\lceil \frac{n}{3} \right\rceil $$ **Step 4 — Verify with an example: $n = 150$ bytes.** $$ \begin{aligned} \left\lceil \frac{150}{3} \right\rceil &= 50 \\ E &= 4 \times 50 = 200 \text{ characters} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Verify with a non-multiple: $n = 151$ bytes.** $$ \begin{aligned} \left\lceil \frac{151}{3} \right\rceil &= \lceil 50.333\ldots \rceil = 51 \\ E &= 4 \times 51 = 204 \text{ characters (2 padding `=' signs at the end)} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6 — Expansion ratio.** $$ \rho_{\text{base64}} = \frac{E}{n} = \frac{4 \cdot \lceil n/3 \rceil}{n} \;\longrightarrow\; \frac{4}{3} \approx 1.3333 \text{ as } n \to \infty $$ ## 3.2 Derivation: Quoted-Printable Encoding Worst-Case Expansion **Step 1 — Encoding rule.** For any non-printable byte $b$ (or any byte with the high bit set, or any of `=`, CRLF rule violations), QP outputs the three characters `=XX` where `XX` is the uppercase hex value of $b$. **Step 2 — Worst case (all bytes require encoding).** $$ \begin{aligned} E_{\text{QP, max}}(n) &= 3n \text{ characters} \\ \rho_{\text{QP, max}} &= 3 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Best case (all bytes are printable ASCII).** $$ E_{\text{QP, min}}(n) = n, \quad \rho_{\text{QP, min}} = 1 $$ **Step 4 — Realistic mixed case.** If the source is UTF-8 with mixed ASCII / multi-byte characters, typical expansion lies between 1.1× and 1.5×. This is why Base64 is preferred for dense binary content (PDFs, images) and QP is preferred for mostly-textual content (e-mail signatures with diacritics). ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: E-Mail Protocol Selection > [!TIP] KTU Examiner's Note > A common 14-mark question asks: *"Compare and contrast SMTP, POP3, and IMAP with respect to directionality, port, message storage, and use case."* Use the matrix below as the model answer skeleton. | Parameter | SMTP | POP3 | IMAP | |-----------|------|------|------| | Direction | **PUSH** (client → server, server → server) | **PULL** (client ← server) | **PULL / SYNC** (client ↔ server) | | Default port | 25 / 587 | 110 | 143 | | Secure port | 465 (SMTPS) | 995 (POP3S) | 993 (IMAPS) | | RFC | 5321 | 1939 | 3501 | | Mail retention | No (transient) | Yes (download-and-delete by default) | Yes (persistent server-side) | | Multiple folders | N/A | Single INBOX | Yes, hierarchical | | Bandwidth cost | Per-message forward | High at first download | Per-operation sync | | Statefulness | Stateless per message | Stateless per session | Stateful, supports `UIDVALIDITY` | | Authentication | `AUTH` (ESMTP) | `USER`/`PASS` (plain) or `AUTH` | `LOGIN`, `AUTH=*` (SASL) | | Best for | Server-to-server handoff | Offline / single-device clients | Multi-device, webmail, mobile | ## 3.4 Worked Example: SMTP Conversation Trace > A KTU favourite: *"Illustrate the SMTP dialogue for sending a message from `alice@ktu.edu.in` to `bob@iisc.ac.in`. Show the actual commands."* **Model Solution:** ``` S: 220 iisc.ac.in ESMTP Postfix C: EHLO ktu.edu.in S: 250-iisc.ac.in S: 250-PIPELINING S: 250-SIZE 10240000 S: 250-STARTTLS S: 250-8BITMIME S: 250 AUTH PLAIN LOGIN C: MAIL FROM: S: 250 2.1.0 Ok C: RCPT TO: S: 250 2.1.5 Ok C: DATA S: 354 End data with . C: From: Alice C: To: Bob C: Subject: Test Mail C: Date: Mon, 12 Aug 2024 10:00:00 +0530 C: MIME-Version: 1.0 C: Content-Type: text/plain; charset=utf-8 C: C: Hello Bob, this is a test. C: . S: 250 2.0.0 Ok: queued as 12345 C: QUIT S: 221 2.0.0 Bye ``` > [!WARNING] KTU Pitfall > Examiners **deduct marks** if students forget: > 1. The period `.` on a line by itself terminates the `DATA` phase. > 2. `EHLO` (not `HELO`) is mandatory for modern servers. > 3. The first digit of the response code carries the class (`2` = success, `5` = permanent failure). ## 3.5 Full E-Mail Architecture Block Flow The complete handoff chain — for an exam answer or a system-design interview: $$ \text{MUA}_A \xrightarrow{\text{SMTP :587}} \text{MSA}_A \xrightarrow{\text{SMTP :25}} \text{MTA}_A \xrightarrow{\text{SMTP :25 (multi-hop)}} \text{MTA}_Z \xrightarrow{\text{local}} \text{MDA}_Z \xrightarrow{\text{disk write}} \text{Mailbox}_Z $$ $$ \text{MUA}_Z \xleftarrow{\text{POP3 :110 / IMAP :143}} \text{MRA}_Z $$ The **envelope sender** (`MAIL FROM`) and **envelope recipient** (`RCPT TO`) are SMTP-layer and may differ from the visible `From:` / `To:` **header** lines (which are part of the message body, not the protocol envelope). --- # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 End-to-End E-Mail Delivery Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Alice MUA
Gmail Web Client"] B["Alice MSA
smtp.gmail.com:587"] C["Alice MTA
gmail-relay:25"] D["Intermediate Relay
MX hop 1"] E["Destination MTA
iisc-mx:25"] F["Destination MDA
procmail / dovecot LDA"] G["Bob Mailbox
Maildir store"] H["Bob MRA
Dovecot:143 IMAP"] I["Bob MUA
Thunderbird"] A -->|SMTP AUTH LOGIN| B B -->|SMTP relay| C C -->|DNS MX lookup| D D -->|SMTP forward| E E -->|Local delivery| F F -->|Disk write| G G -->|IMAP IDLE| H H -->|IMAP fetch| I ``` ## 4.2 SMTP Command–Response State Machine ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Connected : TCP handshake Connected --> Greeted : C: EHLO hostname Greeted --> Authed : C: AUTH LOGIN Authed --> InTransaction : C: MAIL FROM InTransaction --> InTransaction : C: RCPT TO add another InTransaction --> Sending : C: DATA Sending --> InTransaction : 250 OK InTransaction --> Greeted : C: RSET Greeted --> [*] : C: QUIT InTransaction --> [*] : C: QUIT Sending --> InTransaction : 354 ready ``` ## 4.3 MIME Multipart Nesting Hierarchy ```mermaid flowchart TB Root["multipart/mixed
BOUNDARY1"] PartA["text/plain
Human-readable body"] PartB["multipart/alternative
BOUNDARY2"] SubA["text/plain
ASCII version"] SubB["text/html
HTML version"] PartC["application/pdf
Base64-encoded attachment"] Root --> PartA Root --> PartB PartB --> SubA PartB --> SubB Root --> PartC ``` ## 4.4 POP3 vs IMAP — Functional Comparison Flow ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph POP3["POP3 Model"] P1["Client connects :110"] P2["USER / PASS or AUTH"] P3["STAT - read inbox summary"] P4["RETR msg-id - download"] P5["DELE msg-id - mark delete"] P6["QUIT - server purges"] P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4 --> P5 --> P6 end subgraph IMAP["IMAP Model"] I1["Client connects :143"] I2["LOGIN or AUTH"] I3["SELECT INBOX"] I4["FETCH UID body / header"] I5["STORE flags seen/answered"] I6["IDLE - wait for push"] I1 --> I2 --> I3 --> I4 --> I5 --> I6 end ``` ## 4.5 DNS MX Resolution — Routing Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TB Q["SMTP client
MAIL FROM: alice@ktu.edu.in"] R["RCPT TO: bob@iisc.ac.in"] L["DNS query:
MX iisc.ac.in"] P["Sort by priority
lowest first"] C["Open TCP :25
to chosen MX"] S["STARTTLS upgrade"] X["SMTP transaction"] Q --> R --> L --> P --> C --> S --> X ``` --- # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **Define SMTP. List any four SMTP commands with their function.** **Model Answer:** **SMTP (Simple Mail Transfer Protocol)** is the application-layer protocol defined in RFC 5321 used to **push** electronic mail from a sender's mail server to the receiver's mail server, and between intermediate mail servers (MTAs) over the Internet. It is a **text-based, command-response** protocol operating over a **reliable TCP connection** on port **25** (relay) or **587** (submission). | Command | Function | |---------|----------| | `HELO` / `EHLO` | Identify the sending host to the receiver | | `MAIL FROM` | Reverse-path (envelope sender) — opens a transaction | | `RCPT TO` | Forward-path (envelope recipient) — repeated for multiple recipients | | `DATA` | Begin the message body; terminated by `.` | | `QUIT` | Close the SMTP session | | `RSET` | Abort current mail transaction and reset buffers | | `VRFY` | Verify an e-mail address (often disabled) | | `NOOP` | No-operation; keeps the connection alive | --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024] **Differentiate between POP3 and IMAP.** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | POP3 (Post Office Protocol v3) | IMAP (Internet Message Access Protocol) | |-----------|--------------------------------|------------------------------------------| | Default port | 110 (995 encrypted) | 143 (993 encrypted) | | Storage model | Default mode is **download-and-delete**; mail resides on the local machine | Mail is **server-resident**; client only retrieves state | | Folder support | Only a single `INBOX` | Full **hierarchical folders** with flags | | Multiple-device access | Poor — first device to download "owns" the message | Excellent — every connected client sees the same state | | Bandwidth | Heavy one-time download, then no network use | Continuous synchronization overhead | | Idle push | Not supported | `IDLE` command allows server-initiated notifications | | Connection model | Online, Offline, or Quasi-offline | Online, Offline, or Disconnected | | Best suited for | Single-device, bandwidth-limited, local-archive users | Multi-device users, webmail, mobile-first experiences | --- ## Part B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice Questions > **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **Q.A (a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **architecture of the electronic mail system**. Identify and explain the functions of **MUA, MSA, MTA, MDA, and MRA**. (7 marks) **Q.A (b)** Explain the **SMTP protocol** in detail, including its operational model, the typical command–response dialogue (with at least six commands), and the three-digit reply code classes. (7 marks) --- #### Model Solution — Q.A (a) Architecture of E-Mail The e-mail architecture follows a **store-and-forward** model. Five logical agents are involved in the end-to-end flow: ```mermaid flowchart LR A["MUA
Mail User Agent"] B["MSA
Mail Submission Agent"] C["MTA
Mail Transfer Agent"] D["MDA
Mail Delivery Agent"] E["MRA
Mail Retrieval Agent"] A -->|Submit| B B -->|Relay| C C -->|Multi-hop| C C -->|Deliver| D D -->|Disk write| F["Mailbox store"] F -->|Read| E E -->|Protocol| A2["MUA of receiver"] ``` **1. MUA (Mail User Agent)** — The end-user application used to compose, read, and send mail. Examples: **Gmail, Outlook, Thunderbird, Apple Mail, mutt**. It is responsible for **message construction** and for **driving the SMTP/POP/IMAP client**. **2. MSA (Mail Submission Agent)** — A specialised MTA that receives messages from authenticated MUA clients, typically on port **587**. It enforces **authentication** (SASL), validates envelope syntax, and applies **anti-spam policies** before injecting the message into the relay network. **3. MTA (Mail Transfer Agent)** — The **router** of the e-mail network. Performs **DNS MX lookups**, opens outbound SMTP connections on port **25**, and forwards the message hop-by-hop until it reaches the destination domain. Examples: **Postfix, Sendmail, Exim, Microsoft Exchange Transport**. **4. MDA (Mail Delivery Agent)** — Accepts the message from the final MTA and writes it into the recipient's **mailbox file** (mbox, Maildir, database). It also applies **per-user filtering** (procmail, Sieve). Examples: **procmail, dovecot LDA, maildrop**. **5. MRA (Mail Retrieval Agent)** — Serves mailboxes to remote MUAs via **POP3** or **IMAP**. It only reads from the mailbox; it does not deliver. Examples: **Dovecot, Courier, Cyrus IMAP**. #### Model Solution — Q.A (b) SMTP Protocol **Operational Model** SMTP is a **client-server, text-based, request-response** protocol. It runs over **TCP**, providing a reliable byte stream. The default port is **25** (server-to-server relay) or **587** (MUA-to-MSA submission). Modern SMTP supports the **ESMTP** extensions initiated by `EHLO`. **Connection Lifecycle** 1. **Establish TCP** to port 25. 2. **Greet** the server: `EHLO `. 3. **Optionally authenticate** using `AUTH` (PLAIN, LOGIN, CRAM-MD5). 4. **Open a mail transaction** with `MAIL FROM:`. 5. **Declare recipients** with one or more `RCPT TO:`. 6. **Send body** with `DATA`; terminate with `.`. 7. **Reset** the transaction with `RSET` or **close** the session with `QUIT`. **Six Core Commands** | Command | Sample | Server Reply (typical) | |---------|--------|------------------------| | `EHLO ktu.edu.in` | — | `250-mail.iisc.ac.in Hello` | | `MAIL FROM:` | — | `250 2.1.0 Sender OK` | | `RCPT TO:` | — | `250 2.1.5 Recipient OK` | | `DATA` | — | `354 Send message; end with CRLF.CRLF` | | `RSET` | — | `250 2.0.0 OK` | | `QUIT` | — | `221 2.0.0 Bye` | **Three-Digit Reply Code Classes** | First digit | Class | Example | |-------------|-------|---------| | `2yz` | Positive completion | `250 OK` | | `3yz` | Positive intermediate (more input required) | `334 Continue authentication` | | `4yz` | Transient negative completion (retry later) | `421 Service not available` | | `5yz` | Permanent negative completion (do **not** retry) | `550 Mailbox not found` | > [!WARNING] KTU Examiner's Pitfall > Students frequently **forget**: > 1. The `.` terminator in `DATA`. > 2. To mention that SMTP is **stateless per message** (each `MAIL FROM` starts a new transaction). > 3. That **EHLO** (not `HELO`) must be used to enable ESMTP extensions like `STARTTLS`, `SIZE`, `8BITMIME`, and `AUTH`. > 4. The difference between **envelope** addresses (`MAIL FROM`/`RCPT TO`) and **header** addresses (`From:`/`To:` inside the body). --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024] **Q.B (a)** Describe the **MIME standard**. Explain its **five header fields** and the role of **Base64** and **Quoted-Printable** encodings. (7 marks) **Q.B (b)** A user attaches a **750 KB** image to an e-mail. (i) Calculate the size of the encoded payload if **Base64** is used. (ii) State one advantage and one disadvantage of Base64 compared to Quoted-Printable. (7 marks) --- #### Model Solution — Q.B (a) MIME Standard **MIME (Multipurpose Internet Mail Extensions)**, defined in **RFC 2045–2049**, extends the original 7-bit-ASCII-only RFC 5322 message format to support **binary, multimedia, and multi-language** content. RFC 822/5322 itself cannot carry a JPEG, PDF, or non-English text reliably — MIME fixes this. **The Five MIME Header Fields** 1. **`MIME-Version:`** — Declares the MIME version in use. The current value is always `1.0`. Example: `MIME-Version: 1.0`. 2. **`Content-Type:`** — A `type/subtype` pair describing the data. Examples: `text/html; charset="utf-8"`, `image/jpeg`, `application/pdf`, `multipart/mixed; boundary="..."`. 3. **`Content-Transfer-Encoding:`** — Tells the receiver how to decode the bytes back to original. Permitted values: `7bit`, `8bit`, `binary`, `quoted-printable`, `base64`. 4. **`Content-ID:`** — An optional unique identifier (URI-reference) used to reference the part from other parts (e.g., embedding a CID image inside HTML). 5. **`Content-Description:`** — Optional human-readable description of the part, e.g., `Content-Description: Quarterly report PDF`. **Base64 Encoding** Base64 maps every **3 input bytes (24 bits)** to **4 output characters (each 6 bits)**, drawn from a 64-character alphabet (`A–Z`, `a–z`, `0–9`, `+`, `/`) plus `=` for padding. It produces output that is safe for any 7-bit ASCII channel and is the **de-facto standard for binary attachments** in e-mail. **Quoted-Printable Encoding** QP represents mostly-ASCII data with occasional 8-bit characters. Printable ASCII passes through unchanged; non-printable bytes are encoded as `=XX` (hex). It is **readable in transit** (debuggers can read text) but is **inefficient for dense binary** (each non-ASCII byte costs 3 characters). #### Model Solution — Q.B (b) Calculation **Given:** Image size $n = 750 \text{ KB} = 750 \times 1024 \text{ bytes} = 768000 \text{ bytes}$. **(i) Base64 encoded size:** $$ \begin{aligned} E_{\text{base64}} &= 4 \cdot \left\lceil \frac{n}{3} \right\rceil \\ &= 4 \cdot \left\lceil \frac{768000}{3} \right\rceil \\ &= 4 \cdot \left\lceil 256000 \right\rceil \\ &= 4 \cdot 256000 \\ &= 1024000 \text{ characters} \\ &= \frac{1024000}{1024} \text{ KB} \\ &\approx 1000 \text{ KB} \quad (\text{exactly } 1000 \text{ KB since } 1024000/1024 = 1000) \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > The encoded payload is **1024000 characters = 1000 KB**, an expansion of $\tfrac{4}{3}$ over the original 750 KB. **(ii) Base64 vs Quoted-Printable** | Aspect | Base64 | Quoted-Printable | |--------|--------|-------------------| | **Advantage** | **Compact, predictable 4/3 expansion ratio for any binary data** — ideal for JPEG, PDF, ZIP attachments | **Human-readable in transit** — useful for debugging; minimal expansion for mostly-ASCII data | | **Disadvantage** | Output is **opaque** (unreadable to humans) and **inflates every byte** — wasteful for text content | **Worst-case expansion is 3×**; inefficient for dense binary; ASCII lines can exceed 76-char folding limit | | Best fit | Binary attachments (PDF, images, audio) | Multilingual text with diacritics (e.g., French, German signatures) | > [!WARNING] KTU Examiner's Pitfall > Common mistakes on calculation questions: > 1. **Mixing KB and KiB** — Use 1 KB = 1024 bytes for binary MIME encoding (powers of two), not 1 KB = 1000 bytes. > 2. **Forgetting the ceiling** — When $n$ is not a multiple of 3, the answer must be rounded **up** to the next block. > 3. **Forgetting padding characters** — A 1-byte remainder produces `XX==` (2 `=`); a 2-byte remainder produces `XXX=` (1 `=`). --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] Rapid-Revision Checklist — Electronic Mail - **Three pillars of e-mail:** **SMTP** (push), **POP3 / IMAP** (pull), **MIME** (encoding). - **SMTP ports:** **25** (relay), **587** (submission), **465** (legacy SMTPS). Modern systems prefer **STARTTLS** on 587 over implicit 465. - **POP3 port:** **110** (995 encrypted). **IMAP port:** **143** (993 encrypted). - **ESMTP** is initiated with `EHLO`; basic `HELO` is no longer recommended. - **Reply code classes:** `2yz` OK, `3yz` more input needed, `4yz` retry later, `5yz` permanent failure. - **DATA** terminator: a line containing only a single period `.`. - **MIME** requires the header `MIME-Version: 1.0` plus `Content-Type` and `Content-Transfer-Encoding`. - **Base64** expansion: $E = 4 \lceil n/3 \rceil$ characters — always $\tfrac{4}{3}$ ratio. - **Quoted-Printable** expansion: up to 3× in the worst case; pass-through for printable ASCII. - **MX record** preference: **lower priority value wins**; ties broken randomly. - **POP3 default behaviour = download-and-delete**; **IMAP default = server-side persistence**. - **Envelope** (`MAIL FROM`/`RCPT TO`) is **protocol-level**; **Headers** (`From:`/`To:`) are **message-level** and can differ. - **Store-and-forward** is what makes e-mail **asynchronous** — the message may queue at any hop. - **Common real-world MTAs:** Postfix, Sendmail, Exim, Microsoft Exchange. - **Common real-world MDAs/MRAs:** Dovecot (often both LDA and IMAP/POP3), Cyrus IMAP, procmail. - **MIME multipart types to remember:** `multipart/mixed` (attachments), `multipart/alternative` (text+HTML), `multipart/related` (inline images), `multipart/form-data` (web uploads). - **E-mail forensics:** the `Received:` header chain traces the path of the message from origin to destination. - **Security add-ons:** SPF, DKIM, DMARC, S/MIME, PGP, STARTTLS, DANE — all extensions layered on top of SMTP. ---

topics.title: DNS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAMT632 - BASICS OF COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** DNS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Domain Name System (DNS)** is a hierarchical, distributed, and globally coordinated naming database defined under **RFC 1034** and **RFC 1035** (with extensions in RFC 2782, RFC 3596, and RFC 4033 for DNSSEC). It serves as the foundational translator of the internet by mapping **human-readable domain names** (e.g., `www.ktu.edu.in`) into **machine-readable IP addresses** (e.g., `103.25.60.XX`), and vice versa via reverse-lookup PTR records.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - PEAMT632 Module 1):**
> DNS belongs to the **Application Layer** of both the OSI and TCP/IP reference models. It is the *first* service a host consults before any meaningful communication (web, mail, FTP) can be initiated. KTU examiners frequently test DNS as part of *"Network Services and the Internetworking Architecture"* foundational questions.

DNS operates primarily over **UDP Port 53** for standard queries and responses, but transparently falls back to **TCP Port 53** whenever the response payload exceeds **512 bytes** (e.g., DNSSEC, IPv6 AAAA records, or zone transfers via **AXFR/IXFR**).

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Internet's Phone Book"

Imagine you want to call your friend *"Rohit"*, but your phone can only dial raw 10-digit numbers. Each time, you would have to remember Rohit's number, his area code, and his mobile operator's routing prefix. Multiply this by every contact, every website, every service — it becomes impossible.

**DNS is the smart contact-list application living inside every internet-connected device.**

- You type **`google.com`** (the friendly name) into your browser.
- Your device silently consults DNS (the contact list) and obtains the IP address (the phone number).
- The actual connection then proceeds using the IP address, completely transparent to you.

Without DNS, the internet would still function technically, but it would collapse from a usability standpoint — humans cannot memorize the **4.3+ billion** active IPv4 addresses, let alone the **340 undecillion** IPv6 addresses.

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value / Range | Significance |
|-----------|------------------------|--------------|
| **Default Port** | **53** (UDP/TCP) | IANA-assigned well-known port |
| **UDP Payload Limit** | **512 bytes** | Threshold to switch to TCP |
| **Label Length** | **1 to 63 characters** | Per-label in a domain name |
| **Full Domain Length** | **≤ 253 characters** | Including trailing dot (root) |
| **Root Server Clusters** | **13 logical (A to M)** | Physically replicated at **1,500+** anycast sites |
| **TTL (Time-To-Live)** | **300 s to 86,400 s** | Cache lifetime of a DNS record |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Concept — Root of the Namespace:**
> The fully qualified domain name (FQDN) ends with a trailing dot `.` representing the *root zone*. The root zone is served by **13 logical root server clusters** operated by 12 independent organizations (VeriSign, ICANN, ISC, Cogent, NASA, etc.). These are **NOT** 13 physical machines; they are *anycast* clusters deployed globally for resilience.
