# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three-Tier Hierarchical Architecture

DNS is **not** a centralized database. It is a **distributed, delegated, and hierarchical** namespace designed so that no single entity controls more than its own zone. The hierarchy is composed of four logical tiers:

### Tier 1 — Root Zone (` `.`)
- Managed by **ICANN** (Internet Corporation for Assigned Names and Numbers) via the Internet Assigned Numbers Authority (IANA).
- Delegates authority for Top-Level Domains (TLDs) such as `.com`, `.org`, `.in`, `.uk`, `.jp`.
- Currently **13 logical** server identities, each mirrored across hundreds of physical anycast instances.

### Tier 2 — Top-Level Domain (TLD) Servers
- **gTLD (Generic TLDs):** `.com`, `.net`, `.org`, `.edu`, `.gov`, `.mil`, `.info`, and the newer `.app`, `.dev`, `.io`.
- **ccTLD (Country-Code TLDs):** `.in` (India), `.uk` (United Kingdom), `.de` (Germany), `.jp` (Japan) — delegated to each nation's NIC.
- TLD servers hold information about which **Authoritative Name Server** is responsible for each second-level domain.

### Tier 3 — Authoritative Name Servers
- These are the **final source of truth** for a given zone (e.g., `example.com`).
- Two types: **Primary (Master)** and **Secondary (Slave)**.
- The master is where zone files are edited; slaves pull zone data via **zone transfer (AXFR/IXFR)** over **TCP 53**.

### Tier 4 — Subdomain & Host Layer
- Owned and managed by the domain registrant.
- Records here include `www`, `mail`, `ftp`, `blog`, `api`, etc.
- This is where **A**, **AAAA**, **CNAME**, **MX**, and **TXT** records live.

## 2.2 The Two Server Personalities

| Server Type | Role | Mode of Operation |
|-------------|------|-------------------|
| **Recursive Resolver** (also called *Local DNS*, *Caching Resolver*, *Stub Resolver*) | Performs the resolution on behalf of the client. Issues queries iteratively (or recursively to other resolvers) until the answer is found. | ISP-provided, public (8.8.8.8, 1.1.1.1, 9.9.9.9), or enterprise-managed. |
| **Authoritative Name Server** | Holds the *original* zone data and answers queries **without** further recursion. The final source of truth. | Operated by domain registrars, hosting providers, or enterprises. |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** A common trick question is *"Can a single physical server be both recursive and authoritative?"* — **YES**. For performance, many production servers run both roles. However, **logically**, the two functions are independent and should be tested separately in viva questions.

## 2.3 DNS Resource Records (RRs) — The Building Blocks

Every DNS response is a collection of **Resource Records**. The master format (RFC 1035) is:

$$\text{RR} = \{\text{Name}, \text{Type}, \text{Class}, \text{TTL}, \text{RDLength}, \text{RData}\}$$

The most exam-relevant record types are:

| Type | Code | Purpose | Example RDATA |
|------|------|---------|---------------|
| **A** | 1 | IPv4 address mapping | `93.184.216.34` |
| **AAAA** | 28 | IPv6 address mapping | `2606:2800:220:1:248:1893:25c8:1946` |
| **CNAME** | 5 | Canonical Name alias | `example.com` |
| **MX** | 15 | Mail Exchange server | `10 mail.example.com` |
| **NS** | 2 | Authoritative name server | `ns1.example.com` |
| **PTR** | 12 | Reverse-lookup mapping | `example.com` |
| **SOA** | 6 | Start of Authority (zone metadata) | `ns1.example.com hostmaster.example.com 2024010101 3600 1800 1209600 86400` |
| **TXT** | 16 | Arbitrary text (SPF, DKIM, domain verification) | `"v=spf1 include:_spf.google.com ~all"` |
| **SRV** | 33 | Service locator (VoIP, XMPP) | `0 5 5060 sip.example.com` |

## 2.4 Resolution Modes — Recursive vs. Iterative

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **single most-asked DNS concept** in KTU Module 1 university exams. Memorize the distinction.

### Recursive Query
- The client says: *"I want the answer. Don't bother me with referrals."*
- Each server contacted is **obligated** to either return the final answer or return an error.
- Used between **client → local recursive resolver** and (in classic BIND deployments) **resolver → root server**.

### Iterative Query
- The client (resolver) is told: *"I don't know, but here is the address of someone who might."*
- The server returns a **referral (NS record)** instead of the final answer.
- The resolver then queries the referred server directly.
- Used in most modern root → TLD → authoritative chains.

## 2.5 Caching — The Performance Engine

Every DNS record carries a **TTL (Time-To-Live)** field. When a resolver caches a record, it must discard it after the TTL expires. Caching occurs at:

1. **Browser-level cache** (Chrome, Firefox).
2. **Operating-system stub resolver cache** (`ipconfig /displaydns` in Windows, `systemd-resolved` in Linux).
3. **Recursive resolver cache** (ISP, public DNS).

> **Why Caching Matters (Engineering Insight):**
> DNS caching reduces the load on authoritative servers by an estimated **80–95%** in typical enterprise environments. Without caching, every webpage load would generate 5–10 fresh DNS lookups, drowning the global DNS infrastructure.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following table summarizes all quantitative relationships the KTU examiner expects you to handle for DNS problems.

| \# | Formula | Meaning | Typical Units / Range |
|---|---------|---------|------------------------|
| 1 | $T_{total} = T_{root} + T_{tld} + T_{auth}$ | Worst-case RTT for an iterative 3-hop query | **20 – 400 ms** |
| 2 | $T_{eff} = P_{hit} \cdot T_{cache} + (1 - P_{hit}) \cdot T_{total}$ | Effective average query latency with cache | ms |
| 3 | $\eta_{cache} = 1 - \dfrac{T_{eff}}{T_{total}}$ | Cache-induced latency reduction ratio | $0 \le \eta_{cache} \le 1$ |
| 4 | $Q_{saved} = N_{total} \cdot P_{hit}$ | Number of upstream queries avoided per window | Queries / sec |
| 5 | $TTL_{remaining} = TTL_{original} \cdot \dfrac{t_{elapsed}}{T_{period}}$ | Time left in cache after $t_{elapsed}$ seconds | seconds |
| 6 | $\lambda_{query} = \dfrac{N_{queries}}{\Delta t}$ | Query arrival rate (Poisson assumption) | QPS |
| 7 | $N_{max} = \dfrac{L_{domain}}{L_{label\,min}} + 1$ | Maximum labels in a domain of length $L_{domain}$ | Integer $\ge 1$ |
| 8 | $R_{payload} = \min(\text{UDP MTU}, 512) \rightarrow \text{TCP fallback if exceeded}$ | Transport selection rule | bytes |
| 9 | $\text{AS\,rank} = f(\text{hop count, geographic distance})$ | Influences authoritative server reachability (qualitative) | — |
| 10 | $E[T_{iterative}] = \sum_{i=1}^{k} T_i$ | Expected latency over $k$ iterative hops | ms |

> **CRITICAL TYPESETTING NOTE (to prevent KTU answer-script scanning errors):**
> In all the formulas above, the vertical bar symbol $\vert$ is used for *absolute value* / *such that* instead of the unescaped pipe character, ensuring clean rendering in markdown and LaTeX.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cybersecurity:** DNS is the primary attack surface for *DNS spoofing / cache poisoning* (Kaminsky attack, 2008), *DNS amplification DDoS*, and *DNS tunneling* for data exfiltration. **DNSSEC** (RFC 4033) cryptographically signs records to mitigate this.
- **Content Delivery:** CDNs (Akamai, Cloudflare, AWS CloudFront) use **DNS-based geo-routing** — returning different A records based on the client's location to minimize latency.
- **Email Deliverability:** **MX** and **TXT (SPF/DKIM/DMARC)** records directly govern whether your emails land in the inbox or the spam folder.
- **Zero-Trust Architecture:** Modern EDR/XDR platforms monitor DNS query patterns to detect command-and-control (C2) beaconing.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section develops the quantitative models a KTU 2024 student is expected to derive on paper. **No step is omitted.**

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Worst-Case DNS Resolution Latency (Fully Iterative, No Cache)

**Assumption:** A user host `H` wishes to resolve `www.example.com`. The local recursive resolver `LR` has *no* cached entry, so it must perform a full **3-hop iterative query**: Root $\rightarrow$ TLD $\rightarrow$ Authoritative.

Let us define the round-trip times (RTTs) as:

- $T_{H \to LR}$ = RTT from host `H` to local resolver
- $T_{LR \to Root}$ = RTT from local resolver to a root server
- $T_{LR \to TLD}$ = RTT from local resolver to the `.com` TLD server
- $T_{LR \to Auth}$ = RTT from local resolver to the authoritative name server

**Step 1 — Latency from host to local resolver:**

$$T_1 = T_{H \to LR}$$

**Step 2 — Query 1: Local resolver asks the root server (`.`):**

The root server returns a referral containing the NS records for the `.com` TLD. The latency for this single RTT is $T_{LR \to Root}$. So:

$$T_2 = T_{LR \to Root}$$

**Step 3 — Query 2: Local resolver asks the `.com` TLD server:**

The TLD server returns the NS records for `example.com`. Latency:

$$T_3 = T_{LR \to TLD}$$

**Step 4 — Query 3: Local resolver asks the authoritative name server for `example.com`:**

The authoritative server returns the A record for `www.example.com` (say, `93.184.216.34`). Latency:

$$T_4 = T_{LR \to Auth}$$

**Step 5 — Local resolver returns the final answer to host `H`:**

The reply travels back. This is already accounted for in the RTTs of Steps 1–4.

**Step 6 — Total worst-case latency:**

$$T_{total} = T_1 + T_2 + T_3 + T_4 = T_{H \to LR} + T_{LR \to Root} + T_{LR \to TLD} + T_{LR \to Auth}$$

If we assume the resolver is *on the same LAN* as the host ($T_{H \to LR} \approx 0.5$ ms), and average internet RTTs:

$$T_{total} \approx 0.5 + 30 + 40 + 50 = 120.5 \text{ ms (typical)}$$

**Step 7 — Worst-case interpretation:**

The 3-hop iterative model gives an *upper bound*. A *best-case* scenario (warm cache hit) reduces this to:

$$T_{best} \approx T_{H \to LR} \approx 0.5 \text{ ms}$$

A speedup factor of:

$$S = \frac{T_{total}}{T_{best}} = \frac{120.5}{0.5} = 241\times$$

This is why **caching is non-negotiable** in production DNS.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Effective Query Latency Under Probabilistic Caching

Let $P_{hit}$ be the probability that a queried name is already present in the local resolver's cache (assumed independent of query). Let $T_{cache}$ be the time required to look up the cache (a sub-millisecond memory operation).

**Step 1 — Express total expected time as a weighted sum:**

If the query is a hit (probability $P_{hit}$), latency is $T_{cache}$. If it is a miss (probability $1 - P_{hit}$), latency is $T_{total}$ from Derivation 1.

$$T_{eff} = P_{hit} \cdot T_{cache} + (1 - P_{hit}) \cdot T_{total}$$

**Step 2 — Expand and simplify:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{eff} &= P_{hit} \cdot T_{cache} + T_{total} - P_{hit} \cdot T_{total} \\
&= T_{total} - P_{hit} \cdot (T_{total} - T_{cache})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Define the cache efficiency ratio $\eta_{cache}$:**

$$
\eta_{cache} = \frac{T_{total} - T_{eff}}{T_{total}} = P_{hit} \cdot \frac{T_{total} - T_{cache}}{T_{total}}
$$

Since $T_{cache} \ll T_{total}$, we can approximate:

$$\eta_{cache} \approx P_{hit}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical example (KTU-style calculation):**

Suppose $P_{hit} = 0.85$ (a typical enterprise value), $T_{cache} = 0.2$ ms, $T_{total} = 120$ ms.

$$
T_{eff} = 0.85 \cdot 0.2 + 0.15 \cdot 120 = 0.17 + 18.0 = 18.17 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
\eta_{cache} = 1 - \frac{18.17}{120} = 0.8486 \approx 84.86\%
$$

**Interpretation:** Caching reduces DNS latency by **~85%** and saves **85% of upstream bandwidth**.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Aggregate Upstream Query Load

In a network with $N$ clients issuing on average $q$ queries per second each, the upstream DNS traffic to authoritative servers per second is:

$$
Q_{upstream} = N \cdot q \cdot (1 - P_{hit})
$$

For $N = 1000$ clients, $q = 0.1$ queries/sec, $P_{hit} = 0.85$:

$$Q_{upstream} = 1000 \cdot 0.1 \cdot 0.15 = 15 \text{ queries/sec}$$

Without caching:

$$Q_{no\,cache} = 1000 \cdot 0.1 \cdot 1.0 = 100 \text{ queries/sec}$$

**Bandwidth saved:** $\Delta Q = 100 - 15 = 85$ queries/sec.

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Recursive vs. Iterative

| Scenario | Recommended Mode | Reason |
|----------|------------------|--------|
| Stub client $\rightarrow$ ISP resolver | **Recursive** | Client is too dumb/constrained to chase referrals |
| ISP resolver $\rightarrow$ Root server | **Iterative** (modern) | Reduces root server load, distributes work to resolver |
| Internal corporate resolver $\rightarrow$ internal authoritative | **Recursive** (often) | Trusted zone, low latency LAN |
| Anycast root $\rightarrow$ global clients | **Iterative** | Scalability, root servers must remain stateless and fast |

## 3.5 Special Case — Reverse DNS (PTR) Lookup

Reverse lookups use the pseudo-domain `in-addr.arpa` (IPv4) or `ip6.arpa` (IPv6). For an IP `A.B.C.D`, the PTR query is sent to:

$$\text{DNS Query: } \text{PTR } D.C.B.A.\text{in-addr.arpa}$$

This hierarchical inversion allows delegation of reverse zones on octet boundaries, e.g., `192.in-addr.arpa` delegated to the owner of `192.0.0.0/8`.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Hierarchical Namespace Architecture (Tree Structure)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1["ROOT ZONE ICANN MANAGED"] --> B1["GENERIC TLDS COM NET ORG EDU"]
    A1 --> B2["COUNTRY TLDS IN UK JP DE"]
    A1 --> B3["INFRASTRUCTURE ARPA"]
    B1 --> C1["AUTHORITATIVE NS EXAMPLE COM"]
    B1 --> C2["AUTHORITATIVE NS GOOGLE COM"]
    B2 --> C3["AUTHORITATIVE NS KTU EDU IN"]
    C1 --> D1["HOSTS WWW MAIL FTP API"]
    C2 --> D2["HOSTS WWW MAIL DRIVE"]
    C3 --> D3["HOSTS WWW DEPT LIB"]
    D1 --> E1["RESOURCE RECORDS A AAAA MX CNAME TXT"]
    D2 --> E2["RESOURCE RECORDS A AAAA MX CNAME TXT"]
    D3 --> E3["RESOURCE RECORDS A AAAA MX CNAME TXT"]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Each downward edge represents a *delegation of authority*. The root does not know every IP; it only knows who manages `.com`. The `.com` servers do not know `www.example.com`'s IP; they only know who manages `example.com`. And so on. This is the core engineering elegance of DNS — **no single point of knowledge**.

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Iterative + Recursive Resolution Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as USER HOST H
    participant L as LOCAL RECURSIVE RESOLVER LR
    participant R as ROOT SERVER
    participant T as TLD SERVER COM
    participant N as AUTHORITATIVE NS EXAMPLE COM
    U->>L: RECURSIVE QUERY WWW EXAMPLE COM
    L->>R: ITERATIVE QUERY
    R-->>L: REFERRAL TO COM TLD NS RECORDS
    L->>T: ITERATIVE QUERY
    T-->>L: REFERRAL TO EXAMPLE COM NS RECORDS
    L->>N: ITERATIVE QUERY
    N-->>L: FINAL ANSWER A RECORD IP 93 184 216 34
    L-->>U: CACHED RESPONSE WITH TTL 3600 S
    Note over L: CACHE ENTRY ADDED TTL 3600
```

> **Reading the sequence:**
> - Step 1 is **recursive** (H asks LR, LR must return the answer).
> - Steps 2–4 are **iterative** (LR asks, gets referrals, and re-queries).
> - Step 5 returns the final IP to the user, and the resolver caches it for **3600 seconds** (the TTL).

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Caching Resolver

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1["CLIENT APPLICATION BROWSER OS"] --> B1["STUB RESOLVER LIBRESOLV GLIBC"]
    B1 --> C1["RECURSIVE RESOLVER UNBOUND BIND POWERDNS"]
    C1 --> D1["MEMORY CACHE LRU HASH TABLE"]
    C1 --> E1["FORWARDING POLICY ALGORITHM"]
    E1 --> F1["UPSTREAM PROVIDER DNS"]
    E1 --> F2["PUBLIC DNS GOOGLE CLOUDFLARE QUAD9"]
    E1 --> F3["ROOT SERVERS A TO M ANYCAST"]
    F3 --> G1["TLD SERVERS COM NET ORG IN"]
    G1 --> H1["AUTHORITATIVE NAME SERVERS"]
    D1 -.CACHE HIT.-> B1
    H1 -.FINAL ANSWER.-> D1
```

> **Block-level interpretation:** A caching resolver is essentially a **stateful proxy** in front of a stateless, distributed database. The cache lookup is a hash-table operation; the upstream resolution follows a strict fallback chain: cache $\rightarrow$ forwarder $\rightarrow$ root $\rightarrow$ TLD $\rightarrow$ authoritative.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain the Domain Name System (DNS). Why is it considered a distributed and hierarchical system?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

DNS is the internet's naming service that translates human-readable domain names (e.g., `www.ktu.edu.in`) into machine-readable IP addresses (e.g., `103.x.x.x`) and vice versa. It operates at the **Application Layer** using **port 53** over **UDP/TCP**.

It is **distributed** because no single server holds the entire namespace; the database is partitioned into zones and spread across millions of authoritative name servers worldwide. It is **hierarchical** because the namespace follows a tree structure starting from the **root zone (`.`)**, descending through **TLDs** (`.com`, `.in`), **second-level domains** (`example.com`), and **subdomains** (`www.example.com`). Authority is *delegated* downward, so each level only needs to know about its immediate children. This design provides **scalability, fault tolerance, and decentralization**, which are the three pillars of the internet's success.

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List and explain any three DNS Resource Record types with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **A Record (Address Record — Type 1):** Maps a hostname to an **IPv4 address** (32-bit). Example: `www.example.com IN A 93.184.216.34`.

2. **MX Record (Mail Exchange — Type 15):** Specifies the **mail server** responsible for accepting email for a domain, along with a priority value. Example: `example.com IN MX 10 mail.example.com` (lower priority = preferred).

3. **CNAME Record (Canonical Name — Type 5):** Creates an **alias** from one domain name to another (the *canonical* name). It does not point to an IP directly. Example: `www.example.com IN CNAME example.com`.

*(Other acceptable answers: NS, AAAA, PTR, SOA, TXT, SRV — but only three are required for full marks.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### 📘 Question A — Choice 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the hierarchical structure of the Domain Name System with a neat diagram. Discuss the role of root, TLD, and authoritative name servers.

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

The DNS namespace is a **strictly hierarchical inverted tree** rooted at the unnamed root zone denoted by `.`.

**Layer 1 — Root Zone:**
At the top sit the **13 logical root server clusters** (named A through M, e.g., `a.root-servers.net`, `b.root-servers.net`). Operated independently by 12 organizations under ICANN oversight, they do not store individual domain records. Their only job is to **delegate** queries to the appropriate TLD servers. As of 2024, these 13 logical identities are served by **1,500+ physical anycast instances** distributed across the globe, providing massive resilience against DDoS attacks.

**Layer 2 — TLD Servers:**
Below the root are the Top-Level Domain servers, categorized as:
- **gTLDs:** `.com`, `.org`, `.net`, `.edu`, `.gov` (operated by VeriSign, PIR, etc.)
- **ccTLDs:** `.in` (India — NIXI), `.uk` (UK — Nominet), `.de` (Germany — DENIC)
- **Infrastructure TLD:** `.arpa` (used for reverse DNS)

These servers respond with the NS records of the **authoritative name server** for the second-level domain.

**Layer 3 — Authoritative Name Servers:**
Owned and operated by the domain registrant (or their hosting provider). Two types: **Primary (master)** where zone data is edited, and **Secondary (slave)** which pulls data via **AXFR (full) / IXFR (incremental) zone transfers** over **TCP port 53**.

**Layer 4 — Subdomain / Host Records:**
The leaf level contains host records like `www`, `mail`, `ftp`, which hold the actual A, AAAA, MX, CNAME records.

> **Diagram (to be drawn in answer script):**
> ```
>                            . (Root)
>                           / |  \
>                         .com .in .org ...
>                          |
>                     example.com
>                       /  |   \
>                    www mail ftp
>                     |   |    |
>                   [A] [MX] [A]
> ```

**Engineering Significance:** This hierarchy is the reason the internet is **resilient**. Deleting one authoritative server, one TLD operator, or even losing an entire continent's connectivity does not destroy the global DNS — authority is decentralized at every level.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare and contrast **Recursive** and **Iterative** DNS query resolution. Illustrate with a suitable example.

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

| Parameter | Recursive Query | Iterative Query |
|-----------|------------------|------------------|
| **Definition** | Server is *mandated* to return the final answer or an error. | Server returns the *best referral it has*; the client must follow it. |
| **Server load** | High (one server does all the work) | Low (work is distributed) |
| **Client load** | Very low (client waits) | Higher (client may issue many queries) |
| **Used between** | Stub resolver $\rightarrow$ ISP resolver | ISP resolver $\rightarrow$ root / TLD / authoritative |
| **Caching friendliness** | Less granular (full answer only) | Granular (each referral can be cached) |
| **Modern usage** | Stub-to-resolver hop | All upstream hops |

**Worked Example — Resolving `www.example.com` (Assume empty cache):**

**Step 1 (Recursive):** Host `H` sends a single recursive query to its local resolver `LR`: *"Give me the IP of www.example.com."*

**Step 2 (Iterative):** `LR` queries the root server `R`. `R` does not know the IP, so it returns a *referral*: *"I don't know, but ask the .com TLD server at a.gtld-servers.net."* — **referral response, NOT a final answer**.

**Step 3 (Iterative):** `LR` queries `a.gtld-servers.net` (the .com TLD). It returns: *"I don't know either, but ask ns1.example.com (the authoritative server)."*

**Step 4 (Iterative):** `LR` queries `ns1.example.com`. This server **knows the answer authoritatively** and returns: `www.example.com IN A 93.184.216.34` with TTL = 3600.

**Step 5 (Recursive return):** `LR` returns the final answer back to `H` and **caches it for 3600 seconds**.

**Conclusion:** Modern DNS uses a *hybrid* model — recursive at the client edge, iterative at the core. This balances load between clients and the global infrastructure.

---

### 📘 Question B — Choice 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a step-by-step example, explain the complete DNS resolution process. Discuss the role of caching and TTL in this process.

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Scenario:** User opens browser and types `https://www.ktu.edu.in`. The OS issues a DNS query to the configured resolver (e.g., `8.8.8.8`).

**Step 1 — Stub Resolver Activation:**
The browser's DNS client (a stub resolver in glibc / `getaddrinfo`) checks its **in-process cache**. If a valid (non-expired) entry exists, it is returned immediately. **Cache hit = sub-millisecond response.**

**Step 2 — OS-Level Cache Check:**
If the in-process cache misses, the query is passed to the **operating system resolver** (e.g., `systemd-resolved` on Linux, the DNS Client service on Windows). Run `ipconfig /displaydns` to view this cache. If present and not expired, return.

**Step 3 — Query to External Recursive Resolver:**
If both local caches miss, the query is sent (over **UDP 53**) to the configured recursive resolver (`8.8.8.8`, `1.1.1.1`, or ISP DNS).

**Step 4 — Recursive Resolver Cache Check:**
The recursive resolver has a much larger cache. If `www.ktu.edu.in` is present and within its TTL, the answer is returned immediately and the OS cache is updated.

**Step 5 — Iterative Resolution (cache miss at all levels):**
The recursive resolver must now perform the iterative chain:
- Query root server `.` $\rightarrow$ get referral to `.in` TLD.
- Query `.in` TLD (NIXI) $\rightarrow$ get referral to authoritative NS for `ktu.edu.in`.
- Query authoritative NS for `ktu.edu.in` $\rightarrow$ get final A record (e.g., `103.x.x.x`).

**Step 6 — Return and Cache:**
The recursive resolver returns the answer to the client **and caches it for the duration specified by the TTL field** (e.g., 3600 s = 1 hour). It also informs the OS cache and the application cache.

**Step 7 — TTL Expiry:**
After 3600 seconds, the cache entry becomes *stale* and is discarded. The next query re-triggers the full resolution chain.

**Role of TTL:** TTL balances **freshness** (low TTL = quick propagation of changes) against **performance** (high TTL = less load on authoritative servers). A common compromise is **300–3600 seconds**.

**Role of Caching:** Caching is the single most important performance optimization in DNS. It reduces global DNS traffic by an estimated **80–95%** and makes the system economically and technically viable at internet scale.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the following DNS resource records with syntax and purpose: **A, AAAA, MX, CNAME, NS, SOA, PTR**. Why is the **SOA record** considered the most important administrative record of a zone?

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

| Record | Syntax Example | Purpose |
|--------|----------------|---------|
| **A** | `host.example.com IN A 192.0.2.1` | Maps hostname to **IPv4** address (32-bit). Most common record. |
| **AAAA** | `host.example.com IN AAAA 2001:db8::1` | Maps hostname to **IPv6** address (128-bit). The "quad-A" record. |
| **MX** | `example.com IN MX 10 mail.example.com` | Identifies the **mail server** for the domain. Priority value: lower = higher preference. |
| **CNAME** | `www.example.com IN CNAME example.com` | Creates an **alias**. The target must be a canonical name (with its own A/AAAA record). Cannot coexist with other records at the same name. |
| **NS** | `example.com IN NS ns1.example.com` | Declares the **authoritative name servers** for a zone. Required at every delegation point. |
| **SOA** | `example.com IN SOA ns1.example.com admin.example.com 2024010101 3600 1800 1209600 86400` | Declares the **Start of Authority** — zone metadata. Contains: primary NS, responsible person email, serial number, refresh, retry, expire, and minimum TTL. |
| **PTR** | `1.2.0.192.in-addr.arpa IN PTR host.example.com` | **Reverse** mapping from IP to hostname. Used by email servers, logs, and security tools. |

**Why SOA is the Administrative Heart of a Zone:**

The **SOA (Start of Authority)** record is the *first* record in any zone file and contains the seven critical parameters that govern zone behavior:

1. **Primary NS** — the master name server for the zone.
2. **Admin Email** — contact address of the responsible administrator (the `@` is written as a `.`, e.g., `admin.example.com`).
3. **Serial Number** — a monotonically increasing version number (commonly formatted as `YYYYMMDDNN`). Secondary servers compare this to decide whether a zone transfer (AXFR/IXFR) is required. **Any change to the zone file MUST increment this number.**
4. **Refresh** — time (in seconds) a secondary waits before checking the primary for updates. Default: 3600 s.
5. **Retry** — time to wait if a refresh fails. Default: 600–1800 s.
6. **Expire** — time after which a secondary stops answering authoritatively if it cannot reach the primary. Default: 1–4 weeks.
7. **Minimum TTL (Negative TTL)** — how long resolvers cache NXDOMAIN (non-existent domain) responses. Default: 3600 s.

Without a valid SOA record, **secondary servers cannot synchronize, caching is broken, and the zone is considered misconfigured**. It is, in effect, the *constitution* of the DNS zone.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks on DNS Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing recursive with iterative** — the most common error. *Remember:* Recursive = "get me the answer"; Iterative = "tell me who might know."
> 2. **Forgetting the trailing dot** in FQDNs — the root `.` is implicit but examiners expect you to mention it in diagrams.
> 3. **Writing UDP-only** — DNS **can** use TCP 53 for large responses and zone transfers. Saying "DNS uses UDP" without the TCP fallback costs marks.
> 4. **Mixing up port numbers** — DNS is **port 53**, not 80 (HTTP) or 25 (SMTP).
> 5. **Drawing the SOA incorrectly** — the email field uses a dot instead of `@` (e.g., `hostmaster.example.com` not `hostmaster@example.com`).
> 6. **Ignoring caching in latency problems** — if the question mentions a cache, your formula MUST include the $P_{hit}$ term. A bare $T_{total}$ answer is treated as incomplete.
> 7. **Writing "DNS = phonebook" without explaining the hierarchy** — the *analogy* gets you 1 mark; the *technical explanation* of root/TLD/auth hierarchy is where the remaining 2–3 marks are.
> 8. **Forgetting to state TTL units** — TTL is in **seconds**, not minutes. An answer of "3600" without "seconds" loses a half-mark in strict valuation.

---

## 5.4 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

Use this as your **last-30-minutes revision checklist** before the KTU ESE.

- ✅ DNS stands for **Domain Name System** — Application Layer service, **port 53** (UDP/TCP).
- ✅ Primary function: **Name-to-IP resolution** (forward) and **IP-to-Name** (reverse via PTR).
- ✅ Hierarchical and distributed: **Root (`.`) $\rightarrow$ TLD (`.com`, `.in`) $\rightarrow$ Authoritative NS $\rightarrow$ Host records**.
- ✅ **13 logical root server clusters** (A–M), physically replicated at **1,500+** anycast sites.
- ✅ **UDP for queries ≤ 512 bytes**; **TCP for zone transfers and large responses**.
- ✅ Maximum domain length = **253 characters**; maximum label length = **63 characters**.
- ✅ **Recursive query** = server is forced to return the final answer (used client-to-resolver).
- ✅ **Iterative query** = server returns referrals (used resolver-to-upstream).
- ✅ **Caching** occurs at browser, OS, and resolver levels, governed by the **TTL** field.
- ✅ Key resource records: **A** (IPv4), **AAAA** (IPv6), **CNAME** (alias), **MX** (mail), **NS** (name server), **PTR** (reverse), **SOA** (zone metadata), **TXT** (SPF/DKIM).
- ✅ **SOA record** has 7 fields: Primary NS, Admin Email, Serial, Refresh, Retry, Expire, Minimum TTL.
- ✅ Modern DNS uses a **hybrid model**: recursive at the edge, iterative in the core.
- ✅ **DNSSEC** adds cryptographic signatures to prevent cache poisoning (Kaminsky attack, 2008).
- ✅ **Effective latency** with caching: $T_{eff} = P_{hit} T_{cache} + (1 - P_{hit}) T_{total}$.
- ✅ **Cache efficiency** $\eta_{cache} \approx P_{hit}$ when $T_{cache} \ll T_{total}$.
- ✅ **Reverse DNS** uses the pseudo-domains `in-addr.arpa` (IPv4) and `ip6.arpa` (IPv6).
- ✅ **Anycast routing** is what makes 13 root identities serve the entire planet with sub-100 ms latency.
- ✅ **CDNs use DNS for geo-routing**, returning different A records based on client location.
- ✅ **Email deliverability** depends on MX + TXT (SPF, DKIM, DMARC) records — a real-world application often asked in viva.

> **One-line memory trick for the exam:**
> *"DNS is a **D**istributed, **H**ierarchical, **C**ached, **S**igned (with DNSSEC) namespace served on port **53** over **UDP/TCP**, queried **recursively** at the edge and **iteratively** at the core."*

---

*End of Module 1 — DNS Notes. Aligns with KTU 2024 Scheme CO1, Bloom Levels L1–L4 (Remember, Understand, Apply, Analyze).*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Peer-to-Peer Paradigm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAMT632 - BASICS OF COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Peer-to-Peer Paradigm

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Peer-to-Peer (P2P) Paradigm** is a distributed network architecture model in which two or more autonomous computing entities (*peers*) communicate and share resources (CPU cycles, storage, bandwidth, content) directly with each other **without the requirement of a centralized server, coordinator, or hierarchical client-server distinction**. Each peer in the network simultaneously acts as both a **client** (consumer of services/resources) and a **server** (provider of services/resources), and the workload is symmetrically distributed across all participating nodes.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, the Peer-to-Peer Paradigm is taught as a fundamental architectural alternative to the traditional **Client–Server Model**, forming the conceptual foundation for modern overlay networks, file-sharing systems, blockchain, VoIP, and edge/fog computing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Board-Examiner Language):** "A Peer-to-Peer network is a distributed application architecture that partitions tasks or workloads between equally privileged participants (peers), each of which is referred to as a peer-node, and they form a peer-to-peer network."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **neighborhood study group** for an exam:

- **Client–Server Model** is like one student (the *teacher*) standing at the front of a classroom with notes, and every other student must come to the teacher and ask for the notes. If the teacher is absent, *no one* gets the notes. The teacher is a **single point of failure** and a **bottleneck**.

- **Peer-to-Peer Model** is like a study group where every student *both gives* their own notes to others *and asks* for notes from others. If one student leaves, the group still functions. The more students join, the stronger the group becomes. There is **no central authority** — knowledge flows horizontally.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition for Exams:** In a P2P network, "the network IS the computer." Each node contributes and consumes. As `N` (number of peers) grows, the **total capacity of the system grows linearly**, unlike client-server where the server has a fixed capacity.

## 1.3 Key Terminology

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Peer** | An equal-participant node that is both a client and a server. |
| **Overlay Network** | A virtual logical network built *on top of* an existing physical network, used by P2P systems to route queries. |
| **Napster-style (Centralized Directory)** | A hybrid model: peers transfer data directly, but a central index server is used for lookup. |
| **Pure P2P (Decentralized)** | No central server of any kind; lookup and data transfer are fully distributed (e.g., Gnutella). |
| **Structured P2P** | Uses a **Distributed Hash Table (DHT)** for O(log N) lookups (e.g., Chord, Pastry). |
| **Unstructured P2P** | No global topology; peers connect randomly; uses flooding (e.g., Gnutella, KaZaA). |
| **Super-Peer** | A high-capacity node that acts as a regional index/hub for ordinary peers (Hybrid P2P). |

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners *love* testing the difference between **Pure P2P**, **Hybrid P2P**, and **Centralized P2P**. Memorize this taxonomy — it is a guaranteed 3-mark question every cycle.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Working — How P2P Actually Functions

A Peer-to-Peer system operates through a sequence of well-defined logical steps. The following breakdown is the standard KTU answer structure for a 7-mark question.

### Step 1: Peer Discovery & Bootstrap
When a new peer joins the network, it must find at least one existing peer to connect to. This is called **bootstrapping**. Methods include:
- Hard-coded list of well-known peers
- DHCP/manual IP configuration
- Cached peer list from previous sessions

### Step 2: Overlay Construction
After discovery, the peer joins a **logical overlay network** by establishing TCP/UDP connections with a small subset of other peers (its **neighbors** in the overlay). The overlay is *virtual* — links do not correspond to physical wires.

### Step 3: Resource Registration
The peer advertises the resources (files, CPU, storage) it is willing to share. In **structured P2P**, this is done by hashing the resource name into a key and storing the (key, value) pair at the peer whose ID is closest to the key in the DHT. In **unstructured P2P**, no registration occurs — peers simply know their neighbors.

### Step 4: Query / Lookup
To find a resource, the peer issues a query. Two main strategies:
- **Flooding (Unstructured):** Peer sends query to all neighbors, who forward it to *their* neighbors, up to a Time-To-Live (TTL) limit. Uses huge bandwidth.
- **DHT Lookup (Structured):** Peer computes `hash(resource_name)` and routes the query in O(log N) hops using consistent hashing on a ring (Chord) or hypercube (Pastry/CAN).

### Step 5: Data Transfer
Once the resource location is known, the requesting peer establishes a **direct connection** (typically TCP) with the peer(s) holding the resource. Data is transferred peer-to-peer — **no server is involved** in the data path.

### Step 6: Churn Handling
Peers may leave or join at any time. The P2P protocol must handle **churn** (node arrivals/departures) gracefully via periodic heartbeats, replica maintenance, and re-routing.

## 2.2 Architectural Taxonomy of P2P Systems

| Architecture Type | Central Server? | Lookup Mechanism | Example | Scalability | Single Point of Failure? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Centralized P2P** (Napster) | Yes (index only) | Central index server | Napster (1999) | Limited (server bottleneck) | **Yes** — index server |
| **Decentralized Unstructured** (Pure) | No | Flooding / random walk | Gnutella v0.4 | Poor (bandwidth explosion) | **No** |
| **Decentralized Structured** | No | Distributed Hash Table (DHT) | Chord, Pastry, CAN, Kademlia | Excellent (O(log N) lookup) | **No** |
| **Hybrid P2P** (Super-Peers) | No (semi-central) | Super-peer hierarchy | KaZaA, Skype, BitTorrent trackers | Good | No (SPoF distributed) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **BitTorrent** is *technically* a hybrid: it uses a centralized **tracker** for peer discovery, but the actual file chunks are exchanged purely peer-to-peer. This is why it scales to millions of users.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The P2P paradigm has fewer closed-form formulas than physical-layer topics, but the following are **mandatory for KTU numerical/analytical questions**:

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Notation | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Chord Ring Lookup Complexity | $H_{Chord} = \log_2 N$ | $N$ = number of peers, $H$ = hop count | hops |
| Pastry / CAN DHT Lookup | $H_{DHT} = O(\log_b N)$ | $b$ = base of identifier space (e.g., 16) | hops |
| Gnutella Flooding Messages | $M \approx d^{TTL}$ | $d$ = avg. degree, $TTL$ = time-to-live | messages |
| Total System Capacity (P2P) | $C_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} c_i$ | $c_i$ = upload capacity of peer $i$ | bps |
| Server Capacity (Client–Server) | $C_{server} = c_{server}$ | Fixed regardless of $N$ | bps |
| P2P Service Rate per Peer | $\mu_{P2P} \approx u$ | $u$ = upload bandwidth per peer | files/s |
| Client–Server Service Rate | $\mu_{CS} = \dfrac{C_{server}}{L \cdot N}$ | $L$ = file size, $N$ = clients | files/s |
| Replication Factor | $R = \dfrac{N}{k}$ | $k$ = number of replicas per chunk | dimensionless |
| Probability peer has file (random) | $p = 1 - \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{N}\right)^N \approx 1 - \dfrac{1}{e}$ | For random P2P search | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> The famous result $p \approx 1 - 1/e \approx 0.632$ for random-peer search in Gnutella is a **favourite KTU examiner question** under "Analyse the P2P model."

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | P2P Technology Used | Why P2P? |
|---|---|---|
| **File Sharing** | BitTorrent, eMule | Scalable distribution of large content (Linux ISOs) |
| **Cryptocurrency** | Bitcoin, Ethereum | Decentralized consensus (no central bank) |
| **VoIP / Video Call** | Skype (legacy), ToTalk | Low latency, no central media server |
| **Content Delivery (CDN)** | WebRTC, Pando | Reduce origin-server load by offloading to peers |
| **Distributed Storage** | IPFS, Storj | Resilience through redundancy across peers |
| **Grid Computing** | BOINC, Folding@home | Aggregate idle CPU across millions of volunteers |
| **IoT / Edge Computing** | AllJoyn, Kademlia-based DHTs | Direct device-to-device, no cloud round-trip |

## 2.5 Comparative Analysis: P2P vs Client–Server

| Parameter | Client–Server | Peer-to-Peer |
|---|---|---|
| **Architecture** | Hierarchical | Flat / symmetric |
| **Scalability** | Server-bound (vertical) | Scales with users (horizontal) |
| **Single Point of Failure** | Yes (server) | No (pure P2P) |
| **Management** | Centralized, easy | Hard, distributed |
| **Security** | Centralized controls (firewall, AAA) | Trust is decentralized, weaker |
| **Bandwidth Cost** | Concentrated at server | Distributed among peers |
| **Reliability under Churn** | Server crash = total failure | Graceful degradation |
| **Use Cases** | Banking, ERP, Web apps | File sharing, blockchain, streaming |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Chord Ring Lookup — Why It is O(log N)

Chord is the most-cited **structured P2P** protocol. The following derivation is essential for KTU 14-mark questions.

**Setup:**
- Each peer and each key is assigned an $m$-bit identifier, where $m$ is large enough that collisions are negligible.
- Identifiers are arranged on a circular ring of size $2^m$.
- Peer $n$ maintains a **finger table** of size $m$ entries.
- The $i^{th}$ entry in peer $n$'s finger table points to the first peer that succeeds $n$ by at least $2^{i-1}$ positions.

### Step-by-Step:

$$
\text{Start.} \quad \text{Peer } n \text{ wants to find the node responsible for key } k.
$$

**Step 1:** Define the distance from $n$ to $k$ on the ring.

$$
d = (k - n) \mod 2^m
$$

**Step 2:** Express $d$ in binary (since the ring is structured around powers of 2).

$$
d = \sum_{i=0}^{m-1} b_i \cdot 2^{i}, \quad b_i \in \{0, 1\}
$$

**Step 3:** In each hop, the querying peer advances to the largest finger $F[j]$ such that $F[j] \le k$. This zeros out the highest remaining bit of $d$ in one hop.

$$
\text{After hop 1: } d_1 = d \mod 2^{m-1}
$$

$$
\text{After hop 2: } d_2 = d \mod 2^{m-2}
$$

**Step 4:** In the worst case, the highest bit is at position $m-1$, requiring at most $m$ hops to reduce the distance to 0.

$$
H_{max} = m = \log_2(2^m) = \log_2 N
$$

**Step 5:** Therefore, the lookup complexity is:

$$
\boxed{H_{Chord} = O(\log_2 N)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation for Exam:** With $N = 1{,}000{,}000$ peers, Chord needs only about $\log_2(10^6) \approx 20$ hops to find any key, regardless of network size. This is the fundamental reason structured P2P outperforms flooding (which is exponential in TTL).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Gnutella Flooding Message Complexity

**Setup:** Gnutella uses pure flooding with each peer having an average degree $d$ (number of neighbors) and a query Time-To-Live of $TTL$ hops.

### Step-by-Step:

**Step 1:** A peer initiating the query sends the message to all $d$ of its neighbors.
- Messages at hop 1: $M_1 = d$

**Step 2:** Each of those $d$ peers forwards to $d-1$ *new* neighbors (excluding the sender), giving:
- Messages at hop 2: $M_2 = d(d-1)$

**Step 3:** Generalizing for $TTL$ hops with high $d$, we approximate $(d-1) \approx d$:

$$
M_{total} = d + d^2 + d^3 + \ldots + d^{TTL}
$$

**Step 4:** Apply the geometric series formula:

$$
M_{total} = d \cdot \dfrac{d^{TTL} - 1}{d - 1}
$$

**Step 5:** For large $TTL$ and $d > 1$:

$$
\boxed{M_{total} \approx d^{TTL}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Why this matters in KTU:** With $d = 4$ and $TTL = 7$, a single query generates $4^7 = 16{,}384$ messages. This is the **fundamental scalability problem of unstructured P2P** and the reason Gnutella was later replaced by KaZaA's super-peer architecture.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Why P2P Scales Better Than Client–Server for File Distribution

**Problem:** A file of size $F$ bits must be distributed to $N$ peers. Compare the minimum distribution time $T$ under both models.

### Client–Server Model:

**Step 1:** The server has upload capacity $u_s$ and each client has download capacity $d_i$. The server's upload is the bottleneck.

**Step 2:** Minimum time to upload to all clients sequentially:

$$
T_{CS} \geq \dfrac{N \cdot F}{u_s}
$$

**Step 3:** For asymmetric Internet where $\min(d_i) > u_s / N$:

$$
\boxed{T_{CS} = \max\!\left(\dfrac{N F}{u_s},\ \dfrac{F}{\min_i d_i}\right)}
$$

### Peer-to-Peer Model:

**Step 4:** The server must upload at least one full copy $F$. This takes time $F / u_s$.

**Step 5:** After that, every peer becomes a *server*. Total upload capacity of the system is $u_s + \sum_{i=1}^{N} u_i$, where $u_i$ is peer $i$'s upload rate.

**Step 6:** Total bits to deliver: $N F$. Therefore:

$$
T_{P2P} \geq \max\!\left(\dfrac{F}{u_s},\ \dfrac{N F}{u_s + \sum_{i=1}^{N} u_i}\right)
$$

**Step 7:** For large $N$ and assuming symmetric peers with $u_i = u$:

$$
T_{P2P} \approx \max\!\left(\dfrac{F}{u_s},\ \dfrac{N F}{N u}\right) = \max\!\left(\dfrac{F}{u_s},\ \dfrac{F}{u}\right)
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{P2P} \text{ grows as } O(1) \text{ with } N}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Insight:** Client–server time grows **linearly** in $N$, while P2P time grows as $O(1)$ (constant) — provided each peer's upload rate exceeds the server's. This is the **killer advantage** of BitTorrent. Kurose & Ross derive this in Chapter 2 of their textbook — **favourite KTU 14-mark question**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Pros & Cons of P2P

| Aspect | Advantage of P2P | Disadvantage of P2P |
|---|---|---|
| Scalability | Grows with users | Flooding is expensive in unstructured P2P |
| Cost | Low infrastructure cost | ISP transit costs rise (inter-peer traffic) |
| Reliability | No single point of failure | Hard to enforce guarantees (QoS, SLA) |
| Security | Distributed trust | Vulnerable to Sybil, Eclipse, poisoning attacks |
| Manageability | Self-organizing | Difficult to upgrade, monitor, patch |
| Performance | High aggregate throughput | Variable latency, depends on peer location |
| Legal | — | Often misused for piracy (Napster case) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Comparison: Client–Server vs Peer-to-Peer Topology

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CS["Client-Server Model"]
        S1["Central Server"]
        C1["Client 1"]
        C2["Client 2"]
        C3["Client 3"]
        C4["Client 4"]
        S1 --- C1
        S1 --- C2
        S1 --- C3
        S1 --- C4
    end

    subgraph P2P["Peer-to-Peer Model"]
        P1["Peer A\nClient AND Server"]
        P2["Peer B\nClient AND Server"]
        P3["Peer C\nClient AND Server"]
        P4["Peer D\nClient AND Server"]
        P5["Peer E\nClient AND Server"]
        P1 --- P2
        P2 --- P3
        P3 --- P4
        P4 --- P5
        P5 --- P1
        P1 --- P3
        P2 --- P4
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual Insight:** In the left subgraph, every arrow originates or terminates at the central server — a clear **hub-and-spoke** topology. In the right subgraph, every node is an *equal* participant with bidirectional connections — a **mesh topology**. The P2P graph has **no privileged node**.

---

## 4.2 Taxonomy of P2P Architectures

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P2PTree["Peer-to-Peer Paradigm"]

    P2PTree --> Cent["Centralized P2P\nExample: Napster"]
    P2PTree --> Dec["Decentralized P2P"]
    P2PTree --> Hybrid["Hybrid P2P\nExample: KaZaA, BitTorrent"]

    Cent --> CentDesc["Central index for lookup\nDirect peer-to-peer data transfer\nSPoF: index server"]

    Dec --> Unstr["Unstructured\nExample: Gnutella v0.4"]
    Dec --> Str["Structured\nUses DHT"]

    Unstr --> UnstrDesc["Flooding queries\nNo global topology\nO(d^TTL) messages"]

    Str --> StrDesc["O(log N) lookups\nChord, Pastry, CAN, Kademlia\nConsistent hashing"]
```

---

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: BitTorrent (Hybrid P2P)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Peer A\nLeecher with .torrent file"] -->|"HTTP GET .torrent"| TR["Tracker Server\nCentralized index"]
    TR -->|"List of peers\nholding chunks"| A

    A -->|"Direct TCP\nChunk exchange"| B["Peer B\nSeeder"]
    A -->|"Direct TCP\nChunk exchange"| C["Peer C\nLeecher"]
    B -->|"Direct TCP\nChunk exchange"| C

    subgraph ChunkEx["Chunk Swapping Layer"]
        B
        C
        A
    end

    style TR fill:#ffd,stroke:#333
    style A fill:#dfd,stroke:#333
    style B fill:#ddf,stroke:#333
    style C fill:#ddf,stroke:#333
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Note:** The Tracker in BitTorrent is the *only* centralized component — it only handles **peer discovery**. Once peers are known to each other, *all* chunk transfer happens peer-to-peer. The tracker does **not** relay file data.

---

## 4.4 Chord Ring — Structured DHT Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    N0["Node 0\nm=0,1,3,7,15"] --> N8["Node 8\nm=8,9,11"]
    N8 --> N14["Node 14\nm=14,15"]
    N14 --> N21["Node 21\nm=21,23"]
    N21 --> N32["Node 32\nm=32,..."]
    N32 --> N40["Node 40"]
    N40 --> N0

    K54["Key 54\nStored at successor = Node 56"] -.-> N40
    K10["Key 10\nStored at successor = Node 14"] -.-> N14
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Each node's finger table points to successors at distances $2^0, 2^1, 2^2, \ldots, 2^{m-1}$ around the ring. A query for Key 54 starting at Node 0 would jump: $0 \to 8 \to 14 \to 21 \to 40$ in $\log_2 N$ hops — not the full $N/2$ linear scan.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: 2-Mark / 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### **Q1. Define Peer-to-Peer (P2P) Network. List any two advantages over the client-server model.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

> A Peer-to-Peer (P2P) network is a distributed architecture in which each node (peer) acts as both a **client** and a **server**, sharing resources and services directly with other peers **without a centralized coordinator**.
>
> **Two advantages over client–server:**
> 1. **Scalability:** Total system capacity grows linearly with the number of peers, since every new peer contributes upload bandwidth.
> 2. **No single point of failure:** Removal of any one peer does not collapse the network (in pure P2P).

---

### **Q2. Differentiate between Structured and Unstructured P2P networks.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Unstructured P2P | Structured P2P |
|---|---|---|
| Topology | Random / ad-hoc mesh | Deterministic (ring, hypercube) |
| Resource placement | No global rule | DHT-based mapping |
| Lookup | Flooding (O($d^{TTL}$)) | O($\log N$) |
| Example | Gnutella, Freenet | Chord, Kademlia |
| Robustness to churn | High | Requires maintenance on churn |

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Module Internal Choice Questions

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]`

> **(a)** Explain the Peer-to-Peer paradigm in detail. Compare the centralized, decentralized (pure), and hybrid P2P architectures with one real-world example for each. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** With a neat diagram, describe the working of a **Centralized P2P** system (Napster-style). Discuss its single point of failure and explain how the **Hybrid P2P** model (e.g., BitTorrent) overcomes it. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

1. **Definition:** P2P is a distributed application architecture where each node (*peer*) plays the role of both client and server, sharing resources (storage, content, CPU) directly with other peers. There is no central server, no fixed hierarchy, and workload is symmetrically distributed.

2. **Centralized P2P (e.g., Napster):**
   - A **central index server** stores metadata (filename → peer IP mapping).
   - Lookup goes to the server; **actual data transfer is peer-to-peer**.
   - The index server is a single point of failure.

3. **Decentralized / Pure P2P (e.g., Gnutella):**
   - No server at all.
   - Peers use **flooding** to discover resources.
   - Each peer forwards queries to neighbors up to a TTL.
   - Drawback: bandwidth explosion, $O(d^{TTL})$ messages.

4. **Hybrid P2P (e.g., KaZaA, Skype, BitTorrent):**
   - **Super-peers** (high-bandwidth nodes) act as regional indexes for ordinary peers.
   - Combines efficiency of centralized lookup with robustness of decentralization.
   - BitTorrent's **tracker** is a light-weight hybrid: only peer discovery, not data relay.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Working of Centralized P2P (Napster):**

1. Each peer registers the list of files it shares with the **central index server**.
2. Querying peer $P_q$ sends a search request to the index server.
3. Index server returns a list of peers holding the matching file.
4. $P_q$ establishes a **direct TCP connection** with one of those peers and downloads the file.

**Diagram (text representation):**

- Peer A (sharing `song.mp3`) → registers → Index Server
- Peer B (searching `song.mp3`) → query → Index Server → `[A's IP]`
- Peer B → direct TCP → Peer A → file transfer

**Single Point of Failure:** If the index server crashes, **lookup is impossible** (though existing direct connections survive). This is why Napster was shut down — it could not survive legal pressure on the central index.

**How Hybrid (BitTorrent) overcomes it:**
- The **tracker** only handles peer discovery, not authentication or data.
- Once peers are connected, data exchange is **pure P2P**.
- A tracker outage only blocks *new* peer discovery, not ongoing transfers.
- Distributing trackers across many domains removes the single point of failure.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Modified]`

> **(a)** With a suitable example, explain **Distributed Hash Table (DHT)**-based structured P2P. Show how the **Chord protocol** achieves lookup in $O(\log_2 N)$ hops. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Compare and contrast the **Client–Server** and **Peer-to-Peer** models with respect to scalability, fault tolerance, security, and bandwidth cost. Show with a derivation why P2P scales better for large-scale file distribution. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Distributed Hash Table (DHT):**

A DHT is a decentralized key–value store spread across many peers. Each peer is assigned a random $m$-bit ID, and each resource key is hashed to an $m$-bit key. The resource is stored at the peer whose ID is the **successor** of the key on the identifier ring.

**Chord Protocol — Detailed Mechanism:**

1. **ID Space:** Identifiers are integers in $[0, 2^m - 1]$ arranged on a circle.
2. **Successor Rule:** Key $k$ is stored at the **first peer whose ID $\geq k$** (clockwise).
3. **Finger Table:** Each peer $n$ maintains $m$ entries: $F[i]$ = successor of $n + 2^{i-1}$.
4. **Lookup:** To find key $k$, peer $n$ forwards the query to the finger $F[j]$ with the largest ID $\leq k$. In each hop, the gap to $k$ is halved.
5. **Hop Bound:** The maximum distance halves each step, so:

$$
H_{max} = \log_2 N
$$

6. **Example:** With $m = 4$ (16 nodes), a query traverses at most 4 hops regardless of position.

**Real-world example:** Kademlia (used in Ethereum discovery and IPFS) generalizes Chord with multiple parallel lookups for faster resolution.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Comparison Table** (write in exam):

| Parameter | Client–Server | Peer-to-Peer |
|---|---|---|
| Scalability | Bounded by server capacity | Grows with peers |
| Fault tolerance | Single point of failure | High (no central node) |
| Security | Centralized AAA, easy | Distributed trust, weaker |
| Bandwidth cost | Concentrated at server | Distributed; ISP cost rises |

**Derivation of P2P Scalability Advantage:**

For distributing a file of size $F$ to $N$ clients:

**Client–Server minimum time:**

$$
T_{CS} = \dfrac{N \cdot F}{u_s}
$$

This grows **linearly** with $N$.

**P2P minimum time:** Once the server uploads one copy, the **aggregate upload capacity** of the system becomes $u_s + \sum_{i=1}^{N} u_i$. For symmetric peers with upload rate $u$:

$$
T_{P2P} = \max\!\left(\dfrac{F}{u_s},\ \dfrac{N F}{u_s + N u}\right)
$$

For $N u \gg u_s$:

$$
T_{P2P} \approx \dfrac{F}{u}
$$

This is **independent of $N$** — i.e., $O(1)$.

**Conclusion:** $T_{CS} = O(N)$ while $T_{P2P} = O(1)$, so P2P scales **asymptotically better** for large $N$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Before Writing:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse "Centralized P2P" with "Client-Server."** In Napster, only the *lookup* is centralized; *data transfer* is still P2P. Many students lose 2 marks by writing "Napster is just client-server."
> 2. **Always write the formula, not just the conclusion.** Saying "P2P is faster" is 0 marks; writing $T_{CS} = NF/u_s$ vs $T_{P2P} = F/u$ is full marks.
> 3. **Mention DHT explicitly** in any structured P2P answer. The acronym must be expanded: **Distributed Hash Table**.
> 4. **Draw the diagram.** A 7-mark question without a labeled diagram is automatically docked 2 marks per KTU valuation norms.
> 5. **Do not claim P2P has no disadvantages.** Examiners dock marks for one-sided answers. Always include security, ISP cost, and churn issues.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Read 3 times before the exam:**

- [x] **Definition:** P2P = distributed architecture where each node is *both* client and server; no central coordinator.
- [x] **Three architecture types** (memorize for guaranteed 3 marks):
  - **Centralized P2P** → central index, P2P data (Napster)
  - **Decentralized Unstructured** → flooding (Gnutella)
  - **Decentralized Structured** → DHT, $O(\log N)$ (Chord, Pastry, Kademlia)
  - **Hybrid P2P** → super-peers / trackers (KaZaA, BitTorrent, Skype)
- [x] **Chord lookup complexity:** $H = O(\log_2 N)$ — always quote this.
- [x] **Gnutella flooding cost:** $M \approx d^{TTL}$ — explain the bandwidth blow-up.
- [x] **P2P vs Client–Server distribution time:**
  - $T_{CS} = N F / u_s$ → linear in $N$
  - $T_{P2P} = F / u$ → constant in $N$
- [x] **Real-world examples:** BitTorrent (hybrid), Bitcoin (DHT-like), Gnutella (pure unstructured), Napster (centralized).
- [x] **Advantages to list:** scalability, fault tolerance, no SPoF, cost-effective distribution.
- [x] **Disadvantages to list (don't ignore!):** security vulnerabilities, ISP cost, churn, legal issues, hard QoS.
- [x] **Key buzzwords examiners scan for:** *overlay network, DHT, consistent hashing, super-peer, churn, Sybil attack, BitTorrent tracker.*
- [x] **Always draw a labeled diagram** for any 7-mark P2P question.
- [x] **Kurose & Ross Chapter 2** is the standard KTU reference; mention "structured vs unstructured" verbatim.
- [x] **Remember BitTorrent specifics:** tracker ≠ central server; chunk-based downloading; tit-for-tat choking algorithm.
- [x] **Memorize the $1 - 1/e$ result** for random peer-search success probability in unstructured P2P.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Case Study: BitTorrent
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAMT632 - BASICS OF COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Case Study: BitTorrent

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**BitTorrent** is a widely-deployed, peer-to-peer (P2P) **application-layer file distribution protocol** designed for efficient transfer of large files over the Internet. Instead of relying on a single, central server (as in the traditional client-server model), BitTorrent decomposes a file into equal-sized *pieces* and enables every downloading peer to simultaneously upload (re-share) the pieces it already possesses to other peers. This collective, distributed contribution model is what makes BitTorrent highly **scalable** — the aggregate upload capacity of the system grows with the number of users, in direct contrast to the conventional client-server paradigm where server bandwidth becomes the bottleneck.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1 — Application Layer Case Study)**
> BitTorrent is the canonical case study to illustrate how **peer-to-peer architectures** solve the scalability, single-point-of-failure, and bandwidth-bottleneck problems inherent in classical client-server file distribution.

### 1.2 Key Terminology

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Torrent / Swarm** | The entire set of peers (including seeds and leechers) participating in the distribution of one specific file. |
| **Seeder** | A peer that possesses **100%** of the file pieces and continues to upload (serve) to others. |
| **Leecher** | A peer that is still downloading; typically also uploads the pieces it has already collected. |
| **Tracker** | A centralized server that maintains the membership list (IP addresses) of peers in a swarm. |
| **.torrent file** | A small metadata file containing file names, piece hashes, piece length, and the tracker's URL. |
| **Piece** | A segment of the file, typically **256 KB**, that is downloaded and verified as an atomic unit. |
| **Sub-piece (Block)** | A sub-unit (typically **16 KB**) of a piece, used in pipelined request pipelines. |
| **Choking** | A temporary refusal by a peer to upload to a specific peer, used to enforce fair exchange. |
| **DHT (Distributed Hash Table)** | A trackerless alternative (used in Mainline DHT) that decentralizes peer discovery. |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Community Library"

Imagine a brand-new, **very thick novel** (the file) that thousands of students (peers) in a city want to read. The school decides **not** to rely on a single library (the central server) because the librarian would be overworked.

Instead, the school photocopies the book and **rips every copy into 100 chapters** (pieces) and gives each student a *different* random subset of chapters.

- When a student finishes a chapter, the rule says: **"Before you may borrow a chapter from another student, you must lend the chapters you already own."** This is the **tit-for-tat** incentive.
- A student who owns *no* chapter is **barred from borrowing** until they upload something first.
- The school publishes a small index card (the `.torrent` file) listing the book's title, total chapters, and a phone number to call (**tracker**) for a list of students who have the chapters you need.
- Students preferentially exchange the **rarest chapters first** so no single chapter is held by only one person (preventing its accidental loss).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Takeaway:** BitTorrent is essentially a *cooperative, mutually-accountable barter economy* for digital content. The protocol's "currency" is **upload bandwidth**, and the protocol's "fairness law" is **tit-for-tat** (a peer must share to receive).

### 1.4 Historical Context

BitTorrent was conceived by **Bram Cohen** in **2001** to address the inefficiency of distributing large open-source Linux ISOs. It was one of the first protocols to fully exploit the **upload capacity of downstream clients**, converting what was previously wasted bandwidth into the system's primary delivery mechanism. Today, BitTorrent-style swarming underpins many modern content delivery networks (CDNs) and is the conceptual foundation of protocols like μTP, WebRTC-based P2P, and even parts of blockchain dissemination.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Fundamental Problems BitTorrent Solves

1. **Scalability Problem (Server Bottleneck):**
   In the client-server model, the server's upload bandwidth $U_s$ is shared by $N$ clients.
   $$D_{cs} = \frac{F}{U_s}$$
   where $D_{cs}$ is the time taken to distribute a file of size $F$ to all $N$ clients using a single server upload rate $U_s$ (assuming identical download capacities $d_i = U_s/N$, this is a well-known lower bound).
   BitTorrent side-steps this entirely because **every peer is also a server**.

2. **Free-Rider Problem:**
   In an open P2P network, why should a peer contribute upload bandwidth? BitTorrent's answer is the **tit-for-tat** policy — peers earn upload slots in proportion to how much they upload.

### 2.2 Operational Phases of a BitTorrent Session

A BitTorrent session proceeds in four well-defined phases:

**Phase 1 — Bootstrap & Peer Discovery**
- The client opens the `.torrent` file, extracts the *info-hash* (a 160-bit SHA-1 hash identifying the file).
- The client contacts the **tracker** via HTTP/HTTPS and receives a random subset (typically 50) of peer IP:port addresses currently in the swarm.
- *(Optional / modern)* If no tracker exists, the client queries the **DHT** network using the info-hash, following a Kademlia-like lookup.

**Phase 2 — Joining the Swarm (Handshake & Bitfield Exchange)**
- Each pair of peers performs a **Peer Wire Protocol handshake**, exchanging their peer-IDs and the supported extensions.
- After handshake, each peer sends a **BITFIELD message** (an array of bits, 1 = has piece, 0 = does not have).
- From the received bitfield, each peer can determine which pieces the other has and which are missing locally.

**Phase 3 — Piece Selection & Exchange (The Core Loop)**

BitTorrent uses **three smart piece-selection strategies** simultaneously:

| Strategy | Purpose | Rule |
|---|---|---|
| **Strict Priority** | Complete in-progress pieces first. | Once a sub-piece of piece $p$ is requested, all remaining sub-pieces of $p$ are requested before any other piece. |
| **Rarest First** | Promote piece diversity in the swarm. | Always request the piece that is least replicated among the peer set. |
| **Random First Piece** | Bootstrap newcomers quickly. | While the peer has *no* pieces, the very first piece is chosen uniformly at random to begin building something to share. |

**Phase 4 — Choking, Unchoking & Endgame**
- The choking algorithm decides **which peers to upload to** at any moment. (Detailed in §3.)
- When a peer has *all* outstanding requests pending, it enters **Endgame Mode** and broadcasts duplicate requests to all peers holding the last pieces, cancelling responses that arrive from non-responders.

### 2.3 The Choking Algorithm — The Heart of BitTorrent

At any given moment, each peer is choked by some peers and unchoked by others. BitTorrent refreshes this policy every **10 seconds**.

**Rules of the standard choking algorithm:**

1. **Top 4 Unchoke Slots:** Sort all peers by the rate at which they have uploaded to me in the last 20 seconds; unchoke the **top 4**. This enforces **reciprocity** (tit-for-tat).
2. **Optimistic Unchoke (1 slot):** Every **30 seconds**, rotate one extra unchoke slot to a random peer. This *probes* potentially-better peers and gives newcomers a chance to bootstrap.
3. **Anti-Snubbing:** If a peer has been choked by all peers for over **60 seconds**, it stops uploading to them entirely and considers itself *snubbed*. It then **boosts its optimistic unchoke frequency** (unchokes 1 peer every 10 seconds instead of every 30) to escape the snub.
4. **Upload-Only Mode:** Once a peer has the entire file, it drops tit-for-tat and simply unchokes the peers with the highest download rates, to "seed" the swarm as fast as possible.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why 4 unchoke slots and not 1?**
> Four is the empirically tuned sweet spot: enough to enforce fairness via rich-getting-richer, but small enough to limit the damage from a malicious peer who briefly unchokes you, gets pieces, and then rechokes.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Definition | Unit / Domain |
|---|---|---|
| $F$ | Total file size | **bits** or **bytes** |
| $P$ | Piece size (default) | **256 KB** |
| $S$ | Sub-piece / block size (default) | **16 KB** |
| $N_p$ | Number of pieces in the file | dimensionless |
| $N_p = F / P$ | Pieces per file | dimensionless |
| $N_s = P / S$ | Sub-pieces per piece | dimensionless |
| $u_i$ | Upload rate of peer $i$ | **bytes / second** |
| $d_i$ | Download rate of peer $i$ | **bytes / second** |
| $U_{aggregate}$ | Total upload capacity of swarm $= \sum_i u_i$ | **bytes / second** |
| $T_{min}$ | Minimum distribution time $\geq F / U_{aggregate}$ | **seconds** |
| $T_{cs}$ | Client-server distribution time $\approx NF / U_s$ | **seconds** |
| $T_{bt}$ | BitTorrent distribution time (approx.) | $\approx NF / (U_s + U_{aggregate})$ |
| $\eta$ | Protocol efficiency ratio $= T_{cs}/T_{bt}$ | dimensionless |
| $T_{choke}$ | Choke algorithm period | **10 s** |
| $T_{opt}$ | Optimistic unchoke period | **30 s** |
| $T_{snub}$ | Anti-snub timeout | **60 s** |

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically asks students to relate BitTorrent to **P2P architecture** and to **understand the incentives** that prevent free-riding. Memorize the four standard time constants (10 s, 20 s, 30 s, 60 s) and the value 4 for top unchoke slots — they are the most-tested constants.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The BitTorrent design is the conceptual ancestor of:
- **Content Distribution Networks (CDNs)** that use hybrid P2P/edge-caching (e.g., live video streaming over WebRTC).
- **Blockchain block propagation** in Bitcoin/Ethereum, where miners gossip blocks P2P-style.
- **Software update delivery** by large vendors (Linux distros, game patches), where initial seeding is HTTP and bulk distribution is BitTorrent to reduce CDN costs.
- **Datacenter bulk data transfer** tools like *bbcp* and *GridFTP* that borrow piece-level parallelism.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Why BitTorrent Outperforms Client-Server at Scale

Consider distributing a file of size $F$ to $N$ peers. Assume all peers have download rate $d$ and upload rate $u$.

**Step 1 — Client-Server Distribution Time $T_{cs}$:**

The server must transmit the full file $F$ to each of the $N$ clients sequentially (in the worst case where the server's upload is the bottleneck). Time to serve one client is $F/u_s$, where $u_s$ is the server's upload rate. If the server can use its full bandwidth $u_s$ to pump out a *stream* that all clients receive in parallel, the time becomes:
$$T_{cs} = \max\left(\frac{NF}{u_s}, \frac{F}{d}\right)$$

**Step 2 — BitTorrent Aggregate Upload Capacity:**

In BitTorrent, every peer (after acquiring pieces) also uploads. The total upload capacity of the swarm is:
$$U_{agg} = u_s + \sum_{i=1}^{N} u_i$$

**Step 3 — BitTorrent Distribution Time $T_{bt}$:**

A lower bound for $T_{bt}$ is the time to push the equivalent of $NF$ bits into the swarm, divided by aggregate upload capacity:
$$T_{bt} \geq \frac{NF}{u_s + \sum_{i=1}^{N} u_i}$$

**Step 4 — Speedup Ratio:**

$$\eta = \frac{T_{cs}}{T_{bt}} \leq \frac{NF \left(u_s + \sum u_i\right)}{u_s \cdot NF} = 1 + \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} u_i}{u_s}$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:**

If the original server's upload rate is $u_s$ and the average client upload rate is $u$, then:
$$\eta \approx 1 + \frac{Nu}{u_s}$$

> For a popular file ($N$ very large), $\eta$ grows linearly with $N$ — the *more people download, the faster it goes*. This is the **fundamental scaling advantage** of BitTorrent.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Standard Numerical Example (KTU-Favorite)

**Problem (typical KTU 2-mark concept):** File size $F = 1 \text{ GB}$, piece size $P = 256 \text{ KB}$. How many pieces?

**Step-by-step solution:**

**Step 1 — Unit consistency:**
$$F = 1 \text{ GB} = 1024 \text{ MB} = 1{,}048{,}576 \text{ KB}$$

**Step 2 — Number of pieces:**
$$N_p = \frac{F}{P} = \frac{1{,}048{,}576 \text{ KB}}{256 \text{ KB}} = 4096 \text{ pieces}$$

**Step 3 — Verify total bitfield size:**
The bitfield is one bit per piece, so it occupies:
$$\frac{4096}{8} = 512 \text{ bytes}$$

**Step 4 — Number of sub-pieces per piece (at default $S = 16 \text{ KB}$):**
$$N_s = \frac{P}{S} = \frac{256}{16} = 16 \text{ sub-pieces per piece}$$

**Step 5 — Total sub-pieces in the file:**
$$N_{total} = N_p \times N_s = 4096 \times 16 = 65{,}536 \text{ sub-pieces}$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison Table for Exam

| Property | Client-Server | Pure P2P (Gnutella) | BitTorrent |
|---|---|---|---|
| Central server required? | Yes | No | Optional (Tracker) |
| Scalability with $N$ | Poor (server bottleneck) | Good | Excellent (swarm scaling) |
| Free-rider resistance | N/A (no sharing) | Weak | **Strong (tit-for-tat)** |
| Piece integrity check | None inherent | Hash-based | **SHA-1 per piece** |
| Peer discovery | Server gives list | Flooded query | Tracker or DHT |
| Bandwidth efficiency | Low | Medium | High (upload aggregation) |
| Single point of failure | Yes (server) | No | Optional (tracker can be DHT) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block: BitTorrent Swarm Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[User clicks .torrent file] --> B[Parse .torrent file: info-hash, piece hashes, tracker URL]
    B --> C[Contact Tracker via HTTP]
    C --> D[Tracker returns random peer list: 50 IPs]
    D --> E[Open TCP connections and perform Peer Wire Protocol handshake]
    E --> F[Exchange BITFIELD messages with each connected peer]
    F --> G[Build local map of which peer has which piece]
    G --> H{In local piece set empty?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Apply Random First Piece strategy]
    H -- No  --> J[Apply Rarest First strategy]
    I --> K[Send REQUEST messages for sub-pieces]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Receive PIECE messages with sub-piece data]
    L --> M[Verify SHA-1 hash of completed piece]
    M --> N{Hash matches?}
    N -- No  --> O[Discard piece, mark for re-download]
    N -- Yes --> P[Announce HAVE to all connected peers]
    P --> Q[Update local bitfield]
    Q --> R{All pieces downloaded?}
    R -- No --> H
    R -- Yes --> S[Become Seeder, enter Upload-Only mode]
    S --> T[Every 10s run Choking Algorithm: keep top 4 unchokes + 1 optimistic unchoke]
    T --> U[Continue serving until client exit]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block: State Diagram of a Single Peer

```mermaid
graph LR
    state1[Leecher with 0 pieces] -->|Random First Piece| state2[Leecher with some pieces]
    state2 -->|Rarest First + Tit-for-Tat| state3[Leecher with many pieces]
    state3 -->|Endgame Mode triggered| state4[Leecher with all pieces requested]
    state4 -->|Last piece verified| state5[Seeder]
    state5 -->|User exits or stops| state6[Disconnected]
    state2 -->|Snubbed for 60s| state7[Anti-Snub: optimistic unchoke every 10s]
    state7 --> state2
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block: Choking Algorithm Decision Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    chk1[Every 10 seconds, run choking algorithm] --> chk2[Compute download rate from each peer over last 20s]
    chk2 --> chk3[Sort peers by download rate descending]
    chk3 --> chk4[Unchoke top 4 peers: TIT-FOR-TAT]
    chk4 --> chk5{Am I a seeder?}
    chk5 -- Yes --> chk6[Upload-Only: unchoke peers with highest download FROM me]
    chk5 -- No  --> chk7[Add 1 OPTIMISTIC UNCHOKE slot to a random peer]
    chk7 --> chk8{Is this peer snubbed by all others for 60s?}
    chk8 -- Yes --> chk9[Boost optimistic unchoke: rotate every 10s instead of 30s]
    chk8 -- No  --> chk10[Continue normal operation]
    chk9 --> chk10
```

### 4.4 Text-Based Schematic: The .torrent File Structure (Bencode)

The `.torrent` file is encoded using **bencode**, a simple recursive format:

- `announce` — URL of the tracker.
- `info` — dictionary containing:
  - `name` — suggested file/directory name.
  - `piece length` — size of each piece in bytes.
  - `pieces` — concatenation of SHA-1 hashes, one per piece.
  - `files` — list of files with their lengths and paths.
- *(Optional)* `announce-list` — list of backup trackers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **info-hash** is the SHA-1 hash of the *bencoded* `info` dictionary. It is the unique 160-bit identifier of a torrent and is what trackers and DHT nodes use to identify the swarm.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Jun 2024, Model QP, Module 1]**
*Define the terms "seeder", "leecher", and "swarm" in the context of BitTorrent. How does BitTorrent discourage free-riding?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Seeder:** A peer that has downloaded 100% of the file's pieces and continues to upload them to other peers (1 mark).
- **Leecher:** A peer that is still in the process of downloading; it may also upload pieces it has already acquired (1 mark).
- **Swarm:** The complete set of all peers (seeders + leechers) participating in the distribution of one particular torrent (1 mark).

> **Examiner's Note (for free-riding part if asked as a follow-up):** BitTorrent uses the **tit-for-tat** choking algorithm — a peer allocates its limited unchoke slots to those peers from which it has received the highest upload rate, thereby penalising peers that contribute nothing.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model QP, Module 1]**
*List any three piece-selection strategies used in BitTorrent and state the purpose of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):**
1. **Strict Priority** — Once a sub-piece of a piece is requested, all remaining sub-pieces of that piece are requested first; ensures a complete piece is assembled as fast as possible.
2. **Rarest First** — Peers preferentially request the piece that is least replicated in their peer set; promotes swarm-wide piece diversity and prevents rare-piece disappearance.
3. **Random First Piece** — When a peer has *no* pieces, it picks a random piece to download first; enables rapid bootstrapping of the peer's own contribution.
   *(Alternative 4th strategy: **Endgame Mode** — broadcast duplicate requests for the last few pieces to minimise download completion time.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 1, 14 marks]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **architecture of a BitTorrent swarm**. Explain the role of the **tracker**, the **.torrent file**, and the **bitfield** message. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the **choking algorithm** of BitTorrent in detail. How does BitTorrent prevent free-riding, and what is the role of **optimistic unchoking**? *(7 marks)*

#### **Model Answer for Question A:**

**(a) Architecture of a BitTorrent Swarm (7 marks):**

A BitTorrent swarm consists of the following functional blocks (refer §4.1 mermaid diagram for visual):

1. **Tracker (1 mark):** A lightweight HTTP server that maintains a list of all peers currently active in a given swarm. When a new peer joins, it contacts the tracker with the info-hash; the tracker returns a random subset (typically 50) of peer IP:port tuples. Trackers do not transfer any file data — they only coordinate peer discovery.

2. **.torrent metadata file (2 marks):** A small file (typically a few KB) distributed out-of-band (e.g., via a website or magnet link). It contains:
   - The URL of the tracker (`announce`).
   - The piece length and total piece count.
   - The concatenated SHA-1 hashes of all pieces (used to verify integrity).
   - File names, lengths, and directory structure.
   The SHA-1 hash of the `info` dictionary is the **info-hash** that uniquely identifies the torrent.

3. **Bitfield message (2 marks):** After the peer-wire handshake, each peer sends a BITFIELD — an array of bits where bit $i$ = 1 if the peer possesses piece $i$ and 0 otherwise. This allows each peer to instantly know which pieces the other holds without redundant queries. The bitfield length is $\lceil N_p / 8 \rceil$ bytes.
