4. **Peer Wire Protocol messages (1 mark):** After bitfield exchange, peers exchange `INTERESTED`, `UNCHOKE`, `REQUEST`, `PIECE`, `HAVE`, and `CANCEL` messages to drive the piece download.
5. **Swarm operations (1 mark):** All peers cooperatively download and upload in parallel; the swarm's aggregate upload capacity grows with the number of peers, yielding the characteristic scaling advantage.

**(b) Choking Algorithm (7 marks):**

The **choking algorithm** is the *fairness engine* of BitTorrent. At any time, a peer can be *choked* (temporarily refused uploads) or *unchoked* (allowed to download from) by another peer. Each peer re-evaluates its unchoke decisions every **10 seconds**.

**Rules of the algorithm (7 marks — break down as below):**

1. **Top-4 Unchoke (reciprocity / tit-for-tat) (2 marks):** The peer measures how much it has downloaded from each connected peer over the last 20 seconds, ranks them, and unchokes the top 4. This makes upload bandwidth the *currency* of the network — peers who contribute more receive more.

2. **Optimistic Unchoke (probabilistic exploration) (2 marks):** Every 30 seconds, a 5th unchoke slot is rotated to a *random* peer. This serves two purposes:
   - It gives newcomers a chance to bootstrap (they need to download *something* before they can upload).
   - It allows the peer to discover a better, faster peer than its current top 4.

3. **Anti-Snubbing (1 mark):** If a peer has been choked by every other peer for over 60 seconds, it declares itself *snubbed*. It then **boosts** its optimistic-unchoke frequency (rotates once every 10 s instead of 30 s) to escape.

4. **Upload-Only Mode for Seeders (1 mark):** Once a peer has the complete file, it abandons tit-for-tat and simply unchokes the peers that are currently downloading the fastest from it, to maximise the swarm's overall distribution rate.

5. **Free-Riding Prevention (1 mark):** Because unchoke slots are rationed to peers who upload, a peer that never uploads is *starved* — it cannot get unchoke slots, and thus cannot download. This is BitTorrent's economic incentive against free-riding.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Marks Lost on Choking Question:**
> 1. Forgetting the **exact time constants** (10 s, 20 s, 30 s, 60 s) — these are favourite 1-mark questions.
> 2. Confusing **choking** (refusing to *upload*) with throttling download. Choking is purely on the *upload* side; downloads are throttled by the sender's choke state.
> 3. Stating "BitTorrent prevents free-riding completely" — it only *discourages* it. A determined free-rider can still get initial pieces via optimistic unchoke, but cannot sustain high download rates.
> 4. Confusing **bitfield** with **piece map** in your answer — they are the same conceptually, but a 1-mark clarification (bitfield = bit-array, piece map = abstract concept) is sometimes asked.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 1, 14 marks]**

**(a)** Explain **peer-to-peer (P2P) architecture**. Compare it with the **client-server model** in terms of scalability, reliability, and bandwidth utilization. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Describe the **piece distribution mechanism** in BitTorrent. How does BitTorrent ensure **data integrity** and **fairness** among peers? *(7 marks)*

#### **Model Answer for Question B:**

**(a) P2P vs Client-Server (7 marks):**

**Peer-to-Peer Architecture (3 marks):**
- In a P2P network, every node (peer) acts as both a *client* and a *server*. Peers directly exchange data with each other.
- Examples: BitTorrent, Gnutella, Bitcoin.
- Scalability improves with $N$ because aggregate upload bandwidth grows with the swarm.
- Highly fault-tolerant — no single point of failure.

**Client-Server Architecture (2 marks):**
- Dedicated server(s) provide data/services; clients only consume.
- Examples: HTTP web servers, FTP.
- Server bandwidth is a hard bottleneck; reliability depends on server uptime.

**Comparison Table (2 marks):**

| Criterion | Client-Server | P2P (BitTorrent) |
|---|---|---|
| Scalability | Poor (server-bound) | Excellent (swarm-bound) |
| Reliability | Single point of failure | High (no central node) |
| Bandwidth utilization | Server underutilized | Fully utilized |
| Initial deployment cost | High (powerful server) | Low |

**(b) Piece Distribution & Integrity (7 marks):**

**Piece Distribution (3 marks):**
- The file is divided into equal pieces (default 256 KB).
- Each piece is further divided into sub-pieces (16 KB) for request pipelining.
- A peer requests sub-pieces from multiple peers in parallel; the typical pipelined window is 5 outstanding requests.
- On receipt of a complete piece, the peer computes the SHA-1 hash and compares it with the value in the `.torrent` file. **Mismatch → discard and re-request.** *(2 marks for integrity)*
- The **HAVE** message is broadcast to all peers to inform them of the newly acquired piece.

**Fairness (2 marks):**
- Tit-for-tat via the choking algorithm: peers that upload more earn more unchoke slots and thus download more. Free-riders are starved.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Question B:**
> 1. Do not write "BitTorrent has no central server" — it often *does* have a tracker. The correct statement is "BitTorrent has *optional* centralization via the tracker, and the tracker is a **lightweight** server that does **not** transfer file data."
> 2. SHA-1 is the piece integrity check, **not** MD5. Mixing these up is a guaranteed 1-mark loss.
> 3. Sub-piece size (16 KB) and piece size (256 KB) are **defaults** but configurable — say "default" explicitly in your answer.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for BitTorrent (Module 1)**

**Core Concepts:**
- BitTorrent is a **P2P file distribution protocol** — every peer is both a client and a server.
- The file is split into **pieces (256 KB default)**; each piece into **sub-pieces (16 KB default)**.
- The **tracker** is a lightweight server for peer discovery; it does NOT transfer file data.
- The **.torrent file** contains metadata + SHA-1 hashes of all pieces; its `info`-dictionary hash is the **info-hash**.
- The **bitfield** message announces which pieces a peer has (1 bit per piece).
- **HAVE** announces a single newly-acquired piece; **PIECE** delivers a sub-piece of actual data.

**Piece Selection Strategies (all three in use simultaneously):**
- **Random First Piece** — used when a peer has 0 pieces (bootstrap).
- **Rarest First** — used for all subsequent pieces (diversity).
- **Strict Priority** — finish in-progress pieces before starting new ones.
- **Endgame Mode** — broadcast duplicate requests for the very last few pieces.

**Choking Algorithm — Must-Memorize Constants:**
- Top **4** unchoke slots allocated by tit-for-tat.
- Choke evaluation period: **10 seconds**.
- Download rate measured over: **20 seconds**.
- Optimistic unchoke period: **30 seconds** (1 random slot).
- Anti-snub timeout: **60 seconds** → boost optimistic unchoke to every **10 s**.

**Incentive / Fairness:**
- **Tit-for-tat** discourages free-riders.
- **Optimistic unchoke** bootstraps newcomers.
- **Anti-snubbing** rescues stalled peers.

**Performance / Scaling:**
- $T_{cs} \geq NF / u_s$ (client-server lower bound).
- $T_{bt} \geq NF / (u_s + \sum u_i)$ (BitTorrent lower bound).
- Speedup ratio $\eta \approx 1 + N u / u_s$ — scales **linearly** with swarm size.

**Data Integrity:**
- Each piece is verified by **SHA-1** before being accepted.
- Corrupted pieces are silently discarded and re-requested.

**Modern Variants to Know:**
- **Magnet links** — eliminate the need for a separate .torrent file (info-hash only).
- **DHT (Mainline DHT)** — trackerless peer discovery via Kademlia-style distributed hash table.
- **Protocol Encryption (MSE/PE)** — obfuscates BitTorrent traffic to bypass ISP throttling.
- **μTP (Micro Transport Protocol)** — LEDBAT congestion control for low-priority background transfers.

**High-Yield Terms for KTU Viva:**
*"Swarm, seeder, leecher, tracker, DHT, info-hash, bitfield, HAVE, choke, unchoke, tit-for-tat, optimistic unchoke, rarest first, endgame mode, SHA-1, sub-piece, pipelining."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Services
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAMT632 - BASICS OF COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Services

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the **TCP/IP and OSI network architectures**, the term **"Services"** refers to the set of well-defined, standardized operations that each layer provides to the layer directly above it in the protocol stack. A *service* is the formal contractual interface between adjacent layers — the upper layer (service user) requests a service, and the lower layer (service provider) delivers it, typically through **Service Access Points (SAPs)**.

In the **Application Layer (Module 2 focus)**, the term *Services* is more specifically used to describe the user-facing, end-to-end network applications and their supporting protocols that allow humans and software to exchange meaningful data over the Internet. The principal application layer services prescribed in the KTU 2024 syllabus are:

- **WWW (World Wide Web) service** — using **HTTP / HTTPS**
- **File Transfer service** — using **FTP**
- **Electronic Mail (E-mail) service** — using **SMTP, POP3, IMAP**
- **Name Resolution service** — using **DNS**
- **Connection-Oriented vs Connectionless services** at the Transport Layer

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Definition (Board Standard):** *A network service is a collection of primitives (operations) that a lower layer offers to an upper layer so that the upper layer's data can be transported, addressed, and interpreted correctly across heterogeneous networks.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine a **multi-floor hotel**:
- The **top floor (Application)** is where the *guests* (your Web browser, email client) stay.
- The **middle floors (Transport & Network)** are the *service corridors* — they don't know which guest asked for what, they simply deliver trays to the correct room number.
- The **ground floor (Physical/Link)** is the *kitchen and delivery vans* that physically move things.

A **Service** is the "menu" that the kitchen (lower layer) hands to the dining floor (upper layer). The dining floor simply orders from the menu — it never cares *how* the dish is cooked. This is the famous **"Black Box"** view of layering.

For example, when you open `https://www.ktu.edu.in`:
- The browser (Application) calls the **HTTP service** to fetch the page.
- HTTP hands the request down to **TCP** (reliable transport service).
- TCP hands it to **IP** (best-effort delivery service).
- IP hands it to **Ethernet/Wi-Fi** (link service).

The browser only "sees" the **HTTP service** — everything below is invisible to it.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** For Module 2, the KTU 2024 Scheme places maximum weightage on the **four pillars of Internet services**: WWW, FTP, E-Mail, and DNS. Students must know their *purpose, port numbers, underlying transport protocol, and message format*.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Reserved Constants

| Symbol / Constant | Value / Port | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| HTTP | Port **80** | Web service (unsecured) |
| HTTPS | Port **443** | Secure Web service (TLS) |
| FTP Control | Port **21** | File Transfer – commands |
| FTP Data | Port **20** | File Transfer – data channel |
| SMTP | Port **25** | Mail submission (server-to-server) |
| POP3 | Port **110** | Mail retrieval (download) |
| IMAP | Port **143** | Mail retrieval (synchronization) |
| DNS | Port **53** | Name resolution (UDP/TCP) |
| TFTP | Port **69** | Trivial FTP (UDP) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Layered View of Services

Network services are classified along **two orthogonal axes** that KTU examiners repeatedly test:

### Axis 1 — Reliability (by the lower-layer provider)
- **Connection-Oriented Service (CO):** A virtual circuit is established *before* data is exchanged. Includes handshake, sequencing, acknowledgement, flow control, and termination. Example: **TCP**.
- **Connectionless Service (CL):** Each packet (datagram) is routed independently. No handshake, no guarantee. Example: **UDP**.

### Axis 2 — Mode of Addressing
- **Message Service:** Whole message handed to the layer (e.g., Application to Transport).
- **Datagram Service:** Fixed maximum size (e.g., IP packets ≤ 65,535 bytes).
- **Stream Service:** Byte-oriented, no boundaries (e.g., TCP).

## 2.2 The Four Pillars of Application Services (KTU Module 2 Core)

### 2.2.1 World Wide Web (WWW) — HTTP Service
- **Purpose:** Hypertext document retrieval.
- **Architecture:** Client–Server using **Uniform Resource Locators (URLs)**.
- **Underlying transport:** **TCP** (reliable, connection-oriented).
- **Port:** **80** (HTTP) / **443** (HTTPS).
- **Logical model:** Browser ⇄ Proxy ⇄ Origin Server.
- **Message types:**
  - **Request:** `Method URI HTTP/version\r\nHeaders\r\n\r\nBody`
  - **Response:** `HTTP/version Status-Code Reason-Phrase\r\nHeaders\r\n\r\nBody`
- **Statelessness:** Each HTTP transaction is independent; state is maintained via **Cookies**.
- **Persistent vs Non-Persistent:** HTTP/1.0 was non-persistent (one TCP connection per object); HTTP/1.1 introduces **keep-alive** (persistent) by default.
- **HTTPS:** Wraps HTTP inside **TLS/SSL** for confidentiality, integrity, and authentication.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note:** HTTP is a *stateless* protocol. The server forgets the client after every response. Do NOT confuse "connection-oriented TCP" with "stateful HTTP" — they are independent.

### 2.2.2 File Transfer Service — FTP
- **Purpose:** Reliable bulk file transfer between two hosts.
- **Transport:** **TCP** (uses *two* parallel connections).
- **Ports:** Control channel = **21**, Data channel = **20** (active mode).
- **Two-channel design:**
  - **Control Connection (port 21):** persistent; carries user commands (`USER`, `PASS`, `RETR`, `STOR`).
  - **Data Connection (port 20):** transient; opened only when a file is actually transferred.
- **Modes:**
  - **Active Mode:** Server initiates data connection back to client.
  - **Passive Mode:** Client initiates both connections (used behind firewalls/NAT).
- **Commands & Responses:** Text-based, 3-digit reply codes (e.g., `230 Login successful`, `550 File not found`).

### 2.2.3 Electronic Mail Service — SMTP, POP3, IMAP
The e-mail system uses **three distinct sub-protocols** because of its *store-and-forward* nature:

| Stage | Protocol | Direction | Port | Function |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Submit | **SMTP** | UA → Mail Server | **25** | Push mail into server's queue |
| Transfer | **SMTP** | Server ↔ Server | **25** | Relay mail between MTAs |
| Retrieve | **POP3** or **IMAP** | UA ← Mail Server | **110 / 143** | Pull mail from server to client |

- **SMTP (Simple Mail Transfer Protocol):** Push protocol, uses TCP, text-based commands (`HELO`, `MAIL FROM`, `RCPT TO`, `DATA`, `QUIT`).
- **POP3 (Post Office Protocol v3):** Downloads mail to the local client and (typically) deletes from the server. Stateless across sessions.
- **IMAP (Internet Message Access Protocol):** Mail remains on the server; client can create folders, search, partially fetch. State is synchronized between client and server.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often say "SMTP is used to *read* mail." This is wrong. **SMTP pushes, POP/IMAP pull.** Mail retrieval never uses SMTP.

### 2.2.4 Domain Name Service — DNS
- **Purpose:** Translate human-readable names (`www.ktu.edu.in`) into machine-readable IP addresses (`192.168.x.y`).
- **Transport:** Primarily **UDP on port 53**; falls back to TCP for large responses (> 512 bytes) and for **zone transfers** between authoritative servers.
- **Hierarchical namespace:** Root (`.`) → TLD (`.edu`, `.in`, `.com`) → Authoritative (`ktu`) → Host (`www`).
- **Three components:**
  1. **Namespace:** the tree structure of names.
  2. **Name Servers:** servers that hold the database.
  3. **Resolvers:** client-side libraries that query the system.
- **Query types:**
  - **Recursive:** Resolver asks server, server returns final answer.
  - **Iterative:** Server returns the *best* referral, client queries again.
- **Record types (Resource Records):** `A` (IPv4), `AAAA` (IPv6), `MX` (mail exchanger), `NS` (name server), `CNAME` (alias), `TXT` (text), `SOA` (start of authority).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat-Sheet

| Service | Protocol | Transport | Default Port | Connection Type | Mode of Operation |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| WWW | HTTP | TCP | 80 | Connection-oriented | Request-Response, Stateless |
| Secure Web | HTTPS | TCP | 443 | Connection-oriented | Request-Response, Encrypted |
| File Transfer | FTP | TCP | 21 (ctrl), 20 (data) | Connection-oriented | Command-Data, Stateful |
| Mail Send | SMTP | TCP | 25 | Connection-oriented | Push, Text-based |
| Mail Retrieve (basic) | POP3 | TCP | 110 | Connection-oriented | Pull, Download-then-delete |
| Mail Retrieve (advanced) | IMAP | TCP | 143 | Connection-oriented | Pull, Server-side folders |
| Name Resolution | DNS | UDP (TCP fallback) | 53 | Connectionless (mostly) | Query-Reply |
| Trivial File Transfer | TFTP | UDP | 69 | Connectionless | Simple 5-packet read/write |
| Remote Login | Telnet | TCP | 23 | Connection-oriented | Terminal stream |
| Secure Shell | SSH | TCP | 22 | Connection-oriented | Encrypted terminal stream |

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

These services form the **backbone of every modern distributed application**:
- **E-commerce portals** use HTTPS for secure checkout and DNS for load balancing across regional servers.
- **Cloud storage systems** (Google Drive, OneDrive) use variations of FTP-over-TLS (e.g., WebDAV) underneath.
- **Corporate e-mail** uses SMTP for routing and IMAP for synchronized multi-device access.
- **Content Delivery Networks (CDNs)** rely on authoritative DNS to direct users to the *nearest* server.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Service for a Use-Case

For KTU problem-solving, students are often given a scenario and asked: *"Which service/protocol should be used and why?"* The decision can be derived deterministically using the matrix below.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Decision Path} &= f(\text{Reliability}, \text{Size}, \text{Latency}, \text{State}) \\
\text{where} \quad & \text{Reliability} \in \{\text{Required}, \text{Optional}\} \\
& \text{Size} \in \{\text{Small}, \text{Large}\} \\
& \text{Latency} \in \{\text{Critical}, \text{Tolerant}\} \\
& \text{State} \in \{\text{Stateless}, \text{Stateful}\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Decision logic (step-by-step):**

- **Step 1:** If the application *cannot tolerate any packet loss* (e.g., file transfer, e-mail, web page), then Reliability = Required → choose a **TCP-based** service.
- **Step 2:** If the application is *latency-critical* and can tolerate occasional loss (e.g., live video call, VoIP, online gaming), then choose **UDP-based** service (e.g., RTP, DNS).
- **Step 3:** If the data is *name-to-address* lookup, choose **DNS** (UDP port 53).
- **Step 4:** If the user is *submitting mail to a server*, choose **SMTP (port 25, TCP)**.
- **Step 5:** If the user is *reading mail from a server*, choose **POP3 (port 110)** for download-only or **IMAP (port 143)** for synchronized folder access.

## 3.2 Worked Example — DNS Resolution (Derivation of a Typical Query Path)

A user types `http://www.ktu.edu.in` in a browser. Derive the sequence of DNS queries issued to resolve the name.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 0:} \quad & \text{Browser checks its in-memory cache for } \texttt{www.ktu.edu.in}. \\
& \text{If HIT} \rightarrow \text{use cached IP, STOP.} \\
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{If MISS, the OS resolver queries the local DNS server (LDNS) recursively.} \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{LDNS queries a Root Name Server iteratively:} \\
& \text{Query: "Who handles } \texttt{.in} \text{?"} \\
& \text{Reply: IP of } \texttt{.in} \text{ TLD server.} \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{LDNS queries } \texttt{.in} \text{ TLD server iteratively:} \\
& \text{Query: "Who handles } \texttt{ktu.edu.in} \text{?"} \\
& \text{Reply: IP of authoritative server for } \texttt{ktu.edu.in}. \\
\text{Step 4:} \quad & \text{LDNS queries the authoritative server for } \texttt{ktu.edu.in}: \\
& \text{Query: "A record of } \texttt{www.ktu.edu.in} \text{?"} \\
& \text{Reply: } 152.58.x.y \\
\text{Step 5:} \quad & \text{LDNS returns the answer to the OS resolver, which returns it to the browser.} \\
\text{Step 6:} \quad & \text{Browser opens a TCP connection to } 152.58.x.y:80 \text{ and sends an HTTP GET.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Time complexity observation:**
$$
T_{\text{resolution}} = T_{\text{cache}} + T_{\text{LDNS}} + 3 \cdot T_{\text{iterative}} + T_{\text{authoritative}}
$$
where each $T_{\text{iterative}}$ is a single UDP round-trip (RTT) to a geographically distant server. This is why **caching at every level** (browser, OS, LDNS, TLD) is critical for perceived performance.

## 3.3 Worked Example — HTTP Persistent vs Non-Persistent

Suppose a web page references **5 objects** (1 HTML + 4 images). Compute the total time to fetch them under HTTP/1.0 (non-persistent) vs HTTP/1.1 (persistent), assuming one RTT is $R$ and the transmission time of each object is $T$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Non-persistent (HTTP/1.0):} \quad & T_{\text{np}} = 5 \cdot (2R + T) \\
\text{Persistent with pipelining (HTTP/1.1):} \quad & T_{\text{p}} = 2R + 5T \\
\text{Speedup ratio:} \quad & \frac{T_{\text{np}}}{T_{\text{p}}} = \frac{5(2R+T)}{2R+5T} \xrightarrow{R \gg T} \frac{5 \cdot 2R}{2R} = 5
\end{aligned}
$$

When RTT dominates transmission time, **persistent HTTP gives a 5× speedup** for 5 objects — a favourite KTU numerical question.

## 3.4 Worked Example — Mail Flow Derivation

When user Alice (`alice@ktu.edu.in`) sends a mail to user Bob (`bob@gmail.com`):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{Alice's UA connects to } \texttt{ktu.edu.in} \text{ SMTP server on port 25 (TCP).} \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Alice's UA issues: HELO, MAIL FROM:}\textless\textit{alice@ktu.edu.in}\textgreater, \\
& \text{RCPT TO:}\textless\textit{bob@gmail.com}\textgreater, DATA, [message body], \texttt{.} \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{ktu.edu.in SMTP server looks up MX record for } \texttt{gmail.com} \\
& \text{via DNS and obtains the IP of } \texttt{gmail.com} \text{ SMTP gateway.} \\
\text{Step 4:} \quad & \text{ktu.edu.in SMTP server opens a TCP connection to } \texttt{gmail.com:25} \\
& \text{and relays the message.} \\
\text{Step 5:} \quad & \texttt{gmail.com} \text{ SMTP gateway stores the message in Bob's mailbox.} \\
\text{Step 6:} \quad & \text{Bob's UA connects to } \texttt{gmail.com} \text{ on port } \texttt{143 (IMAP)} \\
& \text{or } \texttt{110 (POP3)} \text{ to retrieve the message.}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note the symmetry of the deliverable:** SMTP is used *twice* (UA→server, server→server). The final retrieval is *never* SMTP.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Layered Service Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    appUser["Application User / Browser / Mail Client"]:::user
    appLayer["Application Layer Services HTTP FTP SMTP DNS IMAP POP3"]:::app
    tLayer["Transport Layer Services TCP Connection Oriented UDP Connectionless"]:::trans
    nLayer["Network Layer Service IP Best Effort Datagram Delivery"]:::net
    dLayer["Data Link and Physical Layer Service Frame Transmission over Medium"]:::link

    appUser -->|issues request| appLayer
    appLayer -->|uses SAP to port 80 25 21 53 110 143| tLayer
    tLayer -->|encapsulates into IP packets| nLayer
    nLayer -->|maps to MAC and transmits bits| dLayer

    classDef user fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef app fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef trans fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef net fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef link fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.2 HTTP Request–Response Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as Browser Client
    participant P as Proxy Optional
    participant S as Origin Server

    C->>S: TCP SYN to port 80
    S-->>C: TCP SYN ACK
    C->>S: TCP ACK
    Note over C,S: TCP Connection Established
    C->>S: HTTP GET /index.html HTTP 1.1
    S-->>C: HTTP 1.1 200 OK followed by HTML body
    C->>S: HTTP GET /logo.png HTTP 1.1
    S-->>C: HTTP 1.1 200 OK image bytes
    C->>S: TCP FIN
    S-->>C: TCP FIN ACK
    Note over C,S: Connection Closed
```

## 4.3 FTP Two-Channel Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    cFTP["FTP Client"]:::client
    sFTP["FTP Server"]:::server
    ctrl["Control Channel TCP Port 21 Persistent USER PASS RETR STOR"]:::ctrl
    data["Data Channel TCP Port 20 Transient File bytes"]:::data

    cFTP <-->|Commands and Replies| ctrl
    ctrl -.->|Triggers opening of| data
    data <-->|Actual file transfer| cFTP
    sFTP --- ctrl
    sFTP --- data

    classDef client fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef server fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ctrl fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef data fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.4 E-Mail Service Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    aliceUA["Alice User Agent Outlook"]:::ua
    smtpA["ktu.edu.in SMTP Server MTA"]:::mta
    dns["DNS Server MX Lookup"]:::dns
    smtpG["gmail.com SMTP Server MTA"]:::mta
    bobUA["Bob User Agent Gmail Web"]:::ua

    aliceUA -->|SMTP port 25| smtpA
    smtpA -->|Query MX gmail.com| dns
    dns -->|Returns IP of gmail MX| smtpA
    smtpA -->|SMTP port 25| smtpG
    smtpG -->|Stores in Bob mailbox| smtpG
    bobUA -->|IMAP port 143 or POP3 port 110| smtpG

    classDef ua fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef mta fill:#B0C4DE,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef dns fill:#D8BFD8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.5 DNS Hierarchical Query Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    root["Root Name Server"]:::root
    tldin[".in TLD Name Server"]:::tld
    auth["Authoritative Server ktu edu in"]:::auth
    resolver["Local DNS Resolver LDNS"]:::res
    client["Client Browser"]:::cli

    client -->|1 Recursive Query| resolver
    resolver -->|2 Iterative Query| root
    root -->|3 Referral to .in TLD| resolver
    resolver -->|4 Iterative Query| tldin
    tldin -->|5 Referral to ktu edu in auth| resolver
    resolver -->|6 Iterative Query| auth
    auth -->|7 Final A Record 152 58 x y| resolver
    resolver -->|8 Answer cached and returned| client

    classDef root fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    classDef tld fill:#FFA500,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef auth fill:#9ACD32,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef res fill:#40E0D0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef cli fill:#87CEFA,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```
