topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAMT632 - BASICS OF COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Unicast Routing

# Unicast Routing — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
> **Unicast Routing** is the process of forwarding a data packet from a **single source node** to a **single destination node** along an optimal path through one or more interconnected networks. It forms the foundation of traditional host-to-host communication in the Internet and is governed by routing protocols operating at the **Network Layer (Layer 3)** of the OSI/TCP-IP model.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, unicast routing is studied under two algorithmic families:
1. **Distance Vector Routing (DVR)** — e.g., **Routing Information Protocol (RIP)** based on the **Bellman–Ford Algorithm**.
2. **Link State Routing (LSR)** — e.g., **Open Shortest Path First (OSPF)** based on **Dijkstra's Algorithm**.

A third class, **Path Vector Routing**, used by **BGP**, is also touched upon for completeness.

---

## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Kerala Road Trip Planner":**
> Imagine you are driving a car from **Thiruvananthapuram (TVM)** to **Kannur**. You do not know the entire map. You ask a friend in **Kochi** about the best road to **Kozhikode**, and your friend in turn has asked someone in **Thrissur**, and so on. Each person only knows the *distance to their immediate neighbors*. By sharing these local distance tables, eventually everyone figures out the *shortest* road to every city.

This is exactly how **Distance Vector Routing** works:
- Each router maintains a **table (vector)** of distances to *every* destination.
- Routers **periodically exchange** these tables **only with their directly connected neighbors**.
- Over time, through iteration, every router "learns" the topology — this is called **routing by rumor**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Link State Routing Analogy:**
> Now imagine that the *Government of Kerala* publishes a **complete, up-to-date map** of every road and its condition to *every* city. Each town then independently computes the *best* route to every other town using the **same map**. This is **Link State Routing** — every node has the **full topology** and runs **Dijkstra's algorithm** locally.

---

## Key Terminology at a Glance

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Hop Count** | Number of routers a packet must traverse to reach the destination. |
| **Metric / Cost** | A numerical value assigned to a link (lower = better). |
| **Routing Table** | A database maintained by each router mapping destination → next-hop. |
| **Convergence** | The state when all routers agree on the network topology. |
| **Autonomous System (AS)** | A collection of networks/routers under a single administrative domain. |
| **IGP vs EGP** | Interior Gateway Protocol (intra-AS) vs Exterior Gateway Protocol (inter-AS). |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick Recall:** A *unicast* packet has **one destination IP address** in the destination field — this is the dominant traffic type (≈ 90 %) on today's Internet.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Unicast Routing Protocols

Unicast routing protocols are classified along **two orthogonal axes**:

### Axis 1: Design Philosophy
- **Distance Vector (DV):** Each router tells its neighbors what *it* thinks the world looks like.
- **Link State (LS):** Each router tells *the whole world* what *its immediate links* look like.
- **Path Vector (PV):** Each router advertises the *entire path* (AS sequence) to a destination (used in **BGP**).

### Axis 2: Operational Domain
- **Interior Gateway Protocols (IGP):** Operate *within* an Autonomous System — e.g., **RIP, OSPF**.
- **Exterior Gateway Protocols (EGP):** Operate *between* Autonomous Systems — e.g., **BGP**.

---

## 2.2 Distance Vector Routing — Operational Steps

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bellman–Ford Equation (Core of DVR):**
> For each destination *x* and each neighbor *v* of node *u*:
> $$D_u(x) = \min_v \bigl[ C(u,v) + D_v(x) \bigr]$$
> where $C(u,v)$ is the cost of the link $u \to v$ and $D_v(x)$ is the *distance* advertised by neighbor $v$ to $x$.

### Working Cycle (Repeated Until Convergence)
1. **Initialization:** Each router sets distance to itself = 0, to direct neighbors = link cost, to all others = $\infty$.
2. **Sharing:** Every $T$ seconds, each router sends its entire distance vector to **all directly connected neighbors**.
3. **Update:** On receiving a neighbor's vector, the router recomputes its own distances using the Bellman–Ford equation.
4. **Triggered Updates:** If a link fails, the router immediately broadcasts a new vector (infinity for the failed link) instead of waiting for the timer.
5. **Convergence Detection:** Stop when no router's table changes in a full update cycle.

### Strengths & Weaknesses

| Aspect | Distance Vector (RIP) | Link State (OSPF) |
|---|---|---|
| Complexity | **Low** — simple to implement | **High** — Dijkstra + flooding |
| Memory | Small (vector only) | Large (full topology) |
| Bandwidth | Low (only neighbors) | High (initial flood) |
| Convergence Speed | **Slow** — count-to-infinity problem | **Fast** — hierarchical |
| Scalability | Poor (max 15 hops in RIP) | Good (area-based OSPF) |
| Loop Prone? | **Yes** | No |

---

## 2.3 Link State Routing — Operational Steps

### The Five-Stage Process
1. **Discover Neighbors:** Send **HELLO** packets on each point-to-point link.
2. **Measure Link Cost:** Each router measures the round-trip time or assigned cost to each neighbor.
3. **Construct Link State Packet (LSP):** Contains router ID, neighbor IDs, link costs, sequence number, age.
4. **Flood the LSP:** LSP is sent to *all* routers using controlled flooding — each router forwards it once.
5. **Compute Shortest Path:** Run **Dijkstra's algorithm** on the accumulated link-state database.

### Dijkstra's Algorithm (Pseudocode Logic)

> **Input:** Graph $G(V, E)$ with non-negative edge costs, source node $s$.
> **Output:** Shortest distance $D[v]$ and predecessor $\pi[v]$ for every $v \in V$.

```
1.  INITIALIZE-SINGLE-SOURCE(G, s)
       for each v in V:
           D[v]  = ∞
           π[v]  = NIL
       D[s]   = 0

2.  S = ∅
    Q = MIN-PRIORITY-QUEUE(V)   // keyed by D[v]

3.  while Q ≠ ∅:
       u = EXTRACT-MIN(Q)        // pick node with smallest tentative distance
       S = S ∪ {u}
       for each neighbor v of u:
           RELAX(u, v, w)
              if D[v] > D[u] + w(u, v):
                 D[v] = D[u] + w(u, v)
                 π[v] = u
                 DECREASE-KEY(Q, v, D[v])
```

> [!NOTE]
> The algorithm terminates when $|S| = \vert V \vert$. Time complexity (with binary heap) = $O((E + V) \log V)$.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use `\vert` instead of the pipe symbol `|` inside table cells to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Bellman–Ford (DV) update | $D_u(x) = \min_v [ C(u,v) + D_v(x) ]$ | Hop count or cost |
| 2 | Dijkstra relaxation | $D[v] > D[u] + w(u,v) \Rightarrow D[v] \leftarrow D[u] + w(u,v)$ | Used inside main loop |
| 3 | RIP hop-count metric | $\text{Hops} \in \{0, 1, 2, \dots, 15\}$; **16 = infinity** | Max diameter = **15 hops** |
| 4 | RIP update timer | **30 seconds** full table exchange | Default in Cisco/Unix |
| 5 | OSPF cost formula (Cisco) | $\text{Cost} = \dfrac{\text{Reference BW}}{\text{Link BW}}$ | Reference BW = **100 Mbps** default |
| 6 | OSPF HELLO interval | **10 s** (broadcast) / **30 s** (NBMA) | Dead = 4 × Hello |
| 7 | Triggered update delay | Random in $[1, 5]$ seconds | Prevents storms |
| 8 | Split Horizon | "Don't advertise a route back to the neighbor you learned it from" | Loop avoidance |
| 9 | Poison Reverse | Advertise learned route with metric **16** (in RIP) | Aggressive loop break |
| 10 | Hold-Down Timer | **180 s** (default in RIP) | Router ignores updates for the route |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility:**
> - **DV (RIP):** Used in small office/home networks — simplicity wins.
> - **LS (OSPF):** Used in enterprise/ISP backbones — speed and hierarchy matter.
> - **Path Vector (BGP):** The "glue" of the Internet — inter-AS policy routing.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example — Distance Vector Routing (Bellman–Ford)

Consider the following 4-router topology (a classic KTU textbook problem):

```
            (1)
       A ------- B
       |       /  \
      (3)    (1)   (2)
       |   /       |
       C -------- D
            (2)
```

Edge costs: $c(A,B)=1,\ c(A,C)=3,\ c(B,C)=1,\ c(B,D)=2,\ c(C,D)=2$.

### Initial Distance Tables (t = 0)

Each router knows only its immediate neighbors. The "—" entries are unreachable directly.

| From \ To | A | B | C | D |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | 0 | 1 | 3 | — |
| **B** | 1 | 0 | 1 | 2 |
| **C** | 3 | 1 | 0 | 2 |
| **D** | — | 2 | 2 | 0 |

### Iteration 1 (t = 1) — Update Using Bellman–Ford

**For Router A:** Examine each neighbor $v \in \{B, C\}$:

$$
D_A(B) = \min\bigl[ c(A,B) + D_B(B),\ c(A,C) + D_C(B) \bigr]
$$

$$
D_A(B) = \min\bigl[ 1 + 0,\ 3 + 1 \bigr] = \min[1,\ 4] = 1
$$

$$
D_A(C) = \min\bigl[ 1 + 1,\ 3 + 0 \bigr] = \min[2,\ 3] = 2
$$

$$
D_A(D) = \min\bigl[ 1 + 2,\ 3 + 2 \bigr] = \min[3,\ 5] = 3
$$

**For Router B:**

$$
D_B(A) = \min\bigl[ 1 + 0,\ 1 + 3,\ 2 + \text{—} \bigr] = \min[1, 4, \text{—}] = 1
$$

$$
D_B(C) = \min\bigl[ 1 + 3,\ 1 + 0,\ 2 + 2 \bigr] = \min[4,\ 1,\ 4] = 1
$$

$$
D_B(D) = \min\bigl[ 1 + \text{—},\ 1 + 2,\ 2 + 0 \bigr] = \min[\text{—},\ 3,\ 2] = 2
$$

**For Router C:**

$$
D_C(A) = \min\bigl[ 3 + 0,\ 1 + 1 \bigr] = \min[3, 2] = 2
$$

$$
D_C(B) = \min\bigl[ 3 + 1,\ 1 + 0 \bigr] = \min[4, 1] = 1
$$

$$
D_C(D) = \min\bigl[ 3 + \text{—},\ 1 + 2,\ 2 + 0 \bigr] = \min[\text{—},\ 3,\ 2] = 2
$$

**For Router D:**

$$
D_D(A) = \min\bigl[ 2 + 1,\ 2 + 3 \bigr] = \min[3,\ 5] = 3
$$

$$
D_D(B) = \min\bigl[ 2 + 0,\ 2 + 1 \bigr] = \min[2,\ 3] = 2
$$

$$
D_D(C) = \min\bigl[ 2 + 1,\ 2 + 0 \bigr] = \min[3,\ 2] = 2
$$

### Updated Tables at t = 1

| From \ To | A | B | C | D |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | 0 | 1 | **2** | **3** |
| **B** | 1 | 0 | 1 | 2 |
| **C** | **2** | 1 | 0 | 2 |
| **D** | **3** | 2 | 2 | 0 |

Notice that A & C already learned the **shortest** paths to all destinations in **one** iteration. However, in pathological topologies, DVR can take up to $O(\vert V \vert)$ iterations to converge.

### Final Routing Tables (After Full Convergence)

| Router | Destination | Next-Hop | Cost |
|---|---|---|---|
| A | B | B (direct) | 1 |
| A | C | B | 2 |
| A | D | B | 3 |
| B | A | A (direct) | 1 |
| B | C | C (direct) | 1 |
| B | D | D (direct) | 2 |
| C | A | B | 2 |
| C | B | B (direct) | 1 |
| C | D | D (direct) | 2 |
| D | A | B | 3 |
| D | B | B (direct) | 2 |
| D | C | C (direct) | 2 |

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example — Link State Routing (Dijkstra)

Using the **same** topology, let's trace Dijkstra's algorithm from source node **A**.

### Initialization

- $D[A]=0$, all others $=\infty$.
- $S = \emptyset$, $Q = \{A, B, C, D\}$.
- Predecessors $\pi[\cdot] = \text{NIL}$ for all.

### Iteration 1

- Extract-min from $Q$: **A** (D=0).
- Add A to $S$: $S = \{A\}$.
- **Relax** A's neighbors:
  - B: $D[B] = \min(\infty,\ 0 + 1) = 1$, $\pi[B] = A$.
  - C: $D[C] = \min(\infty,\ 0 + 3) = 3$, $\pi[C] = A$.

### Iteration 2

- Extract-min: **B** (D=1).
- $S = \{A, B\}$.
- **Relax** B's neighbors:
  - C: $D[C] = \min(3,\ 1 + 1) = 2$, $\pi[C] = B$. (Updated!)
  - D: $D[D] = \min(\infty,\ 1 + 2) = 3$, $\pi[D] = B$.

### Iteration 3

- Extract-min: **C** (D=2).
- $S = \{A, B, C\}$.
- **Relax** C's neighbors:
  - D: $D[D] = \min(3,\ 2 + 2) = 3$. (No update — equal)

### Iteration 4

- Extract-min: **D** (D=3).
- $S = \{A, B, C, D\}$.
- $Q$ empty → **terminate**.

### Final Shortest-Path Tree from A

| Destination | Shortest Distance | Path |
|---|---|---|
| A | 0 | A |
| B | 1 | A → B |
| C | 2 | A → B → C |
| D | 3 | A → B → D |

> [!NOTE]
> The shortest-path tree matches the distance-vector result — as it must, since the algorithms are mathematically equivalent for non-negative graphs.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Count-to-Infinity Problem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Count-to-Infinity (CTI) Problem** is a *classic* DVR failure mode. KTU expects you to **explain with a 3-router diagram**.

Suppose routers A, B, C are in a line: **A — B — C**, with link costs all equal to 1. Initially, A believes distance to C is 2 (via B).

**Step 1:** Link B–C fails. B updates its distance to C = 16 (infinity in RIP). However, *before* B can send the update, A sends its vector to B advertising "C is at distance 2".

**Step 2:** B sees A's advertisement and reasons: "A must be reaching C in 2 hops via me. But since my link to C is dead, I must be reaching C *through A* with an extra hop." So B updates $D_B(C) = 2 + 1 = 3$.

**Step 3:** A receives B's new "3" and updates $D_A(C) = 3 + 1 = 4$.

**Step 4:** B receives "4" → updates to $D_B(C) = 5$.

This continues incrementally: 5, 6, 7, ..., 16, 17 (clamped to 16 in RIP). Hence the name.

### Mitigation Techniques (KTU High-Yield)

| Technique | Working Principle | Effectiveness |
|---|---|---|
| **Infinity Cap** | Set a small max metric (e.g., **16 in RIP**) | Bounds CTI, doesn't eliminate it |
| **Split Horizon** | Never advertise route back to the source of information | Breaks simple 2-node loops |
| **Poison Reverse** | Advertise learned route with metric = **$\infty$** | Breaks most loops but not all |
| **Hold-Down Timers** | Ignore updates about a flapping route for 180 s | Slows convergence, may use stale info |
| **Triggered Updates** | Immediately broadcast metric changes | Reduces window of inconsistency |

> [!TIP]
> **Best engineering solution:** *Use a Link State Protocol (OSPF) instead — Dijkstra's algorithm computes shortest paths locally on a globally consistent database, so CTI cannot occur.*
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Routing Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef src fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#fff,color:#fff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef dst fill:#7f0000,stroke:#fff,color:#fff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef rtr fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#fff,color:#fff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef proto fill:#f9a825,stroke:#333,color:#000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef alg fill:#6a1b9a,stroke:#fff,color:#fff,stroke-width:2px

    srcHost["SOURCE HOST"]:::src
    dstHost["DESTINATION HOST"]:::dst

    r1["ROUTER R1"]:::rtr
    r2["ROUTER R2"]:::rtr
    r3["ROUTER R3"]:::rtr
    r4["ROUTER R4"]:::rtr

    rip["RIP DISTANCE VECTOR"]:::proto
    ospf["OSPF LINK STATE"]:::proto
    bgp["BGP PATH VECTOR"]:::proto

    bf["BELLMAN FORD ALGORITHM"]:::alg
    dk["DIJKSTRA ALGORITHM"]:::alg

    srcHost --> r1
    r1 --> r2
    r2 --> r3
    r3 --> r4
    r4 --> dstHost

    rip --> bf
    ospf --> dk

    r1 -. uses .-> rip
    r2 -. uses .-> ospf
    r4 -. uses .-> bgp
```

---

## 4.2 Distance Vector Update Flow (RIP Cycle)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant A as Router A
    participant B as Router B
    participant C as Router C

    Note over A,C: INITIALIZATION (t = 0)
    A->>A: D[A]=0, D[neighbors]=cost, rest=infinity
    B->>B: D[B]=0, D[neighbors]=cost, rest=infinity
    C->>C: D[C]=0, D[neighbors]=cost, rest=infinity

    Note over A,C: PERIODIC EXCHANGE (every 30 s)
    A->>B: Send Distance Vector of A
    B->>A: Send Distance Vector of B
    B->>C: Send Distance Vector of B
    C->>B: Send Distance Vector of C

    Note over A,C: LOCAL UPDATE via Bellman-Ford
    A->>A: D_new[x] = min over v of [C(A,v) + D_v(x)]
    B->>B: D_new[x] = min over v of [C(B,v) + D_v(x)]
    C->>C: D_new[x] = min over v of [C(C,v) + D_v(x)]

    Note over A,C: CONVERGENCE CHECK
    A->>A: Any change? If NO, steady state
    B->>B: Any change? If NO, steady state
    C->>C: Any change? If NO, steady state
```

---

## 4.3 Link State Flooding & Dijkstra Computation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef lsa fill:#0277bd,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef db fill:#558b2f,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef spf fill:#ef6c00,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef rt fill:#4a148c,stroke:#fff,color:#fff

    s1["STEP 1: HELLO NEIGHBORS"]:::lsa
    s2["STEP 2: MEASURE LINK COSTS"]:::lsa
    s3["STEP 3: BUILD LSP PACKETS"]:::lsa
    s4["STEP 4: FLOOD LSPs TO ALL ROUTERS"]:::lsa
    s5["STEP 5: BUILD TOPOLOGY DATABASE"]:::db
    s6["STEP 6: RUN DIJKSTRA ON LOCAL DB"]:::spf
    s7["STEP 7: POPULATE ROUTING TABLE"]:::rt

    s1 --> s2 --> s3 --> s4 --> s5 --> s6 --> s7
```

---

## 4.4 Count-to-Infinity Scenario (Block Functional View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef ok fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef warn fill:#ef6c00,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef bad fill:#c62828,stroke:#fff,color:#fff

    t0["STATE 0: A->B->C, D_A(C)=2, D_B(C)=1"]:::ok
    t1["STATE 1: Link B-C fails. B sets D_B(C)=INF"]:::bad
    t2["STATE 2: A sends stale D_A(C)=2 to B"]:::warn
    t3["STATE 3: B updates D_B(C)=3 via A"]:::warn
    t4["STATE 4: A updates D_A(C)=4 via B"]:::bad
    t5["STATE 5: ITERATE - 5,6,7,... up to infinity"]:::bad
    t6["STATE 6: HOLD-DOWN or SPLIT-HORIZON breaks loop"]:::ok

    t0 --> t1 --> t2 --> t3 --> t4 --> t5 --> t6
```

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Q1. Define Unicast Routing. Differentiate between unicast, multicast, and broadcast communication.
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | Module 2 | CO2]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Unicast Routing (1 mark):** A one-to-one communication model where a single source host sends packets to a single destination host identified by a unique IP address. The network layer is responsible for selecting the best path using routing protocols like RIP/OSPF.
- **Differences (2 marks):**
  - **Unicast:** 1 sender → 1 receiver (e.g., HTTP, email).
  - **Multicast:** 1 sender → group of receivers who have joined a multicast group (uses Class D addresses 224.0.0.0 – 239.255.255.255; protocols: IGMP, PIM).
  - **Broadcast:** 1 sender → *all* hosts in the subnet (uses limited broadcast 255.255.255.255 or directed broadcast on a subnet).

---

### Q2. What is a routing table? List any four fields typically present in a routing table.
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 | Module 2 | CO2]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- A **routing table** is a data file stored in a router's memory that maintains a mapping of destination networks to the next-hop IP address and outgoing interface. (1 mark)
- **Four key fields:** (2 marks)
  1. **Destination Network / Subnet Mask** — identifies the target network.
  2. **Next Hop** — IP address of the next router to forward the packet to.
  3. **Outgoing Interface** — local interface through which the packet leaves the router.
  4. **Metric / Cost** — administrative distance or routing metric (lower = preferred).

---

## Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to show initial tables** in DVR problems → lose 2 marks.
> 2. **Not stating the algorithm's name** (Bellman–Ford vs Dijkstra) → lose 1 mark.
> 3. **Skipping the "infinity" handling** in CTI problems → lose 2 marks.
> 4. **Confusing RIP's 16-hop infinity** with OSPF's cost formula → lose 1 mark.
> 5. **Not drawing the converged shortest-path tree** in LSR problems → lose 2 marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**Q A. (a)** Explain the Distance Vector Routing algorithm. With a suitable example, show how the routing tables converge using the Bellman–Ford equation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Concept (2 marks):**
Distance Vector Routing (DVR) is a decentralized routing algorithm in which each router maintains a table (called a *distance vector*) giving the best known distance to every destination and the next-hop to use. Routers periodically exchange these vectors with their **directly connected neighbors only**. The algorithm is based on the **Bellman–Ford equation**:

$$D_u(x) = \min_{v \in N(u)} \bigl[ C(u,v) + D_v(x) \bigr]$$

where $D_u(x)$ is the distance from router $u$ to destination $x$, $N(u)$ is the set of neighbors of $u$, and $C(u,v)$ is the cost of link $u \to v$.

**2. Algorithm Steps (2 marks):**
- **Step 1 – Initialize:** Each router sets $D(\text{self}) = 0$, $D(\text{direct-neighbor}) = \text{link cost}$, and $D(\text{others}) = \infty$.
- **Step 2 – Share:** Every 30 seconds (in RIP), each router sends its full distance vector to its neighbors.
- **Step 3 – Update:** On receiving a neighbor's vector, the router recomputes its own distances using the Bellman–Ford equation. If any entry changes, the new vector is queued for the next share.
- **Step 4 – Iterate:** Repeat Steps 2–3 until no table changes occur (steady state).

**3. Worked Example (3 marks):** Consider the 4-router topology from Section 3.1. Show the initial tables (t=0), the iteration at t=1, and the final converged tables. *(Refer to the derivation in Section 3.1 for the complete table; the final tables should be reproduced here in the answer script.)*

After convergence, the **shortest-path tree** from A is:
- A → B: cost 1 (direct)
- A → C: cost 2 (via B)
- A → D: cost 3 (via B)

**Q A. (b)** What is the count-to-infinity problem in DVR? Explain any two techniques to mitigate it. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Definition (2 marks):**
The **count-to-infinity (CTI) problem** occurs when a link fails and incorrect routing information propagates slowly through the network, causing routers to repeatedly update their distance to a destination in increments of 1 (or the link cost) until the metric reaches the predefined **infinity** value. In RIP, infinity = **16**. The slow convergence wastes bandwidth and may cause routing loops and packet loss.

**2. Three-Node Scenario (3 marks):**
Topology: A — B — C (costs = 1). Initially $D_A(C) = 2$, $D_B(C) = 1$.
- Link B–C fails → $D_B(C) = 16$ (infinity).
- Before B propagates, A sends $D_A(C) = 2$ to B.
- B updates $D_B(C) = D_A(C) + 1 = 3$ (incorrectly thinking "A can reach C in 3 hops via me").
- A receives B's "3" → updates $D_A(C) = 4$.
- Sequence: 4, 5, 6, ... , 16. This is the *count-to-infinity*.

**3. Mitigation Techniques (2 marks):**
- **Split Horizon:** "Never advertise a route back through the interface from which it was learned." This prevents the 2-node loop immediately.
- **Poison Reverse:** An aggressive variant where the route is advertised with metric = 16 (in RIP), explicitly marking it as unreachable.
- **Hold-Down Timers:** Once a route goes down, the router ignores updates for that destination for 180 seconds.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**Q B. (a)** Explain Link State Routing with the help of Dijkstra's algorithm. Trace the algorithm on the graph given below and construct the shortest-path tree from source **S**. **(7 marks)**

> **Graph:** $V = \{S, A, B, C, D\}$.
> Edge costs: $S\text{-}A = 4$, $S\text{-}B = 2$, $A\text{-}B = 1$, $A\text{-}C = 5$, $B\text{-}C = 8$, $B\text{-}D = 10$, $C\text{-}D = 2$, $A\text{-}D = 6$.

**Model Solution:**

**1. Link State Routing Concept (2 marks):**
In Link State Routing, every router constructs a complete map of the network topology by:
(i) discovering its neighbors via **HELLO packets**,
(ii) measuring link costs,
(iii) building **Link State Packets (LSPs)** and **flooding** them to all routers,
(iv) running **Dijkstra's algorithm** locally on the accumulated link-state database.

**2. Dijkstra's Algorithm (2 marks):**
*(State the algorithm steps and Bellman-Ford comparison as in Section 2.3.)*

**3. Trace from S (3 marks):**

| Step | Visited Set $S$ | D[S] | D[A] | D[B] | D[C] | D[D] | Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Init | $\emptyset$ | 0 | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | — |
| 1 | $\{S\}$ | 0 | **4** | **2** | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | Relax S–A, S–B |
| 2 | $\{S, B\}$ | 0 | **3** | 2 | **10** | **12** | Relax B–A (=2+1=3 < 4 ✓), B–C (=10), B–D (=12) |
| 3 | $\{S, B, A\}$ | 0 | 3 | 2 | **8** | **9** | Relax A–C (=3+5=8 < 10 ✓), A–D (=3+6=9 < 12 ✓) |
| 4 | $\{S, B, A, C\}$ | 0 | 3 | 2 | 8 | **8** | Relax C–D (=8+2=10, no update; C–B already done) — actually 8+2=10 > 9, no update |
| 5 | $\{S, B, A, C, D\}$ | 0 | 3 | 2 | 8 | 8 | Done |

**Final Shortest Path Tree from S:**
- S → B: cost 2
- S → A: cost 3 (via B)
- S → C: cost 8 (via B → A)
- S → D: cost 9 (via B → A)

> *(Note: in some iterations, C–D relaxation may yield 10; the correct minimum is 9 via A.)*

**Q B. (b)** Compare Distance Vector and Link State Routing protocols on at least six parameters. Explain why OSPF is preferred over RIP in large enterprise networks. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Parameter | Distance Vector (RIP) | Link State (OSPF) |
|---|---|---|
| **Algorithm Base** | Bellman–Ford | Dijkstra |
| **Information Shared** | Whole distance vector with neighbors only | LSPs flooded to all routers |
| **Topology Knowledge** | Local (neighbor only) | Global (full topology DB) |
| **Convergence Speed** | Slow (CTI problem) | Fast |
| **Bandwidth Use** | Low (small periodic updates) | High initially, low later |
| **CPU & Memory** | Low | High (DB + SPF tree) |
| **Scalability** | Poor (max 15 hops) | Excellent (hierarchical areas) |
| **Loop Formation** | Possible | Not possible |
| **Example Protocols** | RIP, IGRP | OSPF, IS-IS |

**2. Why OSPF over RIP in Enterprises (3 marks):**
- **Faster convergence:** OSPF converges within seconds using LSP flooding + Dijkstra; RIP can take minutes due to periodic 30-second updates and the CTI problem.
- **Hierarchical design:** OSPF supports a two-level hierarchy (Backbone Area 0 + non-backbone areas), reducing routing table size and LSA flood scope.
- **No hop-count limit:** OSPF uses cost based on link bandwidth, not hops — appropriate for high-speed enterprise backbones (1 Gbps, 10 Gbps).
- **Loop-free:** Since each router computes shortest paths on a globally consistent LSDB using Dijkstra, no routing loops occur.
- **VLSM & CIDR support:** OSPF carries subnet mask information in its LSAs, unlike RIPv1 (which is classful).
- **Authentication:** OSPF supports MD5/SHA authentication on LSPs for security.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Unicast Routing**

- ⭐ **Unicast = 1-to-1** (single destination IP). Dominant Internet traffic type.
- ⭐ **Two main algorithm families:** Distance Vector (Bellman–Ford) and Link State (Dijkstra).
- ⭐ **RIP uses DV:** max **15 hops**, **16 = infinity**, update every **30 s**, port **UDP 520**.
- ⭐ **OSPF uses LS:** cost-based metric, hierarchical areas, port **IP 89**, converges in seconds.
- ⭐ **Bellman–Ford equation:** $D_u(x) = \min_v [C(u,v) + D_v(x)]$ — memorize and apply.
- ⭐ **Dijkstra relaxation:** Update $D[v] = D[u] + w(u,v)$ only if strictly less.
- ⭐ **Count-to-infinity** is the *classic DVR failure*; mitigated by **Split Horizon + Poison Reverse + Hold-Down**.
- ⭐ **OSPF is preferred over RIP** in enterprises for: speed, scalability, no loops, VLSM support, authentication.
- ⭐ **BGP (Path Vector)** is the inter-AS protocol — it advertises the *sequence of AS numbers* to each destination.
- ⭐ **Triggered updates** (RIP) accelerate convergence by sending a vector immediately on metric change.
- ⭐ **Hierarchical routing** (areas in OSPF) reduces memory & bandwidth at the cost of optimality across areas.
- ⭐ **Routing tables always include:** Destination, Mask, Next Hop, Interface, Metric.
- ⭐ **Convergence = steady state** where no router's table changes across an update cycle.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAMT632 - BASICS OF COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Multicast Routing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2: Multicast Routing – Foundational Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Multicast Routing** is a network-layer communication paradigm in which a single source host (or a small group of sources) transmits a single stream of IP datagrams to a **dynamically defined, geographically distributed subset of interested receivers** (a multicast group), such that only **one copy** of the packet traverses any given physical link, even when the packet must be delivered to multiple recipients.

A host signals its interest in receiving traffic destined for a specific multicast group by joining the corresponding **IP Class D address** (range: **224.0.0.0 – 239.255.255.255**). The network infrastructure—specifically multicast-aware routers—then collaborate to construct an efficient **distribution tree** that branches only where necessary, replicating the packet just before the path splits.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU 2024 Definition:**
> *Multicast is a one-to-many (or many-to-many) delivery model. The sender transmits a single packet; the network replicates the packet only at branching routers in the multicast distribution tree, conserving both sender-side bandwidth and backbone link capacity.*

### 1.2 Communication Taxonomy in IP Networks

To anchor the concept firmly in the KTU 2024 syllabus, the three fundamental communication paradigms must be distinguished:

| Communication Type | Recipients | Number of Packets on Source Link | Bandwidth Efficiency |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Unicast** | Exactly one specific host | One packet per receiver (N packets) | Poor for large N |
| **Broadcast** | All hosts on the local subnet | One packet; flooded locally | Wastes bandwidth outside the LAN |
| **Multicast** | Only subscribed group members | One packet, regardless of group size | Optimal for selective distribution |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy – The YouTube Live Stream

Imagine a teacher delivering a live lecture to a classroom:

- **Unicast analogy:** The teacher calls each student one by one on the phone, repeating the entire lecture 60 times. This is exhausting and inefficient.
- **Broadcast analogy:** The teacher shouts the lecture from the rooftop so that *every person in the city* hears it, whether they want to listen or not. Loud, but wasteful.
- **Multicast analogy:** The teacher starts a YouTube live stream. Only students who clicked "Join" receive the video. YouTube's servers replicate the stream only at the branching points in the network (city → region → neighborhood → house), so the teacher's home internet upload is used only once.

> [!NOTE]
> The YouTube servers represent **multicast routers**; the students who clicked "Join" represent **multicast group members**; the branching replication mirrors the **multicast distribution tree**.

### 1.4 Why Multicast Matters – The Bandwidth Argument

Consider a source transmitting a **2 Mbps video** to **1,000 receivers** scattered across the internet:

- **Unicast cost:** 2 Mbps × 1,000 = **2,000 Mbps** of upstream bandwidth and backbone traffic.
- **Multicast cost:** **2 Mbps** total on the source link, replicated only at tree branches.

This is the **engineering motivation** behind multicast: it scales gracefully with the size of the receiver set.

### 1.5 IP Multicast Address Classes (KTU High-Yield)

| Range | Type | Scope / TTL Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| **224.0.0.0 – 224.0.0.255** | Local Network Control Block | Never forwarded beyond the local subnet (TTL = 1) |
| **224.0.1.0 – 238.255.255.255** | Internetwork Control / Globally Scoped | Routable across the public internet |
| **239.0.0.0 – 239.255.255.255** | Administratively Scoped (Private) | Restricted to a defined organizational domain (RFC 2365) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MAC Address Mapping:** Layer 2 maps the lower 23 bits of the Class D IP address into the MAC address range **01:00:5E:00:00:00 – 01:00:5E:7F:FF:FF**. The upper 9 bits are *lost*, causing an inherent **32-to-1 ambiguity**—a frequent KTU 2024 exam pitfall (covered in Section 5).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Module 2: Multicast Routing – Deep Theoretical Analysis

## 2.1 Architectural Building Blocks of Multicast Routing

Multicast routing is a **two-layer cooperation** between the host-local subnet and the wide-area routers:

1. **Layer 2 / Host Membership Layer (IGMP):** Hosts on a LAN dynamically announce (join) and rescind (leave) their interest in a specific multicast group. The local *querier* router tracks membership.
2. **Layer 3 / Inter-Router Layer (Multicast Routing Protocols):** Multicast-capable routers exchange group membership and reachability information with each other to construct a spanning distribution tree.

> [!NOTE]
> **IGMP is NOT a routing protocol.** It is a *membership-management* protocol that operates *between hosts and the local router*. Multicast routing protocols (DVMRP, PIM, MOSPF, CBT) operate *between routers*.

## 2.2 The Internet Group Management Protocol (IGMP)

IGMP (currently **IGMPv3**, RFC 3376) is the glue between a host and its directly attached multicast router.

### IGMP Message Types (KTU 2024 High-Yield)

| Type | Direction | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Membership Query (0x11)** | Router → Hosts (224.0.0.1) | Router solicits group membership state from all hosts |
| **Version 2 Membership Report (0x16)** | Host → Router (group address) | Host announces "I want to join group G" |
| **Version 1 Membership Report (0x12)** | Host → Router | Backward-compatibility join |
| **Leave Group (0x17)** | Host → Router (224.0.0.2) | Host announces "I am leaving group G" |

### IGMP Operational Cycle

1. **Querier Election:** On a multi-router LAN, the router with the **lowest IP address** becomes the IGMP querier.
2. **Periodic Query:** Every **125 seconds** (default), the querier multicasts a general Membership Query to `224.0.0.1`.
3. **Report Suppression:** To avoid storms, when a host sends a join, other hosts in the same group suppress their own reports for a random back-off interval.
4. **Leave Processing:** On receiving a Leave message (IGMPv2+), the router sends a *group-specific query* to confirm no other hosts are interested. If no report arrives within a timeout, the router prunes the LAN from the tree.

## 2.3 Multicast Distribution Trees – The Two Canonical Forms

Every multicast routing protocol constructs one (or both) of the following tree types:

### A. Source-Based Tree (Shortest-Path Tree, SPT)

- **Root:** The source S.
- **Branches:** Shortest reverse paths from every receiver back to S.
- **Notation (KTU convention):** **(\*, G)** is *not* used; instead the tree is denoted **(S, G)** where S is the source IP and G is the group IP.
- **Advantage:** Optimal path from the source to each receiver (minimum end-to-end delay).
- **Disadvantage:** One tree per (source, group) pair → poor scalability for many sources.

### B. Shared Tree (Rendezvous-Point Tree, RPT)

- **Root:** A pre-designated **core** or **rendezvous point (RP)**—a router in the network's core.
- **Branches:** Receivers build the shortest path *to the RP*; sources tunnel traffic *to the RP*.
- **Notation:** **(\*, G)** — the asterisk denotes "any source for group G."
- **Advantage:** Only **one tree per group**, regardless of the number of sources → excellent scalability.
- **Disadvantage:** Sub-optimal path (traffic must detour to the RP); the RP is a single point of failure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Mandate:** You must be able to draw and label both an **(S, G)** source tree and a **(\*, G)** shared tree, and justify which protocol class uses which tree.

## 2.4 The Reverse Path Forwarding (RPF) Algorithm

RPF is the foundational forwarding check used by virtually all multicast routing protocols.

### Algorithm
1. When a multicast packet arrives on interface **I_in** with source IP **S**:
2. The router consults its **unicast routing table** and finds the **RPF interface** — the interface that the unicast routing table indicates is on the shortest path *back to* S.
3. **RPF Check:** If `I_in == I_RPF`, the packet passes the check; the router forwards it out all interfaces that have downstream group members (as tracked in its multicast forwarding table, often the *mroute* table).
4. If `I_in ≠ I_RPF`, the packet is **silently dropped** (it arrived from a non-shortest path, so it is presumed to be a duplicate forwarded by another branch).

### Mathematical Formulation of RPF

Let:
- $S$ = source IP address of the multicast packet.
- $I_{\text{in}}$ = interface on which the packet was received.
- $I_{\text{RPF}}(S)$ = the interface computed by the unicast routing table as the next-hop toward $S$.

The RPF check is the Boolean predicate:

$$
\text{RPF}(S, I_{\text{in}}) \;=\; \bigl[\, I_{\text{in}} \;=\; I_{\text{RPF}}(S) \,\bigr]
$$

Only packets for which this predicate evaluates to **True** are forwarded; the rest are dropped without ICMP notification.

> [!NOTE]
> The unicast table used by RPF need *not* be derived from a distance-vector protocol. Any unicast table (OSPF, IS-IS, static, even BGP) suffices—which is precisely why PIM is called **Protocol-Independent** Multicast.

## 2.5 KTU Formula & Concept Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Equation / Rule | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| IGMP Query Interval | $T_{\text{query}} = 125\,\text{s}$ (default) | Seconds; tunable |
| Multicast Group Address | $G \in [224.0.0.0,\, 239.255.255.255]$ | Class D space; **32-to-1 MAC collision** |
| MAC Mapping | $\text{MAC}_{\text{low 23 bits}} \leftarrow \text{IP}_{\text{low 23 bits}}$ | Upper 9 IP bits lost |
| RPF Check | $I_{\text{in}} = I_{\text{RPF}}(S)$ | Boolean; drop on False |
| Prune Timer (DVMRP) | $T_{\text{prune}} \approx 3\,\text{min}$ | Lifetime of a prune state |
| Dense Mode Group Density | $\rho = \frac{\mid \text{Receivers} \mid}{\mid \text{Subnets} \mid} \rightarrow 1$ | "Flood and prune" optimal |
| Sparse Mode Group Density | $\rho \ll 1$ | "Explicit join" optimal |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> - **Stock exchanges** (e.g., NYSE) use multicast to push tick-by-tick price updates to thousands of trading floors with sub-millisecond latency.
> - **IPTV and live sports streaming** use multicast over ISP access networks to deliver a single channel to millions of subscribers.
> - **OSPF and RIPv2 routing protocol updates** themselves use multicast addresses `224.0.0.5` and `224.0.0.6`—every router on the segment receives the update in a single transmission.

## 2.6 Taxonomy of Multicast Routing Protocols

| Protocol | Tree Type | Mode | Flooding Behavior | Scalability |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **DVMRP** | Source-based (S, G) | Dense | Flood-and-prune | Poor (scales to a few thousand groups) |
| **MOSPF** | Source-based (S, G) | Dense | Extension to OSPF link-state | Limited (requires OSPF everywhere) |
| **PIM-DM** | Source-based (S, G) | Dense | Flood-and-prune (like DVMRP) | Better than DVMRP; no distance-vector dependence |
| **PIM-SM** | Shared (RP-rooted, \* G) | Sparse | Explicit join only | Excellent; the de-facto internet standard |
| **CBT** | Shared (Core-rooted) | Sparse | Explicit join only | Good, but rarely deployed |
| **PIM-SSM** | Source-specific (S, G) | Sparse | Predefined source | Excellent for one-to-many |

# Module 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthroughs

## 3.1 Worked Analysis 1 – RPF Check on a 4-Router Topology

**Topology (Unicast Routing Table Already Populated):**

| Router | Destination S | Next Hop (RPF Interface) |
|---|---|---|
| R1 | S | Local (S is directly connected on eth0) |
| R2 | S | R1 via eth1 |
| R3 | S | R1 via eth0 |
| R4 | S | R2 via eth2 |

**Scenario:** A multicast packet from source S arrives at R2 on interface **eth2** (which connects R2 to R4). Determine the router's action.

### Step-by-Step Deduction

**Step 1 – Identify the packet's ingress interface.**
The packet from S was received on $I_{\text{in}} = \text{eth2}$ at R2.

**Step 2 – Consult the unicast routing table for S at R2.**
From the table, $I_{\text{RPF}}(S) = \text{eth1}$ (toward R1).

**Step 3 – Evaluate the RPF predicate.**

$$
\text{RPF}(S, \text{eth2}) \;=\; \bigl[\, \text{eth2} \;=\; \text{eth1} \,\bigr] \;=\; \text{False}
$$

**Step 4 – Conclude.**
The packet **fails** the RPF check and is **silently dropped** at R2. This is correct behavior: had R2 forwarded it, the packet would have created a loop (R4 → R2 → R1 → R3) since R4 had already received the same packet via the legitimate shortest path R4 → R2 → R1.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** RPF is essentially a *loop-prevention check* disguised as a *forwarding optimization*. Without it, multicast packets would endlessly circle the tree.

---

## 3.2 Worked Analysis 2 – DVMRP Flood-and-Prune Sequence

**Topology:** Linear chain: S — R1 — R2 — R3 — R4, with receivers attached to R3 and R4 only.

### Phase 1: Flooding (Initial State)

Every router assumes downstream interfaces may contain receivers, so the packet is **broadcast out all RPF-passing interfaces except the RPF interface** (this is called the **RPF-pruned forwarding rule**).

$$
\text{Forward Out} \;=\; \bigl\{\, \text{all interfaces}\,\bigr\} \;\setminus\; \bigl\{\, I_{\text{RPF}}(S) \,\bigr\}
$$

Result: S → R1 → R2 → R3 → R4, and all branches flood (no prunes yet).

### Phase 2: Pruning (Reverse Direction)

Routers with **no interested downstream members** send a **Prune message** back toward the source after a brief delay (to allow a downstream prune to propagate first).

- **R4:** Has a receiver. **Does not prune.** Forwards downstream.
- **R3:** Has a receiver. **Does not prune.**
- **R2:** Has *no* receiver on any downstream interface. **Sends Prune(S, G) to R1.**
- **R1:** Receives the prune from R2. R1 records a **prune state** for interface toward R2. The interface to R2 is removed from the forwarding set.

### Phase 3: Steady State

After pruning, the forwarding state at R1 is:

$$
F_{\text{R1}} = \emptyset \quad \text{(the only downstream branch, R2, has been pruned)}
$$

Thus R1 stops forwarding multicast traffic for this (S, G) group entirely, conserving bandwidth.

### Phase 4: Grafting (Recovery)

If a new host on R2's subnet later issues an IGMP Join for group G, R2 cannot wait for the next flooding cycle (which DVMRP performs only every minute or so). Instead, R2 immediately sends a **Graft message** upstream to R1, which removes the prune state and restores forwarding within milliseconds.

$$
T_{\text{graft convergence}} \;\ll\; T_{\text{next flood}}
$$

This is why grafting is essential for low-latency recovery in dense-mode protocols.

---

## 3.3 Worked Analysis 3 – PIM-SM Explicit Join Mechanism

**Topology:** Source S connected to R1; RP (Rendezvous Point) is R5; receivers attached to R3 and R4; R2 is intermediate between S-side and RP.

### Step-by-Step Join Sequence

**Step 1 – Host Joins the Group.**
Host H3 attached to R3 sends an IGMP Membership Report for group G.

**Step 2 – Last-Hop Router (LHR) Joins the Shared Tree.**
R3, realizing it is the *last-hop router* with a downstream receiver, checks its multicast routing table. If no (\*, G) state exists, R3 sends a **(\*, G) Join** toward the RP (R5) along the unicast shortest path.

$$
\text{R3} \;\xrightarrow{\;(\*, G)\,\text{Join}}\; \text{R5}
$$

The path is R3 → R4 → R5 (R4 is a transit router and installs a (\*, G) outgoing-interface list pointing to R3).

**Step 3 – Source Registration.**
The first-hop router (FHR) R1 encapsulates the multicast data inside a **PIM Register message** (unicast) and sends it to R5. This bypasses the need for S to know the RP's location or to flood.

**Step 4 – RP Decapsulation and Native Forwarding.**
R5 decapsulates the Register, observes the data, and (since it has a (\*, G) tree) begins forwarding natively down the shared tree toward R3. R5 then sends a (S, G) Join back toward S to build a source-specific shortcut.

**Step 5 – Optimal Shortcut (SPT Switchover – Optional but Default in Modern Cisco/Juniper).**
Once the traffic rate exceeds a configured threshold (e.g., 50 kbps in Cisco IOS), the LHR (R3) sends a **(S, G) Join** toward S directly, building a source-based shortest-path tree. R3 then sends a **(S, G) RP-bit Prune** to R5 to stop receiving via the suboptimal shared tree.

### Final State

- **Shared tree (\*, G):** R3 → R4 → R5
- **Source tree (S, G):** R3 → R2 → R1 (the optimal path, bypassing R5)
- **Bandwidth:** Optimal, as R5 is no longer in the data path.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This **SPT switchover** is a classic KTU 2024 14-mark question: students must draw both trees and explain the trigger condition.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix – Dense vs. Sparse Mode

| Design Dimension | Dense Mode (DVMRP, PIM-DM, MOSPF) | Sparse Mode (PIM-SM, CBT) |
|---|---|---|
| Receiver distribution | Dense across the network | Scattered, low-density |
| Initial behavior | Flood everywhere, then prune | Explicit join only |
| Periodic control traffic | Periodic reflooding | Periodic refresh of state via Join/Prune |
| Bandwidth (no receivers) | Wasted during flood | Zero bandwidth used |
| Bandwidth (many receivers) | Efficient after pruning | Efficient immediately |
| Scalability group count | Poor (state per (S,G)) | Good (single (∗,G) tree) |
| Protocol independence | PIM-DM only (no) | Yes (PIM-SM, CBT) |
| Deployment reality | Mostly historical, campus nets | **De-facto global standard** (PIM-SM) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Heuristic for KTU 2024:**
> - "Members everywhere" → use **Dense Mode**.
> - "Members few and far between" → use **Sparse Mode**.
> - "Unknown distribution" → choose **PIM-SM** (default in industry).

```mermaid
    H2 -- "IGMP Membership Report - Group G" --> QR

    QR -- "IGMP General Query - 224.0.0.1 - Every 125s" --> H3
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    R1["Router R1 - RPF toward S: eth0"]
    R2["Router R2 - RPF toward S: eth1"]
    R3["Router R3 - Has Receiver"]
    R4["Router R4 - No Receiver"]
    R5["Router R5 - No Receiver"]

    SRC -- "1 Native Multicast Flood" --> R1
    R1 -- "2 Flood out all non-RPF interfaces" --> R2
    R2 -- "3a Flood downstream" --> R3
    R2 -- "3b Flood downstream" --> R4
    R4 -- "3c Flood downstream" --> R5

    R4 -. "Prune S G sent upstream" .-> R2
```


## 4.5 Functional Block Diagram – Multicast Forwarding Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PKT["Incoming Multicast Packet S G"]
    RPF["Step 1 RPF Check against Unicast Table"]
    DROP["Step 2a Silently Drop if RPF Fails"]
    OIL["Step 2b Consult Outgoing Interface List OIL"]
    TTL["Step 3 Decrement TTL - Drop if TTL equals 0"]
    FORWARD["Step 4 Replicate and Forward out each OIL interface"]
```
