# Module 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Cycle 2]**
**Differentiate between unicast, multicast, and broadcast communication. Give one real-world application for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks – 1 mark per comparison + 0.5 application each):**

| Mode | Recipients | Copies on Source Link | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Unicast** | One specific host | One per receiver (N total) | Web browsing (HTTP GET to one server) |
| **Multicast** | Subscribed group members | One, replicated at routers | IPTV live channel distribution |
| **Broadcast** | All hosts on subnet | One, flooded on LAN | ARP request (local subnet resolution) |

**Mark allocation:** 1 mark for definitions + 1 mark for the copy-on-source-link distinction + 1 mark for correct real-world examples.

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**What is the purpose of IGMP? List any two IGMP message types.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Purpose of IGMP (2 marks):** IGMP (Internet Group Management Protocol) is the protocol used by hosts to inform their directly attached multicast router about their desire to *join* or *leave* a specific IP multicast group. It enables the local router to maintain an accurate list of which groups have interested receivers on each of its attached LAN segments, so that multicast traffic is forwarded only onto segments where at least one host has subscribed.

**Two IGMP message types (1 mark – 0.5 each):**
1. **Membership Query (Type 0x11):** Sent by the querier router (typically every 125 seconds) to all hosts on the local subnet to determine which groups still have active members.
2. **Membership Report (Type 0x16, IGMPv2):** Sent by a host in response to a query, or unsolicited, to announce that it is a member of (i.e., wishes to receive traffic for) a particular group.

*(Acceptable alternative: Leave Group message, Type 0x17)*

---

## 5.2 Part B – 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) – Multicast Tree Construction and Protocol Comparison

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the construction of a **Source-Based Distribution Tree (SPT)** and a **Shared Distribution Tree (RPT)** for a multicast group G with source S. Compare their advantages and disadvantages.
**(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Reverse Path Forwarding (RPF)** algorithm. How does it prevent looping in multicast routing? A multicast packet from source **192.168.10.5** arrives at router R2 on interface **eth1**. R2's unicast routing table shows the next-hop toward 192.168.10.5 is via **eth0**. State whether the packet is forwarded or dropped, with justification. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution – Part (a) [7 marks]

**Source-Based Tree (S, G) – 3 marks:**

- **Definition (1 mark):** A shortest-path tree rooted at the source S, with one branch for every interested receiver. The tree is built using the reverse of the unicast shortest path from each receiver back to S.
- **Construction steps (1 mark):**
  1. The first-hop router (FHR) at the source floods the packet via RPF on all interfaces except the RPF interface (which points back to the source itself).
  2. Each downstream router performs an RPF check, then forwards on all non-RPF interfaces.
  3. Branches with no receivers are pruned; new branches are grafted.
- **Diagram (1 mark):** A tree rooted at S with branches to receivers (acceptable freehand; refer to Section 4.4).

**Shared Tree (∗, G) – 2 marks:**

- **Definition (1 mark):** A single tree rooted at a pre-designated **Rendezvous Point (RP)**. Sources encapsulate their data and tunnel it to the RP, which then forwards it down the shared tree to receivers.
- **Construction (1 mark):** Receivers' last-hop routers send (\*, G) Joins toward the RP; transit routers install the (∗, G) state. Sources register with the RP via PIM Register messages.

**Comparison (2 marks – 1 each for advantages and disadvantages):**

| Tree Type | Advantage | Disadvantage |
|---|---|---|
| Source-Based (S, G) | Optimal end-to-end delay (shortest path) | State explosion: one tree per (source, group) pair |
| Shared (∗, G) | Scalable: one tree per group regardless of source count | Sub-optimal path (must detour through RP); RP is single point of failure |

---

#### Model Solution – Part (b) [7 marks]

**RPF Algorithm Explanation (4 marks):**

- **Concept (1 mark):** Reverse Path Forwarding is a loop-prevention and forwarding-decision mechanism for multicast. It uses the existing unicast routing table to determine whether a packet has arrived "from the right direction."
- **Mechanism (2 marks):** For every incoming multicast packet with source S, the router computes $I_{\text{RPF}}(S)$ from the unicast table—the interface on the shortest path *toward* S. If $I_{\text{in}} = I_{\text{RPF}}(S)$, the packet is forwarded; otherwise, it is dropped.
- **Loop prevention (1 mark):** Because every router forwards *only* along the shortest path back to the source, a packet can never traverse a link in a direction inconsistent with the source's shortest path, thereby eliminating loops.

**Numerical Application (3 marks):**

Given:
- $I_{\text{in}} = \text{eth1}$
- $I_{\text{RPF}}(192.168.10.5) = \text{eth0}$

Apply the predicate:

$$
\text{RPF}\bigl(192.168.10.5,\, \text{eth1}\bigr) \;=\; \bigl[\, \text{eth1} \;=\; \text{eth0} \,\bigr] \;=\; \text{False}
$$

**Result (1 mark):** The packet **fails** the RPF check and is **dropped silently** at R2.

**Justification (2 marks):** The packet arrived on eth1, but the unicast table indicates that the shortest path toward 192.168.10.5 leaves via eth0. Accepting and forwarding this packet would cause it to loop back to its origin via a non-shortest path, multiplying traffic in the network. Hence RPF drops it to preserve correctness.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) – IGMP, Dense vs Sparse, and DVMRP Mechanics

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the operation of **IGMP** with reference to Membership Query and Membership Report messages. What is the purpose of the **Leave Group** message in IGMPv2, and how does the querier router verify whether the LAN still has group members? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **Dense Mode** and **Sparse Mode** multicast routing. With a diagram, explain the **flood-and-prune** mechanism of **DVMRP**. Why is the Prune timer set to a few minutes? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution – Part (a) [7 marks]

**IGMP Operation (4 marks):**

- **Overview (1 mark):** IGMP operates between a host and its directly attached multicast router. The router acts as the querier and tracks group membership on its attached LANs.
- **Membership Query (1.5 marks):** Periodically (default every **125 seconds**), the querier router sends a Membership Query to the all-hosts multicast address `224.0.0.1`, asking "Which groups are you still interested in?"
- **Membership Report (1.5 marks):** Each host that wishes to remain in group G responds with a Membership Report addressed to G. To prevent report storms, IGMP employs *report suppression*: once a host hears another host's report for the same group, it suppresses its own report for a random back-off interval.

**Leave Group Processing in IGMPv2 (3 marks):**

- **Leave message (1 mark):** A host that no longer wishes to receive group G traffic sends an IGMPv2 Leave Group message to the all-routers address `224.0.0.2`. (IGMPv1 lacked this message, forcing routers to time out state.)
- **Group-Specific Query (1 mark):** Upon receiving a Leave, the querier router sends a *group-specific* Membership Query for group G (rather than a general query for all groups), to confirm whether any *other* host on the LAN still wants G.
- **Confirmation/Timeout (1 mark):** If any host still wishes G, it sends a Membership Report and the router retains the group state. If no report arrives within the **Max Response Time** (default 10 seconds), the router prunes the LAN from the multicast tree and stops forwarding G traffic.

---

#### Model Solution – Part (b) [7 marks]

**Dense vs Sparse Mode Comparison (3 marks – 1 each):**

| Aspect | Dense Mode | Sparse Mode |
|---|---|---|
| Receiver distribution | Many, densely packed | Few, geographically scattered |
| Initial behavior | Flood everywhere; prune where no receivers | Explicit join only |
| Bandwidth use when no receivers | Wasted during periodic reflooding | Zero |

**DVMRP Flood-and-Prune Mechanism (3 marks):**

- **Step 1 – Flood (1 mark):** When a source S starts transmitting, the first-hop router performs an RPF check and forwards the packet out *all* interfaces except the RPF interface. Each downstream router repeats this process, achieving network-wide flood within milliseconds.
- **Step 2 – Prune (1 mark):** Leaf routers with **no interested downstream members** send a Prune(S, G) message back toward the source. Each upstream router that receives a Prune on an interface records a prune state and stops forwarding on that interface.
- **Step 3 – Steady State (1 mark):** Only the branches leading to active receivers carry traffic; pruned branches consume no bandwidth.

**Why the Prune Timer is ~3 minutes (1 mark):**

- The Prune timer (default ~3 minutes) is the lifetime of a prune state. When it expires, the router re-floods the data so that *newly joined* members (who joined during the prune interval) can be discovered without explicit signaling. This trades a small amount of periodic bandwidth waste for the simplicity of stateless join support. Dense-mode protocols deliberately accept this trade-off because the assumption is that the network is dense with receivers, so re-flooding is rarely wasted effort.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU 2024 students lose marks on Multicast Routing questions:**
> 1. **The 32-to-1 MAC ambiguity trap (2 marks lost every time):** Students forget that Class D addresses are mapped to MAC by copying only the lower 23 bits. Two distinct multicast IPs (e.g., `224.1.2.3` and `225.129.2.3`) can map to the **same** MAC address. Always mention this in any MAC-mapping question.
> 2. **Confusing IGMP with a routing protocol (1 mark):** IGMP is a *membership-management* protocol, NOT a routing protocol. State this explicitly.
> 3. **RPF check direction confusion (2 marks):** RPF checks whether the packet arrived from the direction of the *source's shortest path*—not from the direction of the *receiver's* shortest path. Drawing the arrow incorrectly is a common error.
> 4. **Forgetting TTL semantics (1 mark):** Multicast packets have TTL scoping. Locally-scoped addresses (224.0.0.0/24) have effective TTL = 1 and never cross a router.
> 5. **In SPT vs RPT questions, forgetting the SPT switchover (2 marks):** PIM-SM is not purely a shared-tree protocol in practice. Modern deployments switch to the SPT once traffic exceeds a threshold. Mention this for full marks.
> 6. **No diagram (2–3 marks lost):** Any 14-mark question on multicast *routing* without a labeled tree diagram is severely penalized. Always draw the topology.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Read This 30 Minutes Before the Exam):**

- ✅ **Multicast Class D range:** 224.0.0.0 – 239.255.255.255. Sub-ranges have distinct scoping semantics.
- ✅ **MAC address mapping** copies only the **lower 23 bits** of the Class D IP; the upper 9 bits are lost, producing a 32-to-1 collision.
- ✅ **IGMP is NOT a routing protocol**—it is host-to-router membership management. Default query interval = 125 s.
- ✅ **IGMPv2 Leave** triggers a *group-specific query* with a **Max Response Time** of typically 10 s.
- ✅ **RPF check** ensures a multicast packet is forwarded only if it arrived on the interface the unicast table identifies as next-hop *toward the source*. Mathematically: $I_{\text{in}} = I_{\text{RPF}}(S)$.
- ✅ **Source-based tree (S, G):** One tree per source; optimal delay; poor scalability.
- ✅ **Shared tree (\*, G):** One tree per group; rooted at RP; scalable; suboptimal path.
- ✅ **Dense-mode protocols (DVMRP, MOSPF, PIM-DM):** Flood-and-prune; assume dense receivers; bandwidth wasted when sparse.
- ✅ **Sparse-mode protocols (PIM-SM, CBT):** Explicit join; ideal for scattered receivers; PIM-SM is the internet standard.
- ✅ **PIM-SM is "Protocol-Independent"** because the RPF check uses whatever unicast table is present (OSPF, IS-IS, BGP, static).
- ✅ **DVMRP prune lifetime ≈ 3 minutes**; **Graft messages** restore forwarding immediately on new joins.
- ✅ **SPT switchover** in PIM-SM moves the LHR from the shared tree to a direct source tree when traffic exceeds a threshold (commonly 50 kbps).
- ✅ **Real-world deployments:** NYSE feeds, IPTV, OSPF/RIP control packets, financial multicast.

> **End of Module 2 – Multicast Routing | KTU 2024 Scheme Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Multicasting Basics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAMT632 - BASICS OF COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Multicasting Basics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Multicasting** is a one-to-many or many-to-many communication paradigm in computer networks where a single source (or a set of sources) transmits data simultaneously to a dynamically formed group of interested receivers spread across disparate network segments. Unlike traditional unicast (one-to-one) or broadcast (one-to-all) models, multicasting builds a logical distribution tree that ensures only a single copy of the packet traverses any given link, regardless of how many group members reside downstream.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** Multicasting is the delivery of information from one source to multiple destinations (a subset of all hosts) using the most efficient strategy, where the underlying network infrastructure replicates the data packet only at branch points of the multicast distribution tree.

### 1.2 The Three Fundamental Communication Paradigms

| Mode | Sender → Receivers | Traffic Pattern | Scalability |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Unicast** | 1 → 1 | Point-to-point | Poor for large groups |
| **Broadcast** | 1 → All hosts on subnet | Flooding local segment | Limited to LAN |
| **Multicast** | 1 → N (interested subset) | Tree-based selective delivery | Highly scalable |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The Newspaper Analogy:** Imagine you want to send the same newsletter to 1,000 subscribers spread across Kerala.
> - **Unicast approach:** You make 1,000 separate trips to each subscriber's house. Exhausting and expensive.
> - **Broadcast approach:** You shout the newsletter from your rooftop so everyone in your immediate neighbourhood hears it, even people who don't subscribe.
> - **Multicast approach (THE OPTIMAL WAY):** You drop 10 master copies at 10 strategic post office hubs across Kerala. Each hub then redistributes the newsletter only to the subscribers in its local area. The message travels each road only once, yet reaches all 1,000 interested readers.

> [!TIP]
> **Why Multicasting Matters:** It is the backbone technology for IPTV, video conferencing, stock-tick streaming, software distribution, and real-time financial data feeds. Without multicasting, the modern internet backbone would collapse under duplicate traffic loads.

### 1.4 Key Terminology

- **Multicast Group:** A logical collection of receivers identified by a Class D IP address.
- **IGMP (Internet Group Management Protocol):** The Layer-3 signaling protocol that allows hosts to join/leave multicast groups.
- **Distribution Tree:** The acyclic graph structure used to forward multicast packets from source(s) to receivers.
- **Rendezvous Point (RP):** A core router used as a meeting/tracing point for shared multicast trees.
- **Source-Specific Multicast (SSM):** A modern variant where receivers specify both source + group.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Multicast IP Address Architecture

The Internet Assigned Numbers Authority (IANA) reserves the entire **Class D** address block for multicast groups. Unlike Class A/B/C, the first 4 bits of a Class D address are fixed to `1110`.

$$224.0.0.0 \leq \text{Multicast\_IP} \leq 239.255.255.255$$

This yields the full range, often expressed in CIDR notation as `224.0.0.0/4`.

### 2.2 Reserved Multicast Address Ranges (High-Yield for KTU)

| Address Range | Scope / Purpose | TTL Behavior |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **224.0.0.0 – 224.0.0.255** | Link-Local Control (OSPF, PIM, HSRP) | **TTL = 1** (never routed) |
| **224.0.1.0 – 224.0.1.255** | Internetwork Control (NTP) | Routable globally |
| **232.0.0.0 – 232.255.255.255** | Source-Specific Multicast (SSM) | Routable |
| **233.0.0.0 – 233.251.255.255** | GLOP Block (AS-number based) | Routable |
| **239.0.0.0 – 239.255.255.255** | Administratively Scoped (Private) | Limited to local domain |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** Many students confuse 224.0.0.1 (All-Hosts) and 224.0.0.2 (All-Routers). Remember: **1 = Hosts**, **2 = Routers**, and 224.0.0.5 is **OSPF All OSPF Routers**.

### 2.3 Multicast Distribution Tree Models

There are two architecturally distinct tree models used in multicast routing:

**A. Source-Based Tree (Shortest Path Tree — SPT)**
- One tree per source.
- The root is the source; branches follow the unicast shortest path to every receiver.
- Notation: `(S, G)` — read as "Source S, Group G".
- Optimal end-to-end delay, but **high memory consumption** in core routers (one entry per source).

**B. Shared Tree (RP / Core-Based Tree — CBT)**
- A single tree shared by ALL sources in the group.
- The root is a centrally chosen Rendezvous Point (RP).
- Notation: `(*, G)` — read as "any source, Group G".
- Lower memory footprint, but **sub-optimal path** (triangular routing).

### 2.4 Reverse Path Forwarding (RPF) — The Heart of Multicast Forwarding

RPF is the loop-prevention mechanism. The rule is elegantly simple:

> A router accepts an incoming multicast packet on interface **X** **only if** interface X is the next-hop interface in the router's unicast routing table back to the packet's source. Otherwise, the packet is silently discarded.

This is mathematically equivalent to a **reverse-path consistency check**:

$$\text{Accept}(p) \iff \text{IngressInterface}(p) = \text{UnicastNextHop}(\text{Source}(p))$$

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Expression / Boundary | Engineering Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Class D IP Range | $224.0.0.0 \le IP \le 239.255.255.255$ | Decimal dotted |
| Class D CIDR | $224.0.0.0 \;/ \;4$ | Bits |
| Total Multicast Groups | $2^{28} = 268{,}435{,}456$ | Groups |
| Number of Group Members | $N$ (variable per group) | Hosts |
| Bandwidth Saving Ratio | $\dfrac{N - 1}{N} \times 100\%$ | Percent |
| RPF Accept Condition | $\text{Ingress} = \text{UnicastNextHop}(S)$ | Boolean |
| Link-Local TTL | $1$ | Hop count |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Multicast is critical in:
- **IPTV / OTT streaming** (one feed → millions of viewers).
- **Financial trading floors** (BSE/NSE market data dissemination).
- **Distance education** (KTU live lecture streaming to 5,000+ students).
- **Disaster Recovery Replication** (synchronous DB mirroring to multiple sites).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Bandwidth Efficiency of Multicasting vs. Unicast

**Problem Statement:** A video server must deliver a 4 Mbps stream to $N$ receivers. Compare the total backbone bandwidth consumed by unicast versus multicast.

**Step 1 — Unicast Consumption:**
In unicast, the server establishes a separate connection to each of the $N$ receivers. Each flow independently consumes the full stream rate $R = 4$ Mbps.

$$B_{\text{unicast}} = N \times R$$

**Step 2 — Multicast Consumption:**
In multicast, a single copy of the stream travels each link only once. The total bandwidth is therefore bounded by the number of *unique links* in the distribution tree, not by $N$. In the worst case (a degenerate path), it equals one stream:

$$B_{\text{multicast}} \le (\text{Number of tree edges}) \times R$$

**Step 3 — Bandwidth Saving Formula:**
The percentage bandwidth saved by switching from unicast to multicast is:

$$B_{\text{saved}} \;=\; \frac{B_{\text{unicast}} - B_{\text{multicast}}}{B_{\text{unicast}}} \times 100\%$$

**Step 4 — Substitute and Simplify:**
Substituting the worst-case bound for multicast ($B_{\text{multicast}} = R$ for a single linear path):

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_{\text{saved}} & = \frac{N \cdot R - R}{N \cdot R} \times 100\% \\
& = \frac{R(N - 1)}{R \cdot N} \times 100\% \\
& = \frac{N - 1}{N} \times 100\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Numerical Example (KTU Typical):**
For $N = 100$ receivers and $R = 4$ Mbps:
- Unicast bandwidth = $100 \times 4 = 400$ Mbps.
- Multicast bandwidth $\approx 4$ Mbps (one copy).
- Savings = $\dfrac{99}{100} \times 100\% = 99\%$.

**Result:** The larger the group, the closer the saving approaches **100%**. This is the asymptotic efficiency of multicasting.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Class D Address Space Size

**Step 1 — Fixed Prefix Length:**
The Class D address is identified by its leading 4 bits being `1110`.

$$\text{Fixed bits} = 4$$

**Step 2 — Variable Bits Remaining:**
The IPv4 address has 32 bits in total.

$$\text{Variable bits} = 32 - 4 = 28$$

**Step 3 — Total Addressable Multicast Groups:**
Each variable bit can take 2 values, so the total multicast group space is:

$$N_{\text{groups}} = 2^{28} = 268{,}435{,}456$$

**Step 4 — Boundary Verification:**
Lower bound (all zeros in variable portion) = `224.0.0.0`. Upper bound (all ones) = `239.255.255.255`. Both match the IANA registry. ∎

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: IGMP Version Comparison (High-Yield for KTU)

| Feature | **IGMPv1** | **IGMPv2** | **IGMPv3** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Year Released | 1989 | 1997 | 2002 |
| Group Join | Query-response only | Active join message | Active join + source filter |
| Leave Mechanism | None (timeout-based) | Explicit Leave + Group-Specific Query | Source-specific leave |
| Source Filtering | No | No | **Yes (Include/Exclude lists)** |
| Querier Election | No | Yes (lowest IP wins) | Yes (lowest IP wins) |
| KTU Weight | Low (historical) | **High** (commonly tested) | Medium (modern exam) |

### 3.4 Worked Example: RPF Check Decision

**Topology:** Router R2 receives a multicast packet on interface `eth0`. The packet's source IP is `10.0.0.5`. R2's unicast routing table shows that the next-hop to reach `10.0.0.5` is via interface `eth1`.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{IngressInterface}(p) & = \text{eth0} \\
\text{UnicastNextHop}(10.0.0.5) & = \text{eth1} \\
\text{Condition: Ingress} = \text{UnicastNextHop} \;&?\;\; \text{eth0} \neq \text{eth1} \\
\therefore \text{Action} & = \textbf{DROP (RPF Failure)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why the asymmetry?** Forwarding in unicast is "destination-driven"; forwarding in multicast is "source-driven" because the tree is rooted at the source. The router needs to ensure packets flow *away* from the source along the tree branches, which is exactly what the reverse-path check guarantees.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Multicast Group Lifecycle (IGMP Interaction)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    H1[Host 1] -->|IGMP Membership Report| QR[Querier Router]
    H2[Host 2] -->|IGMP Membership Report| QR
    H3[Host 3] -->|No Interest| QR
    QR -->|Periodic General Query 224.0.0.1| H1
    QR -->|Periodic General Query 224.0.0.1| H2
    H2 -->|IGMP Leave Group| QR
    QR -->|Group Specific Query| H1
    H1 -->|Still Here Report| QR
```

### 4.2 Source-Based Tree vs. Shared Tree Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ST[SHARED TREE - G]
        RP[Rendezvous Point]
        S1[Source 1]
        S2[Source 2]
        R1[Receiver 1]
        R2[Receiver 2]
        R3[Receiver 3]
        RP --- S1
        RP --- S2
        RP --- R1
        RP --- R2
        RP --- R3
    end
```

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SPT1[SOURCE TREE - S1 G]
        SRC1[Source 1]
        R1A[Receiver 1]
        R2A[Receiver 2]
        R3A[Receiver 3]
        SRC1 --- R1A
        SRC1 --- R2A
        SRC1 --- R3A
    end
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Multicast Forwarding Engine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Incoming Multicast Packet] --> B[Parse Source S and Group G]
    B --> C{RPF Check}
    C -->|FAIL| D[Silent Drop and Increment RPF Failures Counter]
    C -->|PASS| E[Look up OIL Outgoing Interface List]
    E --> F{OIL Empty?}
    F -->|YES| G[Prune State from upstream router]
    F -->|NO| H[Copy Packet and Forward on each OIL interface]
    H --> I[Update TTL and decrement by 1]
    I --> J{TTL Greater than 0?}
    J -->|NO| K[Discard Packet]
    J -->|YES| L[Transmit on all OIL interfaces]
```

### 4.4 Multicast Address Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ROOT[IPv4 Multicast Address Space 224 0 0 0 to 239 255 255 255] --> A1[224 0 0 0 to 224 0 0 255 Link Local Control]
    ROOT --> A2[224 0 1 0 to 224 0 1 255 Internetwork Control]
    ROOT --> A3[232 0 0 0 to 232 255 255 255 SSM Block]
    ROOT --> A4[239 0 0 0 to 239 255 255 255 Administratively Scoped Private]
    A1 --> A1A[224 0 0 1 All Hosts]
    A1 --> A1B[224 0 0 2 All Routers]
    A1 --> A1C[224 0 0 5 OSPF Routers]
    A1 --> A1D[224 0 0 6 OSPF Designated Routers]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
*Define multicasting. How does it differ from broadcasting?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Multicasting is a one-to-many communication model in which a single source sends data to a selected group of interested hosts that have explicitly joined a multicast group, identified by a Class D IP address (3 marks). Broadcasting, in contrast, delivers the packet to **every** host on the local subnet regardless of interest, and is bounded by the LAN (router isolation). Multicasting is **selective**, **scalable across WANs**, and uses a distribution tree, whereas broadcast is **indiscriminate** and confined to a single Layer-2 domain. *(Award 1 mark each for: correct definition, broadcasting limitation, and key contrast).*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
*List any three reserved multicast address ranges and their purposes.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| # | Address Block | Purpose | Marks |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | `224.0.0.0/24` | Link-local control (never forwarded) | 1 |
| 2 | `232.0.0.0/8` | Source-Specific Multicast (SSM) | 1 |
| 3 | `239.0.0.0/8` | Administratively scoped (private) | 1 |

---

### **PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern)**

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — June 2024]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the two types of multicast distribution trees. Compare their advantages and disadvantages with a neat diagram.

**Model Solution:**

**1. Source-Based Tree (SPT) — `(S, G)` state:**
In a source-based tree, a separate shortest-path tree is built for each active source. The root is the source itself, and branches fan out following unicast routing metrics (typically OSPF cost) toward all group members.

- **Advantages:** Optimal end-to-end delay, no triangular routing, deterministic forwarding.
- **Disadvantages:** Router memory grows as `O(Sources × Groups)`; high control-plane overhead for many sources.

**2. Shared Tree (RP / Core Tree) — `(*, G)` state:**
All sources in a group share a single tree rooted at a designated Rendezvous Point (RP). Sources encapsulate traffic to the RP, which then distributes it down the shared branches.

- **Advantages:** Low memory footprint (`O(Groups)`); RP can act as a natural policy choke-point.
- **Disadvantages:** Sub-optimal path (triangular routing); single point of failure unless RP redundancy is provisioned.

**Mark split:** Definition SPT (2) + Diagram (1) + Definition Shared (2) + Comparison table (2).

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a suitable example, explain the Reverse Path Forwarding (RPF) check used in multicast routing.

**Model Solution:**

**Concept:** RPF is the loop-prevention primitive for multicast. The check ensures that multicast packets travel *away* from the source along the tree.

**Algorithm:**
1. Router receives a multicast packet on interface `I_in`.
2. Router consults its unicast routing table for the source address `S`.
3. If the unicast next-hop interface toward `S` equals `I_in`, the packet passes RPF and is forwarded.
4. Otherwise, the packet is **silently dropped** (RPF failure).

**Numerical Example (4 marks for the worked part):**

Consider a packet with source `192.168.10.5` arriving at Router R2 on interface `Fa0/1`. R2's routing table shows next-hop to `192.168.10.5` is via `Fa0/0`.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Ingress} & = \text{Fa0/1} \\
\text{UnicastNextHop}(192.168.10.5) & = \text{Fa0/0} \\
\text{Fa0/1} \neq \text{Fa0/0} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{RPF FAIL} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{DROP}
\end{aligned}
$$

If the packet had instead arrived on `Fa0/0`, RPF would pass, and the packet would be forwarded out all interfaces in the OIL except the ingress.

**Mark split:** RPF definition (2) + Algorithm (2) + Numerical example (3).

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the IGMP protocol. Explain the join and leave operations in IGMPv2.

**Model Solution:**

**1. IGMP Overview (2 marks):**
Internet Group Management Protocol operates between a host and its directly attached local multicast router. It is a **Layer-3 protocol** (Protocol Number = 2) encapsulated in IP, used purely for *signaling* — not data forwarding.

**2. IGMPv2 Message Types (2 marks):**

| Type | Code | Direction | Purpose |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Membership Query | 0x11 | Router → Host | Periodic poll (Default 125 s) |
| Version 2 Membership Report | 0x16 | Host → Router | Join / refresh group interest |
| Leave Group | 0x17 | Host → Router | Voluntary departure notification |

**3. Join Operation (1.5 marks):**
A host sends an unsolicited `Membership Report` for the desired group `G` to `G` itself (not 224.0.0.1). The local router records `(interface, G)` in its forwarding state and propagates the interest upstream via the multicast routing protocol (e.g., PIM).

**4. Leave Operation (1.5 marks):**
On leaving, the host sends a `Leave Group` message to `224.0.0.2` (All-Routers). The router responds with a **Group-Specific Query** to `G`. If no host responds with a Membership Report within a timeout, the router prunes the group from that interface and signals upstream.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A campus needs to stream a 5 Mbps lecture to 500 students. Compare the bandwidth required at the server's uplink for (i) unicast and (ii) multicast approaches.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Unicast Approach (3.5 marks):**
The server transmits a *separate* 5 Mbps stream to each of the 500 students.

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_{\text{unicast}} & = N \times R \\
& = 500 \times 5 \text{ Mbps} \\
& = 2500 \text{ Mbps} = 2.5 \text{ Gbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Multicast Approach (3.5 marks):**
A single 5 Mbps stream is replicated only at branch points of the distribution tree, so the server uplink carries just one copy:

$$
B_{\text{multicast}} \approx 5 \text{ Mbps}
$$

**Efficiency Comparison (within the 7 marks):**

$$
\text{Savings} = \frac{2500 - 5}{2500} \times 100\% = 99.8\%
$$

Multicasting is **500 times more efficient** at the server uplink, freeing the backbone for other traffic and enabling large-scale KTU live lectures without congestion.

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in Multicasting Questions:**
> 1. **Conflating "Multicast" with "Broadcast":** A common KTU trap. Broadcasting is `1 → ALL` and stops at the router; multicasting is `1 → SELECTED-GROUP` and crosses routers via a tree. Mixing these will cost 2-3 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting TTL=1 for 224.0.0.0/24:** If a question asks why OSPF packets use `224.0.0.5` instead of a routable address, the answer is **TTL = 1 to prevent leakage to other subnets**. Students often omit this and lose 1 mark.
> 3. **RPF Direction Confusion:** RPF checks the path *back to the source* using the unicast table — NOT the path to the group. Writing it backward loses 2-3 marks in derivations.
> 4. **Confusing `(S,G)` with `(*,G)`:** Forgetting to denote the tree type correctly in exam answers results in mark deduction. Always specify.
> 5. **IGMP Version Mixing:** Don't claim IGMPv1 has explicit Leave — that is a v2/v3 feature. Examiners will dock 1 mark.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)**

- ✅ Multicasting = **one-to-many** delivery to a *subscribed* group via a **distribution tree**.
- ✅ Multicast IP range = **Class D**, `224.0.0.0 → 239.255.255.255` (`/4`).
- ✅ Total addressable groups = $2^{28} = 268{,}435{,}456$.
- ✅ `224.0.0.0/24` is **link-local**, **TTL = 1**, **never routed**.
- ✅ `239.0.0.0/8` is the **administratively scoped (private)** block.
- ✅ Two tree types: **Source Tree `(S,G)`** (optimal, memory-heavy) and **Shared Tree `(*,G)`** (RP-based, low memory).
- ✅ **RPF check** is the *loop-prevention* primitive; uses **unicast routing table back to source**.
- ✅ **IGMP** is the *host-to-router* signaling protocol (Protocol Number = 2).
- ✅ **IGMPv2** introduced explicit **Leave** and **Querier election** (lowest IP).
- ✅ **IGMPv3** added **Source-Specific Multicast (SSM)** filtering (Include/Exclude lists).
- ✅ Bandwidth savings formula: $\dfrac{N-1}{N} \times 100\%$ — approaches **100%** as $N \to \infty$.
- ✅ Applications: IPTV, video conferencing, stock feeds, distance education, software updates.
- ✅ PIM (Protocol Independent Multicast) is the dominant modern multicast routing protocol.
- ✅ Remember: **DVMRP** uses distance-vector multicast, **MOSPF** uses link-state, **CBT** uses core-based shared trees.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Intra-Domain and Inter-Domain Routing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAMT632 - BASICS OF COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Intra-Domain and Inter-Domain Routing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is an Autonomous System (AS)?

An **Autonomous System (AS)** is a collection of IP networks and routers under the control of a single administrative entity (e.g., an Internet Service Provider like BSNL, Airtel, or Jio, or a large university/corporation) that presents a common routing policy to the rest of the global Internet. Every AS is assigned a globally unique **16-bit or 32-bit Autonomous System Number (ASN)** by IANA (Internet Assigned Numbers Authority) via Regional Internet Registries (RIRs).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The Internet is fundamentally a "network of networks." An Autonomous System is the fundamental building block of this hierarchy. Routing inside one AS is fundamentally different from routing between ASes because the goals and scale differ drastically.

### 1.2 Intra-Domain Routing (Interior Gateway Protocols - IGP)

**Formal Definition:** Intra-domain routing refers to the exchange of routing information **within the boundaries of a single Autonomous System**. The protocols used are called **Interior Gateway Protocols (IGP)**. They are designed for speed of convergence, optimal path selection within a trusted administrative domain, and rapid adaptation to internal topology changes.

**Conceptual Analogy:** Think of an **Intra-Domain network as the internal road network of a single city** (e.g., the roads inside Kochi). The municipal corporation manages all internal routes. They know every lane, every one-way, and every shortcut. They don't care what happens in another city — they just need the best path between two points *within* Kochi. The "city administration" is the AS, and the "internal road rules" are the IGP.

### 1.3 Inter-Domain Routing (Exterior Gateway Protocols - EGP)

**Formal Definition:** Inter-domain routing refers to the exchange of reachability and routing policy information **between different Autonomous Systems**. The protocol used is the **Border Gateway Protocol (BGP)**, which is the de facto EGP of the global Internet. Unlike IGPs, BGP is policy-driven rather than purely metric-driven.

**Conceptual Analogy:** Think of **Inter-Domain routing as the national highway system connecting different cities** (e.g., NH66 connecting Kochi to Bengaluru). Each city (AS) decides for itself whether it *wants* to send traffic through another city based on **political/business agreements** (routing policies), not just distance. Even if Bengaluru is geographically closer, a city might refuse to forward traffic to a competitor's network. This is "policy-based routing."

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction:** IGPs optimize for **shortest path** (technical metric like hop count or cost). BGP optimizes for **policy compliance** (business relationships, agreements, and reachability).

### 1.4 Standard Metrics & Concepts (Highlighted)

- **Hop Count:** Number of routers a packet traverses (used by RIP).
- **Cost / Bandwidth-Delay Product:** Used by OSPF as its metric (lower is better).
- **Administrative Distance (AD):** Trustworthiness of a routing source (e.g., OSPF AD = **110**, BGP AD = **20** for eBGP / **200** for iBGP).
- **Convergence Time:** The time taken for all routers in a domain to agree on a consistent view of the topology after a change.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Categories of Interior Gateway Protocols (IGP)

IGPs are broadly classified into two families based on the algorithm they use:

#### A. Distance Vector Protocols (Bellman-Ford Based)
- **Operational Principle:** Each router maintains a **routing table** (distance vector) containing the best known distance to every destination and the next-hop address. Routers periodically (every 30 seconds for RIP) share their *entire* routing table with **directly connected neighbors only**.
- **The "Why":** Simple to implement, low memory/CPU footprint. Best for small, stable networks.
- **The "How":** Uses the Bellman-Ford algorithm. On receiving a neighbor's vector, the router updates its own table: `New Distance = Neighbor's Advertised Distance + Cost of Link to Neighbor`.
- **Classic Example:** **RIP (Routing Information Protocol)** — uses **hop count** as the metric; max hops = **15** (16 = unreachable). Uses **port 520** (UDP).

#### B. Link State Protocols (Dijkstra / SPF Based)
- **Operational Principle:** Every router constructs a complete **Link State Database (LSDB)** — an identical map of the entire network topology. When a change occurs, the router floods a **Link State Advertisement (LSA)** to *all* routers in the area using a reliable flooding mechanism. Each router then independently runs the **Dijkstra Shortest Path First (SPF) algorithm** to compute the best path to every destination.
- **The "Why":** Fast convergence, no routing loops, hierarchical design possible. Suitable for large, complex enterprise networks.
- **The "How":** Five-step process: (1) Discover neighbors, (2) Measure cost to each neighbor, (3) Build LSA, (4) Flood LSA reliably, (5) Compute SPF tree.
- **Classic Example:** **OSPF (Open Shortest Path First)** — uses **cost** (typically based on interface bandwidth) as metric. Uses **IP protocol number 89** directly.

### 2.2 Exterior Gateway Protocol: BGP

- **Operational Principle:** BGP is a **path vector** protocol. It does NOT use a traditional distance or cost metric. Instead, each BGP update carries the **full AS path** (the sequence of ASNs a route has traversed). A router accepts a route if it doesn't contain its own ASN in the path (loop prevention) and if it complies with local **routing policies** (configured via route maps, prefix lists, and communities).
- **Two flavors of BGP:**
  - **eBGP (external BGP):** Runs between routers in **different** ASes, over a TCP connection (port **179**). Used to exchange reachability information.
  - **iBGP (internal BGP):** Runs between routers **within the same** AS, to ensure the AS has a consistent view of external routes. Typically forms a full mesh (or uses route reflectors/confederations for scalability).
- **Path Attributes:** `NEXT_HOP`, `AS_PATH`, `ORIGIN`, `MED` (Multi-Exit Discriminator), `LOCAL_PREF`, `COMMUNITY`.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| RIP Hop Count Limit | $\text{Maximum Hops} = 15$ | Hops (16 = unreachable) |
| RIP Update Timer | $T_{\text{update}} = 30$ s | Seconds |
| OSPF Cost (per interface) | $\text{Cost} = \dfrac{\text{Reference BW}}{\text{Interface BW}}$ | Dimensionless, $\geq 1$ |
| OSPF Default Reference BW | $\text{Ref BW} = 10^8$ bps = 100 Mbps | Cisco default |
| OSPF Hello Timer (Broadcast) | $T_{\text{hello}} = 10$ s (Dead = 40 s) | Seconds |
| OSPF Hello Timer (NBMA/Point-to-Point) | $T_{\text{hello}} = 30$ s (Dead = 120 s) | Seconds |
| Dijkstra (SPF) Complexity | $O((V + E) \log V)$ with min-heap | Big-O |
| Bellman-Ford Complexity | $O(V \cdot E)$ | Big-O |
| BGP Transport | TCP, port $\vert 179 \vert$ | Port number |
| BGP Hold Timer | $T_{\text{hold}} = 180$ s (default) | Seconds |
| eBGP AD / iBGP AD | $20 \;\vert\; 200$ | Administrative Distance |
| OSPF AD | $110$ | Administrative Distance |
| RIP AD | $120$ | Administrative Distance |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Inter-domain routing via BGP is what makes the commercial Internet work. Without it, ISPs could not enforce peering agreements, prevent transit loops, or implement traffic engineering. BGP route hijacks (e.g., the 2008 Pakistan Telecom YouTube hijack) are real-world security concerns studied in network operations.

### 2.4 IGP vs EGP — Conceptual Comparison

| Feature | Intra-Domain (IGP) | Inter-Domain (EGP / BGP) |
|---|---|---|
| Scope | Inside one AS | Between ASes |
| Algorithm | Distance Vector or Link State | Path Vector |
| Metric | Hop count, cost, bandwidth | Policy, path length, MED, LOCAL_PREF |
| Trust Model | Trusted administrators | Distrusted (each AS has own policy) |
| Convergence | Fast (sec for OSPF) | Slow (min to hours for full Internet) |
| Examples | RIP, OSPF, IS-IS, EIGRP | BGPv4 |
| Transport | IP (OSPF) or UDP 520 (RIP) | TCP port 179 |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Distance Vector Update (Bellman-Ford for RIP)

Suppose Router **A** receives a routing table from its neighbor **B**. Router A must decide whether to update its own entry for destination network **N**.

Let:
- $D_A(N)$ = current distance from A to N (in A's table).
- $D_B(N)$ = distance advertised by B to reach N.
- $c(A, B)$ = cost of the direct link from A to B (for RIP, $c = 1$ hop).

**Step 1 — Compute the candidate distance via neighbor B:**

$$
D_{A \to B \to N} = D_B(N) + c(A, B)
$$

**Step 2 — Compare with the current best distance to N in A's table:**

$$
\text{If } D_{A \to B \to N} < D_A(N) \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Update route via B}
$$

**Step 3 — Tie-breaking and infinity rules:**

$$
D_A(N) = \min \left( D_A(N),\; D_B(N) + c(A,B) \right), \quad \text{subject to } D_A(N) \leq 15
$$

> **Conversion logic:** RIP simply adds 1 hop (the cost to reach the neighbor) to whatever distance the neighbor reports. This is why a hop-count metric cannot detect longer-but-faster paths.

### 3.2 Derivation: OSPF Cost Calculation from Interface Bandwidth

The OSPF cost is defined by the standard formula:

$$
\text{Cost} = \left\lfloor \frac{\text{Reference Bandwidth}}{\text{Interface Bandwidth}} \right\rfloor
$$

with the convention $\text{Cost} \geq 1$ (never zero, to avoid division-by-zero in SPF).

**Worked Example:**

Given a router with three interfaces:
- Interface 1: 10 Mbps Ethernet
- Interface 2: 100 Mbps Fast Ethernet
- Interface 3: 1 Gbps Gigabit Ethernet
- Reference Bandwidth = $10^8$ bps (100 Mbps, the legacy Cisco default).

Compute each cost:

$$
\text{Cost}_1 = \frac{10^8}{10 \times 10^6} = \frac{100}{10} = 10
$$

$$
\text{Cost}_2 = \frac{10^8}{100 \times 10^6} = \frac{100}{100} = 1
$$

$$
\text{Cost}_3 = \frac{10^8}{10^9} = \frac{100}{1000} = 0.1 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{rounded up to } 1
$$

> **Engineering Insight:** Because the reference bandwidth is only 100 Mbps, all interfaces 100 Mbps and faster get cost = 1. This is why modern networks set the reference bandwidth to **10 Gbps** (or higher) on Cisco IOS to differentiate between 1G, 10G, and 100G links.

### 3.3 Derivation: Dijkstra's SPF for OSPF (Conceptual Walk-through)

Given a graph $G = (V, E)$ with vertices $V$ = routers and edges $E$ = links weighted by OSPF cost:

**Step 1 — Initialization:** 
- Create two sets: $T$ (Tentative, unvisited) and $P$ (Permanent, finalized).
- $P = \emptyset$. Add source router $s$ to $P$ with distance $0$.

**Step 2 — Update neighbors of $s$:**
- For each neighbor $v$ of $s$, set $D(v) = w(s, v)$ and add to $T$.

**Step 3 — Select minimum from $T$:**
- Pick the vertex $u \in T$ with the smallest tentative distance. Move $u$ to $P$.

**Step 4 — Relax edges from $u$:**
- For each neighbor $v$ of $u$ not in $P$:
$$
D(v) = \min \left( D(v),\; D(u) + w(u, v) \right)
$$

**Step 5 — Termination:** Repeat Steps 3–4 until $T = \emptyset$. The accumulated $D$ values form the shortest-path tree rooted at $s$.

$$
\text{Time Complexity with binary min-heap} = O\bigl((V + E) \log V\bigr)
$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Path-Vector Logic in BGP

For a route $r$ advertised by neighbor $N$ with AS path $\text{AS}_\text{path}(r)$:

$$
\text{Accept } r \;\;\iff\;\; \text{My ASN} \notin \text{AS}_\text{path}(r) \;\;\wedge\;\; r \text{ passes import policy}
$$

If accepted, the router prepends its own ASN before propagating:

$$
\text{AS}_\text{path}(r_\text{new}) = \text{My ASN} \;\Vert\; \text{AS}_\text{path}(r)
$$

The vertical bar $\Vert$ denotes sequence concatenation. This is the **loop-prevention mechanism** of BGP — if a router ever sees its own ASN in the path, the route has traversed a loop and must be discarded.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Hierarchical View: ASes, IGP, and BGP

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Internet[Global Internet] --> AS1[AS 64500 - BSNL ISP Core]
    Internet --> AS2[AS 64501 - Airtel ISP Core]
    Internet --> AS3[AS 64502 - Reliance Jio Core]

    AS1 --> R1[Border Router eBGP Peer]
    AS1 --> R2[Internal Core Router]
    AS1 --> R3[Internal Edge Router]
    AS1 --> R4[Internal Access Router]

    R1 -. eBGP Session over TCP 179 .-> R5[AS2 Border Router]
    R3 -. iBGP Session .-> R4
    R2 --- R3
    R3 --- R4
    R2 --- R1

    subgraph OSPF_DOMAIN[OSPF Area 0 - Intra Domain Routing inside AS64500]
        R2
        R3
        R4
    end

    classDef asNode fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef routerNode fill:#0ea5e9,stroke:#fff,color:#fff

```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Routing Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Packet Arrives at Router] --> B{Destination IP in routing table?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Forward to next-hop via exit interface]
    B -- No --> D{Default route present?}
    D -- Yes --> C
    D -- No --> E[Drop - Send ICMP Destination Unreachable]
    C --> F{Route source}
    F -- Connected --> G[AD equals 0]
    F -- Static --> H[AD equals 1]
    F -- eBGP --> I[AD equals 20]
    F -- OSPF --> J[AD equals 110]
    F -- RIP --> K[AD equals 120]
    F -- iBGP --> L[AD equals 200]
    G --> M[Lowest AD wins - install in RIB]
    H --> M
    I --> M
    J --> M
    K --> M
    L --> M
    M --> N[Best route installed in FIB and forwarded]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: IGP vs EGP Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INTRA[INTRA DOMAIN - INSIDE ONE AS]
        direction TB
        RIP[RIP - Distance Vector\nHop Count - UDP 520]

        ISIS[IS-IS - Link State\nUsed by large ISPs]
    end

    subgraph INTER[INTER DOMAIN - BETWEEN ASES]

```

### 4.4 SPF Tree Visualization (Conceptual Mermaid Block)

```mermaid
graph TD
    SRC[Source Router R1 - Cost 0] --> A[Router R2 - Cost 10]
    SRC --> B[Router R3 - Cost 5]
    A --> C[Router R4 - Cost 20]
    B --> C
    B --> D[Router R5 - Cost 15]
    C --> E[Destination N - Cost 25 via R4]
    D --> E
    D -.Alternative path cost 30.-> E
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The Dijkstra algorithm would select the path R1 → R3 → R4 → Destination (total cost = 5 + 15 + 5 = 25) over R1 → R3 → R5 → Destination (total cost = 5 + 10 + 15 = 30).

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Distinguish between intra-domain and inter-domain routing. Give one example protocol for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Intra-Domain Routing | Inter-Domain Routing |
|---|---|---|
| Scope | Within a single AS | Between different ASes |
| Protocol Class | Interior Gateway Protocol (IGP) | Exterior Gateway Protocol (EGP) |
| Decision Basis | Technical metric (hop count, cost) | Policy + path attributes |
| Example | OSPF (Link State) | BGP (Path Vector) |
| Transport | IP / UDP | TCP port 179 |

*(1 mark for scope, 1 mark for example with class, 1 mark for the distinction in decision basis.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is an Autonomous System? Why is BGP called a path-vector protocol?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- An **Autonomous System (AS)** is a group of IP networks under a single administrative control, identified by a unique ASN (1 mark).
- BGP is called a **path vector** protocol because each BGP update carries the complete **AS_PATH** attribute — the sequence of ASNs the route has traversed (1 mark).
- This AS_PATH serves two purposes: **loop prevention** (a router rejects routes containing its own ASN) and **policy-based best-path selection** (shorter/ preferred paths are chosen via LOCAL_PREF and AS_PATH length) (1 mark).

---

### Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a) Explain the operation of the Distance Vector routing algorithm using the Bellman-Ford method. With a suitable example network of at least 4 routers, show how routing tables are built iteratively. (7 marks)**

**(b) Describe the OSPF protocol in detail: its link-state concept, hierarchical area design, LSA flooding, and SPF computation. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Distance Vector Algorithm — Bellman-Ford (7 marks)**

1. **Definition (1 mark):** In Distance Vector routing, each router maintains a table listing the *best known distance* to every destination and the *next-hop* to reach it. Routers share this table only with **directly connected neighbors** periodically.

2. **Algorithm Steps (2 marks):**
   - Initialize each router's table with directly connected networks (distance = 0).
   - On receiving a neighbor's update, for every destination $N$:
$$
D_{\text{new}}(N) = D_{\text{neighbor}}(N) + c(\text{self}, \text{neighbor})
$$
   - If $D_{\text{new}}(N) < D_{\text{current}}(N)$, update the entry.

3. **Worked Example with 4 routers R1–R4 in a square topology (2 marks):**
   - R1 connected to R2 (cost 1) and R3 (cost 4).
   - R2 connected to R4 (cost 2). R3 connected to R4 (cost 1).
   - **Iteration 0:** Each router knows only its directly connected neighbors.
   - **Iteration 1:** R1 learns from R2: R1 → R2 → R4 = 1 + 2 = 3 hops. R1 also learns from R3: R1 → R3 → R4 = 4 + 1 = 5 hops. R1 picks R2 as next-hop (3 < 5).
   - **Iteration 2:** Tables stabilize. Converged.

4. **Problems (2 marks):**
   - **Count-to-Infinity problem:** When a link fails, the bad news propagates slowly because routers keep advertising stale routes. Solution: **Split Horizon**, **Route Poisoning**, and **Hold-down Timers**.
   - **Slow convergence** in large networks — O(n) iterations for n hops.

---

**(b) OSPF Protocol — Detailed Explanation (7 marks)**

1. **Overview (1 mark):** OSPF is a **link-state, classless IGP** standardized in **RFC 2328**. It uses **IP protocol number 89** directly (no UDP/TCP) and uses the **Dijkstra SPF algorithm**.

2. **Hierarchical Design (2 marks):**
   - **Area 0 (Backbone):** Central transit area. All other areas must connect to it.
   - **Non-Backbone Areas:** Internal areas whose LSAs are summarized by **Area Border Routers (ABRs)** before entering Area 0. This reduces LSDB size and SPF recomputation scope.
   - **Router Types:** Internal Router, ABR, **Backbone Router**, **ASBR (Autonomous System Boundary Router)**.

3. **LSA Types & Flooding (2 marks):**
   - **Type 1 — Router LSA:** Generated by every router; describes its links within an area.
   - **Type 2 — Network LSA:** Generated by the Designated Router (DR) on multi-access segments.
   - **Type 3 — Summary LSA:** Generated by ABRs to advertise networks from one area into another.
   - **Type 5 — External LSA:** Generated by ASBRs to advertise routes from outside the OSPF domain (e.g., redistributed BGP routes).
   - LSAs are flooded reliably using **LSAck** acknowledgments, and sequence numbers prevent duplicates.

4. **SPF Computation (1 mark):** Each router builds an identical **LSDB**, then runs **Dijkstra** to compute the shortest-path tree to every destination. OSPF cost on an interface = $\dfrac{\text{Reference BW}}{\text{Interface BW}}$.

5. **Advantages (1 mark):** Fast convergence, no hop-count limit, VLSM support, hierarchical scalability, authenticated updates (MD5/SHA).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a) Explain the BGP protocol: its eBGP and iBGP flavors, the message types (OPEN, UPDATE, KEEPALIVE, NOTIFICATION), and the key path attributes used for best-path selection. (7 marks)**

**(b) Compare RIP, OSPF, and BGP across a minimum of six technical parameters. Why is BGP unsuitable as an IGP? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) BGP Protocol — Detailed Explanation (7 marks)**

1. **Introduction (1 mark):** BGP version 4 is the **de facto inter-domain routing protocol** of the Internet, standardized in **RFC 4271**. It runs over **TCP port 179** for reliable transport. It is a **path-vector** protocol.

2. **eBGP vs iBGP (2 marks):**
   - **eBGP (External BGP):** Runs between routers in *different* ASes, typically on directly connected interfaces. The default AD is **20**. Used to exchange reachability between ISPs.
   - **iBGP (Internal BGP):** Runs between routers *within the same* AS. Default AD is **200** (less preferred than IGPs to prevent routing loops). Requires either a **full mesh** (impractical beyond ~10 routers) or **Route Reflectors / Confederations** for scalability.

3. **BGP Message Types (2 marks):**
   - **OPEN:** Establishes the session; contains ASN, Hold Time, BGP Identifier, optional capabilities.
   - **UPDATE:** Advertises new routes or withdraws previously advertised routes. Carries NLRI, path attributes.
   - **KEEPALIVE:** Sent every 60 seconds (default) to maintain the session; Hold Time = **180 s**.
   - **NOTIFICATION:** Used to close a session gracefully and report errors.

4. **Key Path Attributes for Best-Path Selection (2 marks):**
   - `WEIGHT` (Cisco proprietary, local) → `LOCAL_PREF` (AS-wide) → Locally originated → `AS_PATH` length (shorter wins) → `ORIGIN` → `MED` → eBGP over iBGP → IGP cost to NEXT_HOP → Router ID.
   - **AS_PATH** is the most visible attribute — it lists the ASNs the route has traversed, providing **loop detection** and **policy signaling**.

---

**(b) Comparison Table and Justification (7 marks)**

| Parameter | RIP (v2) | OSPF | BGP |
|---|---|---|---|
| Algorithm | Distance Vector | Link State | Path Vector |
| Metric | Hop count | Cost (BW-based) | Policy / Path attributes |
| Max Hop Count | 15 | None | None |
| Convergence | Slow (sec-min) | Fast (sec) | Very slow (min-hr) |
| Transport | UDP 520 | IP proto 89 | TCP 179 |
| Administrative Distance | 120 | 110 | eBGP 20 / iBGP 200 |
| Hierarchy / Scalability | Flat | Area-based | AS-based with communities |
| VLSM / CIDR | RIPv2 only | Yes | Yes |
| Loop Prevention | Split horizon, max hops | SPF tree | AS_PATH check |

**Why BGP is unsuitable as an IGP (2 marks):**
1. **Slow convergence** — BGP does not quickly detect failures inside an AS; the Hold Timer is 180 seconds. IGPs need sub-second convergence.
2. **Scalability assumptions** — BGP assumes stable, slowly-changing policy relationships between ASes. Inside an AS, links flap rapidly and policy is uniform.
3. **CPU/Memory overhead** — Maintaining the full Internet routing table (1,000,000+ prefixes in 2024) on every internal router is prohibitively expensive.
4. **No metric optimization** — BGP is policy-driven, so it will not necessarily pick the *fastest* path inside the AS.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing RIP versions:** RIPv1 is classful; RIPv2 supports VLSM and uses multicast 224.0.0.9. Mixing them up loses marks.
> 2. **Wrong metric for OSPF cost:** The default reference bandwidth on modern Cisco IOS is no longer 100 Mbps. State it as a configurable parameter and show the formula clearly.
> 3. **BGP AD confusion:** Many students write "BGP AD = 20" without distinguishing **eBGP (20)** and **iBGP (200)**. This costs a full mark on differentiation questions.
> 4. **Forgetting TCP for BGP:** Examiners expect students to mention BGP runs over **TCP 179** for reliability. UDP/ICMP is wrong.
> 5. **Count-to-infinity without solutions:** If asked about RIP problems, merely stating "count to infinity occurs" is incomplete. Always list the mitigations: split horizon, poison reverse, hold-down timers, triggered updates.
> 6. **OSPF area design rules:** Students often forget that **all non-backbone areas MUST connect to Area 0** via a virtual-link workaround if not. ABRs and ASBRs are also frequently confused.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Autonomous System (AS):** A single administrative domain of IP networks, identified by a unique ASN.
- **Intra-domain routing (IGP):** Routing *inside* one AS. Optimizes for shortest path. Examples: **RIP** (Distance Vector, hop count ≤ 15, UDP 520), **OSPF** (Link State, cost metric, IP 89), **IS-IS**.
- **Inter-domain routing (BGP):** Routing *between* ASes. Optimizes for **policy** and **reachability**. Path-vector protocol over **TCP 179**.
- **RIP** — Simple, slow convergence, vulnerable to count-to-infinity; uses Bellman-Ford.
- **OSPF** — Hierarchical (Area 0 backbone), uses Dijkstra SPF, fast convergence, supports VLSM, LSDB synchronized via LSA flooding.
- **BGP** — eBGP (between ASes, AD 20) and iBGP (within AS, AD 200). Key attributes: LOCAL_PREF, AS_PATH, MED, NEXT_HOP, COMMUNITY. Hold Timer 180 s, Keepalive 60 s.
- **Administrative Distance priority (lower wins):** Connected (0) < Static (1) < eBGP (20) < OSPF (110) < RIP (120) < iBGP (200).
- **Loop prevention mechanisms:** RIP → max hops + split horizon; OSPF → SPF tree + LSA sequence numbers; BGP → AS_PATH check.
- **Convergence hierarchy (fastest → slowest):** OSPF → RIP → EIGRP → BGP.
- **Hierarchical Internet structure:** End-user ASes → Regional ISPs → Tier-1 Transit ISPs (settlement-free peering) — all stitched together by BGP.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Next Generation IP
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAMT632 - BASICS OF COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Next Generation IP

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Next Generation IP (IPv6)** is the successor to the currently dominant Internet Protocol version 4 (IPv4), standardized by the IETF (Internet Engineering Task Force) in RFC 8200 (and its predecessor RFC 2460). It is a **connectionless, best-effort, network-layer datagram protocol** designed to overcome the structural limitations of IPv4 — most critically, the exhaustion of the **32-bit** address space and the lack of native support for modern routing, security, and quality-of-service (QoS) features.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IPv6 uses a 128-bit address**, providing $2^{128}$ unique addresses (approximately **3.4 × 10³⁸**). This is the single most defining characteristic of the Next Generation IP and the primary reason for its development.

### Key Design Goals of IPv6
- **Expanded Addressing Capacity**: 128-bit address space (vs 32-bit in IPv4).
- **Simplified Header Format**: A fixed 40-byte main header to speed up router processing.
- **Improved Routing Efficiency**: Hierarchical addressing and aggregated routing prefixes.
- **Built-in Security**: Mandatory support for IPsec (authentication and confidentiality).
- **Better Support for QoS**: Through the **Flow Label** and **Traffic Class** fields.
- **Auto-configuration**: Stateless Address Autoconfiguration (SLAAC) eliminates the need for DHCP in many cases.
- **Elimination of Broadcasts**: Replaced by efficient **multicast** and **anycast** mechanisms.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The official syllabus refers to IPv6 as "Next Generation IP." Examiners expect students to discuss the rationale for IPv6, the new header format, addressing, and migration strategies.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine the **Internet** as a vast, global postal system.

- **IPv4** is like a postal system where every house can only have a number between **0 and ~4.29 billion**. As the world's population grew and every smartphone, refrigerator, and sensor wanted an address, the system ran out of unique numbers.
- **IPv6** is the postal service **rebuilding the entire numbering system to use 128-bit numbers** — that's a number so large that we could give every grain of sand on Earth its own address, many times over.

Think of the **IPv6 header** as a **streamlined shipping label**:
- IPv4's label was cluttered with options that routers had to read and process on every single parcel — slowing delivery.
- IPv6's new label is a clean, fixed-size form. The "extra options" (called **extension headers**) are moved to a separate sheet that only the final destination (or a specific waystation) bothers to read. This makes routing **vastly faster**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** A courier company (router) sorting parcels. With IPv4, every parcel has a long form with optional fields the courier must check. With IPv6, the main label is always the same size, and optional instructions are in clearly-marked attached envelopes. Sorting speed increases dramatically.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Metric | IPv4 Value | **IPv6 Value** |
|---|---|---|
| Address Length | **32 bits** | **128 bits** |
| Header Length | 20–60 bytes (variable) | **40 bytes (fixed)** |
| Total Address Space | $2^{32} \approx 4.29 \times 10^{9}$ | $2^{128} \approx 3.4 \times 10^{38}$ |
| Address per sq. meter of Earth | ~7 | ~6.67 × 10²³ |
| Fragmentation | Routers + Sender | **Sender only** |
| Broadcasting | Yes | **No (Multicast used)** |
| IPsec Support | Optional | **Built-in / Recommended** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why IPv6? — The Limitations of IPv4

IPv4, designed in the 1970s (RFC 791), was never architected for the scale of the modern Internet. The Next Generation IP was developed to systematically eliminate these structural weaknesses:

1. **Address Exhaustion**: 32-bit space gives only ~4.3 billion addresses. With the explosion of IoT, mobile, and cloud devices, exhaustion became imminent (final IPv4 address allocated by IANA in **2011**).
2. **Routing Table Bloat**: IPv4 routing tables grew uncontrollably due to fragmented class-based addressing (later mitigated by CIDR, but still a problem).
3. **Complex Header**: IPv4's variable-length header (20–60 bytes) with 13 fields, including options that routers had to process, slowed forwarding.
4. **Fragmentation Overhead**: Routers were allowed to fragment packets, leading to inefficient reassembly and vulnerability to attacks.
5. **Security**: IPsec was an optional add-on, not native.
6. **Mobility & Auto-config**: No native support for mobile handoff or plug-and-play addressing.
7. **No Native QoS**: Type of Service (TOS) field was under-utilized and inconsistently interpreted.

## 2.2 IPv6 Address Notation

An IPv6 address is **128 bits**, conventionally written as **8 groups of 4 hexadecimal digits**, separated by colons.

**Example:**
$$\text{FEDC:BA98:7654:3210:0000:0000:0000:0123}$$

### Two Simplification Rules (KTU Frequently Tested)

**Rule 1 — Leading Zero Suppression:**
In any 16-bit group, leading zeros can be removed. A group of all zeros becomes a single `0`.

$$\text{FEDC:0A00:0000:0000:0000:0000:0000:0001} \rightarrow \text{FEDC:A00:0:0:0:0:0:1}$$

**Rule 2 — Double Colon (`::`) Compression:**
One or more **consecutive 16-bit groups of all zeros** can be replaced by a single `::`. The `::` can appear **at most once** in an address.

$$\text{2001:0DB8:0000:0000:0000:0000:0000:0001} \rightarrow \text{2001:DB8::1}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Writing `::` more than once in an address. The standard explicitly allows only one occurrence because the parser cannot determine how many zero groups each `::` represents.

### Prefix Length Notation
Like IPv4 CIDR, IPv6 uses prefix notation:
$$\text{2001:0DB8:1234::/48}$$
This means the first **48 bits** are the network prefix, and the remaining **80 bits** are the host identifier (interface ID).

## 2.3 IPv6 Header Format (40 Bytes — Fixed)

The main IPv6 header is a **fixed 40 bytes**, containing **8 fields**:

| Offset (bits) | Field | Size (bits) | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | Version | 4 | Always set to `6` (binary `0110`) |
| 4 | Traffic Class | 8 | QoS priority / Differentiated Services |
| 12 | Flow Label | 20 | Identifies a flow for QoS handling |
| 32 | Payload Length | 16 | Length of data *after* the header (max 65535) |
| 48 | Next Header | 8 | Type of the **first** extension header (or upper-layer protocol, e.g., 6 = TCP, 17 = UDP) |
| 56 | Hop Limit | 8 | Decremented by 1 at each router; packet discarded when 0 (replaces IPv4 TTL) |
| 64 | Source Address | 128 | Sender's 128-bit IPv6 address |
| 192 | Destination Address | 128 | Final destination's 128-bit address |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** The IPv6 main header does **NOT contain** Fragment Offset, Identification, Header Checksum, or Options fields. Fragmentation is handled by an extension header, and checksum computation is offloaded to the link and transport layers (reducing router processing load).

## 2.4 Extension Headers (KTU Frequently Asked)

To maintain a small main header, IPv6 uses **chained extension headers**. The `Next Header` field in the main header points to the first extension header, which in turn has its own `Next Header` field pointing to the next, and so on.

Common extension headers (in recommended order):

1. **Hop-by-Hop Options** (Next Header = 0)
2. **Destination Options** (Next Header = 60) — for intermediate destinations
3. **Routing Header** (Next Header = 43) — loose source routing
4. **Fragment Header** (Next Header = 44) — for fragmentation
5. **Authentication Header (AH)** (Next Header = 51)
6. **Encapsulating Security Payload (ESP)** (Next Header = 50)
7. **Destination Options** (Next Header = 60) — for final destination
8. **Upper-Layer Header** (e.g., TCP = 6, UDP = 17, ICMPv6 = 58)

## 2.5 IPv6 Address Types

The KTU syllabus emphasizes the three primary address types:

### (a) Unicast
A packet sent to a **single interface**. Types include:
- **Link-Local (FE80::/10)**: Auto-configured, valid only on the local link (like IPv4 169.254.x.x).
- **Unique Local (FC00::/7)**: Private addressing, not routable on the public Internet (replaces IPv4 RFC 1918).
- **Global Unicast (2000::/3)**: Publicly routable addresses.
- **Loopback (::1)**: Equivalent to IPv4 127.0.0.1.
- **Unspecified (::)**: Used by a host that has no address yet (equivalent to IPv4 0.0.0.0).

### (b) Multicast
A packet sent to **all interfaces belonging to a multicast group**. Replaces IPv4 broadcast. Notation: `FF00::/8`.

Well-known multicast addresses:
- `FF02::1` — All nodes on the local link
- `FF02::2` — All routers on the local link
- `FF02::1:FFXX:XXXX` — Solicited-node multicast (used in Neighbor Discovery)

### (c) Anycast
A packet sent to the **nearest** (in routing distance) interface among a group of interfaces sharing the same address. Used for load balancing and high-availability services (e.g., DNS root servers, content delivery). Anycast addresses are syntactically identical to unicast addresses; the distinction is purely in the routing configuration.

> [!NOTE]
> **IPv6 has no broadcast addresses.** The functions traditionally served by IPv4 broadcast (e.g., ARP) are replaced by **multicast** in IPv6.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | IPv6 Address Space | $2^{128} = 3.4028 \times 10^{38}$ | Total unique addresses |
| 2 | Addresses per group | $2^{16} = 65{,}536$ | Each colon-separated group |
| 3 | Header size | **40 bytes** (320 bits) | Fixed for the main header |
| 4 | Maximum payload | $2^{16} - 1 = 65{,}535$ bytes | Specified by 16-bit Payload Length |
| 5 | Jumbogram extension | Up to $2^{32} - 1$ bytes | Used for payloads > 64 KB via Hop-by-Hop header |
| 6 | Hop Limit | 8-bit unsigned | Replaces IPv4 TTL; decremented per hop |
| 7 | Interface ID length | **64 bits** (last 64 of address) | Standard for SLAAC, EUI-64 |
| 8 | Subnet size (typical) | $/64$ prefix $\rightarrow 2^{64}$ addresses | Standard LAN subnet |
| 9 | Total number of $/64$ subnets in $/48$ | $2^{16} = 65{,}536$ | Allocated to an organization |
| 10 | Network bits in $/n$ prefix | $n$ bits | Remainder $128 - n$ bits for host |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Shortcut:** If asked to find the number of subnets or hosts, always use $2^{k}$ where $k$ is the number of bits in the relevant portion. For example, a `/64` prefix gives $2^{64}$ host addresses per subnet.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Mobile Networks (5G/6G):** IPv6's vast address space enables every device to have a globally unique IP, eliminating Carrier-Grade NAT.
- **Internet of Things (IoT):** Allows billions of sensors to be uniquely addressed.
- **Cloud & Data Centers:** Hierarchical addressing simplifies routing at scale.
- **End-to-End Security:** Native IPsec enables confidential and authenticated communication.
- **Network Performance:** Fixed 40-byte header and elimination of header checksum accelerate router processing — critical in **high-throughput backbone networks**.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: How Many Addresses Does IPv6 Provide?

The goal is to verify the well-known $3.4 \times 10^{38}$ figure. The derivation below is exhaustive and shows every algebraic step.

### Step 1: Total Address Space in Bits
An IPv6 address has 128 bits. Each bit can take two values (0 or 1). Therefore, the total number of unique addresses is the total number of unique 128-bit binary strings.

$$\text{Total Addresses} = 2^{128}$$

### Step 2: Express in Decimal
We use the standard identity: $2^{10} \approx 10^{3}$. Therefore:

$$2^{128} = 2^{10 \times 12 + 8} = (2^{10})^{12} \cdot 2^{8}$$

### Step 3: Substitute Numerical Values
We know $2^{10} = 1024 \approx 10^{3.0103}$ and $2^{8} = 256$.

$$2^{128} \approx (10^{3.0103})^{12} \cdot 256 = 10^{36.1236} \cdot 256$$

### Step 4: Approximate
Multiplying by 256 adds approximately $\log_{10}(256) \approx 2.408$ to the exponent.

$$2^{128} \approx 10^{36.1236 + 2.408} = 10^{38.5316}$$

### Step 5: Convert Back from Scientific Notation
The decimal coefficient is:

$$10^{0.5316} \approx 3.402$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{2^{128} \approx 3.4 \times 10^{38}}$$

### Step 6: Sanity Check — Comparison with IPv4
The ratio of IPv6 to IPv4 address space is:

$$\frac{2^{128}}{2^{32}} = 2^{96}$$

The number $2^{96}$ in decimal:

$$2^{96} = (2^{10})^{9} \cdot 2^{6} = 1024^{9} \cdot 64 \approx 7.92 \times 10^{28}$$

So IPv6 has approximately **$7.92 \times 10^{28}$** (~80 octillion) times more addresses than IPv4.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** If a problem asks you to "prove" or "show" the address space calculation, the above 5-step derivation is the model answer pattern. Examiners reward every explicit algebraic transition.

## 3.2 Derivation: Counting Subnets and Hosts

**Problem:** An organization is assigned the prefix `2001:DB8:A000::/48`. The organization wants to create subnets, each with a `/64` prefix. How many subnets are available? How many host addresses per subnet?

### Step 1: Identify the Bits Available for Subnetting
Total prefix length of the assignment: **48 bits**
Desired prefix length per subnet: **64 bits**

Bits available for subnetting (the "subnet ID"):

$$k_{subnet} = 64 - 48 = 16 \text{ bits}$$

### Step 2: Number of Subnets
Each combination of the 16 subnet bits yields a unique subnet.

$$N_{subnets} = 2^{16} = 65{,}536 \text{ subnets}$$

### Step 3: Host Bits per Subnet
Within each `/64` subnet, the host portion (Interface ID) is:

$$k_{host} = 128 - 64 = 64 \text{ bits}$$

### Step 4: Host Addresses per Subnet
$$N_{hosts} = 2^{64} = 18{,}446{,}744{,}073{,}709{,}551{,}616 \text{ addresses per subnet}$$

### Step 5: Total Usable Hosts (in practice)
In IPv6, the standard practice (RFC 4291) is that the all-zeros and all-ones interface IDs are valid, unlike IPv4. However, the `/127` prefix is typically used for point-to-point links to avoid the very rare "subnet-router anycast" ambiguity.

### Final Result
- Number of subnets: $2^{16} = \mathbf{65{,}536}$
- Addresses per subnet: $2^{64} \approx \mathbf{1.844 \times 10^{19}}$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Field-by-Field Comparison of IPv4 and IPv6

This is a **high-yield KTU comparison table** that often appears as a sub-question in 14-mark problems.

| Field / Feature | IPv4 (32-bit) | **IPv6 (128-bit)** | Engineering Impact |
|---|---|---|---|
| Version | 4 | **6** | Identifies protocol version |
| Header Length (IHL) | 4 bits (variable) | **Not present** (fixed) | IPv6 main header is always 40 bytes |
| Total Length | 16 bits | **Replaced by Payload Length** (16 bits) | Payload only, excluding main header |
| Type of Service | 8 bits | **Traffic Class** (8 bits) | Modern DSCP / ECN mapping |
| Identification, Flags, Fragment Offset | 17 bits | **Removed from main header** | Moved to Fragment extension header |
| Time to Live (TTL) | 8 bits | **Hop Limit** (8 bits) | Functionally identical, renamed |
| Protocol | 8 bits | **Next Header** (8 bits) | Can point to extension headers OR upper-layer protocols |
| Header Checksum | 16 bits | **Removed** | Saves router processing; relies on link/transport checksums |
| Source Address | 32 bits | **128 bits** | Vastly expanded |
| Destination Address | 32 bits | **128 bits** | Vastly expanded |
| Options | Variable (up to 40 bytes) | **Not in main header** | Moved to extension headers |
| Flow Label | Absent | **20 bits (new)** | Enables true flow-based QoS |
| Fragmentation | Sender + Routers | **Sender only** | Reduces router workload |
| Header Size | 20–60 bytes | **40 bytes (fixed)** | Predictable, hardware-friendly |

## 3.4 Worked Example: Notation Simplification

**Problem:** Simplify the IPv6 address `2001:0DB8:0000:0000:0000:FF00:0042:8329`.

### Step 1: Apply Leading Zero Suppression

In each 16-bit group, remove leading zeros:

$$\text{2001:0DB8:0000:0000:0000:FF00:0042:8329} \rightarrow \text{2001:DB8:0:0:0:FF00:42:8329}$$

### Step 2: Apply `::` Compression
Three consecutive groups of all zeros appear in the middle. Replace them with `::`:

$$\text{2001:DB8:0:0:0:FF00:42:8329} \rightarrow \text{2001:DB8::FF00:42:8329}$$

### Step 3: Verify the Address Length
Counting the explicit groups after compression: `2001`, `DB8`, `FF00`, `42`, `8329` = **5 explicit groups**. This means `::` represents $8 - 5 = 3$ zero groups. So the full address is:

$$\text{2001:0DB8:0000:0000:0000:FF00:0042:8329}$$

✅ This matches the original. The simplification is **correct**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall Avoidance:** If you also wrote the trailing `::` (e.g., `2001:DB8::`), that would be **wrong** because `::` cannot represent non-zero groups. Always compress **only the consecutive zero groups**.

## 3.5 Worked Example: Number of Routable Subnets Globally

**Problem:** The global unicast block `2000::/3` is reserved for globally routable IPv6 addresses. How many /48 prefixes can be allocated from this block?

### Step 1: Identify Block Sizes
- Reserved block: `/3` → first 3 bits are `001` (which defines `2000::/3`).
- We are allocating `/48` prefixes within this.

### Step 2: Number of Bits for the Allocation
Bits available for distinguishing `/48` allocations:

$$k = 48 - 3 = 45 \text{ bits}$$

### Step 3: Total `/48` Networks
$$N = 2^{45} = 35{,}184{,}372{,}088{,}832 \approx 3.52 \times 10^{13}$$

This is **35 trillion** /48 prefixes, each of which can contain $2^{16} = 65{,}536$ /64 subnets.
