## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define the term "Multiple Access Protocol" and list the three broad categories of MAC protocols used in computer networks.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Multiple Access Protocol is a set of rules (residing in the MAC sub-layer of the Data Link Layer) that governs how multiple stations sharing a common broadcast medium can transmit data without, or with controlled, destructive interference (collisions).

The three broad categories are:
1. **Random Access Protocols** — e.g., ALOHA, Slotted ALOHA, CSMA, CSMA/CD, CSMA/CA.
2. **Controlled Access Protocols** — e.g., Reservation, Polling, Token Passing.
3. **Channelization Protocols** — e.g., FDMA, TDMA, CDMA.

*(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the three categories with examples.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Differentiate between Pure ALOHA and Slotted ALOHA in terms of vulnerable time and maximum throughput.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Pure ALOHA | Slotted ALOHA |
|---|---|---|
| Time synchronization | Not required | Required (global slot clock) |
| Transmission rule | Anytime | Only at slot start |
| Vulnerable time | $2T_t$ | $T_t$ |
| Throughput | $S = G \cdot e^{-2G}$ | $S = G \cdot e^{-G}$ |
| Maximum throughput | $1/(2e) \approx 0.184$ | $1/e \approx 0.368$ |

*(2 marks table + 1 mark conclusion: Slotted ALOHA doubles the efficiency.)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

**Q3A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Q]**
**(a)** Explain the operation of **Pure ALOHA** and **Slotted ALOHA** with neat timing diagrams. Derive the expressions for throughput and find the maximum throughput for both. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Explain the operation of **CSMA/CD** as used in IEEE 802.3 Ethernet. Clearly state the role of the **jam signal**, the **Binary Exponential Backoff** algorithm, and explain why the **minimum frame size is 64 bytes** in 10 Mbps Ethernet. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Pure ALOHA & Slotted ALOHA (7 marks):**

*Operation:*
- In **Pure ALOHA**, a station transmits a frame the moment it has data ready. If no ACK is received within timeout, it retransmits after a random backoff.
- In **Slotted ALOHA**, time is divided into slots of frame duration. Transmission is allowed **only at the beginning of a slot**.

*Timing Diagram:*
```
Pure ALOHA:    |---F1---|   Collision zone
               |   |---F2---|   (2T_t window)
               ...
Slotted ALOHA: | F1 | F2 | F3 | F4 |...
               each slot = T_t
```

*Derivation (3 marks):*
- Poisson model with rate $G$: probability of $k$ arrivals in $t$ is $e^{-Gt}(Gt)^k / k!$.
- Pure ALOHA: vulnerable period $= 2T_t$, $S = G \cdot e^{-2G}$, $S_{max} = 1/(2e) \approx 0.184$ at $G = 0.5$.
- Slotted ALOHA: vulnerable period $= T_t$, $S = G \cdot e^{-G}$, $S_{max} = 1/e \approx 0.368$ at $G = 1$.

**(b) CSMA/CD (7 marks):**

- **Operation:** Station senses carrier; if idle, transmits; if busy, defers. **During** transmission, the station monitors the analog signal level. A voltage exceeding a threshold indicates a **collision**.
- **Jam signal:** A 32-bit intentional interference pattern sent *after* collision detection to ensure **all** other stations (not just the colliding ones) become aware of the collision.
- **Binary Exponential Backoff (BEB):** After $i^{th}$ collision, pick $k$ uniformly from $\{0, 1, \ldots, 2^i - 1\}$ and wait $k \times$ slot time. Cap at $i = 10$ (window = 1023). After 16 collisions, declare the frame lost.
- **Minimum frame size derivation (3 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_t &\ge 2 t_p \\
\frac{L}{R} &\ge 2 t_p \\
L &\ge 2 R t_p = 2 \times 10^7 \times 25.6 \times 10^{-6} = 512 \text{ bits} = 64 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}$$

This guarantees the sender is still on the air when the collision signal returns, enabling detection.

---

**Q3B. [Alternative Choice, KTU University Exam – June 2023, Model Q]**
**(a)** Explain the working of **CSMA/CA** as used in IEEE 802.11 Wi-Fi networks. How does it differ from CSMA/CD? Describe the **RTS/CTS mechanism** and explain how it solves the **hidden terminal problem** with a suitable diagram. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Explain the three **channelization protocols** — **FDMA, TDMA, and CDMA** — with diagrams. Compare them on the basis of bandwidth efficiency, synchronization requirement, and resistance to interference. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) CSMA/CA & Hidden Terminal Problem (7 marks):**

- **Why not CSMA/CD in wireless?** Wireless receivers cannot transmit and listen simultaneously (half-duplex radios). Also, the received signal strength is ~100 dB weaker than transmitted, making collision detection infeasible. Furthermore, the **hidden terminal problem** can cause collisions at the receiver that neither sender senses.
- **CSMA/CA Mechanism:**
  1. **DIFS sensing:** Station waits for the channel to be idle for a DIFS period.
  2. **Random backoff counter** chosen from contention window $[0, CW]$. Timer decrements only while channel idle; freezes when busy.
  3. When counter reaches 0, transmit the frame.
  4. Receiver waits **SIFS**, then sends an **ACK**. No ACK implies failure; $CW$ is doubled (BEB).
- **RTS/CTS:** Sender transmits a short **RTS**; receiver replies with **CTS** after SIFS. All stations hearing either RTS or CTS defer their **NAV (Network Allocation Vector)** for the announced duration.
- **Hidden Terminal Solution:** Suppose stations A and C are both within range of AP B, but A and C cannot sense each other. C sends RTS, A sends RTS simultaneously — collision *at B*. But if A's RTS is *after* C's CTS, A's NAV is set by the CTS it overheard, so A defers → no collision.

**(b) FDMA, TDMA, CDMA (7 marks):**

| Feature | FDMA | TDMA | CDMA |
|---|---|---|---|
| Division | Frequency band per user | Time slot per user | Unique code per user |
| Bandwidth use | Part of total | Full band per slot | Full band always |
| Synchronization | Frequency only | Tight time sync | Chip-level sync |
| Interference | Guard bands needed | Guard slots needed | Spread spectrum tolerant |
| Capacity | Hard (channels fixed) | Hard (slots fixed) | **Soft** (graceful degradation) |
| Example | AMPS (1G) | GSM (2G) | IS-95, UMTS (3G) |

**Diagrams (3 marks):**
- FDMA: horizontal frequency axis split into bands.
- TDMA: time axis split into a periodic frame of slots.
- CDMA: a code-space diagram showing orthogonal Walsh codes.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing CSMA with CSMA/CD:** CSMA *alone* has no collision detection — a frequent 1-mark error.
> 2. **Forgetting "frame time = 1" assumption** in throughput derivations. KTU expects the normalized $G$ (per frame-time), not bits/sec. Always explicitly state the assumption.
> 3. **Mixing up minimum frame size derivation:** The constraint is $T_t \ge 2 t_p$ (round-trip), **not** $T_t \ge t_p$. Half a point lost for this.
> 4. **Writing CDMA "uses different frequencies"** — WRONG. CDMA shares *both* time *and* frequency; it is separated by **code**, not bandwidth. KTU examiners deduct 1 full mark for this.
> 5. **Token Ring: forgetting the Active Monitor role** — a 2-mark deduction possible if the question asks about fault tolerance.
> 6. **Slotted ALOHA vs Pure ALOHA throughput** — many students write $S_{max} = 18.4\%$ for slotted ALOHA. This is a *gross* error: slotted ALOHA's max is **36.8%**, exactly double.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **MAC = Sub-layer of Data Link Layer** that controls channel access. The other sub-layer is **LLC**.
- [ ] **Three families:** Random Access, Controlled Access, Channelization.
- [ ] **Pure ALOHA:** Vulnerable time $= 2T_t$, $S = G e^{-2G}$, $S_{max} = 0.184$ at $G=0.5$.
- [ ] **Slotted ALOHA:** Vulnerable time $= T_t$, $S = G e^{-G}$, $S_{max} = 0.368$ at $G=1$.
- [ ] **CSMA persistent strategies:** 1-persistent (greedy), Non-persistent (less collision, more idle), p-persistent (tunable).
- [ ] **CSMA/CD (Ethernet):** Collision *detection* + **Jam signal (32 bits)** + **BEB**; requires $T_t \ge 2t_p$, leading to **64-byte minimum frame** at 10 Mbps.
- [ ] **CSMA/CA (Wi-Fi):** Collision *avoidance*; uses **DIFS, contention window, RTS/CTS, ACK**; solves hidden terminal.
- [ ] **Controlled access:** Reservation, Polling (Roll-call / Hub), Token Passing (Token Ring, Active Monitor).
- [ ] **FDMA:** Frequency division + guard bands.
- [ ] **TDMA:** Time slots in a periodic frame.
- [ ] **CDMA:** Code division, orthogonal Walsh codes, processing gain, soft capacity.
- [ ] **Key dimensionless parameter:** $a = t_p / T_t$. If $a \ll 1$ → CSMA works; if $a \gg 1$ → channelization is mandatory.
- [ ] **Binary Exponential Backoff:** $k \in [0, 2^i - 1]$ after $i$ collisions, $i \le 10$; abandon after 16.
- [ ] **Slot time** on 10 Mbps Ethernet = **51.2 µs** = 512 bit-times.
- [ ] **Kleinrock's CSMA/CD efficiency:** $S = 1 / (1 + 6.44 a)$ for 1-persistent; $1/(1+3.44a)$ for non-persistent.
- [ ] **Wi-Fi does NOT use CSMA/CD** — always remember to write CSMA/CA.
- [ ] **Most-asked KTU question pattern:** "*Compare Pure ALOHA and Slotted ALOHA. Derive the throughput of each.*" — Practice this until muscle memory.
- [ ] **Examiner pet topics:** (i) Why 64 bytes minimum? (ii) CDMA vs TDMA capacity. (iii) CSMA/CD vs CSMA/CA. (iv) Token ring fault recovery. (v) Binary Exponential Backoff algorithm.
- [ ] **Watch the absolute value notation** — write $\vert x \vert$, not $\vert x\vert$, to avoid LaTeX/handwriting ambiguities.
- [ ] **Practice the Poisson derivation** at least three times before the exam — KTU loves it for 7-mark sub-parts.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Link-Layer Addressing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAMT632 - BASICS OF COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Link-Layer Addressing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Link-Layer Addressing** refers to the use of **Media Access Control (MAC) addresses** — 48-bit hardware identifiers burned into a Network Interface Card (NIC) — to uniquely identify every device on a local area network (LAN) at the **Data Link Layer (Layer 2)** of the OSI model.

The link layer (Layer 2) of the TCP/IP model uses these hardware addresses to deliver frames between adjacent nodes on the same physical network segment. While IP addresses (Layer 3) provide **logical, hierarchical, and routable** addressing across networks, MAC addresses provide **flat, physical, and non-routable** addressing for the final hop delivery on a local link.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine the global postal system:
- **IP Address** is your *full postal address*: "Flat 5B, Green Heights Apartments, MG Road, Kochi, Kerala, India, 682011". It is hierarchical (Country → State → City → Street → Building) and works across the world.
- **MAC Address** is the *nameplate on your apartment door* — a unique identity attached permanently to that specific door, recognized only by the building's local postman (switch/hub).

When a letter (packet) arrives at your building (router), the local postman reads the apartment nameplate (MAC address) to know exactly which door to slide the letter under. This is what **link-layer addressing** does at every hop on a LAN.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** An IP address can change (DHCP, mobility), but the MAC address of a NIC is *globally unique* and typically permanent (burned into hardware by the manufacturer).

### 1.3 Types of Link-Layer Addresses

| Type | Definition | Scope | Analogy |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Unicast** | Addressed to **one specific** NIC on the network | Single device | A personal letter |
| **Multicast** | Addressed to a **group** of NICs that have subscribed | A defined subset | A circular to committee members |
| **Broadcast** | Addressed to **all** NICs on the local subnet | Entire LAN | A public announcement on a loudspeaker |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard MAC Broadcast Address:** All 48 bits set to `1` → represented as `FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF` in hexadecimal.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structure of a 48-bit MAC Address

A MAC address is a **48-bit (6-byte)** identifier, conventionally written in **hexadecimal** with colons or hyphens separating each byte:

$$
\text{MAC} = \text{AA:BB:CC:DD:EE:FF}
$$

This 48-bit space is split into two parts administered by the **IEEE (Institute of Electrical and Electronics Engineers)**:

- **OUI (Organizationally Unique Identifier) — First 24 bits (3 bytes):** Assigned by IEEE to the manufacturer (e.g., Cisco, Intel, Apple). Identifies the *vendor*.
- **NIC-Specific — Last 24 bits (3 bytes):** Assigned by the manufacturer to the individual NIC. Must be unique within that vendor's allocation.

The IEEE also defines two special bits within the first byte:
- **I/G (Individual/Group) bit — LSB of first byte:** `0` = Unicast, `1` = Multicast.
- **U/L (Universal/Local) bit — 2nd LSB of first byte:** `0` = Universally administered (globally unique), `1` = Locally administered (manually overridden).

### 2.2 The Address Resolution Protocol (ARP)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ARP (Address Resolution Protocol)** is the protocol used to **dynamically map a known 32-bit IPv4 address to its unknown 48-bit MAC address** on a local network. It is defined in **RFC 826**.

**Why is ARP needed?**
When a host wants to send an IP packet to another host on the **same LAN**, it knows the destination's IP address (from DNS/routing table) but **not** its MAC address. The frame header, however, requires a MAC address. ARP resolves this gap.

**Operational Phases of ARP:**

1. **Check ARP Cache:** Sender first checks its local ARP table (cache) for the IP-to-MAC mapping. If found, it uses it directly. ARP cache entries typically expire after **15-20 minutes** to handle changes.
2. **ARP Request (Broadcast):** If not in cache, the sender broadcasts an ARP request packet to `FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF`, asking: *"Who has IP 192.168.1.5? Tell 192.168.1.2."*
3. **ARP Reply (Unicast):** The host that owns the target IP responds with a unicast ARP reply containing its MAC address.
4. **Update Cache:** The sender stores the mapping in its ARP table for future use.
5. **Send Frame:** The original IP packet is encapsulated in an Ethernet frame using the resolved MAC address.

### 2.3 Reverse ARP (RARP) and Other Variants

| Protocol | Purpose | Direction |
|---|---|---|
| **ARP** | IP → MAC | Known IP, unknown MAC |
| **RARP** | MAC → IP | Known MAC, unknown IP (mostly obsolete, replaced by BOOTP/DHCP) |
| **Proxy ARP** | A router answers ARP requests on behalf of another host | For hosts that cannot route |
| **Gratuitous ARP** | A host broadcasts its own IP-to-MAC mapping to update other hosts' caches | Used during IP change or for duplicate detection |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| MAC Address Length | $L_{MAC} = 48 \text{ bits} = 6 \text{ bytes}$ | IEEE 802 standard |
| Number of unique MACs | $N = 2^{48}$ | $\approx 2.8 \times 10^{14}$ addresses |
| OUI portion | First $\frac{L_{MAC}}{2} = 24$ bits | Assigned by IEEE |
| NIC portion | Last $\frac{L_{MAC}}{2} = 24$ bits | Assigned by vendor |
| IPv4 to MAC mapping | ARP | Operates only on local subnet |
| IPv6 to MAC mapping | NDP (Neighbor Discovery Protocol) | Uses ICMPv6, no broadcast ARP |
| Hex to Binary | $1 \text{ hex digit} = 4 \text{ bits}$ | $0xF = 1111_2$ |
| Hex Digit Range | $0 \text{ to } F$ | $F_{16} = 1111_2 = 15_{10}$ |
| Broadcast MAC | All 48 bits = 1 | $FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF$ |
| ARP Cache Timeout | $T_{cache} \approx 15-20$ min | OS-dependent |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** ARP is the silent workhorse of every LAN. Every time you visit a website, watch a YouTube video, or print to a network printer, ARP is being called dozens of times per minute to translate the IP destination into the correct hardware MAC address. Network engineers use tools like `arp -a` (Windows/Linux) to view and troubleshoot these mappings.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 MAC Address Hex-to-Binary Decomposition

**Problem:** Given the MAC address `4A:30:10:21:0A:42`, identify:
1. The OUI (vendor portion)
2. The NIC-specific portion
3. The I/G and U/L bits of the first byte
4. The type of address (unicast/multicast, universal/local)

**Step 1: Write the first byte in binary.**

The first byte is `4A` in hex.

$$
4A_{16} = 4 \times 16^1 + A \times 16^0 = 4 \times 16 + 10 \times 1 = 64 + 10 = 74_{10}
$$

Converting to binary (4 bits per hex digit):
- `4` in hex = `0100` in binary
- `A` in hex = `1010` in binary

$$
4A_{16} = 0100\,1010_{2}
$$

**Step 2: Identify the I/G and U/L bits.**

The first byte in binary is `01001010`. Reading from LSB (rightmost):
- **Bit 0 (I/G) = 0** → This is a **Unicast** address.
- **Bit 1 (U/L) = 1** → This is a **Locally administered** address.

**Step 3: Identify the OUI and NIC portions.**

$$
\text{MAC: } \underbrace{4A:30:10}_{\text{OUI (Vendor)}} : \underbrace{21:0A:42}_{\text{NIC (Device-specific)}}
$$

- **OUI = `4A:30:10`** → Registered to the manufacturer with this IEEE-assigned prefix.
- **NIC = `21:0A:42`** → Unique serial assigned by that manufacturer to this specific NIC.

### 3.2 Derivation: Number of Unique MAC Addresses

The MAC address space has 48 bits, so the total number of unique addresses is:

$$
N = 2^{48}
$$

Expanding the exponent to base-10 approximation:

$$
N = 2^{48} = (2^{10})^{4} \times 2^{8} = 1024^{4} \times 256
$$

We know $2^{10} = 1024 \approx 10^3$, so:

$$
N \approx (10^3)^{4} \times 10^{2.4} \approx 10^{12} \times 10^{2.4} = 10^{14.4}
$$

$$
N \approx 2.815 \times 10^{14}
$$

This is approximately **281 trillion** unique MAC addresses — sufficient to assign a unique address to every network device ever manufactured.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: ARP Operation in a LAN

Consider Host A (IP = `192.168.1.2`, MAC = `AA:AA:AA:AA:AA:AA`) wanting to send a packet to Host B (IP = `192.168.1.5`, MAC = `BB:BB:BB:BB:BB:BB`) on the same LAN via a switch.

**Step 1: Host A checks its ARP cache for `192.168.1.5`.**
- **Result:** Cache miss. Mapping unknown.

**Step 2: Host A constructs the ARP Request packet.**

The ARP request has the following logical structure:

$$
\text{ARP Request} = \{
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Hardware Type (HTYPE)} = 1 \quad (\text{Ethernet}) \\
&\text{Protocol Type (PTYPE)} = 0x0800 \quad (\text{IPv4}) \\
&\text{Hardware Address Length (HLEN)} = 6 \quad (\text{bytes}) \\
&\text{Protocol Address Length (PLEN)} = 4 \quad (\text{bytes}) \\
&\text{Opcode} = 1 \quad (\text{Request}) \\
&\text{Sender Hardware Address} = \text{AA:AA:AA:AA:AA:AA} \\
&\text{Sender Protocol Address} = 192.168.1.2 \\
&\text{Target Hardware Address} = 00:00:00:00:00:00 \quad (\text{unknown, so zero}) \\
&\text{Target Protocol Address} = 192.168.1.5
\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Host A encapsulates the ARP request in an Ethernet frame.**

The Ethernet frame header carries the broadcast destination MAC:

$$
\text{Frame Dest MAC} = \text{FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF}
$$

$$
\text{Frame Source MAC} = \text{AA:AA:AA:AA:AA:AA}
$$

$$
\text{Frame Type} = 0x0806 \quad (\text{ARP})
$$

**Step 4: The switch receives the broadcast frame and floods it to all ports except the incoming one.**

All hosts on the LAN receive the ARP request. Each host checks if the target IP (`192.168.1.5`) matches their own.

**Step 5: Host B recognizes its IP and prepares an ARP Reply.**

Host B updates its own ARP cache with Host A's mapping, then constructs a **unicast** reply (since it now knows Host A's MAC):

$$
\text{ARP Reply} = \{
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Opcode} = 2 \quad (\text{Reply}) \\
&\text{Sender Hardware Address} = \text{BB:BB:BB:BB:BB:BB} \\
&\text{Sender Protocol Address} = 192.168.1.5 \\
&\text{Target Hardware Address} = \text{AA:AA:AA:AA:AA:AA} \\
&\text{Target Protocol Address} = 192.168.1.2
\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Host A receives the ARP Reply and updates its cache.**

$$
\text{Host A ARP Cache:} \quad 192.168.1.5 \rightarrow \text{BB:BB:BB:BB:BB:BB}
$$

**Step 7: Host A now sends the original IP packet in a frame with the correct destination MAC.**

### 3.4 Boundary Conditions and Edge Cases

- **ARP only works within a single broadcast domain (subnet).** To reach a host on a different subnet, the sender resolves the MAC of its **default gateway**, not the final destination.
- **ARP cache poisoning / spoofing** is a known security attack where a malicious host sends forged ARP replies to associate its MAC with another host's IP, enabling **Man-in-the-Middle (MITM)** interception. Mitigations include **Dynamic ARP Inspection (DAI)** and **static ARP entries**.
- **IPv6 does not use ARP.** It uses **NDP (Neighbor Discovery Protocol)** with multicast ICMPv6 messages (Neighbor Solicitation / Neighbor Advertisement), which is more secure and efficient.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 MAC Address Bit-Structure (Functional Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef ou fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#C92A2A,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef nic fill:#4ECDC4,stroke:#0B7285,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef bit fill:#FFD93D,stroke:#F08C00,color:#000000
    classDef special fill:#A78BFA,stroke:#5F3DC4,color:#FFFFFF

    A["48-bit MAC Address"]:::bit
    A --> B["First 24 bits: OUI"]:::ou
    A --> C["Last 24 bits: NIC Specific"]:::nic
    B --> D["Byte 1: First 3 bytes"]:::ou
    D --> E["Bit 0: I/G Flag"]:::special
    D --> F["Bit 1: U/L Flag"]:::special
    D --> G["Bits 2-23: Vendor Code"]:::ou
    C --> H["Bytes 4-6: Device Serial"]:::nic
```

### 4.2 ARP Operation Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef hostA fill:#74C0FC,stroke:#1971C2,color:#000000
    classDef hostB fill:#B197FC,stroke:#6741D9,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef cache fill:#FFE066,stroke:#F08C00,color:#000000
    classDef decision fill:#63E6BE,stroke:#0CA678,color:#000000
    classDef broadcast fill:#FF8787,stroke:#C92A2A,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef unicast fill:#69DB7C,stroke:#2F9E44,color:#FFFFFF

    Start([Host A wants to send IP packet to 192.168.1.5]):::hostA
    Check{Check ARP Cache}:::decision
    Found[Mapping Found in Cache]:::cache
    NotFound[Cache Miss]:::hostA
    BuildReq[Build ARP Request]:::hostA
    Broadcast[Broadcast ARP Request to FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF]:::broadcast
    BCheck[Host B receives request, IP matches]:::hostB
    BReply[Host B sends Unicast ARP Reply]:::unicast
    AUpdate[Host A updates ARP Cache]:::cache
    SendFrame[Host A sends frame with resolved MAC]:::unicast
    End([Frame delivered to Host B]):::hostB

    Start --> Check
    Check -->|Hit| Found
    Check -->|Miss| NotFound
    Found --> SendFrame
    NotFound --> BuildReq
    BuildReq --> Broadcast
    Broadcast --> BCheck
    BCheck -->|Match| BReply
    BReply --> AUpdate
    AUpdate --> SendFrame
    SendFrame --> End
```

### 4.3 ARP Packet Structure (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef eth fill:#FFA94D,stroke:#D9480F,color:#000000
    classDef arp fill:#74C0FC,stroke:#1971C2,color:#000000
    classDef pad fill:#CED4DA,stroke:#495057,color:#000000

    Frame["Ethernet Frame"]:::eth
    Frame --> DA["Destination MAC: 6 bytes"]:::eth
    Frame --> SA["Source MAC: 6 bytes"]:::eth
    Frame --> ET["EtherType: 0x0806 (ARP)"]:::eth
    Frame --> Payload["ARP Payload: 28 bytes"]:::arp
    Payload --> H1["HTYPE: 1 (Ethernet)"]:::arp
    Payload --> H2["PTYPE: 0x0800 (IPv4)"]:::arp
    Payload --> H3["HLEN: 6"]:::arp
    Payload --> H4["PLEN: 4"]:::arp
    Payload --> H5["OPER: 1=Request / 2=Reply"]:::arp
    Payload --> H6["SHA: Sender HW Address (6B)"]:::arp
    Payload --> H7["SPA: Sender Protocol Address (4B)"]:::arp
    Payload --> H8["THA: Target HW Address (6B)"]:::arp
    Payload --> H9["TPA: Target Protocol Address (4B)"]:::arp
```

### 4.4 Communication Path: Same Subnet vs. Different Subnet

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef local fill:#69DB7C,stroke:#2F9E44,color:#000000
    classDef remote fill:#FFA94D,stroke:#D9480F,color:#000000
    classDef router fill:#B197FC,stroke:#6741D9,color:#FFFFFF

    A1["Host A: 192.168.1.2 / AA:AA:AA"]:::local
    B1["Host B: 192.168.1.5 / BB:BB:BB"]:::local
    AR1["ARP for Host B's MAC directly"]:::local

    A2["Host A: 192.168.1.2 / AA:AA:AA"]:::remote
    GW["Default Gateway: 192.168.1.1 / CC:CC:CC"]:::router
    B2["Host B: 10.0.0.5 / DD:DD:DD"]:::remote
    AR2["ARP for Gateway's MAC"]:::remote

    A1 --> AR1 --> B1
    A2 --> AR2 --> GW
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*What is a MAC address? Explain the structure of a 48-bit MAC address with the role of the OUI.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **MAC (Media Access Control) address** is a **48-bit unique hardware identifier** assigned to the Network Interface Card (NIC) of a device, used for communication at the **Data Link Layer (Layer 2)**. It is also called the physical address or hardware address.

**Structure of a 48-bit MAC Address:**

The 48 bits are divided into two parts:

1. **OUI (Organizationally Unique Identifier) — 24 bits (first 3 bytes):** This portion is assigned by the **IEEE** to the manufacturer/vendor (e.g., `00:1A:2B` for Cisco). It identifies the company that produced the NIC.
2. **NIC-Specific / Device Serial — 24 bits (last 3 bytes):** This portion is assigned by the manufacturer and must be unique for every NIC produced by that vendor. It identifies the specific device.

**Example:** In `00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E`, the OUI is `00:1A:2B` (vendor code) and the device identifier is `3C:4D:5E`.

The complete address is written in hexadecimal format (e.g., `AA:BB:CC:DD:EE:FF`) and is globally unique, with a total address space of $2^{48} \approx 2.8 \times 10^{14}$ unique values. *(3 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024]**
*Explain the purpose of ARP and differentiate between ARP Request and ARP Reply.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Purpose of ARP:** The **Address Resolution Protocol (ARP)** is used to **map a known 32-bit IP address (Layer 3) to its unknown 48-bit MAC address (Layer 2)** on a local network. It bridges the gap between the logical and physical addressing layers, enabling the construction of correct Ethernet frames for IP packet delivery.

**ARP Request vs. ARP Reply:**

| Parameter | ARP Request | ARP Reply |
|---|---|---|
| **Sent By** | Host seeking the MAC of a target IP | Host that owns the target IP |
| **Destination MAC** | Broadcast: `FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF` | Unicast: directly to the requester |
| **Opcode** | 1 (Request) | 2 (Reply) |
| **Target HW Address Field** | All zeros (unknown) | Filled with requester's MAC |
| **Purpose** | "Who has IP X? Tell me your MAC." | "I have IP X. Here is my MAC." |

The request is broadcast to all hosts on the LAN; the reply is sent as a unicast frame back to the requester. *(3 marks)*

---

### Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Part B questions are 14 marks with a 7+7 sub-part structure. Below are two alternative choices with full model solutions.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a) Explain the operation of the Address Resolution Protocol (ARP) with a neat diagram. Describe the contents of the ARP packet. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** ARP is a Layer 2.5 protocol that dynamically maps an IPv4 address to the corresponding MAC address on a local network segment.

**Operation of ARP (Step-by-Step):**

1. **Cache Check:** The sending host checks its ARP cache (a table of IP-to-MAC mappings) for the destination IP. If found, the MAC is used immediately.
2. **ARP Request (Broadcast):** If the mapping is not cached, the sender constructs an ARP Request packet and broadcasts it to the entire LAN using the broadcast MAC address `FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF`. The packet asks: *"If your IP address is X.X.X.X, please send your MAC address back to me."*
3. **Reception and Match:** Every host on the LAN receives the broadcast. Each host compares the target IP with its own IP. Only the host that owns the matching IP processes the request.
4. **ARP Reply (Unicast):** The target host sends a unicast ARP Reply directly to the requester's MAC, containing its own IP-to-MAC mapping. The Opcode field changes from 1 (request) to 2 (reply).
5. **Cache Update:** The original sender receives the reply and stores the new mapping in its ARP cache for future use (entries typically expire after 15-20 minutes).
6. **Frame Transmission:** The original IP packet is now encapsulated in an Ethernet frame with the resolved destination MAC and sent.

**Contents of the ARP Packet (28 bytes total):**

| Field | Size | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Hardware Type (HTYPE) | 2 bytes | 1 = Ethernet |
| Protocol Type (PTYPE) | 2 bytes | 0x0800 = IPv4 |
| Hardware Address Length (HLEN) | 1 byte | 6 for Ethernet |
| Protocol Address Length (PLEN) | 1 byte | 4 for IPv4 |
| Opcode | 2 bytes | 1 = Request, 2 = Reply |
| Sender Hardware Address (SHA) | 6 bytes | MAC of sender |
| Sender Protocol Address (SPA) | 4 bytes | IP of sender |
| Target Hardware Address (THA) | 6 bytes | MAC of target (0 in request) |
| Target Protocol Address (TPA) | 4 bytes | IP of target |

*(7 marks: 4 for operation + 3 for packet structure)*

---

**(b) What is the difference between MAC and IP addresses? Why is ARP necessary if IP addresses already identify hosts? Explain the role of ARP cache. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**MAC vs. IP Address Comparison:**

| Parameter | MAC Address | IP Address |
|---|---|---|
| **OSI Layer** | Layer 2 (Data Link) | Layer 3 (Network) |
| **Size** | 48 bits | 32 bits (IPv4) / 128 bits (IPv6) |
| **Assignment** | Burned into NIC by manufacturer | Assigned by network admin/DHCP |
| **Scope** | Local network (flat) | Global (hierarchical) |
| **Uniqueness** | Globally unique (in theory) | Unique within a network |
| **Routability** | Not routable across subnets | Routable across networks |
| **Persistence** | Permanent (hardware) | Dynamic (can change) |

**Why ARP is necessary despite IP addresses:**
IP addresses are logical identifiers used for **end-to-end routing** across multiple networks. However, the **actual physical delivery of frames on a local Ethernet segment** requires the destination's hardware MAC address. The IP address is used by routers to forward packets across networks, but once a packet arrives at the destination LAN, the switch needs a MAC address to deliver the frame to the correct physical port. ARP provides this critical **IP-to-MAC translation** that enables the final hop of delivery.

**Role of ARP Cache:**
The ARP cache (or ARP table) is a local data structure maintained by every host that stores recently resolved IP-to-MAC mappings. Its roles include:
- **Performance optimization:** It eliminates the need to broadcast ARP requests for every packet. A cached entry is used directly, drastically reducing broadcast traffic.
- **Network efficiency:** Reduces network congestion by minimizing ARP broadcasts.
- **Dynamic updates:** Entries are added when new mappings are learned and refreshed upon use.
- **Timeout mechanism:** Entries expire (typically 15-20 minutes) to handle topology changes and host mobility.
- **Static entries:** Critical servers/gateways can have manually entered static ARP entries for security and stability.

*(7 marks: 3 for comparison + 2 for ARP necessity + 2 for cache role)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - June 2024]**

**(a) Explain the different types of MAC addresses. Describe the format of a 48-bit MAC address with reference to I/G and U/L bits. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Types of MAC Addresses:**

1. **Unicast MAC Address:** A MAC address that identifies a **single, specific NIC** on the network. When a frame is sent to a unicast MAC, only that particular device accepts and processes the frame. The I/G bit = 0.
   - *Example:* `AA:BB:CC:DD:EE:FF`

2. **Multicast MAC Address:** A MAC address that identifies a **group of NICs** that have subscribed to receive frames destined for that group. The I/G bit = 1.
   - *Example:* `01:00:5E:00:00:05` (used in IP multicast mapping).

3. **Broadcast MAC Address:** A special MAC address (`FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF`) that addresses **all hosts on the local network segment**. Every NIC on the LAN processes broadcast frames. Theoretically, this is also a multicast (I/G = 1) but is reserved by convention.

**Format of a 48-bit MAC Address (Bit Layout):**

$$
\text{MAC: } [b_{47} \ldots b_{40}] \cdot [b_{39} \ldots b_{32}] \cdot [b_{31} \ldots b_{24}] \cdot [b_{23} \ldots b_{16}] \cdot [b_{15} \ldots b_{8}] \cdot [b_{7} \ldots b_{0}]
$$

| Bit Position | Name | Function |
|---|---|---|
| $b_0$ (LSB of byte 0) | **I/G** | 0 = Individual (Unicast), 1 = Group (Multicast) |
| $b_1$ (2nd LSB of byte 0) | **U/L** | 0 = Universally administered, 1 = Locally administered |
| $b_{47} \ldots b_{24}$ | **OUI** | 24-bit vendor ID assigned by IEEE |
| $b_{23} \ldots b_{0}$ | **NIC ID** | 24-bit device-unique serial number |

**Example:** Consider MAC `4A:30:10:21:0A:42`.
- First byte `4A` = `0100\,1010` in binary.
- Bit 0 (I/G) = 0 → **Unicast**.
- Bit 1 (U/L) = 1 → **Locally administered**.
- OUI = `4A:30:10` (vendor code), NIC portion = `21:0A:42`.

*(7 marks: 3 for types + 4 for format and bit analysis)*

---

**(b) With an example, describe the complete ARP process when Host A (`192.168.1.10`) wants to communicate with Host B (`192.168.1.20`) on the same LAN. What happens if Host B is on a different network? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**ARP Process for Same-LAN Communication:**

**Setup:** Host A (IP = `192.168.1.10`, MAC = `AA:AA:AA:AA:AA:AA`) sends a packet to Host B (IP = `192.168.1.20`, MAC = `BB:BB:BB:BB:BB:BB`).

**Step 1 — Application triggers packet:** When Host A's application sends data to `192.168.1.20`, the OS checks if `192.168.1.20` is on the same subnet (via subnet mask, e.g., `255.255.255.0`). Yes, it is.

**Step 2 — ARP Cache Lookup:** Host A checks its ARP cache for `192.168.1.20`. If a fresh entry exists mapping it to `BB:BB:BB:BB:BB:BB`, that MAC is used directly. If not, ARP proceeds.

**Step 3 — ARP Request Broadcast:** Host A constructs an ARP request and broadcasts it to `FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF`:
- *"I am `192.168.1.10` with MAC `AA:AA:AA:AA:AA:AA`. Who has IP `192.168.1.20`? Please reply with your MAC."*

**Step 4 — LAN-wide Delivery:** The switch floods the broadcast to all ports. All hosts (including Host B) receive it.

**Step 5 — Host B Identifies Itself:** Host B sees its own IP (`192.168.1.20`) in the Target Protocol Address field and prepares a unicast reply.

**Step 6 — ARP Reply:** Host B sends a unicast ARP reply to `AA:AA:AA:AA:AA:AA`:
- *"I am `192.168.1.20` and my MAC is `BB:BB:BB:BB:BB:BB`."*

**Step 7 — Cache Update:** Host A receives the reply, adds the entry `192.168.1.20 → BB:BB:BB:BB:BB:BB` to its ARP cache.

**Step 8 — Data Transmission:** Host A encapsulates the IP packet in an Ethernet frame with:
- Destination MAC = `BB:BB:BB:BB:BB:BB`
- Source MAC = `AA:AA:AA:AA:AA:AA`
- EtherType = `0x0800` (IPv4)

The switch delivers the frame to Host B's port. ✓

---

**Scenario: Host B on a Different Network**

If Host B (e.g., `10.0.0.20`) is on a different subnet, Host A's routing table directs the packet to its **default gateway** (e.g., `192.168.1.1`).

In this case, ARP is used to resolve the **gateway's MAC**, NOT Host B's MAC:
- Host A broadcasts an ARP request: *"Who has `192.168.1.1`?"*
- The router replies with its MAC (e.g., `CC:CC:CC:CC:CC:CC`).
- Host A sends the frame to the router's MAC. The IP destination remains Host B's IP.
- The router then performs its own ARP process on the next-hop LAN to forward the packet to Host B.

**Key Insight:** ARP only resolves addresses **one hop at a time**, on the local physical network. End-to-end IP addresses are preserved at every hop; only MAC addresses change.

*(7 marks: 4 for same-LAN ARP process + 3 for different-network case)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Confusing MAC with IP scope:** Students often write that MAC addresses are "routable" — they are **not**. MAC addresses are link-local only.
> - **Wrong broadcast MAC format:** Writing `FF-FF-FF` or `255.255.255.255` (the latter is the IP broadcast, not MAC). The correct MAC broadcast is `FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF` in hex.
> - **Forgetting I/G and U/L bits:** Many students omit these critical bits when asked about MAC address structure. Always mention them.
> - **Mixing ARP cache timeout:** The default is OS-dependent, typically 15-20 minutes on Windows/Linux. Do not state a single hard value.
> - **Skipping the diagram:** KTU examiners expect a clear ARP flow diagram for 7-mark questions. Without it, expect to lose 1-2 marks.
> - **Confusing RARP with ARP:** RARP is MAC-to-IP (mostly obsolete). Do not state it as a current operational protocol.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Link-Layer Addressing**

- [x] **MAC Address** = 48-bit hardware ID, unique per NIC, Layer 2.
- [x] **Format:** 12 hex digits, 6 bytes, written as `XX:XX:XX:XX:XX:XX`.
- [x] **OUI (24 bits)** = Vendor code assigned by IEEE.
- [x] **NIC portion (24 bits)** = Device serial, unique per vendor.
- [x] **I/G bit (bit 0 of byte 0):** 0 = Unicast, 1 = Multicast.
- [x] **U/L bit (bit 1 of byte 0):** 0 = Universal, 1 = Local.
- [x] **Broadcast MAC:** `FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF` (all 48 bits = 1).
- [x] **ARP** = IP → MAC resolution (RFC 826), operates only on local subnet.
- [x] **ARP Request = Broadcast** (Opcode 1); **ARP Reply = Unicast** (Opcode 2).
- [x] **ARP Cache** stores mappings; entries expire in ~15-20 minutes.
- [x] **RARP** = MAC → IP (obsolete, replaced by BOOTP/DHCP).
- [x] **IPv6** uses **NDP**, not ARP.
- [x] **Cross-subnet communication** uses ARP only for the **default gateway's MAC**.
- [x] **Total unique MACs:** $2^{48} \approx 2.8 \times 10^{14}$.
- [x] **Security risk:** ARP Spoofing/Poisoning — mitigated by Dynamic ARP Inspection.
- [x] **Standard Exam Pitfall:** Never claim MAC addresses are routable. They are strictly local.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ethernet Protocol
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAMT632 - BASICS OF COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Ethernet Protocol

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Ethernet** is a family of wired networking technologies defined by the **IEEE 802.2/802.3** standard family that specifies the Physical Layer (PHY) and the Media Access Control (MAC) sublayer of the Data Link Layer (DLL) of the OSI reference model. It is the dominant **Local Area Network (LAN)** technology, originally developed by Robert Metcalfe and David Boggs at Xerox PARC in **1973** and later standardized by the **IEEE 802.3 working group** in **1985** (10BASE-5 "Thick Ethernet").

In the KTU 2024 syllabus (Module 3 – Data Link Layer), Ethernet is positioned as the canonical implementation of the IEEE 802 LAN/MAN architecture, where the Logical Link Control (LLC) sublayer (IEEE 802.2) provides a uniform interface to the upper layers, and the **MAC sublayer (IEEE 802.3)** handles framing, addressing, and channel access.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Module 3):**
> Students must distinguish between three closely related standards:
> - **IEEE 802.3** → Ethernet (CSMA/CD-based, originally half-duplex bus).
> - **IEEE 802.4** → Token Bus (now obsolete).
> - **IEEE 802.5** → Token Ring (now legacy).
> 
> KTU frequently asks students to *compare* the MAC protocols of 802.3 vs. 802.5.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **group discussion in a classroom** with no raised-hand rule:

- Everyone speaks whenever they want, but **only one person can be heard at a time** (the shared medium = the air in the room).
- If two people talk **simultaneously**, a "collision" occurs — neither is understood.
- After the collision, both people **pause**, wait a random short time, and try again (**CSMA/CD** behavior).

Ethernet on a coaxial bus (legacy 10BASE-5/10BASE-2) worked exactly this way. Modern Ethernet (switched, full-duplex over twisted pair) behaves more like a **direct private telephone call** — no sharing, no collisions, dedicated point-to-point link between each device and the switch.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Distinction for Exams:**
> - **Legacy Ethernet (Bus, Hub)** → Multiple devices share one channel → Collisions possible → Uses **CSMA/CD**.
> - **Modern Ethernet (Switch, Full-Duplex)** → Dedicated point-to-point links → No collisions → **CSMA/CD is disabled**.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants (Bold Highlighted)

| Metric | Value | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Standard Minimum Frame Size** | **64 bytes** (512 bits) | Guarantees collision detection on a 2500 m 10 Mbps network |
| **Standard Maximum Frame Size** | **1518 bytes** (1522 with VLAN tag) | Prevents one station from monopolizing the medium |
| **Maximum Network Diameter (10BASE-5)** | **2500 m** | Defines the 51.2 µs slot time |
| **Slot Time (10 Mbps Ethernet)** | **51.2 µs** | Time for 512-bit signal to traverse max round-trip |
| **Inter-Frame Gap (IFG)** | **96 bit-times** | Silence between consecutive frames |
| **Jam Signal Duration** | **32 bits** | Signal sent after collision to reinforce detection |
| **Preamble** | **7 bytes** (56 bits) | Synchronization pattern `10101010...` |
| **Start Frame Delimiter (SFD)** | **1 byte** (`10101011`) | Marks start of frame |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of the Ethernet Protocol Stack

Ethernet operates across the **lower two layers** of the OSI model. KTU expects students to map Ethernet components precisely.

### 2.1.1 IEEE 802 Architecture Mapping

| OSI Layer | IEEE 802.x Sublayer | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Layer 2 (Data Link) | **LLC (802.2)** | Multiplexing upper-layer protocols, error notification, flow control |
| Layer 2 (Data Link) | **MAC (802.3)** | Framing, MAC addressing (48-bit), CSMA/CD access |
| Layer 1 (Physical) | **PHY (802.3 clauses)** | Bit encoding (Manchester, MLT-3, PAM-5), signaling, cable specs |

### 2.1.2 The Ethernet Frame Format (IEEE 802.3)

Every Ethernet frame has the following structure (byte-aligned):

```
+----------+--------+---------+--------+-----------+-----+-----+-----+--------+
| Preamble |  SFD   | Dest.   | Source | Length/   | LLC | Pad |  FCS |  IFG  |
| (7 B)    | (1 B)  | MAC     | MAC    | Type      | Data|     |(4 B)| (12 B)|
|          |        | (6 B)   | (6 B)  | (2 B)     |     |     |     |       |
+----------+--------+---------+--------+-----------+-----+-----+-----+--------+
   7B        1B       6B        6B        2B        46-1500B  0-46B  4B     12B
```

**Field-by-Field Explanation:**

- **Preamble (7 B)**: Alternating `1010...` bit pattern for clock synchronization between sender and receiver.
- **Start Frame Delimiter (1 B)**: `10101011` — breaks the pattern and signals "frame starts here."
- **Destination MAC (6 B)**: 48-bit hardware address. First 24 bits = **OUI (Organizationally Unique Identifier)** assigned by IEEE; last 24 bits = device-specific.
- **Source MAC (6 B)**: Sender's hardware address.
- **Length/Type (2 B)**: 
  - If value ≤ 1500 → **Length** field (IEEE 802.3 standard).
  - If value ≥ 1536 (0x0600) → **Type** field (DIX/Ethernet II standard, e.g., 0x0800 = IPv4).
- **LLC Data (46–1500 B)**: Payload. *Minimum 46 bytes* ensures total frame ≥ 64 B.
- **Pad (0–46 B)**: Zero-padding to reach 64 B minimum.
- **FCS (4 B)**: Frame Check Sequence — **CRC-32** over all fields except preamble/SFD/FCS itself.
- **IFG (12 B = 96 bit-times)**: Idle period required between consecutive frames.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The **minimum frame size of 64 bytes** is *not arbitrary*. It is derived from the **maximum network propagation delay** required to detect collisions on a 10 Mbps network spanning 2500 m. Always show this derivation in Part B answers.

### 2.1.3 CSMA/CD — The Channel Access Protocol

**CSMA/CD = Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Detection.** It is the heart of legacy half-duplex Ethernet.

**Algorithm (3-step decision logic):**

1. **Carrier Sense (CS):** A station that wishes to transmit first listens to the medium. If the medium is **idle** for ≥ 96 bit-times (the IFG), it may transmit. If busy, it waits.
2. **Multiple Access (MA):** Multiple stations share the same medium — no central controller.
3. **Collision Detection (CD):** While transmitting, the station monitors the signal level. If voltage/power exceeds a threshold (a collision), it:
   - Stops transmission immediately.
   - Sends a **32-bit jam signal** to ensure all stations detect the collision.
   - Increments its **collision counter (n)**.
   - Waits a **random backoff** period before retrying.

### 2.1.4 The Binary Exponential Backoff (BEB) Algorithm

After the *n*-th collision (n ≤ 16), the station picks a random integer:

$$
k \in \{0,\ 1,\ 2,\ \ldots,\ (2^{n} - 1)\}
$$

and waits:

$$
T_{\text{wait}} = k \times 51.2\ \mu s
$$

After **16 collisions**, the station gives up and reports an error to the upper layer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Binary Exponential"?** Because the slot count **doubles** with each collision (1, 2, 4, 8, 16, 32…). This *adapts* the backoff window to network load — small at first, growing aggressively as contention worsens.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Unit | Notes / Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Slot Time** | $T_{\text{slot}} = \dfrac{2 \times T_{\text{prop(max)}}}{\text{(safety factor)}}$ | µs | 51.2 µs for 10 Mbps (round-trip 2500 m) |
| 2 | **Min Frame Size** | $L_{\min} = 2 \times B \times T_{\text{prop(max)}}$ | bits | 512 bits = 64 B at 10 Mbps |
| 3 | **Throughput Efficiency** | $E = \dfrac{1}{1 + 6.44a}$ | fraction | Peterson & Davie, 10BASE-5 |
| 4 | **Parameter "a"** | $a = \dfrac{T_{\text{prop}}}{T_{\text{trans}}}=\dfrac{L/B}{R/B}=\dfrac{L}{R\cdot \tau}$ | dimensionless | Ratio of prop to trans time |
| 5 | **Ethernet Network Diameter** | $d_{\max} = \dfrac{v \times T_{\text{prop(max)}}}{2}$ | meters | v ≈ 2 × 10⁸ m/s in coax |
| 6 | **Jam Signal Duration** | $T_{\text{jam}} = 3.2\ \mu s$ | µs | 32 bit-times @ 10 Mbps |
| 7 | **Inter-Frame Gap** | $T_{\text{IFG}} = 9.6\ \mu s$ | µs | 96 bit-times @ 10 Mbps |
| 8 | **Backoff Slots (BEB)** | $k \in \{0, \ldots, 2^{\min(n,10)} - 1\}$ | slots | Capped at 1023 after 10 collisions |
| 9 | **Maximum Retry Limit** | n_max = 16 | — | Error reported after this |
| 10 | **Frame Size Range** | $64 \le L \le 1518$ | bytes | Data: 46 ≤ D ≤ 1500 B |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL: Always replace `|` with `\vert` or `\mid` in math to avoid breaking the markdown table.** In all formulas above, absolute value bars are written as `\vert` where needed (e.g., $a = \vert T_{\text{prop}}/T_{\text{trans}} \vert$ if used).

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Ethernet underpins virtually every wired network infrastructure:

- **Data Centers:** 10/25/40/100/200/400 Gigabit Ethernet interconnects servers.
- **Industrial Automation:** Deterministic variants (TSN — Time-Sensitive Networking, IEEE 802.1Qbv).
- **Automotive:** 100BASE-T1 and 1000BASE-T1 for in-vehicle networks.
- **Telecommunications:** Carrier-grade Ethernet (MEF standards) for metro networks.
- **Storage:** Fibre Channel over Ethernet (FCoE) and iSCSI run on the same physical infrastructure.

> The IEEE 802.3 working group has ratified speeds up to **400 Gbps** (IEEE 802.3bs-2017) and is developing **800 GbE** and **1.6 TbE**, demonstrating that Ethernet's original CSMA/CD simplicity is now augmented (and largely replaced) by **full-duplex switched operation** at higher layers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Minimum Frame Size of 64 Bytes

This is the **single most important derivation** in the KTU 2024 Module-3 syllabus. The minimum frame size is a *physical constraint* designed so that the *first bit* of a frame is still in transit when the *last bit* is transmitted — guaranteeing the sender is still transmitting when a collision can possibly occur at the far end of the network.

### Step 1: Define the maximum network diameter

For 10BASE-5 Thick Ethernet, the maximum cable length between the two farthest stations (round trip) is:

$$
d_{\text{rt}} = 2500\ \text{m} = 2.5\ \text{km}
$$

### Step 2: Compute one-way propagation delay

Signals travel in coax at approximately $v = 2 \times 10^{8}$ m/s (≈ 2/3 the speed of light in vacuum).

$$
T_{\text{prop,one-way}} = \frac{d}{v} = \frac{2500}{2 \times 10^{8}} = 12.5\ \mu s
$$

### Step 3: Compute round-trip propagation delay

A signal must travel from sender → far end → back, so:

$$
T_{\text{prop,rt}} = 2 \times T_{\text{prop,one-way}} = 2 \times 12.5\ \mu s = 25.0\ \mu s
$$

### Step 4: Add safety margins (repeater delays, hardware latency)

IEEE 802.3 adds repeater delays and safety margins. The total allowed maximum round-trip delay is defined as the **slot time**:

$$
T_{\text{slot}} = 51.2\ \mu s
$$

### Step 5: Relate slot time to minimum frame size

To ensure the sender is still transmitting when the collision returns, the transmission time of the shortest frame must be **at least equal to the slot time**:

$$
T_{\text{trans,min}} \ge T_{\text{slot}}
$$

$$
\frac{L_{\min}}{B} \ge T_{\text{slot}} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad L_{\min} \ge B \times T_{\text{slot}}
$$

### Step 6: Substitute the numbers (B = 10 Mbps, T_slot = 51.2 µs)

$$
L_{\min} = 10 \times 10^{6}\ \text{bits/s} \times 51.2 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{s} = 512\ \text{bits}
$$

Convert to bytes:

$$
L_{\min} = \frac{512\ \text{bits}}{8\ \text{bits/byte}} = 64\ \text{bytes}
$$

### Step 7: Therefore the minimum data payload

The data field (LLC + Pad) must be sized so that the *total frame* (excluding preamble/SFD) is ≥ 64 B. Header = 6 (DA) + 6 (SA) + 2 (Len) + 4 (FCS) = 18 B. So:

$$
L_{\text{payload,min}} = 64 - 18 = 46\ \text{bytes}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** The 64 B minimum frame is not a design choice but a **physical necessity** — it ensures that on a 10 Mbps, 2500 m network, the sender is guaranteed to be still transmitting when a collision signal returns from the farthest station.

## 3.2 Derivation: CSMA/CD Throughput Efficiency (Peterson & Davie Model)

The standard efficiency of CSMA/CD (assuming 1-persistent CSMA with collision detection) is given by:

$$
E = \frac{1}{1 + 6.44a} \quad \text{where} \quad a = \frac{T_{\text{prop}}}{T_{\text{trans}}}
$$

### Step 1: Define the parameter *a*

$$
a = \frac{T_{\text{prop}}}{T_{\text{trans}}} = \frac{L/B}{R} = \frac{L}{R \cdot \tau}
$$

where L is the frame length, R is the bit rate, and τ is the one-way propagation delay. For 10BASE-5 with L = 512 bits and T_prop = 25 µs, T_trans = 51.2 µs, so:

$$
a = \frac{25.0}{51.2} \approx 0.488 \approx 0.5
$$

### Step 2: Compute efficiency for a typical Ethernet frame (L = 1500 B)

For a **maximum-size frame** on 10BASE-5:

$$
T_{\text{trans}} = \frac{1500 \times 8}{10 \times 10^{6}} = 1200\ \mu s
$$

$$
a = \frac{25.0}{1200} \approx 0.0208
$$

### Step 3: Plug into efficiency formula

$$
E = \frac{1}{1 + 6.44 \times 0.0208} = \frac{1}{1 + 0.1340} = \frac{1}{1.1340} \approx 0.8818
$$

### Step 4: Compute practical throughput

$$
\text{Throughput} = E \times B = 0.8818 \times 10\ \text{Mbps} \approx 8.82\ \text{Mbps}
$$

### Step 3 Alternative: Efficiency for a minimum-size frame (L = 64 B)

$$
T_{\text{trans,min}} = \frac{64 \times 8}{10 \times 10^{6}} = 51.2\ \mu s
$$

$$
a_{\max} = \frac{25.0}{51.2} \approx 0.488
$$

$$
E_{\min} = \frac{1}{1 + 6.44 \times 0.488} = \frac{1}{1 + 3.143} = \frac{1}{4.143} \approx 0.2414
$$

$$
\text{Throughput}_{\min} \approx 0.2414 \times 10\ \text{Mbps} = 2.414\ \text{Mbps}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Even with full load and zero idle time, the *worst-case* CSMA/CD efficiency is only **~24%** for minimum frames. This is why Ethernet fragments were discouraged and why the **maximum frame size is 1518 B** — large frames amortize the protocol overhead and approach the theoretical channel capacity.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 2024 Exam-Style)

**Problem:** Consider a 10BASE-T Ethernet segment of length **2000 m** with a signal propagation speed of $2 \times 10^{8}$ m/s. A station transmits a 1000-byte frame. Compute: (a) the one-way propagation delay, (b) the parameter *a*, and (c) the CSMA/CD efficiency.

### Solution:

**Part (a) — One-way propagation delay:**

$$
T_{\text{prop}} = \frac{d}{v} = \frac{2000}{2 \times 10^{8}} = 10 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{s} = 10\ \mu s
$$

**Part (b) — Parameter *a*:**

Transmission time for 1000-byte frame at 10 Mbps:

$$
T_{\text{trans}} = \frac{1000 \times 8}{10 \times 10^{6}} = \frac{8000}{10^{7}} = 800\ \mu s
$$

Therefore:

$$
a = \frac{T_{\text{prop}}}{T_{\text{trans}}} = \frac{10\ \mu s}{800\ \mu s} = 0.0125
$$

**Part (c) — CSMA/CD efficiency:**

$$
E = \frac{1}{1 + 6.44a} = \frac{1}{1 + 6.44 \times 0.0125} = \frac{1}{1 + 0.0805} = \frac{1}{1.0805} \approx 0.9255
$$

**Effective throughput:**

$$
\text{Throughput} = E \times B = 0.9255 \times 10 = 9.255\ \text{Mbps}
$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU 2024)

| Parameter | 10BASE-5 | 10BASE-2 | 10BASE-T | 100BASE-TX | 1000BASE-T |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Year** | 1980 | 1985 | 1990 | 1995 | 1999 |
| **Medium** | Thick coax | Thin coax | Twisted pair | Cat-5 UTP | Cat-5e/6 UTP |
| **Topology** | Bus | Bus | Star | Star | Star |
| **Max Segment** | 500 m | 185 m | 100 m | 100 m | 100 m |
| **Encoding** | Manchester | Manchester | Manchester | MLT-3 (4B/5B) | PAM-5 |
| **Connector** | AUI | BNC | RJ-45 | RJ-45 | RJ-45 |
| **CSMA/CD** | Yes | Yes | Yes (half) / No (full) | Yes (half) / No (full) | No (always full-duplex) |
| **Slot Time** | 51.2 µs | 51.2 µs | 51.2 µs | 5.12 µs | 0.512 µs |
| **Min Frame** | 64 B | 64 B | 64 B | 64 B | 64 B |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Ethernet Frame Architecture (Block-Level Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PRE[Preamble 7 Bytes 10101010] --> SFD[Start Frame Delimiter 1 Byte 10101011]
    SFD --> DA[Destination MAC Address 6 Bytes 48 bits]
    DA --> SA[Source MAC Address 6 Bytes 48 bits]
    SA --> LT{Length or Type 2 Bytes}
    LT -->|less than 1500| LEN[802.3 Length]
    LT -->|greater than 1535| TY[802.2 Type e.g. 0x0800 IPv4]
    LEN --> PAY[LLC Payload 46 to 1500 Bytes]
    TY --> PAY
    PAY --> PAD[Pad 0 to 46 Bytes]
    PAD --> FCS[Frame Check Sequence CRC32 4 Bytes]
    FCS --> IFG[Inter Frame Gap 12 Bytes 96 bit times]
    IFG --> NEXT[Next Frame or Idle]

    style PRE fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd
    style SFD fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#ef6c00
    style DA fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a
    style SA fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a
    style LT fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style PAY fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32
    style FCS fill:#ffebee,stroke:#c62828
    style IFG fill:#f5f5f5,stroke:#424242
```

## 4.2 CSMA/CD Transmission State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> SenseMedium: Frame Ready to Send
    SenseMedium --> WaitIFG: Medium Idle for 96 bit times
    SenseMedium --> Defer: Medium Busy
    Defer --> SenseMedium: Wait for medium to go idle
    WaitIFG --> Transmit: Start sending bits
    Transmit --> MonitorSignal: While transmitting
    MonitorSignal --> Transmit: No collision detected
    MonitorSignal --> CollisionDetected: Signal voltage exceeds threshold
    CollisionDetected --> SendJam: Transmit 32 bit jam signal
    SendJam --> IncrementCounter: n is incremented
    IncrementCounter --> Abort: n greater than 16
    IncrementCounter --> Backoff: n less than or equal to 16
    Backoff --> SenseMedium: Wait random k slot times k in 0 to 2 n minus 1
    Abort --> ReportError: Notify upper layer
    ReportError --> [*]
    Transmit --> FrameComplete: All bits sent
    FrameComplete --> Idle
```

## 4.3 Evolution of Ethernet Topology

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph BusLegacy["Legacy Bus Topology 10BASE5 10BASE2"]

    end

    subgraph HubLegacy["Hub Based Star 10BASE-T Half Duplex"]
        HUB[Central Hub Repeater]

    end
```
