
## 4.4 KTU 2024 Module 3 Mapping — Ethernet in the Layered Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    APP[Application Layer HTTP FTP DNS] --> TRAN[Transport Layer TCP UDP]
    TRAN --> NET[Network Layer IP ICMP]
    NET --> LLC["LLC Sublayer IEEE 802.2 Logical Link Control"]
    LLC --> MAC["MAC Sublayer IEEE 802.3 CSMA CD Framing MAC Addressing"]
    MAC --> PHY["Physical Layer IEEE 802.3 PHY Encoding Signaling 10BASE T 100BASE TX 1000BASE T"]

    style LLC fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1
    style MAC fill:#ffe082,stroke:#ff6f00
    style PHY fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1:** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define CSMA/CD. Why is the minimum Ethernet frame size fixed at 64 bytes?**

**Model Answer:**

**CSMA/CD (Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Detection)** is the MAC protocol defined by IEEE 802.3 for half-duplex Ethernet networks. It works in three steps:

1. **Carrier Sense** — A station wanting to transmit first listens to determine if the medium is idle.
2. **Multiple Access** — Multiple stations share the common medium (no central arbitration).
3. **Collision Detection** — During transmission, the station monitors for voltage fluctuations; on detecting a collision, it transmits a 32-bit jam signal and retries after a random binary exponential backoff.

**Why minimum frame size is 64 bytes:**

To guarantee that the sender is still transmitting when a collision signal from the farthest station returns. On a 10 Mbps, 2500 m network, the maximum round-trip propagation delay (slot time) is 51.2 µs. Therefore:

$$
L_{\min} = B \times T_{\text{slot}} = 10\ \text{Mbps} \times 51.2\ \mu s = 512\ \text{bits} = 64\ \text{bytes}
$$

If the frame were shorter, transmission would complete *before* the collision signal returns, and the sender would not detect the collision, causing data corruption. **Hence the minimum frame size is fixed at 64 bytes.**

---

### **Question 2:** `[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024]`
**What is the role of the Jam Signal in CSMA/CD? What is the duration of the jam signal on a 10 Mbps Ethernet?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Jam Signal** is a 32-bit pattern transmitted by a station immediately after it detects a collision. Its role is twofold:

1. **To ensure that all other stations on the segment also detect the collision** — the collision signal might otherwise attenuate and become undetectable by some stations.
2. **To reinforce the collision event** so that all stations know the medium is now in a collision state and will execute the backoff algorithm.

**Duration on 10 Mbps Ethernet:**

$$
T_{\text{jam}} = \frac{32\ \text{bits}}{10\ \text{Mbps}} = 3.2\ \mu s
$$

This is the standard 32-bit jam signal duration, equal to approximately 3.2 µs at 10 Mbps. **The duration of the jam signal is 3.2 µs on a 10 Mbps Ethernet.**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / 2024 Model Paper]`

**Part (a)** — **7 Marks**
With a neat diagram, explain the IEEE 802.3 Ethernet frame format. Discuss the function of each field.

**Part (b)** — **7 Marks**
Explain the CSMA/CD protocol in detail. Discuss the Binary Exponential Backoff algorithm and compute the maximum backoff delay after the 5th collision on a 10 Mbps Ethernet.

---

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

#### **Part (a) — IEEE 802.3 Ethernet Frame Format (7 Marks)**

The IEEE 802.3 standard defines a frame consisting of the following fields (in order):

```
+--------+--------+---------+--------+--------+---------+-----+-----+--------+
|Preamb. |  SFD   | Dest.   | Source | Length/|   LLC  | Pad | FCS |  IFG   |
| (7B)   |  (1B)  |  MAC    |  MAC   | Type   |  Data  |     |(4B) | (12B)  |
|        |        |  (6B)   |  (6B)  |  (2B)  |        |     |     |        |
+--------+--------+---------+--------+--------+---------+-----+-----+--------+
```

| Field | Size | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Preamble** | 7 B | Alternating 1010... bit pattern for receiver clock synchronization |
| **Start Frame Delimiter (SFD)** | 1 B | Marks the start of the frame; pattern `10101011` |
| **Destination MAC Address** | 6 B | 48-bit hardware address of intended receiver |
| **Source MAC Address** | 6 B | 48-bit hardware address of sender |
| **Length/Type** | 2 B | If ≤ 1500: length of data; if ≥ 1536: protocol type (e.g., 0x0800 = IPv4) |
| **LLC Data** | 46–1500 B | Payload from upper layer; minimum 46 B ensures total frame ≥ 64 B |
| **Pad** | 0–46 B | Zero-padding to meet the 64 B minimum frame requirement |
| **Frame Check Sequence (FCS)** | 4 B | CRC-32 for error detection over the entire frame |
| **Inter-Frame Gap (IFG)** | 12 B | 96 bit-times of silence between consecutive frames |

**Key Observation:** The frame is **bounded between 64 and 1518 bytes** (excluding preamble/SFD/IFG). This is critical for collision detection in half-duplex Ethernet.

---

#### **Part (b) — CSMA/CD and Binary Exponential Backoff (7 Marks)**

**CSMA/CD Algorithm:**

1. A station that wants to transmit waits until the medium is **idle** for at least 96 bit-times (IFG).
2. The station begins transmitting the frame.
3. While transmitting, the station monitors the signal level on the medium.
4. If a collision is detected:
   - Transmission is stopped.
   - A 32-bit jam signal is sent.
   - Collision counter *n* is incremented.
   - If n > 16, the frame is dropped and an error is reported to the upper layer.
   - Otherwise, the station waits a random backoff time:
     - 
     $$
     T_{\text{wait}} = k \times 51.2\ \mu s, \quad k \in \{0, 1, \ldots, 2^{n} - 1\}
     $$

**Binary Exponential Backoff after 5th collision (n = 5):**

The number of slot times *k* is chosen randomly from:

$$
k \in \{0, 1, 2, 3, \ldots, 2^{5} - 1\} = \{0, 1, 2, 3, \ldots, 31\}
$$

**Maximum backoff delay** (when k = 31):

$$
T_{\text{max backoff}} = 31 \times 51.2\ \mu s = 1587.2\ \mu s \approx 1.587\ \text{ms}
$$

**Average backoff delay** (k_avg = 15.5):

$$
T_{\text{avg backoff}} = 15.5 \times 51.2\ \mu s = 793.6\ \mu s
$$

**Conclusion:** The maximum backoff delay after the 5th collision on a 10 Mbps Ethernet is **1.587 ms**, demonstrating how the backoff window grows exponentially to handle increased contention.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024]`

**Part (a)** — **7 Marks**
Explain the difference between 10BASE-5, 10BASE-2, 10BASE-T, and 100BASE-TX Ethernet. Compare their physical media, topologies, and maximum segment lengths.

**Part (b)** — **7 Marks**
A 10BASE-5 Ethernet network has a propagation speed of $2 \times 10^{8}$ m/s and maximum segment length of 500 m. There are 4 repeaters, each adding 2 µs of delay. The network has a maximum round-trip distance of 2500 m.
- (i) Calculate the round-trip propagation delay.
- (ii) Find the minimum frame size required to ensure collision detection.
- (iii) Calculate the efficiency of CSMA/CD for a 1500-byte frame.

---

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

#### **Part (a) — Comparison of Ethernet Variants (7 Marks)**

| Standard | Physical Medium | Topology | Max Segment | Encoding | Connector | Speed |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **10BASE-5** | Thick Coax (50Ω) | Bus | 500 m | Manchester | AUI / Vampire Tap | 10 Mbps |
| **10BASE-2** | Thin Coax (50Ω) | Bus | 185 m | Manchester | BNC T-connector | 10 Mbps |
| **10BASE-T** | Cat-3/5 UTP | Star (Hub) | 100 m | Manchester | RJ-45 | 10 Mbps |
| **100BASE-TX** | Cat-5 UTP | Star (Switch) | 100 m | MLT-3 (4B/5B) | RJ-45 | 100 Mbps |

**Key Differences:**

1. **10BASE-5 (Thick Ethernet):** Original standard; uses a heavy coaxial cable, requires external transceivers, supports up to 100 stations per segment. Mostly obsolete.

2. **10BASE-2 (Thin Ethernet / Cheapernet):** Uses thinner, more flexible coax; cheaper to install; BNC T-connectors link stations directly to the cable. No repeaters between stations. Obsolete.

3. **10BASE-T (Twisted Pair Ethernet):** Major architectural shift from bus to **star topology** using a central hub. Easier troubleshooting, better noise immunity, supports full-duplex. Still uses CSMA/CD in half-duplex mode.

4. **100BASE-TX (Fast Ethernet):** Ten times faster; uses **4B/5B** block encoding and **MLT-3** line encoding for higher spectral efficiency. Operates over Cat-5 UTP. CSMA/CD is preserved but slot time is reduced to 5.12 µs; full-duplex operation eliminates collisions.

---

#### **Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 Marks)**

**Given:**
- v = $2 \times 10^{8}$ m/s
- Maximum one-way segment = 500 m
- 4 repeaters × 2 µs each = 8 µs total repeater delay
- Maximum round-trip distance = 2500 m

**(i) Round-trip propagation delay:**

One-way propagation delay for the entire network:

$$
T_{\text{prop,one-way}} = \frac{2500\ \text{m}}{2 \times 10^{8}\ \text{m/s}} = 12.5\ \mu s
$$

Round-trip propagation delay:

$$
T_{\text{prop,rt}} = 2 \times 12.5 = 25\ \mu s
$$

Including repeater delays (each repeater contributes to *both* the forward and return path; conservatively, total repeater contribution = 8 µs):

$$
T_{\text{rt,total}} = 25 + 8 = 33\ \mu s
$$

**The round-trip propagation delay is 33 µs (with repeater delays).**

**(ii) Minimum frame size required:**

Using the rule:

$$
L_{\min} = 2 \times B \times T_{\text{prop,rt}} = 2 \times 10 \times 10^{6} \times 33 \times 10^{-6} = 660\ \text{bits}
$$

**L_min = 660 bits = 82.5 bytes.** In practice, Ethernet conservatively uses 64 bytes (512 bits) as the minimum, but the *physical* minimum for this specific network would be **82.5 bytes** — implying the network would have a *reduced* maximum reach. In standard 10BASE-5 with proper 51.2 µs slot time, the minimum frame remains **64 B**.

**(iii) CSMA/CD efficiency for a 1500-byte frame:**

Transmission time:

$$
T_{\text{trans}} = \frac{1500 \times 8}{10 \times 10^{6}} = 1200\ \mu s
$$

Parameter *a*:

$$
a = \frac{T_{\text{prop}}}{T_{\text{trans}}} = \frac{25\ \mu s}{1200\ \mu s} \approx 0.0208
$$

(Using T_prop = 25 µs; for full 51.2 µs slot time, a = 51.2/1200 ≈ 0.0427.)

Efficiency (Peterson–Davie formula):

$$
E = \frac{1}{1 + 6.44a} = \frac{1}{1 + 6.44 \times 0.0208} = \frac{1}{1.134} \approx 0.882 = 88.2\%
$$

**The CSMA/CD efficiency is approximately 88.2%, and the effective throughput is:**

$$
\text{Throughput} = 0.882 \times 10\ \text{Mbps} = 8.82\ \text{Mbps}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Marks Lost:**
> 
> 1. **Mixing Ethernet II and 802.3 frames:** Students often treat the DIX (Ethernet II) "Type" field and the 802.3 "Length" field as the same. They are NOT — distinguish by threshold (≤ 1500 vs. ≥ 1536).
> 
> 2. **Forgetting to include the IFG:** The 96 bit-times (12 B) of inter-frame gap is *not part of the frame* but *is* part of channel occupancy. For throughput problems, do not include it in frame size but DO include it in time calculations.
> 
> 3. **Wrong CRC name:** The Ethernet FCS is **CRC-32**, not CRC-16. Examiners deduct marks if you write the wrong polynomial.
> 
> 4. **Confusing 802.3 with 802.11:** 802.3 is wired Ethernet with CSMA/CD; 802.11 is wireless with CSMA/CA (Collision **Avoidance**). This is a high-frequency KTU question.
> 
> 5. **In binary exponential backoff:** Students forget that *k* is in the range $\{0, \ldots, 2^n - 1\}$ and that 16 collisions lead to abort. The maximum value of k is 1023 (capped after 10 collisions).
> 
> 6. **Slot time assumption:** 51.2 µs slot time is for **10 Mbps only**. For 100 Mbps it is 5.12 µs; for 1 Gbps it is 0.512 µs.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD RAPID REVISION — KTU 2024 Module 3**

### **Ethernet Frame Essentials**
- ☐ Ethernet frame size: **64 B minimum, 1518 B maximum** (excluding preamble/SFD).
- ☐ Preamble = 7 B (`1010...`), SFD = 1 B (`10101011`).
- ☐ MAC address = 48 bits (24-bit OUI + 24-bit device).
- ☐ FCS = 4-byte **CRC-32**.
- ☐ IFG = 12 B (96 bit-times).
- ☐ Length field ≤ 1500; Type field ≥ 1536 (DIX/Ethernet II).
- ☐ Minimum payload (LLC data) = 46 B; maximum = 1500 B.

### **CSMA/CD Mechanics**
- ☐ **1-persistent** CSMA/CD for half-duplex Ethernet.
- ☐ Jam signal = 32 bits = 3.2 µs @ 10 Mbps.
- ☐ Backoff: $k \in \{0, \ldots, 2^{n} - 1\}$, multiplied by slot time.
- ☐ Maximum 16 collisions → abort.
- ☐ Slot time @ 10 Mbps = **51.2 µs** (= 512 bit-times).

### **Key Derivations & Numbers**
- ☐ $L_{\min} = B \times T_{\text{slot}} = 512\ \text{bits} = 64\ \text{B}$ at 10 Mbps.
- ☐ $E = 1 / (1 + 6.44a)$ (Peterson–Davie).
- ☐ Max efficiency ~88% for max-size frames; ~24% for min-size frames.
- ☐ Round-trip prop delay on 2500 m @ $2 \times 10^8$ m/s = 25 µs.

### **Ethernet Variants & Evolution**
- ☐ 10BASE-5 (thick coax, 500 m, bus), 10BASE-2 (thin coax, 185 m, bus).
- ☐ 10BASE-T (UTP Cat-3, 100 m, star/hub, half-duplex CSMA/CD).
- ☐ 100BASE-TX (Fast Ethernet, Cat-5, MLT-3 encoding).
- ☐ 1000BASE-T (Gigabit, Cat-5e, PAM-5 encoding, always full-duplex).
- ☐ Modern switched Ethernet is **full-duplex** → **CSMA/CD disabled**.

### **Exam-Specific Traps**
- ☐ 802.3 (Ethernet) ≠ 802.11 (Wi-Fi). Don't confuse CSMA/CD with CSMA/CA.
- ☐ Manchester encoding for 10 Mbps; MLT-3 for 100 Mbps; PAM-5 for 1 Gbps.
- ☐ MAC is the lower sublayer of Data Link; LLC (802.2) is the upper sublayer.
- ☐ FCS detects errors but does NOT correct them; retransmission is handled by upper layers.
- ☐ Address types: Unicast (single), Multicast (group), Broadcast (all 1s = FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Connecting Devices
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🔌 Connecting Devices in Computer Networks

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the **APJ Abdul Kalam Technological University (KTU) 2024 Scheme** curriculum for *PEAMT632 – Basics of Computer Networks*, **Connecting Devices** (also called *networking devices* or *internetworking devices*) refer to the hardware components used to interconnect computers and other digital devices across a computer network. These devices operate at different layers of the **OSI (Open Systems Interconnection) Reference Model** and the **TCP/IP protocol suite**, enabling communication, signal regeneration, traffic management, and protocol conversion between heterogeneous network segments.

The KTU syllabus categorizes these devices strictly according to the **OSI layer at which they function** — from the simplest physical-layer repeaters to sophisticated application-layer gateways.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):**
> Students must be able to *identify, classify, and explain the operational role of each connecting device with respect to its corresponding OSI layer*. Examiner questions almost always begin with: *"Classify the following devices based on OSI layers and justify."*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Highway System

Imagine a **city road network**:

| Road Network Element | Network Device | Function |
|---|---|---|
| A **traffic policeman with a whistle** at a junction | **Switch** | Directs cars (data frames) to the correct street based on address |
| A **highway toll booth** that checks your destination | **Router** | Decides which highway (network) your car (packet) should take |
| A **signboard translator** that converts Malayalam to Hindi | **Gateway** | Converts one "language" (protocol) to another |
| A **road reflector** that brightens a faded lane marking | **Repeater** | Regenerates a weakening signal |
| A **roundabout** where all cars merge | **Hub** | Broadcasts incoming traffic to all outgoing ports |

This analogy makes it instantly clear *why* different devices exist — each solves a different problem in the journey of a data packet from source to destination.

---

## 1.3 The Five Principal Connecting Devices (KTU-Listed)

The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically emphasizes **five devices**:

1. **Repeater** — *Physical Layer (L1)*
2. **Hub** — *Physical Layer (L1)*
3. **Bridge** — *Data Link Layer (L2)*
4. **Switch** — *Data Link Layer (L2)*
5. **Router** — *Network Layer (L3)*

A sixth, the **Gateway**, is often co-listed for higher-layer protocol conversion.

> [!NOTE]
> **Two Crucial Terminology Anchors (must memorize):**
> - **Collision Domain** → A network segment where data packets can collide with one another. Devices at L1 (repeater, hub) **do NOT separate** collision domains.
> - **Broadcast Domain** → A network segment where a broadcast frame reaches all connected devices. Devices up to L2 (bridge, switch) **do NOT separate** broadcast domains; only **routers (L3)** and above do.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# ⚙️ Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Device-by-Device Operational Breakdown

### 🔁 2.1.1 Repeater (Layer 1 — Physical)

- **What it is:** A *2-port* device that **regenerates, retimes, and amplifies** a weakened signal so it can travel a longer distance on the medium.
- **Why it is needed:** Signals **attenuate** (lose strength) due to resistance, noise, and electromagnetic interference over distance.
- **How it works:** Receives the analog/digital bit stream on one port, reconstructs the original bit pattern, and retransmits it on the other port.
- **Limitation:** It cannot filter traffic, cannot connect different network types, and **propagates collisions**.
- **5-4-3 Rule:** In classic Ethernet (10BASE5/10BASE2), you can have a maximum of **5 segments**, connected through **4 repeaters**, with only **3 segments** populated (i.e., having end devices).

> [!NOTE]
> Repeaters operate purely on **bits** — they have no understanding of frames, packets, MAC addresses, or IP addresses.

---

### 🕸️ 2.1.2 Hub (Layer 1 — Physical)

- **What it is:** A **multi-port repeater** (typically 4, 8, 12, 16, or 24 ports).
- **Operational rule:** When a hub receives a bit on one port, it **broadcasts** that bit out of **all other ports**. This is the famous *shared-medium* behavior.
- **Collision domain:** All ports of a hub belong to the **same collision domain**. The more devices, the more collisions, the lower the throughput.
- **Types of Hubs:**
  1. **Passive Hub** — Acts as a connector only; no signal regeneration.
  2. **Active Hub** — Regenerates and amplifies signals (essentially a multi-port repeater).
  3. **Intelligent Hub** — Includes management, monitoring, and SNMP support.

---

### 🌉 2.1.3 Bridge (Layer 2 — Data Link)

- **What it is:** A device that connects **two (or more) LAN segments** and **forwards or filters frames** between them based on **MAC (Media Access Control) addresses**.
- **Why it is needed:** To reduce collision domains and improve bandwidth efficiency.
- **How it works:** Maintains a **MAC address table** (also called *forwarding table* or *filtering database*). When a frame arrives, the bridge checks the destination MAC:
  - If the destination is on the **same segment** → *filter* (drop) the frame.
  - If the destination is on a **different segment** → *forward* the frame.
- **Transparent Bridging:** The bridge is "transparent" to end devices — they don't need to know it exists.
- **Collision domain separation:** Each port of a bridge is a **separate collision domain**, but all ports share **one broadcast domain**.

#### Bridge Loop Problem & Spanning Tree Protocol (STP)

If two bridges are connected in parallel between the same two LANs (for redundancy), a **loop** forms and broadcast frames circulate forever → **broadcast storm**.

**Spanning Tree Protocol (IEEE 802.1D)** solves this by:
1. Electing a **Root Bridge** (lowest Bridge ID).
2. Each bridge computes the **shortest path cost** to the root.
3. Ports that create loops are placed in a **blocking state**.

**Path Cost Formula (original IEEE 802.1D):**

$$
\text{Cost} = \frac{1000 \text{ Mbps}}{\text{Link Bandwidth (Mbps)}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Modern STP revisions (RSTP — IEEE 802.1w, MSTP — IEEE 802.1s) are improvements but the KTU syllabus focuses on the classical STP concept.

---

### 🔀 2.1.4 Switch (Layer 2 — Data Link)

- **What it is:** A **multi-port bridge** with enhanced performance, hardware-based MAC learning (using *ASIC* chips), and full-duplex communication.
- **Operational advantages over Hub:**
  - Each port = **its own collision domain** (micro-segmentation).
  - **Dedicated bandwidth** per port (no sharing).
  - **Full-duplex** transmission — simultaneous send/receive.
- **MAC Address Learning Algorithm (the "Learn, Flood, Forward, Filter" rule):**

| Step | Action | Table State |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Frame arrives on Port X with source MAC = A | Table empty → A is learned on Port X |
| 2 | Frame destination = B (unknown) | Flood frame out all other ports |
| 3 | Reply arrives with source MAC = B on Port Y | B is learned on Port Y |
| 4 | Subsequent frame to B | Forwarded **only** to Port Y |

- **Types of Switches:**
  1. **Store-and-Forward Switch** — Buffers the entire frame, checks CRC, then forwards. *High latency, low error rate.*
  2. **Cut-Through Switch** — Forwards as soon as destination MAC is read. *Low latency, may forward corrupted frames.*
  3. **Fragment-Free Switch** — Reads first 64 bytes (minimum Ethernet frame), then forwards. Compromise choice.
- **Layer-3 Switch:** A hybrid device with router-like capabilities (IP routing in hardware).

---

### 🛰️ 2.1.5 Router (Layer 3 — Network)

- **What it is:** A device that **routes packets** between different IP networks based on **logical IP addresses** using a **routing table**.
- **Why it is needed:** To interconnect **dissimilar networks** (e.g., LAN ↔ WAN, Ethernet ↔ Wi-Fi) and to determine the optimal path.
- **Key operations:**
  1. **Routing** — Building/maintaining the routing table (static or dynamic via protocols like OSPF, BGP, RIP).
  2. **Forwarding** — Looking up destination IP in routing table and sending packet out the correct interface.
  3. **Fragmentation** — Breaking oversized packets to fit the MTU of the next network.
- **Broadcast domain separation:** Routers **do NOT forward broadcast frames** by default — each interface is a separate broadcast domain.
- **Routing Table Format:**

$$
\text{Routing Table Entry} = \{ \text{Destination Network}, \text{Subnet Mask}, \text{Next Hop}, \text{Interface}, \text{Metric} \}
$$

- **Default Gateway:** The router interface that a host sends packets to when the destination IP is **not** in its own subnet.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **default gateway IP** must be in the **same subnet** as the host's IP address. This is a classic KTU short-answer question.

---

### 🚪 2.1.6 Gateway (Layer 4–7)

- **What it is:** A device (or software) that operates at the **highest layers** of the OSI model to **convert between different network protocols**.
- **Function:** Translates between completely different protocol stacks (e.g., TCP/IP ↔ IBM SNA, IPv4 ↔ IPv6, SMTP ↔ X.400).
- **Difference from Router:** A router works only at Layer 3 with IP; a gateway can work across **any** protocol mismatch.
- **Examples:** Email gateway, VoIP gateway, cloud API gateway, IoT protocol gateway (Modbus ↔ MQTT).

---

## 2.2 Master Comparison Table (KTU High-Yield)

| Device | OSI Layer | Operates On | Collision Domains | Broadcast Domains | Address Used | Smart Decision? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Repeater** | L1 (Physical) | Bits | 1 (combined) | 1 (combined) | None | ❌ No |
| **Hub** | L1 (Physical) | Bits | 1 (combined) | 1 (combined) | None | ❌ No |
| **Bridge** | L2 (Data Link) | Frames | Separates | 1 (combined) | MAC | ✅ Yes |
| **Switch** | L2 (Data Link) | Frames | Separates each port | 1 (combined) | MAC | ✅ Yes |
| **Router** | L3 (Network) | Packets | Separates each port | Separates each port | IP | ✅ Yes |
| **Gateway** | L4–L7 (All) | Messages/Segments | Separates | Separates | Multiple | ✅ Yes |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet & Decision Rules

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Unit / Constraint |
|---|---|---|
| 5-4-3 Rule (Legacy Ethernet) | 5 segments, 4 repeaters, 3 populated | Applies to 10BASE5 / 10BASE2 |
| STP Path Cost | $\text{Cost} = \dfrac{1000}{\text{Bandwidth (Mbps)}}$ | Old IEEE 802.1D |
| Modern STP Cost (802.1t) | $\text{Cost} = \dfrac{20\,000}{\text{Bandwidth (Mbps)}}$ | Newer networks |
| Minimum Ethernet Frame | 64 bytes | Collision detection requirement |
| Max hosts per subnet (IPv4) | $2^h - 2$, where $h$ = host bits | Excludes network & broadcast |
| Default Gateway Rule | Must be in same subnet as host | Common KTU trick question |
| Hub Throughput | $\dfrac{\text{Link BW}}{N}$ (where $N$ = active hosts) | Shared medium |
| Switch Throughput | Link BW (per port) | Dedicated bandwidth |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners **love** asking: *"Why is a switch more efficient than a hub?"* The answer must mention: *collision domain segmentation, dedicated bandwidth, MAC learning, and full-duplex support*.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Data Centers** use **Layer-3 switches** as core routers for micro-segmentation and VXLAN overlays.
- **Smart Homes** use **gateway devices** to bridge Zigbee/Z-Wave (IoT) with Wi-Fi/Ethernet.
- **Industrial Automation** uses **bridges** to segment noisy factory-floor Ethernet from office LANs.
- **ISPs** deploy **routers** at every Point-of-Presence (PoP) with BGP routing.
- **5G/6G networks** use **programmable gateways** for protocol translation between RAN and core.

# 📐 Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Worked Examples

> [!NOTE]
> Although "Connecting Devices" is largely conceptual, KTU 2024 scheme includes **numerical/computational sub-questions** on STP cost, subnetting via router interfaces, and switch port allocation. The derivations below cover every type likely to appear.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Number of Collision Domains and Broadcast Domains

### Problem Setup
A network has:
- **2 Hubs**, each connecting **4 hosts**
- **1 Bridge** connecting the two hubs
- **1 Switch** connected to one of the hubs with **3 more hosts**
- **1 Router** connecting the switch to **another switch** with **5 hosts**

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Count collision domains contributed by hubs.**
A hub is a single collision domain.

$$
\text{CD}_{\text{hubs}} = 2
$$

**Step 2: The bridge separates the two hub domains into separate collision domains.**
So bridge adds 1 more collision domain (per port).

$$
\text{CD}_{\text{bridge ports}} = 2
$$

Total so far = 2 (from hubs) — but actually, the bridge does **not** split the hub's internal collision domain; it splits at its **port boundary**. So we re-evaluate:

- Hub A internal: 1 CD
- Hub B internal: 1 CD
- Bridge separates → 1 CD per bridge port
- Each switch port: 1 CD each
- Each router port: 1 CD each

Let us list explicitly:

| Segment | Collision Domain ID |
|---|---|
| Hub A (4 hosts) | CD1 |
| Hub B (4 hosts) | CD2 |
| Bridge ↔ Hub A link | CD1 (same as Hub A) |
| Bridge ↔ Hub B link | CD2 (same as Hub B) |
| Bridge ↔ Switch link | CD3 |
| 3 hosts on switch | CD4, CD5, CD6 (one per port) |
| Router ↔ Switch link | CD7 |
| Router ↔ Other Switch link | CD8 |
| 5 hosts on other switch | CD9–CD13 |

**Step 3: Count broadcast domains.**

- All L1/L2 devices share **one** broadcast domain.
- Each **router interface** starts a **new** broadcast domain.

$$
\text{BD}_{\text{total}} = 2
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick Rule:**
> - **Hubs, Bridges, Switches** → do **not** separate broadcast domains.
> - **Routers and Gateways** → do **separate** broadcast domains.
> - A single switch with 24 ports = 1 broadcast domain, 24 collision domains.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Spanning Tree Protocol (STP) Path Cost Calculation

### Problem
A bridge has three ports with link speeds **10 Mbps**, **100 Mbps**, and **1 Gbps**. Compute the STP path cost using the **original IEEE 802.1D** formula.

### Solution

The original (legacy) STP cost formula is:

$$
\text{Cost} = \frac{1000 \text{ Mbps}}{\text{Link Bandwidth (Mbps)}}
$$

**Step 1:** Cost for 10 Mbps link.

$$
\text{Cost}_{10} = \frac{1000}{10} = 100
$$

**Step 2:** Cost for 100 Mbps link.

$$
\text{Cost}_{100} = \frac{1000}{100} = 10
$$

**Step 3:** Cost for 1000 Mbps link.

$$
\text{Cost}_{1000} = \frac{1000}{1000} = 1
$$

### Interpretation Table

| Link Bandwidth | Path Cost | Role in STP |
|---|---|---|
| 10 Mbps | 100 | Highest cost → least preferred |
| 100 Mbps | 10 | Medium cost |
| 1 Gbps | 1 | Lowest cost → preferred (root port) |

**Step 4:** If a total path consists of 1 Gbps + 100 Mbps + 10 Mbps links, the cumulative root-path cost is:

$$
\text{Cost}_{\text{total}} = 1 + 10 + 100 = 111
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often use the **modern** cost formula $\frac{20\,000}{\text{BW}}$ by mistake. Always read the question — if it says *original IEEE 802.1D*, use 1000; if it says *revised/802.1t*, use 20 000.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Switch MAC Learning (Table-Building)

### Problem
A 4-port switch receives the following frames in order. Build the MAC address table.

| Frame # | Source MAC | Destination MAC | Arriving Port |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | AA:AA:AA:AA:AA:01 | BB:BB:BB:BB:BB:01 | P1 |
| 2 | CC:CC:CC:CC:CC:01 | AA:AA:AA:AA:AA:01 | P2 |
| 3 | BB:BB:BB:BB:BB:01 | AA:AA:AA:AA:AA:01 | P3 |
| 4 | DD:DD:DD:DD:DD:01 | CC:CC:CC:CC:CC:01 | P4 |

### Step-by-Step Solution

**After Frame 1:**
- Learn: `AA:AA:AA:AA:AA:01 → P1`
- Destination `BB:BB:BB:BB:BB:01` is unknown → **Flood** out P2, P3, P4.

**After Frame 2:**
- Learn: `CC:CC:CC:CC:CC:01 → P2`
- Destination `AA:AA:AA:AA:AA:01` is known at P1 → **Forward** to P1 only.

**After Frame 3:**
- Learn: `BB:BB:BB:BB:BB:01 → P3`
- Destination `AA:AA:AA:AA:AA:01` is known at P1 → **Forward** to P1 only.

**After Frame 4:**
- Learn: `DD:DD:DD:DD:DD:01 → P4`
- Destination `CC:CC:CC:CC:CC:01` is known at P2 → **Forward** to P2 only.

### Final MAC Address Table

| MAC Address | Port | Type |
|---|---|---|
| AA:AA:AA:AA:AA:01 | P1 | Dynamic |
| BB:BB:BB:BB:BB:01 | P3 | Dynamic |
| CC:CC:CC:CC:CC:01 | P2 | Dynamic |
| DD:DD:DD:DD:DD:01 | P4 | Dynamic |

**Flooding count:** Only **1 frame** was flooded (Frame 1). All others were selectively forwarded — this is the **efficiency** switch provides over a hub.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Subnetting for Router Interfaces

### Problem
A router has two interfaces. Interface **G0/0** connects to LAN-A with 50 hosts. Interface **G0/1** connects to LAN-B with 20 hosts. Design the subnets using a single classful network **192.168.10.0/24**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Determine required host bits per subnet.**

For LAN-A (50 hosts):

$$
2^h - 2 \geq 50 \implies 2^h \geq 52 \implies h = 6
$$

Subnet mask for LAN-A: $/24 + 2 = /26$ → mask `255.255.255.192`.

For LAN-B (20 hosts):

$$
2^h - 2 \geq 20 \implies h = 5
$$

Subnet mask for LAN-B: $/24 + 3 = /27$ → mask `255.255.255.224`.

**Step 2: Subnet calculations (Variable Length Subnet Masking).**

**Subnet 1 — LAN-A (/26):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Block size} &= 256 - 192 = 64 \\
\text{Subnet 0} &: 192.168.10.0 \;\;\;\text{to}\;\; 192.168.10.63 \\
\text{Subnet 1} &: 192.168.10.64 \;\,\text{to}\;\; 192.168.10.127 \\
\text{Network} &: 192.168.10.64/26 \\
\text{Usable range} &: 192.168.10.65 \;\text{to}\; 192.168.10.126 \\
\text{Broadcast} &: 192.168.10.127
\end{aligned}
$$

**Subnet 2 — LAN-B (/27):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Block size} &= 256 - 224 = 32 \\
\text{First usable} &: 192.168.10.128/27 \\
\text{Usable range} &: 192.168.10.129 \;\text{to}\; 192.168.10.158 \\
\text{Broadcast} &: 192.168.10.159
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Router interface configuration.**

| Interface | IP Address | Mask | Default Gateway for Hosts |
|---|---|---|---|
| G0/0 | 192.168.10.65 | 255.255.255.192 | 192.168.10.65 |
| G0/1 | 192.168.10.129 | 255.255.255.224 | 192.168.10.129 |

> [!TIP]
> **VLSM Rule:** Always allocate the **largest subnet first** to avoid address exhaustion. This problem follows KTU's standard VLSM question pattern.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Device Selection for Scenarios

| Scenario | Recommended Device | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Two office floors, reduce collisions | **Switch** | Micro-segmentation, dedicated bandwidth |
| Connect two buildings 500 m apart (copper) | **Repeater** | Signal regeneration over distance |
| Connect LAN to Internet | **Router** | Inter-network routing, broadcast isolation |
| Translate IPv4 ↔ IPv6 traffic | **Gateway** | Protocol conversion at L4–L7 |
| Add redundancy to a switched network | **Bridge + STP** | Loop prevention |
| Low-cost 4-host home network | **Hub** (legacy) or **small switch** | Cost-effective, modern switches are now cheaper |

# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model)]**
**Differentiate between a Hub and a Switch. Mention how each affects collision domains.**

#### Model Answer (10 lines):

A **Hub** is a Layer-1 physical device that broadcasts incoming bits to **all ports** regardless of destination, making **all connected devices share a single collision domain**. In contrast, a **Switch** is a Layer-2 device that uses a **MAC address table** to forward frames only to the **specific destination port**, giving each port its **own dedicated collision domain (micro-segmentation)**. A switch also supports **full-duplex** communication, effectively eliminating collisions on its ports, while a hub allows **half-duplex** only with high collision probability. Thus, a switch provides far better throughput, security, and scalability than a hub.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Note:** A 3-mark answer needs the **3 key contrasts** — *(i) layer, (ii) collision domain, (iii) forwarding method*. Missing any one = loss of 1 mark.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]**
**What is the role of a Gateway in a computer network? How is it different from a Router?**

#### Model Answer (8 lines):

A **Gateway** is a connecting device that operates at the **higher layers (Layer 4 to 7)** of the OSI model and acts as a **protocol converter** between two networks that use **dissimilar protocol stacks** (e.g., TCP/IP ↔ OSI, IPv4 ↔ IPv6). A **Router**, in contrast, operates strictly at **Layer 3** and routes IP packets between networks that already use the **same network-layer protocol**. While both interconnect networks, a router cannot translate protocols — it only makes forwarding decisions based on IP addresses. Hence, gateways are essential in **heterogeneous** networks, whereas routers are sufficient in **homogeneous IP** networks.

---

## Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE regulations**, every Module-3 question in Part B provides an **internal choice**. Below, **Question A** and **Question B** are two completely independent alternatives that a student can opt between.

---

### 📘 Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper]**

**a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **internal architecture and working of a Bridge**. Discuss how a bridge builds its forwarding table and handles unknown destinations. (7 Marks)

**b)** Explain the **Spanning Tree Protocol (STP)** in detail. Compute the **root path cost** for the network topology given below. (7 Marks)

**Topology:** Switch-A (Root Bridge) → 1 Gbps link → Switch-B → 100 Mbps link → Switch-C → 10 Mbps link → Switch-D

#### Model Answer:

**Part (a) — Bridge Architecture & Working (7 marks)**

A **bridge** operates at the **Data Link Layer (L2)** and has two main functional blocks:

1. **MAC Address Table (Forwarding Database):** A CAM (Content Addressable Memory) or hash-based table mapping each learned MAC address to a port.
2. **Frame Forwarding Logic:** A controller that decides whether to forward, filter, or flood each frame.

**Working — Step-by-step:**

1. **Frame Arrives:** A frame is received on Port 1 with source MAC = `AA-...-01` and destination MAC = `BB-...-02`.
2. **Learning Phase:** The bridge records `AA-...-01 → Port 1` in its table (this is *learning*).
3. **Lookup Phase:** It searches the table for `BB-...-02`.
4. **Decision Phase:**
   - If `BB-...-02` is on the **same segment (Port 1)** → **Filter** the frame (drop it — local traffic).
   - If `BB-...-02` is on a **known different port (e.g., Port 3)** → **Forward** selectively to Port 3 only.
   - If `BB-...-02` is **unknown** → **Flood** the frame out of all ports **except** the source port.
5. **Aging:** Entries are removed after a timeout (typically 300 seconds) to keep the table fresh.

**Key Properties:**
- Transparent to end hosts (no configuration needed on hosts).
- Reduces unnecessary traffic between segments.
- Each port = separate **collision domain**; entire bridge = single **broadcast domain**.

*(Insert neat block diagram: 2 LAN segments connected by a bridge, showing MAC table.)*

**Part (b) — STP & Root Path Cost (7 marks)**

**Spanning Tree Protocol (IEEE 802.1D)** prevents **bridge loops** and **broadcast storms** in switched networks with redundant links.

**STP Algorithm Steps:**

1. **Root Bridge Election:** All bridges exchange BPDUs (Bridge Protocol Data Units). The bridge with the **lowest Bridge ID** (priority + MAC address) is elected as the **Root Bridge**.
2. **Root Port Selection:** On every non-root bridge, the port with the **lowest cumulative path cost** to the root becomes the **Root Port**.
3. **Designated Port Selection:** On each segment, the bridge with the lowest cost to the root elects a **Designated Port**.
4. **Blocking:** All other ports are placed in a **Blocking** state to break loops.

**Root Path Cost Computation (using legacy IEEE 802.1D formula):**

$$
\text{Cost} = \frac{1000 \text{ Mbps}}{\text{Link Bandwidth (Mbps)}}
$$

**Step 1:** Cost of 1 Gbps link:

$$
\text{Cost}_{1\text{G}} = \frac{1000}{1000} = 1
$$

**Step 2:** Cost of 100 Mbps link:

$$
\text{Cost}_{100\text{M}} = \frac{1000}{100} = 10
$$

**Step 3:** Cost of 10 Mbps link:

$$
\text{Cost}_{10\text{M}} = \frac{1000}{10} = 100
$$

**Step 4:** Cumulative root path cost from Switch-D to Root Bridge Switch-A:

$$
\text{Cost}_{\text{total}} = 1 + 10 + 100 = 111
$$

**Conclusion:** The cumulative root path cost from Switch-D back to the root bridge is **111**. Switch-B and Switch-C are intermediate bridges; their respective root path costs are **1** and **1 + 10 = 11**.

---

### 📗 Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper]**

**a)** Compare and contrast the functions of **Repeater, Hub, Bridge, Switch, Router, and Gateway** with respect to the OSI layer they operate on and the domains they separate. (7 Marks)

**b)** An organization is allocated the IP block **192.168.50.0/24**. It has **four departments** with the following host requirements:
- **Department A:** 60 hosts
- **Department B:** 28 hosts
- **Department C:** 14 hosts
- **Department D:** 6 hosts

Design the subnets using **VLSM** and provide the network address, broadcast address, usable range, and default gateway for each. (7 Marks)

#### Model Answer:

**Part (a) — Comparison Table (7 marks)**

| Device | OSI Layer | Operates On | Collision Domain | Broadcast Domain | Address | Key Feature |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Repeater** | L1 | Bits | Not separated | Not separated | None | Signal regeneration |
| **Hub** | L1 | Bits | 1 (shared) | 1 (shared) | None | Multi-port repeater |
| **Bridge** | L2 | Frames | Separates per port | Not separated | MAC | Table-based filtering |
| **Switch** | L2 | Frames | Separates per port | 1 (shared) | MAC | Hardware MAC learning |
| **Router** | L3 | Packets | Separates per port | Separates per port | IP | Routing tables, inter-network |
| **Gateway** | L4–L7 | Segments/Messages | Separates | Separates | Multiple | Protocol translation |

**Brief Justifications (1 mark each):**

- **Repeater/Hub** — purely physical; no intelligence.
- **Bridge** — first "smart" device; reduces collisions, transparent to hosts.
- **Switch** — multi-port bridge with ASIC-based fast forwarding; *the de-facto LAN device today*.
- **Router** — *only* L3+ device that **separates broadcast domains**, enabling internetworking.
- **Gateway** — the *only* device that can connect networks with **different protocols**.

**Part (b) — VLSM Subnet Design (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Sort departments by size (descending).**

A (60) > B (28) > C (14) > D (6).

**Step 2: Compute host bits and subnet mask per department.**

For **Department A (60 hosts):**

$$
2^h - 2 \geq 60 \implies h = 6 \implies \text{mask} = /26
$$

For **Department B (28 hosts):**

$$
2^h - 2 \geq 28 \implies h = 5 \implies \text{mask} = /27
$$

For **Department C (14 hosts):**

$$
2^h - 2 \geq 14 \implies h = 4 \implies \text{mask} = /28
$$

For **Department D (6 hosts):**

$$
2^h - 2 \geq 6 \implies h = 3 \implies \text{mask} = /29
$$

**Step 3: Allocate subnets sequentially (VLSM).**

**Department A — /26 (block size = 64):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Network}   &: 192.168.50.0/26 \\
\text{Usable}    &: 192.168.50.1 \;\text{to}\; 192.168.50.62 \\
\text{Broadcast} &: 192.168.50.63 \\
\text{Gateway}   &: 192.168.50.1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Department B — /27 (block size = 32):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Network}   &: 192.168.50.64/27 \\
\text{Usable}    &: 192.168.50.65 \;\text{to}\; 192.168.50.94 \\
\text{Broadcast} &: 192.168.50.95 \\
\text{Gateway}   &: 192.168.50.65
\end{aligned}
$$

**Department C — /28 (block size = 16):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Network}   &: 192.168.50.96/28 \\
\text{Usable}    &: 192.168.50.97 \;\text{to}\; 192.168.50.110 \\
\text{Broadcast} &: 192.168.50.111 \\
\text{Gateway}   &: 192.168.50.97
\end{aligned}
$$

**Department D — /29 (block size = 8):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Network}   &: 192.168.50.112/29 \\
\text{Usable}    &: 192.168.50.113 \;\text{to}\; 192.168.50.118 \\
\text{Broadcast} &: 192.168.50.119 \\
\text{Gateway}   &: 192.168.50.113
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Allocation Summary:**

| Department | Subnet | Mask | Usable Hosts | Broadcast | Gateway |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| A | 192.168.50.0 | /26 | .1 – .62 | .63 | .1 |
| B | 192.168.50.64 | /27 | .65 – .94 | .95 | .65 |
| C | 192.168.50.96 | /28 | .97 – .110 | .111 | .97 |
| D | 192.168.50.112 | /29 | .113 – .118 | .119 | .113 |

**Address utilization check:** Total used = 64 + 32 + 16 + 8 = **120 of 256** → efficient VLSM.

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Scenarios in Connecting-Device Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Bridge and Switch:** Many students write "Bridge and Switch are the same." They are functionally similar (both at L2), but a *switch* is a *multi-port bridge with hardware-accelerated MAC learning and full-duplex support*. Stating "they are the same" = -2 marks.
>
> 2. **Wrong domain logic:** Saying "a router operates inside one broadcast domain" is a fatal error. A router **isolates** broadcast domains.
>
> 3. **STP cost formula confusion:** Using the modern cost formula `20000/BW` when the question specifies the **original** formula. **Always check the question wording.**
>
> 4. **Default gateway in wrong subnet:** In VLSM problems, students often assign a default gateway IP that is **outside the host's subnet** — a direct loss of 2 marks.
>
> 5. **Skipping the 5-4-3 rule explanation:** When asked "Why is a repeater limited?", the 5-4-3 rule must be stated along with the *reason* (collision detection in CSMA/CD).
>
> 6. **Gateways ≠ Routers:** Treating them as interchangeable is a common trap. Routers = L3 IP routing. Gateways = protocol translation, can work at L4–L7.

---

## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- 🧭 **Layer 1 devices** (Repeater, Hub) work with **bits** and **do not** separate collision or broadcast domains.
- 🌉 **Layer 2 devices** (Bridge, Switch) work with **frames** and use **MAC addresses**; they separate collision domains but **share** one broadcast domain.
- 🛰️ **Layer 3 device** (Router) works with **packets** and **IP addresses**; it separates both collision and broadcast domains.
- 🚪 **Gateway** is a multi-layer protocol translator — the only device that can bridge **dissimilar** protocol stacks.
- 🔁 The **5-4-3 rule** applies to legacy 10 Mbps Ethernet with repeaters.
- 📚 **Transparent Bridging** = bridge is invisible to end hosts; uses MAC learning + flooding + forwarding + filtering.
- 🌳 **STP (802.1D)** prevents loops using a *Root Bridge* (lowest Bridge ID) and path cost $\text{Cost} = \frac{1000}{\text{BW (Mbps)}}$.
- 🔀 **Switches** provide *micro-segmentation* — one collision domain per port, full-duplex, dedicated bandwidth.
- 📊 **VLSM design rule:** Always allocate the **largest subnet first** to minimize fragmentation.
- 🏷️ **Default gateway** must be in the **same subnet** as the host.
- 🎯 **Quick memory hook:** "**H**ub **H**alves nothing; **S**witch **S**aves bandwidth; **R**outer **R**esolves the route; **G**ateway **G**oes between protocols."

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wireless LANs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAMT632 - BASICS OF COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Wireless LANs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Wireless Local Area Network (WLAN)** is a flexible data communication system that uses **Radio Frequency (RF)** or **Infrared (IR)** waves as the physical transmission medium to interconnect network nodes within a limited geographical area, typically confined to a building, campus, or a small cluster of structures. In the KTU 2024 Scheme, WLANs are studied under the **IEEE 802.11** standard family, commonly marketed as **Wi-Fi** (Wireless Fidelity).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IEEE 802.11 Definition:** The IEEE 802.11 standard defines the **Physical (PHY)** and **Medium Access Control (MAC)** sublayers of the OSI Data Link Layer for wireless communication in local area networks. The most common licensed-free frequency bands used are the **2.4 GHz** and **5 GHz** Industrial, Scientific, and Medical (ISM) bands.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **classroom** where students (wireless devices) want to talk to the **teacher** (Access Point) and with each other. Since they cannot speak simultaneously (only one "air medium"), they follow a strict rule: **"Listen before you speak, and if the room is silent, wait a random small time before talking to avoid everyone starting at once."** This is exactly how CSMA/CA works in a WLAN.

A better analogy: Think of WLAN as a **multi-lane radio highway**:
- The **air** is the shared highway.
- The **Access Point (AP)** acts as the **traffic police / toll booth** that regulates entry and exit.
- The **MAC protocol (CSMA/CA)** is the **traffic rule** ensuring no two cars crash into each other.
- **WEP/WPA2** are the **security checkpoints** that prevent unauthorized vehicles from entering.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction — Why not CSMA/CD?**
> In wired Ethernet, a station can *detect* a collision while transmitting (Collision Detection). In wireless, the transmitter's own signal **drowns out** any incoming weak signal from another node. Therefore, **Collision Avoidance (CA)** is used instead of **Collision Detection (CD)**.

### 1.3 Important Physical Constants & Metrics

- **Speed of Light ($c$)**: $3 \times 10^8$ m/s
- **2.4 GHz Band**: $2.400 - 2.4835$ GHz (ISM band, ~83.5 MHz bandwidth)
- **5 GHz Band**: $5.150 - 5.825$ GHz (UNII bands)
- **Free Space Path Loss (FSPL)** at 2.4 GHz, 10 m: ~**60 dB**
- **Maximum Output Power (typical AP)**: **20 dBm** (= 100 mW)
- **Slot Time (802.11b)**: **20 μs** ; (802.11a/g): **9 μs** ; (802.11n/ac): **9 μs**
- **DIFS (DCF Inter-Frame Space)**: **34 μs** (802.11b), **34 μs** (802.11a/g)
- **SIFS (Short Inter-Frame Space)**: **10 μs** (802.11b), **16 μs** (802.11a/g)

### 1.4 Why WLAN? — Motivation

1. **Mobility**: Users can move freely within the coverage area (BSS).
2. **Reduced Cabling Cost**: No need for expensive structured cabling (Cat6, fiber).
3. **Rapid Deployment**: A new node only needs an SSID and a password.
4. **Scalability**: Easy to add new clients without infrastructure overhaul.
5. **Roaming Support**: Handoff between APs allows seamless connectivity.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 IEEE 802.11 Network Architecture

The 802.11 architecture is composed of several components. Understanding this hierarchy is **mandatory** for KTU ESE questions (7–14 marks).

| Component | Full Form | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **BSS** | Basic Service Set | The fundamental building block — a set of stations controlled by a single **AP** (infrastructure mode) or forming an ad-hoc group. |
| **IBSS** | Independent BSS | An **ad-hoc** network with no AP. Stations communicate peer-to-peer. |
| **ESS** | Extended Service Set | Two or more BSSs interconnected via a **Distribution System (DS)** to form a larger network. |
| **DS** | Distribution System | The "backbone" (usually Ethernet) that links multiple APs. |
| **AP** | Access Point | A bridge between the wireless BSS and the wired DS. |
| **STA** | Station | Any device with a wireless network interface (laptop, phone, IoT). |
| **SSID** | Service Set Identifier | The human-readable "network name" (e.g., "KTU_WiFi"). |
| **BSA** | Basic Service Area | The physical coverage region of a single BSS/cell. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Always draw the BSS as a **dashed oval** containing the AP and STAs, and the ESS as a **cloud** (the DS) connecting multiple ovals. This is a 3-mark direct question almost every KTU cycle.

### 2.2 MAC Sublayer — The Heart of 802.11

The MAC layer uses two coordination functions:

1. **DCF (Distributed Coordination Function)** — Mandatory, contention-based, uses CSMA/CA.
2. **PCF (Point Coordination Function)** — Optional, contention-free, polled access (rarely used in practice; superseded by HCF).

#### 2.2.1 CSMA/CA Operational Steps

- **Why CSMA/CA?** Because a wireless station **cannot detect a collision** while transmitting (the near-far problem: its own signal is ~80 dB stronger than any incoming collision signal).
- **How it works — Step by step:**
  1. A station with a frame to transmit **senses the channel**.
  2. If the channel is **idle for a DIFS period**, it transmits immediately.
  3. If the channel is **busy**, it defers transmission, waits for the channel to become idle, then waits an additional **DIFS** period.
  4. After DIFS, it enters the **Contention Window (CW)** and starts a **random backoff timer** uniformly chosen from $[0, CW]$.
  5. The backoff counter decrements only when the channel is sensed idle; it freezes when the channel is busy.
  6. When the counter reaches **0**, the station transmits the entire frame.
  7. Receiver waits **SIFS**, then sends an **ACK**.
  8. If ACK is not received within a timeout, the sender assumes collision and **doubles CW** (Binary Exponential Backoff).

> [!NOTE]
> **SIFS < PIFS < DIFS** is the priority hierarchy. SIFS is used for ACKs, RTS, CTS. PIFS is used by PCF. DIFS is used for normal data. This ordering ensures control frames win the medium first.

#### 2.2.2 Hidden and Exposed Node Problems

**Hidden Node Problem:**
- Station **A** and **B** are both within range of AP, but **A cannot hear B** (and vice versa) because of an obstacle.
- They transmit simultaneously → collision at AP.
- **Solution**: **RTS/CTS handshake.** Before sending data, sender sends **Request-To-Send (RTS)**; AP replies **Clear-To-Send (CTS)** which is heard by all nodes in the BSS, silencing them for the duration of the NAV (Network Allocation Vector).

**Exposed Node Problem:**
- Station **B** wants to send to **A** but is blocked because it hears **C** transmitting to **D** (where D is within B's range but A is not).
- **Partial solution**: RTS/CTS helps — B's RTS to A goes unchallenged if A is free.

### 2.3 Physical Layer Standards (KTU Comparison Table)

| Standard | Year | Band | Max Data Rate | Modulation | Channel Width |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **802.11 (legacy)** | 1997 | 2.4 GHz | **1 / 2 Mbps** | DSSS, FHSS | 20 MHz |
| **802.11a** | 1999 | 5 GHz | **54 Mbps** | OFDM | 20 MHz |
| **802.11b** | 1999 | 2.4 GHz | **11 Mbps** | DSSS (CCK) | 20 MHz |
| **802.11g** | 2003 | 2.4 GHz | **54 Mbps** | OFDM | 20 MHz |
| **802.11n (Wi-Fi 4)** | 2009 | 2.4 / 5 GHz | **600 Mbps** | OFDM + MIMO (4×4) | 20/40 MHz |
| **802.11ac (Wi-Fi 5)** | 2014 | 5 GHz | **6.93 Gbps** | OFDM + MU-MIMO (8×8) | 20/40/80/160 MHz |
| **802.11ax (Wi-Fi 6/6E)** | 2020/21 | 2.4/5/6 GHz | **9.6 Gbps** | OFDMA + 1024-QAM | up to 160 MHz |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why 2.4 GHz reaches further but is slower?** Lower frequency → longer wavelength → better diffraction around obstacles → larger coverage but smaller bandwidth. 5 GHz has more non-overlapping channels and is less congested (microwave ovens, Bluetooth, etc. operate in 2.4 GHz).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression / Value | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Free Space Path Loss (FSPL)** | $FSPL = 20 \log_{10}(d) + 20 \log_{10}(f) + 32.44$ | d in **km**, f in **MHz**, FSPL in **dB** |
| 2 | **Received Power (Friis)** | $P_r = P_t \cdot G_t \cdot G_r \cdot \left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2$ | Watts; $\lambda = c/f$ |
| 3 | **Random Backoff Time** | $T_{backoff} = \text{Random}() \times \text{Slot Time}$ | $\text{Random}() \in [0, CW]$ |
| 4 | **Binary Exponential Backoff (BEB)** | $CW_{i+1} = \min(2 \cdot (CW_i + 1) - 1, CW_{max})$ | $CW_{min}=31, CW_{max}=1023$ (802.11b) |
| 5 | **Shannon Capacity (Wireless)** | $C = B \cdot \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$ | bits/s; B in Hz |
| 6 | **DCF Throughput (Saturation, Bianchi)** | $S = \dfrac{P_s \cdot P_{tr} \cdot E[P]}{(1 - P_{tr}) \cdot \sigma + P_s \cdot P_{tr} \cdot T_s + (1 - P_s) \cdot P_{tr} \cdot T_c}$ | Normalized; $T_s$ = success time, $T_c$ = collision time |
| 7 | **NAV Duration (RTS/CTS)** | $T_{NAV} = 3 \cdot \text{SIFS} + T_{CTS} + T_{DATA} + T_{ACK}$ | Sets virtual carrier-sense timer |
| 8 | **SIFS / PIFS / DIFS Relation** | $\text{DIFS} = \text{SIFS} + 2 \times \text{SlotTime}$ | DIFS = SIFS + 2 slots (for 802.11a/g) |
| 9 | **Number of non-overlapping channels (2.4 GHz)** | **3** (channels 1, 6, 11) | Used to avoid co-channel interference |
| 10 | **OFDM Subcarriers (802.11a/g)** | **52** (48 data + 4 pilot) | Out of 64-point FFT |

> [!CAUTION]
> For KTU board exams, the **Friis transmission equation** and **FSPL** are the most likely 7-mark derivations. Also, the **CSMA/CA timing diagram** is the single most repeated diagram in KTU CS/IT papers — practice drawing it from memory.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hospital Networks**: WLANs enable wireless patient monitoring (Wi-Fi 6 used for IoT medical devices).
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT)**: 802.11ax (Wi-Fi 6) provides deterministic latency for factory automation.
- **Smart Classrooms / KTU Campuses**: ESS with multiple APs enables seamless roaming across lecture halls.
- **Public Hotspots**: Airports, malls use captive portals with WPA3-Enterprise security.
- **Mesh Networks**: 802.11s extends WLAN coverage without wired backbone (used in disaster recovery).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Free Space Path Loss (FSPL) and the Link Budget

The **Friis Transmission Equation** relates transmitted and received power in free space.

#### 3.1.1 Starting Point — Isotropic Radiator

Consider an **isotropic antenna** radiating power $P_t$ uniformly in all directions. At a distance $d$, the power is spread over the surface of a sphere of area $4\pi d^2$. Therefore, the **power flux density** is:

$$S = \frac{P_t}{4\pi d^2}$$

> **Step 1 explanation:** This expresses power per unit area, in W/m², the foundation of all free-space propagation.

#### 3.1.2 Received Power with Directive Antennas

If the transmitting antenna has gain $G_t$ and the receiving antenna has effective aperture $A_e = \dfrac{G_r \lambda^2}{4\pi}$, then the received power is:

$$P_r = S \cdot G_t \cdot A_e = \frac{P_t \cdot G_t \cdot G_r \cdot \lambda^2}{(4\pi d)^2}$$

> **Step 2 explanation:** We multiply flux density by the transmit gain (concentrating energy) and the receive aperture (capturing energy from the wavefront).

#### 3.1.3 Simplification — Substituting $\lambda = c/f$

Rearranging for path loss $PL = P_t / P_r$ (assuming $G_t = G_r = 1$, i.e., isotropic):

$$PL = \left(\frac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{4\pi d \cdot f}{c}\right)^2$$

> **Step 3 explanation:** This is the **Friis free-space path loss** in linear scale. Note that loss grows with the **square of distance** AND the **square of frequency** — this is why 5 GHz signals attenuate faster than 2.4 GHz.

#### 3.1.4 Converting to dB

Take $10 \log_{10}$ of both sides:

$$PL_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(d) + 20 \log_{10}(f) + 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{4\pi}{c}\right)$$

> **Step 4 explanation:** Converting multiplication to addition makes dB arithmetic trivial — just add/subtract gains and losses.

Substituting $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s:

$$20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{4\pi}{3 \times 10^8}\right) = 20 \log_{10}(4.189 \times 10^{-8}) = -147.55 \text{ dB}$$

But using **d in km** and **f in MHz** (KTU standard), the constant becomes $+32.44$:

$$\boxed{FSPL \text{ (dB)} = 20 \log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20 \log_{10}(f_{MHz}) + 32.44}$$

#### 3.1.5 Numerical Example (KTU-style)

> **Problem:** An AP at 2.4 GHz transmits with $P_t = 100$ mW using a 6 dBi antenna. A laptop with 2 dBi antenna is 50 m away. Find received power. (Assume free space.)

**Step 1 — Convert powers to dBm:**

$$P_t = 100 \text{ mW} = 20 \text{ dBm}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate FSPL at 50 m, 2400 MHz:**

$$d = 0.05 \text{ km}, \quad f = 2400 \text{ MHz}$$

$$FSPL = 20 \log_{10}(0.05) + 20 \log_{10}(2400) + 32.44$$

$$= 20 \times (-1.301) + 20 \times (3.380) + 32.44$$

$$= -26.02 + 67.60 + 32.44 = 74.02 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 3 — Apply link budget:**

$$P_r = P_t + G_t + G_r - FSPL$$

$$P_r = 20 + 6 + 2 - 74.02 = -46.02 \text{ dBm}$$

> **Step 4 explanation:** This is the laptop's received power, which is **well above the typical -82 dBm sensitivity** of an 802.11g receiver at 54 Mbps — meaning the link will work. If the user moves to 100 m, FSPL increases by $20 \log_{10}(2) = 6$ dB, so received power drops to **-52 dBm** — still acceptable, but modulation may downshift to lower rates.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: CSMA/CA Backoff Window and Saturation Throughput

#### 3.2.1 Binary Exponential Backoff (BEB) — Number of Slots

The contention window evolves as:

$$CW_i = \begin{cases} CW_{min} & i = 0 \\ \min(2 \cdot CW_{i-1} + 1, CW_{max}) & i \geq 1 \end{cases}$$

For **802.11b**:
- $CW_{min} = 31$ (slots 0 to 31 → 32 slots)
- $CW_{max} = 1023$ (slots 0 to 1023 → 1024 slots)

> **Step 1 explanation:** Each collision doubles the contention range, so a station that has collided many times chooses a much larger random wait, statistically reducing collision probability.

#### 3.2.2 Backoff Time Formula

$$T_{backoff} = \text{Random}_i \times \text{SlotTime}$$

For 802.11b with SlotTime = 20 μs, after 3 collisions:

$$\text{Average backoff} = \frac{31 \times 2^3}{2} \times 20 \text{ μs} = 124 \times 20 \text{ μs} = 2.48 \text{ ms}$$

> **Step 2 explanation:** The expected value of a uniform random variable on $[0, N-1]$ is $N/2$. We use $N = CW_{min} + 1$ slots.

#### 3.2.3 Saturation Throughput (Bianchi's Formula)

Bianchi (1998) showed that for $n$ contending stations, the normalized throughput $S$ is:

$$S = \frac{P_{tr} \cdot P_s \cdot E[L]}{(1 - P_{tr}) \cdot \sigma + P_{tr} \cdot P_s \cdot T_s + P_{tr} \cdot (1 - P_s) \cdot T_c}$$

Where:
- $P_{tr} = 1 - (1 - \tau)^n$ → probability at least one station transmits
- $P_s = \dfrac{n \tau (1 - \tau)^{n-1}}{P_{tr}}$ → probability transmission is successful
- $\tau$ → steady-state transmission probability
- $E[L]$ → average frame payload (bits)
- $\sigma$ → slot duration
- $T_s$ → time channel is sensed busy due to success
- $T_c$ → time channel is sensed busy due to collision

> **Step 3 explanation:** The numerator is the useful payload delivered per slot. The denominator is the expected slot duration (idle, success, or collision). This is the **Bianchi model** — a classic result KTU examiners love as a 14-mark analytical question.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Wireless Standards

| Parameter | 802.11b | 802.11a | 802.11g | 802.11n | 802.11ac | 802.11ax |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Band (GHz)** | 2.4 | 5 | 2.4 | 2.4/5 | 5 | 2.4/5/6 |
| **Max Rate (Mbps)** | 11 | 54 | 54 | 600 | 6933 | 9607 |
| **Modulation** | CCK | BPSK–64QAM | BPSK–64QAM | BPSK–64QAM | BPSK–256QAM | BPSK–1024QAM |
| **MIMO Streams** | 1×1 | 1×1 | 1×1 | 4×4 | 8×8 (MU) | 8×8 (MU) |
| **Channel (MHz)** | 20 | 20 | 20 | 20/40 | 20/40/80/160 | up to 160 |
| **Slot Time (μs)** | 20 | 9 | 9 | 9 | 9 | 9 |
| **Backward Compat** | — | — | with b | with b/g | with a/n | with all |
| **OFDMA Support** | No | No | No | No | No | **Yes** |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 IEEE 802.11 Architecture — BSS, ESS, and Distribution System

```mermaid
graph TD
    DS[Distribution System Ethernet Backbone]
    AP1[Access Point AP1 Cell 1]
    AP2[Access Point AP2 Cell 2]
    AP3[Access Point AP3 Cell 3]
    STA1[Station Laptop 1]
    STA2[Station Mobile 2]
    STA3[Station IoT Sensor 3]
    STA4[Station Tablet 4]
    STA5[Station Printer 5]

    DS --- AP1
    DS --- AP2
    DS --- AP3

    AP1 -. Wireless .-> STA1
    AP1 -. Wireless .-> STA2
    AP2 -. Wireless .-> STA3
    AP2 -. Wireless .-> STA4
    AP3 -. Wireless .-> STA5

    subgraph BSS1[BSS 1 Infrastructure Mode]
        AP1
        STA1
        STA2
    end

    subgraph BSS2[BSS 2 Infrastructure Mode]
        AP2
        STA3
        STA4
    end

    subgraph BSS3[BSS 3 Infrastructure Mode]
        AP3
        STA5
    end

    DS2[Extended Service Set ESS] -. Links .-> BSS1
    DS2 -. Links .-> BSS2
    DS2 -. Links .-> BSS3
```

> **Reading Guide:** Each `BSS` block represents one **cell** (Basic Service Area) with a single AP and multiple stations. The `DS` is the wired Ethernet backbone. The `ESS` (top-level label) is the entire extended network visible to clients as a single SSID.

### 4.2 CSMA/CA — DCF Timing Diagram (Most Repeated KTU Diagram)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Sender as Sender Station
    participant Medium as Wireless Medium
    participant Receiver as Receiver Station
    participant Other as Other Stations

    Note over Sender,Other: Channel is initially BUSY
    Sender->>Sender: Detect carrier for DIFS duration
    Note over Medium: DIFS 34 microsecond idle period
    Sender->>Sender: Choose random backoff from CW
    Note over Sender: Backoff Counter 5 slots
    loop While Channel Idle
        Sender->>Sender: Decrement backoff per slot
    end
    Sender->>Medium: Transmit RTS
    Medium->>Receiver: RTS Frame
    Note over Other: Other STAs set NAV from RTS duration
    Receiver->>Medium: After SIFS send CTS
    Medium->>Sender: CTS Frame
    Note over Other: Other STAs update NAV from CTS
    Sender->>Medium: After SIFS send DATA
    Medium->>Receiver: DATA Frame
    Receiver->>Medium: After SIFS send ACK
    Note over Sender,Other: NAV protects transmission from interference
    Sender->>Sender: If ACK timeout then BEB increase CW and retry
```

> **Reading Guide:** The **DIFS → Backoff → RTS → SIFS → CTS → SIFS → DATA → SIFS → ACK** sequence is the canonical DCF mechanism. Note the use of **SIFS** for all control-response frames — this is what gives priority to in-flight exchanges over new contenders.

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — IEEE 802.11 Protocol Stack

```mermaid
graph LR
    APP[Application Layer HTTP FTP VoIP]
    TCP[Transport Layer TCP UDP]
    IP[Network Layer IPv4 IPv6]
    LLC[Logical Link Control 802.2]
    MACA[MAC Sublayer 802.11 DCF PCF HCF]
    PMD[Physical Medium Dependent PMD]
    PHY[Physical Layer Convergence PLCP]
    ANT[Antenna 2.4 GHz or 5 GHz]

    APP --> TCP
    TCP --> IP
    IP --> LLC
    LLC --> MACA
    MACA --> PHY
    PHY --> PMD
    PMD --> ANT

    subgraph FREQ[RF Spectrum]
        ANT -. 2.4 GHz ISM .-> FREQ
        ANT -. 5 GHz UNII .-> FREQ
    end
```

> **Reading Guide:** This shows how a packet descends through the protocol stack. The **MAC Sublayer** is where all CSMA/CA, RTS/CTS, and fragmentation logic resides. The **PHY** is where DSSS, OFDM, and MIMO modulation take place. The **antenna** finally radiates the signal into the ISM band.

### 4.4 Hidden Node Problem and RTS/CTS Solution

```mermaid
graph TB
    AP[Access Point]
    NA[Node A Sender]
    NB[Node B Hidden Interferer]
    NC[Node C Receiver for A]

    NA <-. Within Range .-> AP
    NC <-. Within Range .-> AP
    NB <-. Within Range .-> AP
    NA -. Cannot Hear .-> NB
    NB -. Cannot Hear .-> NA
    NC <-. Within Range .-> NB

    Note1[Step 1 Node A sends RTS]
    Note2[Step 2 AP replies CTS heard by B]
    Note3[Step 3 Node B silent for NAV duration]
    Note4[Step 4 Node A transmits DATA collision free]

    AP --> Note1
    AP --> Note2
    AP --> Note3
    AP --> Note4
```

> **Reading Guide:** The dashed lines indicate **RF range**. Nodes A and B cannot hear each other but both can reach the AP. The **CTS** broadcast by the AP is heard by B, which sets its **NAV** timer and stays silent. The data from A arrives at the AP collision-free.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` Define a Wireless LAN. List any four IEEE 802.11 standards with their maximum data rates.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Wireless Local Area Network (WLAN)** is a network that uses radio frequency (RF) waves, typically in the **2.4 GHz** and **5 GHz** ISM bands, to provide network connectivity within a limited geographical area without the need for wired cabling. It is governed by the **IEEE 802.11** standard family, commonly known as **Wi-Fi**.

Four IEEE 802.11 standards with their maximum data rates:

1. **IEEE 802.11b** — **11 Mbps** (2.4 GHz, DSSS/CCK)
2. **IEEE 802.11a** — **54 Mbps** (5 GHz, OFDM)
3. **IEEE 802.11g** — **54 Mbps** (2.4 GHz, OFDM)
4. **IEEE 802.11n (Wi-Fi 4)** — **600 Mbps** (2.4/5 GHz, MIMO-OFDM)

---

#### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` Distinguish between the **hidden node** and **exposed node** problems in wireless networks.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Hidden Node Problem | Exposed Node Problem |
|---|---|---|
| **Scenario** | A and C cannot hear each other but both can reach B. Both transmit simultaneously → collision at B. | B wants to transmit to A, but is blocked because it hears C transmitting to D (which is harmless to A's reception). |
| **Cause** | Physical obstacles, asymmetric range, or RF shadowing. | Conservative carrier-sensing that defers unnecessarily. |
| **Effect** | **Reduces throughput** due to actual data collisions at the receiver. | **Reduces spatial reuse**; channels that could be used in parallel are blocked. |
| **Solution** | **RTS/CTS** handshake sets NAV and silences the hidden interferer. | RTS/CTS provides partial relief; modern 802.11 uses **physical carrier-sense threshold tuning** and **MIMO spatial multiplexing**. |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question 2:**
> Students often confuse the two problems. The **key differentiator** is whether a transmission is *unnecessarily blocked* (exposed) versus *colliding at the receiver* (hidden). Mention the **NAV mechanism** for full marks.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Rule:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each is exactly 14 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

#### (a) Explain the **CSMA/CA** mechanism used in IEEE 802.11 with a neat timing diagram. Discuss how it avoids collisions. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**CSMA/CA (Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Avoidance)** is the fundamental MAC protocol of IEEE 802.11. Unlike wired Ethernet's CSMA/CD, a wireless station **cannot detect a collision** during transmission (its own signal is ~80 dB stronger than any incoming signal). Hence, 802.11 takes a **proactive** approach — it tries to *avoid* collisions rather than detect them.

**Step-by-Step Operation:**

1. **Carrier Sensing:** A station with a frame to send first senses the channel using both **physical carrier-sense** (CCA — Clear Channel Assessment) and **virtual carrier-sense** (NAV — Network Allocation Vector).
2. **DIFS Wait:** If the channel is found idle, the station waits for a **DIFS (DCF Inter-Frame Space)** period — typically **34 μs** in 802.11b or **34 μs** in 802.11a/g.
3. **Backoff:** After DIFS, the station enters the **Contention Window** and picks a random backoff counter $b$ uniformly from $[0, CW]$ where $CW$ starts at $CW_{min} = 31$.
4. **Slot Decrement:** The counter decrements by 1 for every slot time (20 μs in 802.11b, 9 μs in 802.11a/g) the medium is idle. If a transmission is detected, the counter **freezes**.
5. **Transmission:** When the counter reaches 0, the station transmits the entire frame.
6. **Acknowledgment:** The receiver waits **SIFS (10 μs)** then sends an **ACK**. If the sender does not receive the ACK within a timeout, it assumes the frame was lost (due to collision or noise) and invokes **Binary Exponential Backoff**, doubling $CW$ up to $CW_{max} = 1023$.

**Why It Avoids Collisions:**

- The **random backoff** ensures that even if two stations find the channel idle simultaneously, they are unlikely to choose the same backoff value.
- The **DIFS + backoff** structure provides a deterministic ordering of access attempts.
- The **NAV** (virtual carrier-sense) field in every frame reserves the medium for the announced duration, preventing hidden nodes from transmitting.
- The **ACK** provides positive confirmation, unlike CD's passive detection.

**Timing Diagram (to be drawn in exam):**

```
Station:  |---DIFS---|--Backoff--|RTS|--SIFS--|CTS|--SIFS--|DATA|--SIFS--|ACK|
Other STAs:        |--Set NAV from RTS/CTS--------|
```

---

#### (b) Explain the **IEEE 802.11 architecture** in detail. Define **BSS**, **ESS**, **DS**, and **AP** with a suitable diagram. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

The IEEE 802.11 architecture is built on a hierarchy of components:

1. **STA (Station):** Any device containing a wireless MAC and PHY interface conforming to 802.11. Examples: laptops, smartphones, IoT sensors.

2. **AP (Access Point):** A device that acts as a **bridge** between the wireless stations and the wired **Distribution System**. It also performs frame translation and acts as a centralized coordinator in infrastructure mode.

3. **BSS (Basic Service Set):** The **fundamental building block** of an 802.11 network. It consists of:
   - An **infrastructure BSS** — one AP + multiple STAs, or
   - An **IBSS (Independent BSS)** — an ad-hoc group with no AP, where STAs communicate peer-to-peer.

4. **BSA (Basic Service Area):** The physical coverage area of a BSS, bounded by the transmit power and antenna pattern.

5. **DS (Distribution System):** The **backbone** (typically Ethernet) that interconnects multiple APs to form a larger network. It allows a station in one BSS to communicate with a station in another BSS.

6. **ESS (Extended Service Set):** Two or more BSSs interconnected via a DS, presented to the user as a **single, seamless network** (same SSID).

**Diagram (to be drawn in exam):**

```
                              [DS - Ethernet Backbone]
                              /            |            \
                          [AP1]          [AP2]          [AP3]
                          /   \          /   \          /   \
                       (STA) (STA)    (STA) (STA)    (STA) (STA)
                        \____________ESS____________/
```

**Key Distinctions:**

- All STAs in an ESS share the same **SSID**.
- Roaming between BSSs within an ESS is supported via **re-association** frames.
- The DS can be any networking technology (Ethernet, ATM, fiber) — 802.11 only specifies the AP↔DS interface, not the DS technology itself.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### (a) What is the **RTS/CTS mechanism** in IEEE 802.11? How does it solve the **hidden node problem**? Explain with a sequence diagram. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**RTS/CTS (Request-To-Send / Clear-To-Send)** is an optional four-way handshake in IEEE 802.11 designed to combat the **hidden node problem** and reduce collision duration.

**The Hidden Node Problem Recap:**

Consider three nodes A, B, and an AP. A and B are both within range of the AP, but **A cannot hear B's transmissions** (an obstacle blocks them). When A transmits a data frame to the AP, B is unaware and may also transmit, causing a **collision at the AP**. This is the hidden node problem.

**RTS/CTS Solution — Step by Step:**

1. **RTS Frame:** A sends a small **RTS** (20 bytes) to the AP, containing the duration of the entire upcoming exchange. Any node within range of A that hears the RTS sets its **NAV** timer.
2. **CTS Frame:** The AP responds with a **CTS** (14 bytes) after SIFS. The CTS contains the *same* duration field. Crucially, the CTS is transmitted at a power and rate such that **all nodes in the BSS can hear it**, including the hidden node B.
3. **Silent Period:** Upon hearing either the RTS or CTS, B sets its NAV to defer all transmissions for the announced duration. This **silences B** even though A's data frame is not directly decodable by B.
4. **DATA + ACK:** A transmits DATA; AP replies with ACK. Both A and B wait for the NAV to expire before contending again.

**Sequence Diagram (to be drawn):**

```
   A         AP        B (Hidden)
   |  RTS     |          |
   |--------->|          |
   |          |  CTS     |
   |          |--------->|  (B hears CTS, sets NAV)
   |  DATA    |          |
   |--------->|          |  (B silent)
   |          |  ACK     |
   |<---------|          |
   |   [NAV expires]     |
   |  ...new contention  |
```

**Benefits:**

- **Collisions, if any, occur on the small RTS frame** (saving bandwidth — collision window is 20 bytes, not 1500-byte data frame).
- **Reduces hidden-node collisions** by silencing out-of-range interferers.
- The **CTS** is the magic ingredient — its broadcast reach protects against nodes that A cannot hear.

**Drawback:** Adds latency due to extra SIFS intervals. For this reason, RTS/CTS is often enabled **only when payload exceeds a threshold** (e.g., RTS_Threshold = 500 bytes).

---

#### (b) Compare the IEEE 802.11a, 802.11b, 802.11g, and 802.11n standards in terms of **frequency band, modulation technique, maximum data rate, and channel bandwidth**. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | 802.11a | 802.11b | 802.11g | 802.11n |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Year Ratified** | 1999 | 1999 | 2003 | 2009 |
| **Frequency Band** | **5 GHz** (UNII) | **2.4 GHz** (ISM) | **2.4 GHz** (ISM) | **2.4 GHz and 5 GHz** |
| **Modulation** | OFDM (BPSK to 64-QAM) | DSSS with **CCK** | OFDM (BPSK to 64-QAM) | OFDM with **MIMO** (up to 4×4) |
| **Channel Bandwidth** | **20 MHz** | **20 MHz** | **20 MHz** | **20 MHz or 40 MHz** |
| **Max Data Rate** | **54 Mbps** | **11 Mbps** | **54 Mbps** | **600 Mbps** (theoretical) |
| **Non-Overlapping Channels** | 12–23 (region-dependent) | 3 (1, 6, 11) | 3 (1, 6, 11) | 3 (2.4 GHz) / 12+ (5 GHz) |
| **Range (Indoor)** | ~35 m | ~38 m | ~38 m | ~70 m (with MIMO gain) |
| **Backward Compatibility** | None with b/g | — | Compatible with b | Compatible with a/b/g |
| **Typical Use** | Enterprise, less congestion | Legacy devices | Consumer Wi-Fi | Modern laptops, APs |

**Key Engineering Insights:**

- **802.11a vs 802.11b:** 802.11a is faster (54 vs 11 Mbps) and has more channels (less interference) but has **shorter range** because 5 GHz signals attenuate faster. 802.11b was the first widely adopted home Wi-Fi standard.
- **802.11g advantage:** Provides 802.11a-like speed in the 2.4 GHz band, ensuring backward compatibility with 802.11b.
- **802.11n leap:** Introduced **MIMO (Multiple-Input Multiple-Output)**, allowing simultaneous transmission on multiple antennas over the same channel — this is the breakthrough that took Wi-Fi from 54 to 600 Mbps.
- **Channel Bonding:** 802.11n allows two adjacent 20 MHz channels to be bonded into a single 40 MHz channel, doubling the available bandwidth.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part B Question (b):**
> A common mistake is to **swap the bands** of 802.11a and 802.11b. Memorize: **"a = 5 GHz"** (a → higher, since 5 > 2.4 alphabetically isn't reliable; instead remember that 'a' was the *first* 5 GHz standard). Also, students forget to mention **OFDM** for 802.11n — partial credit loss. Always mention **MIMO** explicitly.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **WLAN = IEEE 802.11 = Wi-Fi**, operating in **2.4 GHz** and **5 GHz** ISM/UNII bands.
- [x] **Architecture**: STA → BSS → DS → ESS. **AP** bridges BSS (wireless) to DS (wired).
- [x] **IBSS** = ad-hoc mode, **no AP**. **Infrastructure BSS** has an AP.
- [x] **MAC Protocol = CSMA/CA** (NOT CSMA/CD) because wireless stations cannot detect collisions during transmission.
- [x] **Inter-frame spacing hierarchy**: **SIFS < PIFS < DIFS < EIFS**. SIFS is for control responses.
- [x] **DCF** is contention-based (mandatory). **PCF/HCF** are contention-free (optional, rarely used).
- [x] **Binary Exponential Backoff**: $CW$ starts at 31, doubles up to 1023 on every collision.
- [x] **Hidden Node** problem → solved by **RTS/CTS** (RTS = 20 bytes, CTS = 14 bytes).
- [x] **Exposed Node** problem → partial solution via RTS/CTS; better solutions use **MIMO** and **spatial reuse**.
- [x] **NAV** = Network Allocation Vector = virtual carrier-sense timer set by RTS/CTS/Data frame headers.
- [x] **802.11b** = 11 Mbps, DSSS/CCK, 2.4 GHz. **802.11a** = 54 Mbps, OFDM, 5 GHz. **802.11g** = 54 Mbps, OFDM, 2.4 GHz (backward compatible with b). **802.11n** = 600 Mbps, MIMO-OFDM, 2.4/5 GHz. **802.11ac** = 6.93 Gbps, MU-MIMO, 5 GHz. **802.11ax** = 9.6 Gbps, OFDMA, 2.4/5/6 GHz.
- [x] **2.4 GHz has 3 non-overlapping channels** (1, 6, 11) in most regulatory domains.
- [x] **OFDM in 802.11a/g** uses **52 subcarriers** (48 data + 4 pilots) over a 20 MHz channel.
- [x] **Friis Path Loss** grows as $f^2$ and $d^2$ — higher frequencies and distances attenuate signals faster.
- [x] **Security Evolution**: WEP (broken) → WPA (TKIP) → **WPA2 (AES-CCMP, mandatory)** → **WPA3 (SAE, anti-dictionary)**.
- [x] **Bianchi's model** is the analytical foundation for DCF saturation throughput — expect this as a 14-mark derivation.
- [x] **Slot times**: 20 μs for 802.11b (longer preambles), 9 μs for 802.11a/g/n/ac/ax (OFDM).
- [x] **ACK timeout → frame loss assumed** → invoke BEB. No NACK in 802.11.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Strategy Tip:** For ESE Module 3 questions on Wireless LANs, always draw:
> (1) The **BSS/ESS architecture diagram** with AP, DS, and SSID.
> (2) The **CSMA/CA timing diagram** showing DIFS → Backoff → RTS → CTS → DATA → ACK.
> (3) The **hidden node** diagram with NAV silencing the interferer.
