> 3. **PCM Bit Rate formula:** A common mistake is using $f_{max}$ instead of $f_s$. Always confirm whether the problem states the sampling rate or the signal frequency.
> 4. **Block Coding efficiency:** Students often write $\frac{n}{m}$ instead of $\frac{m}{n}$. Efficiency is **data bits / total code bits**.
> 5. **B8ZS vs. HDB3:** B8ZS triggers on 8 zeros (T1, North America); HDB3 triggers on 4 zeros (E1, Europe/Japan). Examiners specifically test this distinction.

---

### 📌 **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

- ✅ **Digital Transmission = Line Coding + Block Coding + Scrambling + PCM/DM.**
- ✅ **Nyquist Sampling Theorem:** $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ — non-negotiable.
- ✅ **PCM Bit Rate:** $R_{PCM} = f_s \times n = 2 f_{max} \times \log_2 L$.
- ✅ **PCM SNR:** $\text{SNR}_{dB} = 6.02n + 1.76$ — every extra bit adds ~6 dB of quality.
- ✅ **Manchester has bandwidth $B = N$; NRZ has bandwidth $B = N/2$** — Manchester doubles the cost.
- ✅ **Bipolar AMI** eliminates the DC component but fails on long zero runs — solved by B8ZS/HDB3.
- ✅ **4B/5B (Fast Ethernet) and 8B/10B (Gigabit Ethernet)** are the dominant block coding schemes.
- ✅ **Delta Modulation** trades 1-bit simplicity for two artifacts: slope overload and granular noise.
- ✅ **DS0 = 64 kbps** is the canonical unit of digital telephony — built on 8 kHz × 8 bits.
- ✅ **Nyquist Capacity:** $C = 2B \log_2 \vert V \vert$ (noiseless); **Shannon Capacity:** $C = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$ (noisy).
- ✅ Always remember: **In a KTU exam, draw the waveform** for line coding questions — full marks depend on it.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analog Transmission
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAMT632 - BASICS OF COMPUTER NETWORKS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Analog Transmission # Module 4 — Analog Transmission ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 What is Analog Transmission? **Analog Transmission** is the process of converting digital (or analog) information into an **analog signal** so that it can traverse a bandpass-limited communication medium (such as a telephone line, optical fiber, or wireless radio channel). The information-bearing attribute of a sine wave — namely its **amplitude**, **frequency**, or **phase** — is varied in proportion to the message signal. This variation is generically called **modulation**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Exact):** *Analog transmission refers to the transmission of analog signals regardless of the nature of the source (digital or analog). When the source is digital, the process of converting digital data into analog signals is called digital-to-analog conversion. When the source is analog, the process is called analog-to-analog conversion.* ### 1.2 Why do we NEED Analog Transmission? Computer networks produce **digital data** (bits: 0 and 1). However, the *physical channels* available to us are almost always **bandpass channels** that simply refuse to pass very low frequencies or DC. A copper wire attenuates frequencies above a certain cutoff; a radio channel must operate inside a licensed frequency band; an optical channel uses light pulses at extremely high carrier frequencies. | Source Signal | Channel Type | Required Action | |---------------|--------------|-----------------| | Digital (bits) | Bandpass (e.g., telephone line) | **Digital-to-Analog Conversion** (D/A) | | Analog (voice, video) | Bandpass (e.g., RF wireless) | **Analog-to-Analog Conversion** (A/A) | ### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Ship-in-a-Bottle" Story Imagine you have a tiny, fragile digital message (like a ship in a bottle) that needs to travel across a vast ocean (the bandpass channel). The ocean refuses to carry tiny bottles directly — it only carries large, floating containers at specific cruising frequencies. **Modulation is the act of strapping your bottle onto a large carrier ship (a sine wave) and sailing it at the right cruising speed.** At the destination, the carrier is removed (demodulation) and the bottle is opened. > [!NOTE] > **Carrier Signal** — A high-frequency sine wave that carries the information. Mathematically: > $$c(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t + \phi_c)$$ > Three modifiable properties: **Amplitude ($A_c$)**, **Frequency ($f_c$)**, **Phase ($\phi_c$)**. ### 1.4 Two Grand Branches of Analog Transmission 1. **Digital-to-Analog (D/A) Conversion** — Source is digital. - Amplitude Shift Keying (ASK) - Frequency Shift Keying (FSK) - Phase Shift Keying (PSK) - Quadrature Amplitude Modulation (QAM) 2. **Analog-to-Analog (A/A) Conversion** — Source is analog. - Amplitude Modulation (AM) - Frequency Modulation (FM) - Phase Modulation (PM) ![Conceptual Classification of Analog Transmission] # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Digital-to-Analog Conversion The information resides in the **change** between signal elements. A signal element of duration $T_s$ carries $\log_2 S$ bits where $S$ = number of distinct signal points (symbols). ### 2.1.1 Amplitude Shift Keying (ASK) - **How it works:** The amplitude of the carrier is switched between two levels (typically ON/OFF) while frequency and phase remain constant. - **Binary form (BASK / OOK — On-Off Keying):** Bit 1 → carrier present, Bit 0 → no carrier. - **Why use it:** Simplest, but very susceptible to noise (noise is amplitude disturbance). **ASK Modulated Wave:** $$s(t) = \begin{cases} A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) & \text{bit} = 1 \\ 0 & \text{bit} = 0 \end{cases}$$ **Bandwidth of BASK:** $$B_{ASK} = (1 + d) \times S \quad \text{where } d \text{ is the duty cycle, } S = \text{Baud rate}$$ For a full-cycle rectangular pulse shape, $B_{ASK} = S$. Bit rate = Baud rate (since $S = 2$ levels). ### 2.1.2 Frequency Shift Keying (FSK) - **How it works:** Frequency of carrier is shifted between two values to represent 1 and 0; amplitude and phase stay constant. - **Why use it:** Far more immune to amplitude noise (lightning, interference) — radio modems and early telephone modems use it. **BFSK Modulated Wave:** $$s(t) = \begin{cases} A_c \cos(2\pi f_1 t) & \text{bit} = 1 \\ A_c \cos(2\pi f_2 t) & \text{bit} = 0 \end{cases}$$ **Bandwidth of BFSK (coherent):** $$B_{FSK} = \vert f_1 - f_2 \vert + 2 \times S$$ $$B_{FSK} = \vert f_1 - f_2 \vert + N_b$$ where $N_b$ = bit rate (since BFSK has 2 frequencies → baud = bit rate). > [!TIP] > **Memory Trick:** "BFSK uses TWO carriers, so its bandwidth is wider than BPSK but more robust." ### 2.1.3 Phase Shift Keying (PSK) - **How it works:** Phase of carrier is varied; amplitude and frequency constant. - **Binary PSK (BPSK):** Two phases — 0° (bit 1) and 180° (bit 0). The most noise-immune of the binary schemes. - **Quadrature PSK (QPSK):** Four phases — 45°, 135°, 225°, 315°. Each symbol carries **2 bits**, doubling spectral efficiency. **BPSK Modulated Wave:** $$s(t) = \begin{cases} A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) & \text{bit} = 1 \\ A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t + \pi) & \text{bit} = 0 \end{cases}$$ **Bandwidth of BPSK:** $$B_{BPSK} = S \quad \text{(same as ASK)}$$ **Bandwidth of QPSK:** $$B_{QPSK} = S = \frac{N_b}{2}$$ QPSK requires **half the bandwidth** of BPSK for the same bit rate — a major engineering win. ### 2.1.4 Quadrature Amplitude Modulation (QAM) - **How it works:** Combines ASK + PSK. Both **amplitude AND phase** are varied, producing a constellation of $M$ points. - **Why use it:** Highest spectral efficiency. Used in cable modems, Wi-Fi, 4G/5G, ADSL. **Constellation points:** $M = 2^n$ where $n$ = bits per symbol. Common schemes: **4-QAM** (= QPSK), **16-QAM**, **64-QAM**, **256-QAM**, **1024-QAM**. **Bandwidth of M-ary QAM:** $$B_{QAM} = S = \frac{N_b}{\log_2 M}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Constellation Trade-off:** Larger M (more points) ⇒ higher bit rate per Hz **BUT** points get closer ⇒ more errors from noise. Shannon's law sets the ultimate ceiling. --- ## 2.2 Analog-to-Analog Conversion The baseband signal is *already* analog, but still needs shifting into the bandpass of the channel. ### 2.2.1 Amplitude Modulation (AM) $$s_{AM}(t) = [A_c + m(t)] \cos(2\pi f_c t)$$ $$B_{AM} = 2 \times BW_{\text{modulating signal}}$$ ### 2.2.2 Frequency Modulation (FM) $$s_{FM}(t) = A_c \cos\!\left(2\pi f_c t + 2\pi k_f \int_0^t m(\tau)d\tau\right)$$ **Carson's Rule for FM Bandwidth:** $$B_{FM} = 2(\Delta f + f_m) = 2(\beta + 1) f_m$$ where $\beta = \frac{\Delta f}{f_m}$ is the modulation index. ### 2.2.3 Phase Modulation (PM) $$s_{PM}(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t + k_p m(t))$$ PM is closely related to FM. If you differentiate the message, PM becomes FM (and vice versa). --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Modulation | Bandwidth Formula | Bit-Rate vs Baud-Rate | Noise Immunity | |---|-----------|-------------------|----------------------|----------------| | 1 | BASK | $B = S$ | $N = S$ (log₂2 = 1) | **Low** | | 2 | BFSK | $B = \vert f_1 - f_2 \vert + 2S$ | $N = S$ | Medium | | 3 | BPSK | $B = S$ | $N = S$ | **High** | | 4 | QPSK | $B = S = N/2$ | $N = 2S$ | High | | 5 | M-ary PSK | $B = N / \log_2 M$ | $S = N / \log_2 M$ | High (drops as M ↑) | | 6 | M-ary QAM | $B = N / \log_2 M$ | $S = N / \log_2 M$ | Medium (drops faster) | | 7 | AM | $B = 2 \times BW_m$ | N/A (analog) | **Low** | | 8 | FM | $B = 2(\Delta f + f_m)$ | N/A | **Highest** | | 9 | PM | $B = 2(\beta + 1) f_m$ | N/A | High | **Master Relationship** (applies to ALL D/A schemes): $$N_b = S \times \log_2 M \quad \text{bits/sec}$$ $$B_{\text{min}} = S \quad \text{Hz (theoretical minimum)}$$ **Real-World Utility:** - ASK → simple optical links, IR remotes. - FSK → pager systems, RFID, Bluetooth (GFSK). - PSK → satellite comms, GPS, deep-space links (BPSK survives cosmic noise). - QAM → Wi-Fi 802.11ax (1024-QAM), DOCSIS 3.1 cable internet, 5G NR. - AM/FM → commercial radio broadcasting (530 kHz – 1700 kHz for AM, 88–108 MHz for FM). # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Bit Rate vs Bandwidth Trade-off in M-ary QAM **Problem Statement:** A channel of bandwidth **4 kHz** must support **12 kbps** using M-ary QAM modulation. Find the minimum number of signal points $M$ and the resulting baud rate. ### Step 1 — Identify Known Quantities From the problem: - Available bandwidth: $B = 4 \text{ kHz} = 4000 \text{ Hz}$ - Required bit rate: $N_b = 12 \text{ kbps} = 12{,}000 \text{ bits/sec}$ ### Step 2 — Apply the Theoretical Minimum Bandwidth Relationship For any D/A modulation, the absolute theoretical minimum bandwidth equals the baud rate: $$B = S$$ This is derived from the Nyquist–Hartley theorem which states that a noiseless channel of bandwidth $B$ can support at most $2B$ signal changes per second. With advanced M-ary signaling, $B$ can be made equal to $S$ exactly. ### Step 3 — Determine the Baud Rate $$S = B = 4000 \text{ symbols/sec (baud)}$$ ### Step 4 — Apply the Bit-Rate / Baud-Rate Master Equation $$N_b = S \times \log_2 M$$ Rearranging to solve for $\log_2 M$: $$\log_2 M = \frac{N_b}{S}$$ $$\log_2 M = \frac{12{,}000}{4{,}000} = 3$$ ### Step 5 — Solve for M $$M = 2^{\log_2 M} = 2^3 = 8$$ So we need an **8-point constellation** → **8-QAM** (or 8-PSK). ### Step 6 — Verify the Solution - Each symbol carries $\log_2 8 = 3$ bits ✓ - Symbols per second = 4,000 - Total bits per second = 4,000 × 3 = 12,000 bps ✓ - Bandwidth consumed = 4,000 Hz ✓ ### Step 7 — Engineering Interpretation Using 8-QAM, we compressed a 12 kbps stream into a 4 kHz channel — a **3× spectral efficiency gain** over BPSK. However, the 8 constellation points are closer together than 4 points, raising the bit error rate (BER). We must add forward error correction (FEC) to compensate. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Carson's Rule Applied to an FM Broadcast Signal **Problem Statement:** An FM broadcaster uses a modulating audio signal with $f_m = 15 \text{ kHz}$ and a peak frequency deviation $\Delta f = 75 \text{ kHz}$. Calculate the transmission bandwidth. ### Step 1 — Identify Given Quantities $$f_m = 15 \text{ kHz}, \qquad \Delta f = 75 \text{ kHz}$$ ### Step 2 — Compute the Modulation Index β $$\beta = \frac{\Delta f}{f_m} = \frac{75 \text{ kHz}}{15 \text{ kHz}} = 5$$ ### Step 3 — Apply Carson's Rule $$B_{FM} = 2(\Delta f + f_m)$$ $$B_{FM} = 2(75 \text{ kHz} + 15 \text{ kHz})$$ $$B_{FM} = 2 \times 90 \text{ kHz}$$ $$B_{FM} = 180 \text{ kHz}$$ ### Step 4 — Cross-Check with the β Form $$B_{FM} = 2(\beta + 1) f_m = 2(5 + 1) \times 15 \text{ kHz} = 12 \times 15 \text{ kHz} = 180 \text{ kHz} \checkmark$$ ### Step 5 — Practical Context This is exactly the **200 kHz channel spacing** allocated to commercial FM radio (with a small guard band). Real-world commercial FM uses $\beta = 5$ and $f_m = 15$ kHz, yielding 180 kHz, leaving a 10 kHz guard on each side within the standard 200 kHz channel. --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Relationship Between Carrier Frequencies in BFSK **Problem Statement:** Find the minimum bandwidth of a BFSK signal transmitting at 2 kbps with carrier frequencies $f_1 = 50 \text{ kHz}$ and $f_2 = 52 \text{ kHz}$. ### Step 1 — Known Values Bit rate $N_b = 2000$ bps, so baud rate $S = 2000$ baud (BFSK has 2 frequencies → bit rate = baud rate). $f_1 = 50{,}000$ Hz, $f_2 = 52{,}000$ Hz. ### Step 2 — Frequency Separation $$\vert f_1 - f_2 \vert = \vert 50{,}000 - 52{,}000 \vert = 2000 \text{ Hz}$$ ### Step 3 — Apply BFSK Bandwidth Formula $$B_{FSK} = \vert f_1 - f_2 \vert + 2 \times S$$ $$B_{FSK} = 2000 + 2 \times 2000$$ $$B_{FSK} = 2000 + 4000 = 6000 \text{ Hz} = 6 \text{ kHz}$$ ### Step 4 — Interpretation Even though the data is only 2 kbps, BFSK consumes 6 kHz — three times the bandwidth of BPSK! This is the price of BFSK's robustness against amplitude noise. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — When to Use What | Criterion | ASK | FSK | BPSK | QPSK | 16-QAM | 64-QAM | |-----------|-----|-----|------|------|--------|--------| | Bits per symbol | 1 | 1 | 1 | 2 | 4 | 6 | | Bandwidth efficiency | 1 bps/Hz | 0.5 bps/Hz | 1 bps/Hz | 2 bps/Hz | 4 bps/Hz | 6 bps/Hz | | Noise immunity | Very Low | Medium | High | High | Medium | Low | | Power efficiency | Low | Medium | High | High | Medium | Low | | Hardware cost | Lowest | Low | Medium | Medium | High | Highest | | Typical use | IR remote | Pager | Satellite | Wi-Fi legacy | Wi-Fi 5 | 5G NR, Wi-Fi 6 | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Digital-to-Analog Conversion Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Digital Data
bits 10110010] --> B[Source Encoder
Pulse shaping] B --> C[Modulator
ASK FSK PSK QAM] C --> D[Carrier
Sine wave fc] D --> C C --> E[Bandpass Channel
Telephone RF Fiber] E --> F[Demodulator
Symbol Detection] F --> G[Decision Device
Threshold compare] G --> H[Recovered Digital Data] ``` ## 4.2 Decision Tree — Which D/A Modulation to Choose? ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[Need to send digital data
over bandpass channel] --> Q1{Power budget
constrained?} Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Channel very
noisy?} Q1 -- No --> Q3{Need high
bit rate per Hz?} Q2 -- Yes --> BPSK[Use BPSK
1 bit per symbol] Q2 -- No --> Q4[Use FSK
robust against amp noise] Q3 -- Yes --> Q5{Channel SNR
is high?} Q3 -- No --> BPSK2[Use QPSK or BPSK] Q5 -- Yes --> QAM[Use 64 256 1024 QAM] Q5 -- No --> QPSK[Use QPSK 16 QAM] ``` ## 4.3 Constellation Diagram (Conceptual) — M-ary QAM ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Const16[16 QAM Constellation] direction LR N1[Q1: 0000] --- N2[Q2: 0001] --- N3[Q3: 0010] --- N4[Q4: 0011] end subgraph ConstBPSK[BPSK Constellation] B1[bit 0: phase 180] --- B2[bit 1: phase 0] end ``` ## 4.4 Functional Architecture of an FM Transmitter ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[Microphone
audio source] --> A2[Pre-emphasis
filter] A2 --> A3[Frequency Modulator
VCO or Armstrong] A3 --> A4[Power Amplifier
RF stage] A4 --> A5[Antenna
88 to 108 MHz] A5 --> A6[Free Space
Channel] ``` ## 4.5 Receiver-Side Demodulation Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR R1[Antenna + Tuner] --> R2[RF Amplifier] R2 --> R3[IF Stage
Intermediate Frequency] R3 --> R4[Demodulator
ASK FSK PSK QAM detector] R4 --> R5[Decision Circuit
threshold logic] R5 --> R6[Bit Stream
recovered] ``` ## 4.6 Signal Element vs Bit Relationship (M-ary Encoding) ```mermaid flowchart LR S1[S bits per second
Baud rate] --> M{Signal points M?} M -- M equals 2 --> A[Bits per symbol equals 1
BASK BFSK BPSK] M -- M equals 4 --> B[Bits per symbol equals 2
QPSK 4 QAM] M -- M equals 8 --> C[Bits per symbol equals 3
8 PSK 8 QAM] M -- M equals 16 --> D[Bits per symbol equals 4
16 PSK 16 QAM] M -- M equals 64 --> E[Bits per symbol equals 6
64 QAM] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1: Define Digital-to-Analog Conversion. Why is it required in computer networks? **Model Answer:** Digital-to-Analog Conversion is the process of converting a digital signal (a stream of bits) into an analog signal (a continuously varying sine wave) by varying one or more of its fundamental characteristics — **amplitude, frequency, or phase** — in accordance with the digital data. **Why required:** 1. Communication channels are typically **bandpass** in nature (telephone lines, optical fibers, wireless RF links) and **cannot transmit baseband digital pulses** directly without severe distortion and attenuation of low-frequency components. 2. Analog modulation allows the digital data to be **shifted into a higher frequency band** suitable for the medium. 3. It enables **multiplexing** of multiple signals using FDM (Frequency Division Multiplexing) over a single channel. 4. Antenna size for efficient radiation is feasible only at high carrier frequencies ($f_c$). > [!NOTE] > **Examiner expects:** You must mention the *bandpass limitation* explicitly. A 3-mark answer missing this reason loses 1 mark. --- ### Question A2: Differentiate between Bit Rate and Baud Rate. How are they related? **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Bit Rate ($N_b$) | Baud Rate ($S$) | |--------|------------------|-----------------| | Definition | Number of **bits** transmitted per second | Number of **signal elements (symbols)** transmitted per second | | Unit | bits per second (bps) | symbols per second (baud) | | Meaning | Information rate | Signaling rate | | Depends on | Number of bits packed per symbol | Channel bandwidth | **Relationship:** $$N_b = S \times \log_2 M$$ where $M$ = number of distinct signal points (signal levels or phases) in the constellation. - For **BASK, BFSK, BPSK**: $M = 2$ → $\log_2 M = 1$ → $N_b = S$ - For **QPSK / 4-QAM**: $M = 4$ → $N_b = 2S$ (baud is half the bit rate) - For **16-QAM**: $M = 16$ → $N_b = 4S$ --- ## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern) > Each question below contains two 7-mark sub-parts — exactly matching the KTU End-Semester Evaluation (ESE) pattern for a 14-mark main question. --- ### ❑ QUESTION A (14 Marks) #### Part (a) [7 Marks]: Explain the four Digital-to-Analog Conversion techniques with neat signal diagrams and bandwidth formulas. Compare their bandwidth efficiency and noise immunity. **Model Solution:** **Introduction:** D/A conversion modulates a high-frequency carrier with digital data. The four principal techniques vary one of the carrier's three properties (amplitude, frequency, or phase) or a combination of both amplitude and phase. **1) Amplitude Shift Keying (ASK):** In BASK (binary ASK, also called OOK), the carrier is transmitted for bit '1' and suppressed for bit '0'. The amplitude varies; frequency and phase remain constant. $$s(t) = \begin{cases} A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) & \text{bit 1} \\ 0 & \text{bit 0} \end{cases}$$ Bandwidth: $B_{ASK} = S$ (baud rate) **2) Frequency Shift Keying (FSK):** Two carriers at frequencies $f_1$ and $f_2$ are used. Bit 1 → $f_1$, Bit 0 → $f_2$. $$s(t) = \begin{cases} A_c \cos(2\pi f_1 t) & \text{bit 1} \\ A_c \cos(2\pi f_2 t) & \text{bit 0} \end{cases}$$ Bandwidth: $B_{FSK} = \vert f_1 - f_2 \vert + 2S$ **3) Phase Shift Keying (PSK):** The phase of the carrier is varied. In BPSK, phase 0° represents bit 1 and 180° represents bit 0. QPSK uses four phases (45°, 135°, 225°, 315°) carrying 2 bits per symbol. $$s(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t + \pi b(t))$$ where $b(t) = 0$ for bit 1 and $b(t) = 1$ for bit 0. Bandwidth: $B_{BPSK} = S$, $B_{QPSK} = S = N_b/2$ **4) Quadrature Amplitude Modulation (QAM):** Combines ASK and PSK — both amplitude and phase are varied, producing a 2-D constellation of $M = 2^n$ points. For 16-QAM, $n = 4$ bits per symbol. Higher spectral efficiency than PSK alone. Bandwidth: $B_{QAM} = N_b / \log_2 M = S$ **Comparison Table:** | Technique | Bit per Symbol | Bandwidth | Noise Immunity | Power Efficiency | |-----------|---------------|-----------|----------------|------------------| | ASK | 1 | $S$ | Poor | Poor | | FSK | 1 | $\vert f_1 - f_2 \vert + 2S$ | Moderate | Moderate | | BPSK | 1 | $S$ | Excellent | Excellent | | QPSK | 2 | $S$ | Excellent | Excellent | | 16-QAM | 4 | $S$ | Moderate | Moderate | | 64-QAM | 6 | $S$ | Poor | Poor | **Conclusion:** BPSK is preferred for power-limited, noisy channels (satellites). QAM variants are preferred when bandwidth is the bottleneck (Wi-Fi, cable). --- #### Part (b) [7 Marks]: A 4 kHz bandwidth channel is to transmit data at 16 kbps using QPSK. Find (i) the baud rate, (ii) the minimum number of signal points, and (iii) verify whether the channel is sufficient. **Model Solution:** **Given:** - Bandwidth $B = 4 \text{ kHz}$ - Bit rate $N_b = 16 \text{ kbps}$ - Modulation: QPSK **(i) Baud Rate:** For QPSK, the minimum bandwidth equals the baud rate: $$B = S$$ $$S = 4 \text{ kbaud} = 4000 \text{ symbols/sec}$$ **(ii) Minimum Number of Signal Points (M):** QPSK has $M = 4$ signal points, each carrying $\log_2 4 = 2$ bits per symbol. Verification using the master relation: $$N_b = S \times \log_2 M$$ $$16{,}000 = 4{,}000 \times \log_2 M$$ $$\log_2 M = 4$$ $$M = 2^4 = 16$$ > **Note:** Strict QPSK uses M=4, giving $N_b = 4{,}000 \times 2 = 8 \text{ kbps}$, which is **less** than the 16 kbps requirement. So we need a higher M-ary scheme — specifically **16-QAM** with $M = 16$ to hit 16 kbps in a 4 kHz channel. **(iii) Channel Sufficiency Check:** With 16-QAM ($M = 16$, $\log_2 M = 4$): - Bits per symbol = 4 - Required baud rate = $16{,}000 / 4 = 4{,}000$ symbols/sec - Bandwidth needed = 4,000 Hz = 4 kHz ✓ **Conclusion:** The 4 kHz channel is **sufficient** if we upgrade from QPSK to 16-QAM. Otherwise, with plain QPSK the maximum throughput is 8 kbps, which is **insufficient** for 16 kbps. --- ### ❑ QUESTION B (14 Marks) #### Part (a) [7 Marks]: Explain Amplitude Modulation and Frequency Modulation in detail. Derive the expression for AM signal and state Carson's rule for FM bandwidth with an example. **Model Solution:** **Amplitude Modulation (AM):** The amplitude of the carrier is varied in proportion to the instantaneous amplitude of the modulating signal. Mathematically: Let carrier: $c(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ Let modulating signal: $m(t)$ Then the AM signal is: $$s_{AM}(t) = [A_c + m(t)] \cos(2\pi f_c t)$$ Expanding: $$s_{AM}(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) + m(t) \cos(2\pi f_c t)$$ The spectrum of the AM signal consists of: - The original carrier frequency $f_c$ - An upper sideband (USB) from $f_c$ to $f_c + f_m$ - A lower sideband (LSB) from $f_c - f_m$ to $f_c$ The total bandwidth: $$B_{AM} = 2 \times f_m \text{ (twice the message bandwidth)}$$ **Frequency Modulation (FM):** The instantaneous frequency of the carrier varies in proportion to the modulating signal. The FM signal is: $$s_{FM}(t) = A_c \cos\!\left(2\pi f_c t + 2\pi k_f \int_{-\infty}^{t} m(\tau)\, d\tau\right)$$ where $k_f$ is the frequency sensitivity (Hz/volt). **Carson's Rule for FM Bandwidth:** The total transmission bandwidth of an FM signal is given by: $$B_{FM} = 2(\Delta f + f_m) = 2(\beta + 1) f_m$$ where: - $\Delta f$ = peak frequency deviation = $k_f \cdot \vert m(t) \vert_{max}$ - $f_m$ = highest frequency in the modulating signal - $\beta = \Delta f / f_m$ = modulation index **Example:** For $\Delta f = 75$ kHz and $f_m = 15$ kHz: $$B_{FM} = 2(75 + 15) = 180 \text{ kHz}$$ This matches the 200 kHz channel allocation in commercial FM broadcasting (with guard band). --- #### Part (b) [7 Marks]: With neat constellation diagrams, compare BPSK, QPSK, and 16-QAM. Compute the baud rate required to send 64 kbps over a 16 kHz channel using each scheme. **Model Solution:** **Constellation Diagrams (Conceptual):** - **BPSK:** 2 points on the I-axis at (1,0) and (-1,0). Bit 1 → 0°, Bit 0 → 180°. Distance between points = 2 (maximum). - **QPSK:** 4 points at corners of a square: (1,1), (-1,1), (-1,-1), (1,-1) — phases 45°, 135°, 225°, 315°. Each point = 2 bits. Distance between points = $\sqrt{2}$. - **16-QAM:** 16 points in a 4×4 grid. Each point = 4 bits. Distance = $2/\sqrt{10}$ between adjacent points. **Comparison Table:** | Parameter | BPSK | QPSK | 16-QAM | |-----------|------|------|--------| | Signal points M | 2 | 4 | 16 | | Bits per symbol | 1 | 2 | 4 | | Symbol distance (normalized) | 2.0 | $\sqrt{2}$ | 0.632 | | Noise immunity | Highest | High | Medium | | Bandwidth (same bit rate) | Maximum | Half of BPSK | Quarter of BPSK | | Best application | Satellite, deep-space | Wireless legacy, GPS | Wi-Fi, LTE | **Baud Rate Calculation for $N_b = 64$ kbps, $B = 16$ kHz:** The minimum theoretical bandwidth equals the baud rate: $B = S$, so $S = 16$ kbaud (maximum allowed). Using $N_b = S \cdot \log_2 M$: | Scheme | $\log_2 M$ | Required Baud Rate $S$ | Achievable Bit Rate at $S = 16$ kbaud | |--------|------------|------------------------|--------------------------------------| | BPSK | 1 | $S = 64{,}000$ baud (channel **insufficient**) | 16 kbps only | | QPSK | 2 | $S = 32{,}000$ baud (channel **insufficient**) | 32 kbps only | | 16-QAM | 4 | $S = 16{,}000$ baud ✓ | 64 kbps ✓ | | 64-QAM | 6 | $S = 10{,}667$ baud ✓ | 96 kbps (more than needed) | **Conclusion:** The 16 kHz channel can support 64 kbps only if we use **16-QAM or higher**. BPSK and QPSK would require wider bandwidth. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Common Mark Losers):** > 1. **Confusing bit rate and baud rate** — always state the unit (bps vs baud) explicitly. > 2. **Writing $B = S$** without justifying *why* (Nyquist minimum) — examiners expect the reference. > 3. **Forgetting to compute $M$** when the problem says "M-ary PSK" — $M$ is the number of constellation points, not the bit count. > 4. **Using ASK in noise-prone environments** — most wrong answers choose ASK simply because it is "simple"; lose marks if justification missing. > 5. **Confusing FM with PM** — they are mathematically related but physically distinct; always state $\Delta f$ in FM, $\Delta \phi$ in PM. > 6. **Not drawing the constellation / waveform diagrams** — a 7-mark question without a diagram is auto-cut 2 marks in ESE valuation. > 7. **Ignoring the duty cycle $d$** in ASK bandwidth formula — full-marks require the general formula $B = (1+d)S$. --- ## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > Use this section as a one-page "cheat sheet" on the day of the exam. - [ ] **Analog Transmission** = conversion to analog signal for bandpass channels. - [ ] **Two branches:** D/A (digital source) and A/A (analog source). - [ ] **Carrier** = high-frequency sine wave $A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t + \phi_c)$. - [ ] **Modifiable property** of carrier = amplitude, frequency, OR phase. - [ ] **D/A techniques:** ASK, FSK, PSK, QAM. - [ ] **A/A techniques:** AM, FM, PM. - [ ] **BASK** = OOK (On-Off Keying); bit 1 = carrier present, bit 0 = no carrier. - [ ] **BFSK** uses TWO frequencies; bit 1 → $f_1$, bit 0 → $f_2$. - [ ] **BPSK** uses 2 phases (0° and 180°); most noise-immune of binary schemes. - [ ] **QPSK** uses 4 phases; carries 2 bits/symbol; halves bandwidth vs BPSK. - [ ] **QAM** combines ASK + PSK; highest spectral efficiency. - [ ] **Master formula:** $N_b = S \cdot \log_2 M$. - [ ] **Minimum bandwidth:** $B = S$ (theoretical limit). - [ ] **AM bandwidth** = $2 f_m$ (two sidebands). - [ ] **FM bandwidth (Carson's Rule):** $B = 2(\Delta f + f_m)$. - [ ] **PM is to FM** what differentiation is to integration. - [ ] **Trade-off:** Higher M → more bits/Hz, but lower noise tolerance. - [ ] **Real-world examples:** BPSK in GPS, QPSK in Wi-Fi 802.11b, 64-QAM in Wi-Fi 5, AM in radio broadcast (530–1700 kHz), FM in radio broadcast (88–108 MHz). - [ ] **Constellation diagram** = plot of possible symbols on I-Q axes; show 2-D mapping of amplitude + phase. - [ ] **Noise immunity ranking (best → worst):** BPSK > QPSK > FSK > ASK ≈ AM > QAM (high M). - [ ] **Bandwidth efficiency ranking (best → worst):** 64-QAM > 16-QAM > QPSK > BPSK = ASK > FSK. - [ ] **Shannon limit** is the absolute ceiling; practical schemes never reach it. - [ ] **Why modulate?** (1) shift to bandpass, (2) smaller antennas, (3) FDM multiplexing, (4) noise immunity improvement. --- **End of Module 4 — Analog Transmission Notes** *Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech syllabus | PEAMT632*

topics.title: Bandwidth Utilization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAMT632 - BASICS OF COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Bandwidth Utilization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 Module 4 — Bandwidth Utilization

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bandwidth Utilization** is the systematic, engineered use of available link bandwidth so that the **maximum possible volume of information** can be transmitted reliably across a single communication channel. It is achieved through two principal techniques: **Multiplexing** and **Spreading**.

### 1.1 Formal Definitions (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

- **Multiplexing:** The set of techniques that allow multiple communication signals to be combined and transmitted simultaneously over a single physical medium (or logical channel) without mutual interference.
- **Spreading (Spread Spectrum):** A bandwidth utilization technique in which a narrowband signal is deliberately spread over a much wider band of frequencies using a special code, primarily to provide robustness against interference, jamming, and unauthorized interception.
- **Multiplexer (MUX):** A hardware device that combines several input signals into one output signal.
- **Demultiplexer (DEMUX):** A hardware device that separates a multiplexed signal back into its original component signals at the receiving end.

### 1.2 Classification of Bandwidth Utilization Techniques

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus classifies Bandwidth Utilization into the following hierarchy. Memorize this taxonomy — it is a high-frequency KTU short-answer point.

| # | Technique | Sub-Type |
|---|-----------|----------|
| 1 | **Multiplexing** | FDM, TDM (Synchronous & Statistical), WDM |
| 2 | **Spreading** | FHSS, DSSS |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Multi-Lane Super-Highway"

Imagine a **single-lane road** carrying cars at 30 km/h. To increase throughput, the highway engineer has two choices:

1. **Multiplexing** (Divide the Resource): Widen the road into **4 lanes** (FDM), or convert it into a **time-shared shuttle** where each lane is allotted fixed micro-second time slots to different vehicles (TDM). Each lane/slot carries a different driver — total throughput is multiplied.
2. **Spreading** (Defensive Dispersion): Take each individual car's signal and **broadcast it on all four lanes simultaneously with a unique code** (DSSS) — even if 2 lanes are jammed or noisy, the original message can still be recovered.

This analogy maps directly to:
- **FDM** → Lanes are divided by **frequency band**
- **TDM** → Lanes are divided by **time slot**
- **WDM** → Lanes are divided by **color of light** (in fiber optics)
- **FHSS** → Lanes keep **hopping to new lanes** in a pseudo-random pattern
- **DSSS** → Signal is **spread across all lanes** with a code

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick-Recall Mantra:** *"FDM = Frequency, TDM = Time, WDM = Wavelength (Light), FHSS = Hopping Frequency, DSSS = Direct Code."*

### 1.4 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics (Bolded)

- **Bandwidth (B)** — Measured in **Hz (Hertz)**. KTU convention: use **kHz, MHz, GHz**.
- **Data Rate (R)** — Measured in **bps (bits per second)**. KTU convention: use **kbps, Mbps, Gbps**.
- **Wavelength (λ)** — In WDM, measured in **nanometers (nm)**. Standard optical windows: **850 nm, 1310 nm, 1550 nm**.
- **Chip Duration (Tc)** — In DSSS, each "chip" of the spreading code.
- **Guard Band (Bg)** — Small unused frequency band inserted between adjacent FDM channels to prevent overlap. Standard values: **200 Hz to 500 Hz** in voice-grade FDM.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Frequency Division Multiplexing (FDM)

### What is FDM?
**FDM** is an **analog** multiplexing technique in which the available bandwidth of a transmission link is divided into **non-overlapping frequency bands**, and each band carries a separate signal simultaneously.

### Step-by-Step Operational Logic
1. Each input signal modulates a **different carrier frequency** $f_1, f_2, f_3, \dots, f_n$.
2. The modulated signals are summed in a **linear combiner** to form a composite baseband signal.
3. A **guard band** is inserted between adjacent channels to prevent inter-channel interference.
4. The composite signal is transmitted over a single high-bandwidth link.
5. At the receiver, **band-pass filters** extract the original individual signals before demodulation.

### Why Use FDM?
- Used in **analog telephone systems** (long-distance carrier systems like AT&T's L-carrier hierarchy).
- Used in **radio & TV broadcasting** (each station gets a 200 kHz / 6 MHz slot).
- Used in **DSL broadband** (upstream vs. downstream frequency split).
- **Limitation:** Requires precise analog filters; susceptible to inter-modulation noise.

## 2.2 Time Division Multiplexing (TDM)

### 2.2.1 Synchronous TDM
In **Synchronous TDM**, the multiplexer allocates **one fixed time slot** to each input line, **regardless of whether the line has data to send**. The slots are pre-assigned and rotate in a fixed cyclic order.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Critical Definition:** In Synchronous TDM, the **frame rate = input source data rate**, and the **frame duration = 1 / (source data rate)**. If any input is idle, its slot goes **wasted** (filled with zeros or dummy bits).

### 2.2.2 Statistical (Asynchronous) TDM
**Statistical TDM** dynamically allocates time slots **only to active sources**, eliminating idle slots. The multiplexer must append a small **address/identifier** field to each slot to tell the demultiplexer which source the data came from. This is also called **Intelligent TDM**.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Compare & Contrast Trap:** Examiners love asking "What is the **drawback of Synchronous TDM** that Statistical TDM overcomes?" — Answer: **Wasted bandwidth due to idle slots** for inactive sources.

### 2.2.3 TDM Frame Structure
A TDM frame is **one complete cycle of slots**, one slot per input source.

## 2.3 Wavelength Division Multiplexing (WDM)

**WDM** is the optical equivalent of FDM. Multiple light beams of **different wavelengths (λ)** are transmitted simultaneously through a single optical fiber.

- **DWDM (Dense WDM):** Uses closely spaced wavelengths (channel spacing < 1 nm). Supports **80–160 channels** in a single fiber.
- **CWDM (Coarse WDM):** Uses wider spacing (~20 nm). Supports up to **18 channels** over shorter distances.
- Standard C-band window: **1530 nm – 1565 nm** for DWDM.

## 2.4 Spread Spectrum Techniques

### 2.4.1 Frequency Hopping Spread Spectrum (FHSS)
The signal rapidly **hops** between different carrier frequencies in a **pseudo-random pattern** known to both transmitter and receiver. The hopping rate may be:
- **Slow FHSS:** Multiple bits transmitted per hop.
- **Fast FHSS:** Multiple hops per bit.

### 2.4.2 Direct Sequence Spread Spectrum (DSSS)
The original data bit is **XORed** with a high-rate **pseudo-random chip sequence** (e.g., the 11-chip **Barker code** in IEEE 802.11b Wi-Fi). The result is a wideband signal of much greater bandwidth than the original.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Classic Fact:** In DSSS, the bit is replaced by N chips. The original bit period T becomes a chip period $T_c = T/N$. Bandwidth is multiplied by a factor called the **Processing Gain**.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Technique | Real-World Application |
|-----------|------------------------|
| FDM | FM radio broadcasting, analog trunk telephone lines, Cable TV |
| Synchronous TDM | T1/E1 digital trunk lines (1.544 Mbps / 2.048 Mbps) |
| Statistical TDM | Modern digital switches, ATM networks |
| WDM/DWDM | Undersea optical cables, Internet backbone |
| FHSS | Bluetooth (79 hops/sec in 2.4 GHz ISM band), military radios |
| DSSS | Wi-Fi 802.11b, GPS, CDMA cellular (IS-95, 3G) |

## 2.6 📊 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **all critical formulas** required for Module 4 numerical and analytical questions. **Memorize every row** — these are the most-tested equations in KTU 2024 Scheme ESE.

| # | Formula (LaTeX) | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|----------------|-------------|----------------|
| 1 | $N = \left\lfloor \dfrac{B_{link}}{B_{channel} + B_{guard}} \right\rfloor$ | Maximum number of FDM channels | $N$ = channels, $B$ = Hz |
| 2 | $B_{total} = N \cdot B_{channel} + (N-1) \cdot B_{guard}$ | Total FDM link bandwidth | Hz |
| 3 | $T_{frame} = N \cdot T_{slot}$ | TDM frame duration | $N$ = number of sources |
| 4 | $f_{frame} = \dfrac{1}{T_{frame}} = \dfrac{R_{src}}{N}$ | TDM frame frequency | Hz |
| 5 | $R_{mux} = N \cdot R_{src}$ | Multiplexer output bit rate | bps |
| 6 | $T_{slot} = \dfrac{T_{frame}}{N}$ | Duration of one TDM slot | seconds |
| 7 | $N_{chips} = \dfrac{T_{bit}}{T_{chip}}$ | Number of chips per data bit | Dimensionless |
| 8 | $R_{chip} = R_{bit} \cdot N_{chips}$ | DSSS chip rate | chips/sec |
| 9 | $G_p = \dfrac{BW_{RF}}{BW_{info}} = \dfrac{R_{chip}}{R_{bit}}$ | DSSS Processing Gain | Dimensionless (dB) |
| 10 | $B_{DSSS} = B_{original} \cdot N_{chips}$ | DSSS spread bandwidth | Hz |

> [!TIP]
> **Formula Sheet Trick (KTU Examiner's Favorite):** In DSSS, **Processing Gain in dB** is calculated as $G_p (\text{dB}) = 10 \log_{10}(G_p)$. For the 11-chip Barker code of 802.11b, $G_p = 10 \log_{10}(11) \approx 10.41$ dB.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Mandate:** Every numerical answer must show **complete intermediate steps**. Marks are awarded for method, not just the final number. The derivations below are calibrated to the **14-mark KTU ESE valuation pattern** (3 marks for setup, 7 for logical progress, 4 for final answer).

---

## 3.1 Derivative Problem 1: Synchronous TDM Frame Parameters

> **Problem (Typical 7-Mark KTU Question):** A Synchronous TDM multiplexer combines **4 input sources**, each operating at **2 kbps**. Determine: (a) Bit duration of each source, (b) TDM frame duration, (c) Output bit rate of multiplexer, (d) TDM frame frequency.

### Given
- Number of sources: $N = 4$
- Source data rate: $R_{src} = 2$ kbps $= 2000$ bps

### (a) Bit Duration of Each Source

The bit duration is the time taken to transmit one bit on the source line.

$$
T_{bit} = \frac{1}{R_{src}}
$$

Substituting the value:

$$
T_{bit} = \frac{1}{2000} \text{ sec} = 0.5 \text{ ms} = 5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ sec}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Since 1 kbps = 1000 bps, and 1/1000 = 1 ms, the answer is naturally in milliseconds.

### (b) TDM Frame Duration

In Synchronous TDM, the frame contains **one slot per source**, and each slot carries exactly one bit.

$$
T_{frame} = N \times T_{slot} = N \times T_{bit}
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
T_{frame} = 4 \times 0.5 \text{ ms} = 2.0 \text{ ms}
$$

> **Logical Insight:** The TDM frame duration is **N times the source bit duration** because we are interleaving N bits (one from each source) into a single frame.

### (c) Output Bit Rate of Multiplexer

Each source contributes 1 bit per frame. The multiplexer outputs N bits per frame, so the output bit rate is:

$$
R_{mux} = N \times R_{src}
$$

Substituting:

$$
R_{mux} = 4 \times 2 \text{ kbps} = 8 \text{ kbps} = 8000 \text{ bps}
$$

### (d) TDM Frame Frequency

The frame frequency is the reciprocal of the frame duration:

$$
f_{frame} = \frac{1}{T_{frame}}
$$

Substituting:

$$
f_{frame} = \frac{1}{2.0 \times 10^{-3}} = 500 \text{ frames/second}
$$

### ✅ Final Answer Summary

| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| $T_{bit}$ | **0.5 ms** |
| $T_{frame}$ | **2.0 ms** |
| $R_{mux}$ | **8 kbps** |
| $f_{frame}$ | **500 Hz** |

---

## 3.2 Derivative Problem 2: DSSS Spreading Calculation

> **Problem (Typical 7-Mark KTU Question):** A DSSS system uses an **11-chip Barker code** to spread a data signal. The original data rate is **1 Mbps**. Calculate: (a) Chip rate, (b) Spread bandwidth, (c) Processing Gain in dB.

### Given
- Spreading code length: $N_{chips} = 11$
- Original data rate: $R_{bit} = 1$ Mbps

### (a) Chip Rate

Each data bit is replaced by 11 chips. The chip rate is N times the bit rate:

$$
R_{chip} = R_{bit} \times N_{chips}
$$

Substituting:

$$
R_{chip} = 1 \times 10^{6} \times 11 = 11 \times 10^{6} \text{ chips/second} = 11 \text{ Mcps}
$$

> **Logic:** 1 Mcps (Mega-chip-per-second) is the standard unit in KTU questions.

### (b) Spread Bandwidth

By Nyquist's criterion, the minimum bandwidth required to transmit a signal at rate $R$ is $B = R/2$. The spread bandwidth is the bandwidth of the chip-rate signal:

$$
B_{DSSS} = \frac{R_{chip}}{2}
$$

Substituting:

$$
B_{DSSS} = \frac{11 \times 10^{6}}{2} = 5.5 \text{ MHz}
$$

> **Engineering Insight:** The original signal needed only 0.5 MHz bandwidth. DSSS has spread it 11 times wider (5.5 MHz), providing 10.41 dB of processing gain against interference.

### (c) Processing Gain in dB

Processing Gain is the ratio of chip rate to data rate:

$$
G_p = \frac{R_{chip}}{R_{bit}} = N_{chips} = 11
$$

Converting to decibels:

$$
G_p (\text{dB}) = 10 \log_{10}(11)
$$

Computing numerically:

$$
G_p (\text{dB}) = 10 \times 1.04139 = 10.41 \text{ dB}
$$

### ✅ Final Answer Summary

| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| $R_{chip}$ | **11 Mcps** |
| $B_{DSSS}$ | **5.5 MHz** |
| $G_p$ | **10.41 dB** |

---

## 3.3 Derivative Problem 3: FDM Channel Capacity

> **Problem (Typical 7-Mark KTU Question):** A 100 kHz link is to be used for FDM. Each voice channel occupies 4 kHz, and a 500 Hz guard band is required between channels. How many channels can be multiplexed?

### Given
- Total link bandwidth: $B_{link} = 100$ kHz $= 100{,}000$ Hz
- Voice channel bandwidth: $B_{channel} = 4$ kHz $= 4000$ Hz
- Guard band: $B_{guard} = 500$ Hz

### Channel Count Calculation

Each channel effectively consumes $B_{channel} + B_{guard}$ of bandwidth, **except the last channel** which doesn't need a trailing guard.

$$
B_{required} \text{ per channel} = B_{channel} + B_{guard} = 4000 + 500 = 4500 \text{ Hz}
$$

Total bandwidth used by N channels (with N-1 guard bands between them):

$$
B_{total} = N \cdot B_{channel} + (N-1) \cdot B_{guard}
$$

Equating to link bandwidth:

$$
N \cdot 4000 + (N-1) \cdot 500 = 100{,}000
$$

Expanding:

$$
4000N + 500N - 500 = 100{,}000
$$

$$
4500N = 100{,}500
$$

$$
N = \frac{100{,}500}{4500} = 22.33
$$

Since we cannot have a fractional channel, we take the floor:

$$
N = 22 \text{ channels}
$$

### ✅ Final Answer: **22 channels** can be multiplexed.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU 2-Mark Comparison Question)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This comparison matrix is a **guaranteed KTU question**. Memorize the structure.

| Parameter | FDM | TDM | WDM | DSSS | FHSS |
|-----------|-----|-----|-----|------|------|
| **Domain** | Frequency | Time | Wavelength (Light) | Code (XOR) | Frequency (Hop) |
| **Signal Type** | Analog | Digital | Optical | Digital | Digital |
| **Dividing Resource** | Bandwidth | Time | λ (color) | Code chip | Hop sequence |
| **Isolation Method** | BPF + Guard band | Synchronization | Optical prisms | Pseudo-random code | Hop pattern |
| **Application** | Radio, Cable TV | T1/E1 trunks | Fiber backbone | Wi-Fi, CDMA | Bluetooth, Military |
| **Limitation** | Crosstalk, IMD | Slot wastage | Cost of prisms | Code synchronization | Hop synchronization |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The following **Mermaid block diagrams** illustrate the functional architecture of each bandwidth utilization technique. They are designed to be directly reproducible in your KTU answer scripts and visually intuitive for a 14-mark KTU board question.

---

## 4.1 FDM Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    srcA[INPUT SOURCE A] --> modA[MODULATOR f1]
    srcB[INPUT SOURCE B] --> modB[MODULATOR f2]
    srcC[INPUT SOURCE C] --> modC[MODULATOR f3]
    srcD[INPUT SOURCE D] --> modD[MODULATOR f4]
    modA --> comb[LINEAR COMBINER]
    modB --> comb
    modC --> comb
    modD --> comb
    comb --> link[COMPOSITE CHANNEL f1 to f4]
    link --> recA[RECEIVER FILTER f1]
    link --> recB[RECEIVER FILTER f2]
    link --> recC[RECEIVER FILTER f3]
    link --> recD[RECEIVER FILTER f4]
    recA --> outA[OUTPUT A]
    recB --> outB[OUTPUT B]
    recC --> outC[OUTPUT C]
    recD --> outD[OUTPUT D]
```

> **Reading Guide:** Each input source modulates a **unique carrier frequency** $f_1, f_2, f_3, f_4$. The linear combiner sums them, and the **composite channel** carries all four frequencies simultaneously. At the receiver, **band-pass filters** extract the original signals.

---

## 4.2 Synchronous TDM Interleaving Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TB
    in1[SOURCE 1: a1 a2 a3 a4] --> mux[SYNCHRONOUS TDM MULTIPLEXER]
    in2[SOURCE 2: b1 b2 b3 b4] --> mux
    in3[SOURCE 3: c1 c2 c3 c4] --> mux
    in4[SOURCE 4: d1 d2 d3 d4] --> mux
    mux --> out1[FRAME 1: a1 b1 c1 d1]
    mux --> out2[FRAME 2: a2 b2 c2 d2]
    mux --> out3[FRAME 3: a3 b3 c3 d3]
    mux --> out4[FRAME 4: a4 b4 c4 d4]
```

> **Reading Guide:** The multiplexer takes **one bit from each source** in strict round-robin order, assembling each TDM frame. Notice that **inactive source slots are still transmitted** (as zeros), which is the key drawback of Synchronous TDM.

---

## 4.3 WDM Optical Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    l1[LASER lambda 1 1550 NM] --> prism[OPTICAL COMBINER PRISM]
    l2[LASER lambda 2 1552 NM] --> prism
    l3[LASER lambda 3 1554 NM] --> prism
    l4[LASER lambda 4 1556 NM] --> prism
    prism --> fiber[SINGLE MODE OPTICAL FIBER]
    fiber --> prism2[OPTICAL DECOMBINER PRISM]
    prism2 --> d1[PHOTODETECTOR 1]
    prism2 --> d2[PHOTODETECTOR 2]
    prism2 --> d3[PHOTODETECTOR 3]
    prism2 --> d4[PHOTODETECTOR 4]
```

> **Reading Guide:** Multiple **laser sources** at different wavelengths are merged by a **diffraction prism** into a single fiber. At the receiving end, another prism **splits the wavelengths** to individual photodetectors. This is the foundation of **DWDM** used in undersea cables.

---

## 4.4 DSSS Spreading Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    data[DATA BIT d 1 BIT] --> xor[XOR GATE]
    chip[CHIP SEQUENCE c1 c2 c3 c4 c5 c6 c7 c8] --> xor
    xor --> spread[SPREAD SIGNAL 8 CHIPS]
    spread --> chan[CHANNEL BW 5.5 MHZ]
    chan --> xor2[XOR GATE RECEIVER]
    xor2 --> chip2[SAME CHIP SEQUENCE c1 to c8]
    xor2 --> out[RECOVERED DATA BIT d]
```

> **Reading Guide:** One data bit is XORed with N chips, producing N transmitted chips. The receiver repeats the XOR with the **same chip sequence** to recover the original bit. The spreading factor N determines **processing gain**.

---

## 4.5 FHSS Frequency Hopping Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TB
    data[DATA SIGNAL] --> mod[CARRIER MODULATOR]
    pn[PSEUDO RANDOM GENERATOR] --> freq[FREQUENCY SYNTHESIZER]
    freq --> mod
    mod --> ant[ANTENNA]
    ant --> chan[RADIO CHANNEL F1 F2 F3 F4 F5]
    chan --> ant2[RECEIVER ANTENNA]
    ant2 --> freq2[RECEIVER FREQUENCY SYNTHESIZER]
    pn2[SAME PSEUDO RANDOM GENERATOR SYNCHRONIZED] --> freq2
    freq2 --> demod[DEMODULATOR]
    demod --> out[RECOVERED DATA]
```

> **Reading Guide:** The **pseudo-random generator** drives a frequency synthesizer that **hops the carrier** across multiple sub-bands. Both transmitter and receiver must be **synchronized to the same hop sequence**. This makes FHSS highly resistant to narrowband jamming.

---

## 4.6 Comparative Block Architecture (Master View)

```mermaid
graph TB
    bd[BANDWIDTH UTILIZATION TECHNIQUES] --> mux[MULTIPLEXING]
    bd --> spread[SPREADING]
    mux --> fdm[FDM FREQUENCY DIVISION]
    mux --> tdm[TDM TIME DIVISION]
    mux --> wdm[WDM WAVELENGTH DIVISION]
    tdm --> syn[SYNCHRONOUS TDM]
    tdm --> stat[STATISTICAL TDM]
    spread --> fhss[FHSS FREQUENCY HOPPING]
    spread --> dsss[DSSS DIRECT SEQUENCE]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Direct Short Answer)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`
**Define Multiplexing. List any three advantages of multiplexing in computer networks.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Multiplexing** is a technique used in data communication to combine **multiple signals** (analog or digital) and transmit them simultaneously over a **single transmission medium**, thereby maximizing bandwidth utilization.

**Three Advantages:**
1. **Efficient Bandwidth Utilization:** Multiple independent signals share one link, eliminating the need for separate physical channels.
2. **Cost Reduction:** Reduces the number of physical wires, transmitters, and receivers required — lowering both CAPEX and OPEX.
3. **Scalability:** New sources can be added to the multiplexer without laying new infrastructure, as long as capacity permits.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`
**Differentiate between Synchronous TDM and Statistical TDM.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Differentiate / Compare** type questions in KTU 2024 typically carry **3 marks** and require **at least 3 distinguishing points**.

| Parameter | Synchronous TDM | Statistical TDM |
|-----------|-----------------|-----------------|
| **Slot Allocation** | Pre-assigned fixed slots to each source | Dynamic, only to active sources |
| **Idle Slot Handling** | Idle slots still transmitted (wasted BW) | Idle slots skipped — no wastage |
| **Identifier Required** | No (position implies source) | Yes (address field added per slot) |
| **Efficiency** | Lower | Higher |
| **Complexity** | Simpler multiplexer | More complex, needs address handling |
| **Use Case** | T1/E1 trunk lines | Modern digital switches, ATM |

---

## 📚 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per the **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern**, you must answer **ONE full question (with sub-parts a and b)** out of the two choices provided. Each sub-part carries **7 marks**.

---

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the working of **Frequency Division Multiplexing (FDM)** with a neat block diagram. Discuss its advantages and limitations. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain **Wavelength Division Multiplexing (WDM)** in optical fiber communication. Differentiate between **CWDM** and **DWDM**. **(7 Marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q.A(a): FDM

**1. Definition (2 marks):**
> FDM is an **analog multiplexing technique** in which the available transmission bandwidth is divided into **multiple non-overlapping frequency sub-bands**. Each sub-band is assigned to a separate signal source, and all sub-bands are transmitted simultaneously over a single link.

**2. Working Principle (3 marks):**
- Each input signal modulates a **different carrier frequency** $f_1, f_2, \ldots, f_n$.
- Modulated signals pass through **band-pass filters** to confine each to its assigned band.
- A **linear combiner / summing amplifier** merges them into one composite signal.
- A **guard band** of typically 200–500 Hz is placed between adjacent channels to prevent overlap.
- At the receiver, **band-pass filters** separate each channel, and demodulators recover the original signals.

**3. Block Diagram (1 mark):**
> Refer to **Section 4.1 FDM Functional Architecture** above.

**4. Advantages (1 mark):**
- Allows simultaneous transmission of multiple independent analog signals.
- Simple to implement for voice and radio broadcasting.
- No synchronization required between sources (unlike TDM).

**5. Limitations (1 mark):**
- Susceptible to **inter-modulation distortion** and **crosstalk**.
- Inefficient if some channels are idle (bandwidth cannot be reallocated).
- Requires precise analog filters — costly and difficult to tune.

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q.A(b): WDM

**1. Definition (2 marks):**
> **WDM** is the optical-domain analog of FDM. It multiplexes multiple **light beams of different wavelengths (λ)** into a single optical fiber using a diffraction prism or arrayed waveguide grating (AWG).

**2. Working Principle (2 marks):**
- Multiple **laser sources** emit at different wavelengths (e.g., 1550 nm, 1552 nm, 1554 nm, ...).
- An **optical multiplexer (prism/grating)** combines the wavelengths.
- The composite light travels through a **single-mode optical fiber**.
- At the receiver, an **optical demultiplexer** splits the wavelengths to individual **photodetectors**.

**3. CWDM vs DWDM (3 marks):**

| Parameter | CWDM (Coarse WDM) | DWDM (Dense WDM) |
|-----------|-------------------|------------------|
| Channel Spacing | ~20 nm | < 1 nm (typically 0.8 nm or 0.4 nm) |
| Number of Channels | Up to 18 | 80, 160, or more |
| Laser Source | Uncooled (low cost) | Cooled, temperature-stabilized (high cost) |
| Reach | Short haul (Metro, LAN) | Long haul (Submarine, Continental) |
| Cost | Low | High |

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain **Direct Sequence Spread Spectrum (DSSS)**. Define **chip**, **chip rate**, and **processing gain**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A TDM system combines **8 voice channels**, each sampled at **8 kHz** with **8 bits per sample**. Calculate the **multiplexer output bit rate**, **frame duration**, and **bit duration** of the output. **(7 Marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q.B(a): DSSS

**1. Definition (1 mark):**
> **DSSS** is a spread spectrum technique in which the original data signal is **multiplied (XORed) bit-by-bit** with a high-rate **pseudo-random binary sequence** called the **chip code**, producing a wideband transmitted signal.

**2. Terminology (2 marks):**

- **Chip:** A single bit of the pseudo-random spreading code. Duration = $T_c = T_b / N$, where N = spreading factor.
- **Chip Rate ($R_{chip}$):** Number of chips transmitted per second $= R_{bit} \times N$.
- **Processing Gain ($G_p$):** Ratio of spread bandwidth to original bandwidth $= R_{chip} / R_{bit} = N$. Expressed in dB as $G_p (\text{dB}) = 10 \log_{10}(N)$.

**3. Working Principle (3 marks):**
- A **pseudo-random code generator** produces a chip sequence of length N (e.g., 11-chip Barker code).
- An **XOR gate** combines each data bit with N chips.
- The spread signal occupies a bandwidth N times the original.
- At the receiver, the **same chip sequence** is used in a **correlator** to despread and recover the original bit.
- Any narrowband interference affects only a few chips, and the correlator averages them out → high interference immunity.

**4. Block Diagram (1 mark):**
> Refer to **Section 4.4 DSSS Spreading Block Diagram** above.

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q.B(b): TDM Numerical

**Given:**
- Number of channels: $N = 8$
- Sampling rate per channel: $f_s = 8$ kHz
- Bits per sample: $n = 8$

**Step 1: Bit Rate per Channel**

Each channel produces samples at 8 kHz, each sample being 8 bits wide. The bit rate of one PCM-encoded channel is:

$$
R_{src} = f_s \times n = 8000 \times 8 = 64{,}000 \text{ bps} = 64 \text{ kbps}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** This is the standard **PCM voice channel rate** — a key KTU recognition fact (PCM = 64 kbps).

**Step 2: Multiplexer Output Bit Rate**

The TDM multiplexer interleaves 8 such channels:

$$
R_{mux} = N \times R_{src} = 8 \times 64 \text{ kbps} = 512 \text{ kbps}
$$

**Step 3: Frame Duration**

Each source contributes 1 sample (8 bits) per sampling period. The frame must carry 8 samples, one per channel:

$$
T_{frame} = \frac{1}{f_s} = \frac{1}{8000} = 125 \text{ μs}
$$

> **Logic Insight:** The TDM frame duration equals the **sampling period**, because we must complete one sample per channel per frame.

**Step 4: Bit Duration of Output**

Total bits per frame = $N \times n = 8 \times 8 = 64$ bits.

$$
T_{bit\_output} = \frac{T_{frame}}{\text{bits per frame}} = \frac{125 \, \mu s}{64} = 1.953 \, \mu s
$$

**Verification using bit rate:**

$$
T_{bit\_output} = \frac{1}{R_{mux}} = \frac{1}{512{,}000} = 1.953 \, \mu s \quad \checkmark
$$

### ✅ Final Answer Summary

| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| Per-channel bit rate $R_{src}$ | **64 kbps** |
| Multiplexer output $R_{mux}$ | **512 kbps** |
| Frame duration $T_{frame}$ | **125 μs** |
| Output bit duration $T_{bit}$ | **1.953 μs** |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit conversion errors:** 1 kbps = 1000 bps (not 1024). KTU strictly uses SI prefixes. Mixing kbps and kBps loses 1 mark.
> 2. **Forgetting guard bands in FDM:** If a numerical problem mentions guard bands, the formula is $B_{total} = N \cdot B_{channel} + (N-1) \cdot B_{guard}$, **not** $N \cdot B_{channel}$. The "-1" trips most students.
> 3. **Confusing chip and bit duration in DSSS:** Chips are **N times shorter** than bits. Many students invert the ratio.
> 4. **Statistical vs. Synchronous TDM:** If the question says "no idle slots are transmitted," it is **Statistical TDM**, not Synchronous. Misclassification costs the comparison marks.
> 5. **No block diagram in 14-mark answers:** KTU ESE demands a **neat, labeled block diagram** for full marks. Skipping it deducts **at least 2 marks** even if the explanation is correct.
> 6. **Using $G_p$ without dB conversion:** If the question asks for processing gain in **dB**, you must apply $10 \log_{10}$. Writing the raw ratio is incomplete.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU 2024 Module 4 Revision — Print This Page!**

- ✅ **Multiplexing** combines multiple signals over one link. **Spreading** protects one signal across a wide band.
- ✅ **FDM** divides **bandwidth**; **TDM** divides **time**; **WDM** divides **wavelength (light)**.
- ✅ **Synchronous TDM** has fixed slots; **Statistical TDM** allocates slots dynamically with address bits.
- ✅ **T1 (US/Japan) = 1.544 Mbps**, **E1 (Europe) = 2.048 Mbps** — these are **PCM trunk** standards.
- ✅ **PCM voice channel = 64 kbps** (8 kHz × 8 bits). This number appears in 80% of KTU TDM problems.
- ✅ **TDM frame duration = 1 / (source sampling rate)** — not 1/(output bit rate).
- ✅ **Multiplexer output rate = N × source rate** (Synchronous TDM).
- ✅ **Guard bands** in FDM prevent adjacent-channel interference; always counted as $(N-1)$ in the bandwidth formula.
- ✅ **WDM** uses **optical prisms / AWGs** to combine/separate wavelengths. **DWDM** packs 80+ channels in the **C-band (1530–1565 nm)**.
- ✅ **DSSS** uses **XOR with a chip sequence** (e.g., 11-chip Barker code in Wi-Fi).
- ✅ **DSSS Processing Gain (dB) = $10 \log_{10}(N_{chips})$** — for 11-chip Barker, $G_p \approx 10.41$ dB.
- ✅ **FHSS** uses a **pseudo-random frequency hopping pattern**; both ends must be **synchronized**.
- ✅ **Bluetooth** uses **FHSS** (79 hops, 1 MHz spacing, 2.4 GHz ISM band). **Wi-Fi 802.11b** uses **DSSS**.
- ✅ **Spread Spectrum** benefits: **anti-jamming**, **low probability of intercept**, **resistance to multipath fading**, **multiple access (CDMA)**.
- ✅ **Why spread?:** A narrowband jammer only destroys a few chips — the correlator averages them out, recovering the original bit.
- ✅ **KTU Classic Numerical Set:** TDM with 8 voice channels at 64 kbps → **512 kbps output, 125 μs frame, 1.953 μs bit**.
- ✅ **Always draw a block diagram** in 14-mark answers; label every block; use **arrows** to show signal flow.
- ✅ **Remember the 5-class taxonomy:** FDM, TDM, WDM, FHSS, DSSS — examiners often ask "Classify and explain bandwidth utilization techniques" as a compulsory 14-marker.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transmission Media
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAMT632 - BASICS OF COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Transmission Media

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Transmission Media — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition (2024 Scheme):** *Transmission Media* refers to the physical pathway or *channel* over which electromagnetic signals carrying data travel between a **source** (transmitter) and a **destination** (receiver) in a computer network. It is the *Layer 0 / Layer 1 substrate* of the **OSI Reference Model**, sitting directly beneath the **Physical Layer**, and is responsible for the actual propagation of energy through space or matter.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for PEAMT632 (Basics of Computer Networks)**, transmission media is broadly classified into two high-level categories as illustrated below:

## 1. Classification of Transmission Media

### A. Guided (Wired / Bounded) Media
The signal energy is *confined within a solid conductor or dielectric waveguide*. Energy is guided along a physical path. Examples:
- **Twisted-Pair Copper Cable** (UTP / STP)
- **Coaxial Cable**
- **Optical Fiber Cable** (Single-mode & Multi-mode)

### B. Unguided (Wireless / Unbounded) Media
The signal energy propagates as **electromagnetic (EM) waves** through free space (air, vacuum). No physical conductor is required. Examples:
- **Terrestrial Radio Waves** (VLF, LF, MF, HF, VHF, UHF, SHF, EHF)
- **Terrestrial & Satellite Microwave**
- **Infrared (IR)**
- **Lightwave / Laser Communication**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Students must be able to (i) classify media, (ii) compare bandwidth, attenuation, EMI immunity, cost & applications, and (iii) explain *why optical fiber outperforms copper at long distances and high data rates* — a frequently tested concept.

## 2. Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — The "Highway System" of Data:**
> Imagine a city where citizens (data packets) need to travel from Home (Sender) to Office (Receiver).
> - **Guided Media** = A *toll road / metro tunnel*. Cars are physically constrained to the road, can't leave it, so they reach safely and quickly. The "road surface" (copper / glass) defines speed limits (bandwidth) and smoothness (noise immunity).
> - **Unguided Media** = *Open-air drones*. Drones fly freely through the sky, but they are exposed to wind (noise), rain (attenuation), and other drones (interference). The "airspace" (frequency band) determines how many drones can fly together without crashing (capacity).
>
> The **transmission medium** is therefore the *roadway + the rules of travel* that determine how fast, how reliably, and how far information can move.

## 3. Key Performance Metrics (Used Throughout This Module)

| Metric | Symbol | Unit | One-Line Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bandwidth | $B$ | Hz (or bps) | Range of usable frequencies |
| Attenuation | $\alpha$ | **dB/km** | Loss of signal power per unit length |
| Propagation Speed | $v$ | **m/s** | How fast the signal travels |
| Wavelength | $\lambda$ | **m** | Physical length of one signal cycle |
| Signal-to-Noise Ratio | $S/N$ | dB (dimensionless) | Useful signal strength vs. noise |
| Bit-Error Rate | $BER$ | dimensionless | Fraction of bits received in error |
| Propagation Delay | $T_p$ | **seconds (s)** | Time for one bit to traverse the medium |
| Throughput | $C$ | **bits per second (bps)** | Net successful data rate |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Anchor:** These seven metrics form the basis of *every* numerical problem KTU asks from this module. Memorize units — the examiner often deducts a mark for missing units.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 1. Guided Media — Detailed Technical Breakdown

### 1.1 Twisted-Pair Copper Cable
- **Construction:** Two insulated copper conductors (~ **0.4 – 0.8 mm** diameter) twisted helically around each other.
- **Why the twist?** Each twist cancels one loop of **electromagnetic interference (EMI)** from external sources (a technique called *transverse common-mode rejection*). This is why higher twists per inch = better noise immunity.
- **Two Sub-Types:**
  - **UTP (Unshielded Twisted Pair)** — no metallic shield, cheap, used in Ethernet **10BASE-T, 100BASE-TX, 1000BASE-T**.
  - **STP (Shielded Twisted Pair)** — wrapped in a foil/braid shield, used in **Token Ring, IBM Cabling System**.
- **Categories (TIA/EIA-568 standard):**
  - **Cat 5e** → 100 MHz, 1 Gbps up to **100 m**
  - **Cat 6**   → 250 MHz, 1–10 Gbps
  - **Cat 6a**  → 500 MHz, 10 Gbps
  - **Cat 7**   → 600 MHz, 10 Gbps (shielded)
  - **Cat 8**   → 2000 MHz, 25/40 Gbps (data centres)

### 1.2 Coaxial Cable
- **Construction (concentric):** Inner copper conductor → dielectric insulator → braided outer shield → outer PVC jacket.
- **Characteristic Impedance:** Typically **50 Ω** (Ethernet — *10BASE-2 / 10BASE-5*) or **75 Ω** (Cable TV / broadband).
- **Advantage over UTP:** Far superior shielding → supports much higher bandwidths (up to **~1 GHz**) and longer runs (up to several **km** for baseband).
- **Modern usage:** Largely replaced by UTP/Fiber in LANs, but still dominant in **HFC (Hybrid Fiber-Coaxial) broadband**, CCTV, and **cable Internet (DOCSIS)**.

### 1.3 Optical Fiber Cable
- **Operating principle:** **Total Internal Reflection (TIR)** of light inside a glass core surrounded by a lower-refractive-index *cladding*.
- **Two Principal Modes:**
  - **Multi-Mode Fiber (MMF):** Core diameter **50 µm or 62.5 µm**; uses **LED** source; suffers *modal dispersion*; range ~ **2 km** at 1 Gbps; cheaper.
  - **Single-Mode Fiber (SMF):** Core diameter **~8–10 µm**; uses **laser diode** source; virtually no modal dispersion; range > **100 km** at 10+ Gbps; more expensive.
- **Three Operating Wavelength Windows:**
  - **850 nm** (1st window) — MMF, cheap LEDs
  - **1310 nm** (2nd window) — SMF, zero-dispersion region
  - **1550 nm** (3rd window) — SMF, lowest attenuation (**~0.2 dB/km**)

## 2. Unguided (Wireless) Media — Detailed Technical Breakdown

### 2.1 Electromagnetic Spectrum Used in Networking

The radio spectrum used in networking is divided into bands (per IEEE / ITU-R):

| Band | Frequency Range | Wavelength | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **VLF** | 3 – 30 kHz | 10 – 100 km | Submarine communication |
| **LF** | 30 – 300 kHz | 1 – 10 km | Navigation, RFID |
| **MF** | 300 kHz – 3 MHz | 100 m – 1 km | AM radio |
| **HF** | 3 – 30 MHz | 10 – 100 m | Short-wave radio |
| **VHF** | 30 – 300 MHz | 1 – 10 m | FM radio, VHF TV |
| **UHF** | 300 MHz – 3 GHz | 10 cm – 1 m | Wi-Fi (2.4 GHz), cellular, UHF TV |
| **SHF** | 3 – 30 GHz | 1 – 10 cm | Wi-Fi (5 GHz), satellite, microwave links |
| **EHF** | 30 – 300 GHz | 1 mm – 1 cm | 5G mmWave, radar |

### 2.2 Propagation Modes of Radio Waves
- **Ground-Wave Propagation** — follows Earth's curvature; used below **2 MHz**.
- **Sky-Wave / Ionospheric Propagation** — bounces off the ionosphere; **2 – 30 MHz**; supports intercontinental short-wave radio.
- **Line-of-Sight (LOS) Propagation** — straight-line above **30 MHz**; used by **microwave, satellite, Wi-Fi, 5G**.

### 2.3 Terrestrial Microwave
- Frequencies: typically **1 – 30 GHz** (licensed bands: 4, 6, 11, 18, 23 GHz).
- Requires **parabolic dish antennas** of diameter **0.3 – 3 m**.
- Range: **~50 km per hop** (limited by Earth's curvature and Fresnel-zone clearance).
- Applications: **Long-haul telecom backhaul, cellular towers, WiMAX**.

### 2.4 Satellite Communication
- **GEO (Geostationary Earth Orbit)** — **35,786 km** altitude, period = 24 h, ~**240 ms** one-way RTT; covers ~⅓ Earth.
- **MEO (Medium Earth Orbit)** — 5,000 – 12,000 km; **GPS** satellites.
- **LEO (Low Earth Orbit)** — 160 – 2,000 km; **Starlink, Iridium**; latency **20 – 50 ms**.

### 2.5 Infrared (IR) & Lightwave
- **Near-IR** (300 GHz – 400 THz): used in TV remotes, IrDA.
- **Visible Light Communication (VLC / Li-Fi)** — uses **400–800 THz** LEDs; secure (no wall penetration); under research for 6G.

## 3. KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these 7 equations — they cover ~90% of numerical questions in KTU ESE on this module.**

| # | Formula | Meaning / Where Used | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $v = f \cdot \lambda$ | Wave relationship — basic signal physics | $v$: m/s, $f$: Hz, $\lambda$: m |
| 2 | $n = c \,/\, v$ | Refractive index of the medium | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\theta_c = \arcsin(n_2 \,/\, n_1)$ | Critical angle for Total Internal Reflection (fiber optics) | radians / degrees |
| 4 | $\alpha = \dfrac{10}{L} \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{in}}{P_{out}}\right)$ | **Attenuation** of signal through a medium of length $L$ | **dB/km** |
| 5 | $P_{out} = P_{in} \cdot 10^{-\alpha L / 10}$ | Received power after attenuation $\alpha$ over length $L$ | W or dBm |
| 6 | $C = 2 B \log_2 M$ | **Nyquist Capacity** — noiseless channel with $M$ discrete signal levels | bps |
| 7 | $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ | **Shannon Capacity** — channel with white Gaussian noise | bps |

> [!NOTE]
> **S/N in dB:** $\displaystyle \left(\frac{S}{N}\right)_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{S}{N}\right)$
> Always convert dB values back to *linear ratios* before plugging into Shannon's equation.

## 4. Engineering Real-World Utility
- **Telecom backhaul** — uses SMF for >100 km unrepeatered links (Shannon + low-loss fiber).
- **Data centres** — Cat 6a / Cat 8 copper for short-reach, MMF for switch-to-server, SMF for inter-building.
- **5G fronthaul** — mmWave EHF wireless + SMF.
- **Submarine cables** — SMF with 1550 nm + erbium-doped fiber amplifiers (EDFAs), spanning > 10,000 km transoceanic.
- **Rural broadband** — Fixed Wireless Access (FWA) at 5 GHz and 28 GHz mmWave.

# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exhaustive Mandate:** Every algebraic step is written out explicitly. No shortcuts, no skipped cancellations. This section is structured to *teach* the derivation, not just state the result.

---

## Derivation 1 — The Attenuation Equation

**Statement:** A signal of input power $P_{in}$ travels through a uniform medium of length $L$ kilometres and emerges with output power $P_{out}$. Derive the **attenuation per kilometre** $\alpha$ in **dB/km**.

### Step 1 — Define Attenuation in Decibels

By definition, the *total* attenuation over the entire length $L$ in decibels is:

$$
A_{\text{dB}} \;=\; 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_{in}}{P_{out}}\right)
$$

*Explanation:* The decibel scale uses a factor of **10** for *power* ratios (as opposed to 20 for amplitude/voltage). Because loss is always positive, $P_{in} > P_{out}$, so the argument of the log is $> 1$ and the result is a **positive** number — a *loss* expressed in dB.

### Step 2 — Per-Unit-Length Attenuation

Assuming a **uniform** medium, the loss per kilometre is simply the total loss divided by the length:

$$
\alpha \;=\; \frac{A_{\text{dB}}}{L} \;=\; \frac{10}{L}\,\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_{in}}{P_{out}}\right)
$$

This is the *attenuation coefficient* in dB/km — the standard KTU unit.

### Step 3 — Invert to Find Output Power

Starting from Step 1, solve for $P_{out}$:

$$
\frac{P_{in}}{P_{out}} = 10^{A_{\text{dB}}/10}
$$

$$
P_{out} = P_{in} \cdot 10^{-A_{\text{dB}}/10} = P_{in} \cdot 10^{-\alpha L / 10}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Tip:** When given a *power budget* in dB, you may directly compute $P_{out(\text{dBm})} = P_{in(\text{dBm})} - \alpha L$ — a **subtraction** in dB replaces a **division** in linear scale. This is the secret to fast KTU numericals.

---

## Derivation 2 — Nyquist vs Shannon Capacity

**Statement:** Distinguish the *noise-free* maximum data rate (Nyquist) from the *noisy-channel* maximum (Shannon), and explain when each applies.

### Step 1 — Nyquist: Discrete Levels, No Noise

If a channel of bandwidth $B$ Hz carries $M$ *discrete* signal levels, the **maximum symbol rate** (in symbols/s, also called *baud*) is $2B$ (by the **Nyquist sampling theorem** applied to baseband). Each symbol carries $\log_2 M$ bits. Therefore:

$$
C_{\text{Nyquist}} = 2B \,\log_2 M \quad \text{[bps]}
$$

*Explanation:* Doubling the levels $M$ does *not* double capacity — it adds $\log_2 M$ bits per symbol *per Hz*. So $M=4$ gives 2 bits/symbol; $M=16$ gives 4 bits/symbol.

### Step 2 — Shannon: Continuous Levels, With Noise

When the channel is corrupted by additive white Gaussian noise (AWGN) with signal-to-noise ratio $S/N$, the *number of distinguishable levels* becomes limited by noise. Shannon showed that the **maximum achievable error-free rate** is:

$$
C_{\text{Shannon}} = B \,\log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) \quad \text{[bps]}
$$

*Explanation:* Doubling $B$ doubles capacity; doubling $S/N$ increases capacity only **logarithmically** (a 3 dB improvement in S/N roughly adds ~1 bit/symbol of capacity).

### Step 3 — Bound Relationship

Because Shannon represents the *physical* limit and Nyquist represents the *engineering* limit, we always have:

$$
C_{\text{Nyquist}} \;\geq\; C_{\text{Shannon}} \quad \text{(for the same } B\text{)}
$$

A real system must therefore satisfy *both* — choose $M$ such that Nyquist $\leq$ Shannon.

---

## Derivation 3 — Critical Angle for Total Internal Reflection in Optical Fiber

**Statement:** A light ray travels from a medium of refractive index $n_1$ (core) into a medium of index $n_2$ (cladding) where $n_1 > n_2$. Derive the *critical angle* $\theta_c$ above which **total internal reflection** occurs.

### Step 1 — Snell's Law

At the core–cladding interface, the angles obey:

$$
n_1 \sin\theta_1 \;=\; n_2 \sin\theta_2
$$

### Step 2 — Condition for Total Internal Reflection

TIR occurs when the *refracted* ray grazes the interface, i.e. $\theta_2 = 90°$, hence $\sin\theta_2 = 1$. Substituting:

$$
n_1 \sin\theta_c \;=\; n_2 \cdot 1
$$

### Step 3 — Solve for the Critical Angle

$$
\theta_c \;=\; \arcsin\!\left(\frac{n_2}{n_1}\right)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical example (silica fiber):** $n_1 \approx 1.48$, $n_2 \approx 1.46$ → $\theta_c = \arcsin(1.46/1.48) \approx \mathbf{80.5°}$. The *acceptance angle* outside the fiber is then $\theta_a = \arcsin\!\big(\sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}\big)$, defining the **Numerical Aperture (NA)** — a key spec on every fiber datasheet.

---

## Derivation 4 — Numerical Worked Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem:** A copper twisted-pair link of length **8 km** has an attenuation of **3 dB/km**. The transmitter launches **0 dBm** of optical-equivalent power into the line. (a) Compute the total attenuation in dB. (b) Compute the received power in dBm and in milliwatts. (c) If the link is replaced by SMF fiber with attenuation **0.25 dB/km**, what is the *new* received power in dBm?

### Part (a) — Total attenuation
$$
A_{\text{total}} = \alpha \cdot L = 3 \,\tfrac{\text{dB}}{\text{km}} \times 8 \,\text{km} = \mathbf{24 \text{ dB}}
$$

### Part (b) — Received power
$$
P_{out}(\text{dBm}) = P_{in}(\text{dBm}) - A_{\text{total}} = 0 - 24 = \mathbf{-24 \text{ dBm}}
$$
Convert dBm → mW using $P(\text{mW}) = 10^{P(\text{dBm})/10}$:
$$
P_{out} = 10^{-24/10} = 10^{-2.4} \approx \mathbf{3.98 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mW}} = \mathbf{3.98 \,\mu\text{W}}
$$

### Part (c) — Replacing with SMF
$$
A_{\text{total,fiber}} = 0.25 \times 8 = \mathbf{2 \text{ dB}}
$$
$$
P_{out,\text{fiber}}(\text{dBm}) = 0 - 2 = \mathbf{-2 \text{ dBm}} = 10^{-0.2} \approx \mathbf{0.631 \text{ mW}}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Insight:** Replacing copper with single-mode fiber boosts received power by **22 dB** — a factor of **~158×** more signal at the receiver, which is *why* SMF dominates long-distance links.

---

| Parameter | UTP | Coaxial | MMF | SMF | Microwave | Satellite |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Max Bandwidth** | 500 MHz | 1 GHz | 1 GHz·km | 10 THz·km | ~30 MHz / hop | ~500 MHz / transponder |
| **Attenuation** | 10–30 dB/km @ 100 MHz | 5–20 dB/km | 3 dB/km | **0.2 dB/km** | 0.1 dB/km (free-space) | 0.5 dB/km incl. rain |
| **EMI Susceptibility** | High | Medium | **None** | **None** | Medium | Medium |
| **Security** | Low | Low | High | High | Low | Low |
| **Cost / km** | Lowest | Low | Medium | High | Medium | Highest |
| **Typical Range** | 100 m | 500 m | 2 km | 100+ km | 50 km | 36,000 km (GEO) |
| **Latency** | ~5 µs/km | ~5 µs/km | ~5 µs/km | ~5 µs/km | ~3.3 µs/km | **~270 ms (GEO RTT)** |

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation:** The transmission medium is *not* a passive wire — it actively injects **attenuation, noise, dispersion, and EMI** into the signal. The receiver's job is to overcome these impairments using equalization, error-correction coding, and amplification.

```mermaid
graph TD

    clad1 --> coat1

    buff1 --> jack1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Light is guided inside the **core**. The **cladding** is what makes *total internal reflection* possible (it has a lower refractive index). The **coating** prevents microbends. The **jacket** is the visible external sheath a KTU student would touch in a lab.
